title: HybridTopo: Topology-Aware Supervision and Inference for Connectivity-Preserving Road Network Extraction from Aerial Imagery
authors: Ozer Ozdemir, Asli Kaya Karakutuk
abstract: In this study, we address road network extraction from high-resolution satellite imagery with explicit emphasis on structural connectivity in addition to pixel-wise segmentation accuracy. Although modern deep learning models can achieve strong IoU values, topological discontinuities may still degrade routing usability. To address this issue, we present HybridTopo, a topology-aware supervision and inference framework that integrates topology-sensitive loss design, auxiliary centerline learning, and inference-time refinement for road extraction. Within the Massachusetts Roads benchmark used in this study, our analysis indicates that explicit topology-aware supervision is more strongly associated with structural preservation than the architectural variations evaluated here. All quantitative results are reported as mean ± standard deviation over three independent runs unless otherwise stated. Under the full pipeline, network fragmentation is reduced from 37.4 to 10.3 connected components per tile, corresponding to a 73% relative reduction with respect to the baseline configuration. In addition, APLS-lite evaluation based on random sampling of 64 node pairs indicates improved path-length consistency in the predicted road graphs. These findings highlight the trade-off between pixel accuracy and topological discipline and show that hybrid structural supervision can improve functionally meaningful road extraction on this benchmark, while broader cross-dataset generalization remains future work.

title: BERT and Local Vision Language Models in Hate Speech Detection on Small Multimodal Datasets
authors: Nathanael P. Solagratia, G.S. Thejas
abstract: Social media platforms have made it easier than ever for people to express their opinions. However, society now appears increasingly polarized, particularly online, where it often seems that someone is offended by almost anything.With the rapid pace at which the internet evolves, moderating platforms has become an overwhelming challenge, particularly as new forms of hate speech and cyberbullying constantly emerge. Detecting offensive content in low-resource multimodal settings, presents significant challenges. This study presents a deep learning framework that combines Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (BERT) and EfficientNetV2L to classify offensive content in multimodal form, leveraging text and images, alongside local Vision Language Models (VLMs) for offensive content classification. With a late fusion strategy, features from both modalities are merged for a more comprehensive offensive content classification. This study addresses the detection of multimodal offensive content in low-resource settings, demonstrating that locally deployed open-weight VLMs can achieve competitive performance relative to proprietary, flagship models. Results indicate that the best-performing configuration reaches an accuracy of 0.744 on the Multi3Hate dataset, suggesting that open-weight VLMs represent a viable and computationally feasible alternative for multimodal hate speech classification.

title: Proposal of AI-Based Magnetic Field Estimation Methods
authors: Haruna Takaki, Ryota Kinjo
abstract: We propose projection-to-field network (PF-Net), a deep-learning method for single-shot estimation of the three-dimensional vector magnetic field <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">${{{\bm{B}}}_{\bm{x}}},{{{\bm{B}}}_{\bm{y}}},{{{\bm{B}}}_{\bm{z}}}$</tex-math></inline-formula> on an observation plane from a single magneto-optical polarization rotation angle map <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">${\bm{\theta }}( {{\bm{x}},{\bm{y}}} )$</tex-math></inline-formula>. Although estimating three field components from a single scalar observation is underdetermined at the pixel level, PF-Net stabilizes this inverse problem by exploiting spatial context inherent in convolutional architectures, which provides implicit regularization in the form of smoothness and noise suppression. The model was trained and evaluated using 10,000 synthetic cases generated with the magnetic simulator MagTense. The performance of the model was compared against several baselines: a conventional analytical method, pixel-wise linear regression, and two methods based on regularized least squares (RLS)—namely, a variational RLS-based joint vector-field reconstruction method (JVVR) and an RLS-based two-stage potential-field reconstruction method (TPR). Under the fixed-test single-channel condition, PF-Net achieved MAE = 0.042 mT, rMAE = 0.458 ± 0.039%, and angle error = 0.420 ± 0.023 °, substantially outperforming all baselines. We further evaluated its robustness to nonlinear response, sensor-parameter variations, measurement-noise out-of-distribution (OOD) inputs, observation-model mismatch, and distribution shift. In particular, explicit regularization via in-plane divergence suppression (Div2D) contributed to improved robustness under measurement-degradation conditions. These results demonstrate that PF-Net is a promising data-driven framework for single-shot vector magnetic-field estimation in magneto-optical imaging.

title: Covert Communications: A Generative Spectrum Map Paradigm
authors: Han Jiang, Jiacheng Wang, Junsheng Mu, Chengwen Xing, Henk Wymeersch, Nan Zhao, Dusit Niyato
abstract: Spectrum map is emerging as an effective tool to characterize and visualize the radio environment for covert communications. However, current approaches for spectrum map acquisition still face significant challenges in terms of accuracy and adaptability in highly complex and rapidly changing environments. Generative artificial intelligence (GAI), with its powerful capability in data learning and generation, can provide a promising solution. In this article, we demonstrate a comprehensive study of GAI-based spectrum maps for covert communications. Specifically, we first classify spectrum maps, detail their construction methods from conventional to emerging approaches, and present a comparative analysis. Then, we review how GAI generates spectrum maps, illustrate their applications in covert communications, and highlight research gaps. To this end, we propose a novel hybrid framework with diffusion models to enable the rapid generation of fine-grained spectrum maps. We further present a case study to demonstrate its effectiveness in enhancing the covertness. Finally, three future directions for applying GAI-based spectrum maps to covert communications are outlined to further promote the research progress.

title: Structured Distilled 3D Gait Fields for Clothes-Changing Person Re-Identification in Videos
authors: Yubin Wang, Huimin Yu, Haoji Hu, Yongsheng Gao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: 3D gait representation, intrinsically tied to identity, is of great importance in tackling the challenge of video-based clothes-changing person re-identification (VCCRe-ID), where individuals’ clothing appearances vary across video clips captured over an extended duration. Most existing methods derive 3D gait features from sequential 3D skeletons or parameters of a predefined 3D human model (e.g. SMPL). However, the sparsity of 3D skeletons and the low-dimensional nature of 3D human model parameters not only limit their expressive power and discriminative ability, but also impede alignment with higher-dimensional appearance features. To address these issues, we propose Structured Distilled 3D Gait Fields (SD-3DGF) to learn better aligned, more expressive, and discriminative 3D gait features for VCCRe-ID. SD-3DGF defines distilled feature fields that evolve over time, allowing the simulation of 3D gait dynamics from temporal 2D descriptors. Specifically, it densely transfers frame-level shape knowledge from structured 3D space (i.e., 3D mesh surface) to the 2D image domain via 3D-2D knowledge distillation, and models local spatiotemporal correlations among the distilled features to extract 3D gait features. This learning paradigm makes 3D gait features more compatible with appearance features, as they are naturally aligned in the spatiotemporal domain. To fully exploit this potential, we develop a novel Modality-Representative Interactive Encoding (MRIE) module, which facilitates efficient gait-appearance interaction and joint encoding via global and local representative tokens. Experiments on both VCCRe-ID and standard video-based Re-ID benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of our method. The code is available at https://github.com/yubinwang2021/SD-3DGF.

title: MGAPep: LLM-Augmented Multimodal Graph Attention for Protein-Peptide Binding Site Prediction and Cross-Domain Transfer_supp1-3690953.pdf
authors: Xiangzheng Fu
abstract: Protein-peptide interactions drive peptide therapeutics, precision design, and biomarker discovery, yet most predictors underuse complementary sequence-structure information. LLM-augmented multimodal approaches offer a promising solution to these limitations. We introduce MGAPep, which fuses pre-trained large language model embeddings with protein sequence and structural descriptors via a residual graph attention backbone and a multi-head dual-attention module to capture fine-grained interface patterns. Leveraging large-scale corpora of protein fragment-peptide interaction data, MGAPep employs self-supervised pre-training, transfer learning, and task-specific fine-tuning to obtain rich, transferable representations. Extensive benchmarking shows consistent state-of-the-art accuracy for protein-peptide binding site prediction, with robust generalization to unseen proteins and peptides. The framework also transfers effectively across modalities, yielding superior performance to most baselines on protein-nucleic acid binding site prediction without architecture changes, underscoring broad applicability. Together with evidence that graph-enhanced LLMs improve biomolecular binding modeling, these results establish MGAPep as a general paradigm for protein-biomolecule interaction prediction.

title: Design and Commissioning of Control Algorithms for Optimal ECRH Power Injection in TCV’s Plasma
authors: F. Graziano, F. Braghin, D. Busi, S. Coda, C. Galperti
abstract: This work refers to the development, commissioning, and testing of a control algorithm to optimize the electron cyclotron resonance heating (ECRH) power injection in the Tokamak á configuration variable (TCV). The aim is to understand how the injected microwave polarization, set by corrugated mirrors in the waveguide, influences relevant plasma parameters, namely, electron temperature and stray radiation. To accomplish this task, real-time polarization control is required; therefore, a proportional-integral-derivative (PID) controller is devised, based on an identified plant transfer function of the polarizing mirrors. The performance of the above-mentioned controller is first tested on the real plant without a plasma discharge and then during plasma operations. Sinusoidal trajectories are provided for the polarization angles, and the temperature-polarization and stray radiation-polarization correlations are studied. This development paves the way for future feedback control strategies based on real-time diagnostics to optimize power coupling and prevent component damage.

title: Contour Optimization of DC-GIS Insulator Considering the Influence of Surface Charging Withstanding Transient Voltages
authors: Yu Gao, Pinhao Huang, Di Lu, Tao Han, Boxue Du
abstract: Surface charging on insulator in DC-GIS is influenced by transient voltages like lightning impulse (LI) and polarity reversal (PR), which threatens the safe operation of the equipment. In this paper, a contour optimization method considering the influence of surface charging is proposed for insulator used in DC-GIS of ±320 kV. The insulation reliability under steady DC voltage is promoted, and the property to withstand the transient voltages is guaranteed as well. The normal and tangential components of electric field under steady DC voltage are simultaneously minimized, and the constraints on electric field under the transient voltages are incorporated. The results show that, under the steady DC voltage, the maximal value of normal electric field is reduced by 5.8%, and the tangential field is simultaneously reduced by 7.0% on the insulator with the optimal contour after optimization. The maximal values of electric fields under the LI and PR voltages are 8.82 and 3.49 kV/mm, respectively, meeting the insulation requirements. It is suggested that, the proposed method incorporating the property withstanding the LI and PR voltages yields a more reliable performance of the optimized insulator in DC-GIS.

title:
Study of Heavy-Ion-Induced Degradation in Si
                    <sub>3</sub>
                    N
                    <sub>4</sub>
                    MIM Capacitors for GaN Power Amplifiers
authors: Samrat Roy Chowdhury, Isabella Rossetto, Carlo De Santi, Manuel Fregolent, Andrea. Carlotto, Fabiana Rampazzo, Francesco De Pieri, Alessio Pantellini, Marco Natali, Luca Latessa, Gaudenzio Meneghesso, Matteo Meneghini, Enrico Zanoni
abstract: This work discusses the catastrophic failure in thick SiN metal-insulator-metal (MIM) capacitors aimed at GaN power amplifiers and submitted to heavy-ion irradiation. Results show that: (i) breakdown (V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">BD</inf>) occurs at lower voltage under irradiation; (ii) <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$V_{B D}$</tex> and corresponding dispersion depend on the linear energy transfer (LET) of the ion species used; (iii) trend with LET does not depend on MIM area; (iv) thick SiN layers show cumulative degradation when tested with constant bias stress under irradiation; (v) degradation is identified by two signatures: a recoverable change in leakage current; a non-recoverable variation of charge trapping identified by the hysteresis in I-V curves which, after irradiation, can be modulated by both ultraviolet (UV) light and by the applied bias.

title: Predicting Error Types and Timing in Quay Crane Operations with eXtreme Gradient Boosting
authors: Robert Klar, Vangelis Angelakis
abstract: Efficient port operations depend on the disruptionfree operation of quay cranes (QCs), which transfer containers between vessels and internal trucks. As global container throughput rises, QCs face increased pressure, resulting in accelerated wear and tear. This can lead to QC downtime, which could interrupt the entire chain of port operations. Therefore, timely identification and prediction of critical errors is essential to enable timely maintenance to lower the risk of downtime. This study utilizes two years of QC monitoring data, enriched with weather conditions and terminal operational context, alongside twenty critical error events identified by the terminal operator. The goal is to predict the occurrence and timing of these critical errors through a three-stage machine learning model. The first stage predicts the type of the next critical event based on historical error patterns, warnings, and contextual data. The second stage estimates a time window in which the event will occur. The third stage refines timing predictions when more than one hour remains. The first two stages are formulated as multiclass classification problems, and the third as a regression task. All stages utilize eXtreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost). SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) are used to identify influential features. Results show that the model predicts the next critical error type with $83 \%$ accuracy and its immediacy with $71 \%$ accuracy. However, approximating the timing of events anticipated to occur beyond one hour remains challenging. These findings support proactive maintenance planning and operational adjustments, helping port operators mitigate disruptions and enhance QC reliability.

title: A Physics-Based Modeling Framework for Long-Term Retention Loss in 3D Nand Arrays
authors: Anuj Kumar, Tarun Samadder, Karansingh Thakor, Nikhil Rangarajan, Souvik Mahapatra, Shyam Raghunathan, Yingda Dong
abstract: A physics-based Activated Barrier Double Well Thermionic (ABDWT) framework is validated on Long-Term retention loss in 176-, 232-, and 276- layer 3D TLC NAND Flash arrays under different program levels, patterns, number of Program/Erase cycles and bake temperatures. Consistent segmentation of retention loss into contributing charge loss components is shown. This is the first framework applicable across multiple 3D-NAND nodes with consistent parameters, surpassing empirical methods and enabling broader applicability, versatility and robustness in retention loss modeling for 3D NAND.

title: Modeling and Simulation of a Three-Phase Photovoltaic Grid-Connected Inverter with P&O MPPT
authors: Xuefeng Kong
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: A three-phase photovoltaic grid-connected inverter with maximum power point tracking (MPPT) is modeled and simulated in this paper. To improve the power extraction capability of the photovoltaic array under varying irradiance, a perturb and observe (P&O)-based MPPT strategy is adopted in the front-end Boost stage. On the grid side, a synchronous rotating dq-frame double-loop control scheme is employed to regulate the inverter current and ensure stable power injection. Based on the overall system structure, an integrated simulation model including the photovoltaic source, Boost converter, dc link, three-phase inverter, and grid-side control system is established in Simulink. Simulation results show that the proposed system can track the maximum power point effectively under irradiance variations, while maintaining correct sinusoidal three-phase voltage and current waveforms. In addition, the grid voltage and current of the same phase remain nearly in phase, indicating a high power factor. The active power is maintained around 100 kW and the reactive power remains close to zero. These results verify the effectiveness of the proposed control scheme for both maximum power extraction and high-quality grid connection.

title: Design and Analysis of an UWB Conformal Antenna for In-Body Capsule Endoscopy Technology
authors: Robins Chowdhury, Sheikh Md. Rabiul Islam
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: This paper presents an ultra-compact conformal microstrip patch antenna with a meandered loop configuration for wireless capsule endoscopy (WCE). The antenna is fabricated on a flexible, biocompatible Kapton substrate <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\left(170 \mu ~\mathrm{m}, \varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}}=3.5, \tan \delta=0.0027\right.)$</tex> with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$100 \mu ~\mathrm{m}$</tex> copper metallization, occupying only <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$7.5 ~\text{mm}^{3}$</tex>. Its conformal design enables integration around the capsule's outer surface, preserving internal space for imaging and control circuitry. Electromagnetic simulations in muscle-equivalent tissue phantoms (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">${\varepsilon}_{\mathrm{r}} =52.7, \tan \delta=0.24)$</tex> confirm a wide operational bandwidth of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.1-4$</tex> GHz, covering MedRadio and ISM bands. At 1.5 GHz, the antenna achieves strong impedance matching <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\left(\mathrm{S}_{11}=-17 ~\text{dB}, \text{VSWR}=1.35\right)$</tex> and a power transfer efficiency of 98%. The realized gain of 1.9 dBi and quasi-omnidirectional radiation pattern with a 140.2° beamwidth ensure stable communication regardless of capsule orientation in the gastrointestinal tract. The peak 10 g-averaged specific absorption rate is <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.0718 ~\mathrm{W} / \text{kg}$</tex>, representing 3.6 % of the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2 ~\mathrm{W} / \text{kg}$</tex> safety limit and providing a 27.8:1 thermal safety margin. This design advances antenna technology for WCE and other implantable biomedical telemetry applications through extreme miniaturization, wideband operation, robust radiation characteristics, and high biological safety.

title: A Stochastic Model for Key Supply and Session Demand in Quantum Key Distribution Systems
authors: Hamid Taramit, Leduin José Cuenca Macas, Juan P. Brito, Vicente Martin
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Key material in Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) systems is a scarce resource that must be buffered and served to multiple applications. This paper proposes a stochastic model for the joint dynamics of key material and application sessions in a QKD system. We model these dynamics as a ContinuousTime Markov Chain (CTMC) with a Quasi-Birth-Death (QBD) structure and derive its stationary distribution using a matrixgeometric method. We introduce several performance metrics for the QKD system, including both key-based and session-based metrics. Through numerical results, we investigate the impact of the main parameters governing key supply and session demand on the overall performance of the QKD system. Our findings reveal the existence of operating regions within which these parameters can be tuned to meet desired performance targets. The proposed model and performance metrics provide concrete guidelines for designing session control and key management strategies in practical deployments.

title: Microscopic Self-Healing Mechanism of the Cellulose/Polyurethane Composite System
authors: Jiefeng Liu, Jing Luo, Xianhao Fan, Chuying Liu, Wentao Li, Junhao Song
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: Cellulose insulating paper is prone to the formation of microcracks in the early stages of aging when subjected to stresses such as heat, electricity, and mechanical vibration, which threatens the safe and stable operation of power transformers. Introducing microcapsules to endow the material with self-healing capabilities is an effective strategy to inhibit microcrack propagation and restore its mechanical properties. Therefore, this paper proposes a self-healing method for cellulose insulating paper based on isophorone diisocyanate (IPDI) microcapsules. First, a three-dimensional crack model of cellulose and its cured polyurethane product was constructed using molecular dynamics simulations to simulate and analyze the process of polyurethane filling the microcracks. Then, the diffusion behavior of the crack model was investigated under three temperature conditions (334 <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{K}, 364 \mathrm{K}$</tex>, and 374 K). Finally, the mechanical property recovery after crack repair was evaluated. The results show that the selfhealing process can be divided into two stages: morphological recovery and performance recovery. At 374 K, the repair system exhibits the highest diffusion rate and the most significant recovery of mechanical properties, indicating the best repair effect. The proposed method intuitively demonstrates the entire performance recovery process of transformers under operating temperatures and endows cellulose insulating paper with the ability to achieve self-healing without the need for external conditions.

title: Explainable Multitask Graph Network for Blood Glucose Forecasting and Hypoglycemia Risk Classification
authors: Muhammad Abdullah Sarwar, Sarmad Maqsood, Ali Amini, Irfan Abbas, Egle Belousoviene, Rytis Maskeliūnas
abstract: To effectively manage Type 1 Diabetes (T1D), it is helpful to accurately forecast glucose levels and detect hypoglycemia early, as timely intervention can prevent serious complications associated with hypoglycemia. Contemporary predictive models frequently treat both tasks as isolated processes and often exhibit limited interpretability, thus reducing their practical utility in clinical settings. This study introduces an explainable multitask graph neural network (MT-GNN) designed to simultaneously execute prediction and classification tasks. The framework is based on a physiology-inspired graph structure to depict multimodal signals, including continuous glucose monitoring (CGM), insulin, carbohydrate intake, and activity, within their physiological context, thereby elucidating significant interdependencies. The experimental evaluation utilizing the BrisT1D dataset indicates superior multitask performance, generating a coefficient of determination ($R^{2}$) of 0.95 for glucose prediction and an accuracy of $98.88 \%$ for the detection of the risk of hypoglycemia. MT-GNN has achieved a lower prediction error and significant discriminative ability compared to singletask graph neural networks and classical baseline models. The explanation tools were developed, which help with transparency, trust, and ease of use. Thus, the proposed model is a significant step towards safer, more proactive, and more effective diabetes treatment management, enhancing personalized care and providing a foundation for future clinical implementation.

title: Barrier Integrity Evaluation Methods in Copper Interconnects
authors: Kayla Sanders, Liang-Shan Chen, Ying-Hong Zhao, Ki-Don Lee, Phyo Thuzar, Pingan Fang, Manisha Sharma, Timothy Davis, Ju-Kwang Kim, Dung Dau, Mukyeng Jung
abstract: The electromigration (EM) immortality, exhibiting no sign of failure even after a prolonged duration of stress, can be attributed to the continuous supply of Cu from a wide underlying feeder through the via with poor barrier coverage. This type of barrier defect can artificially inflate the estimated EM lifetime, leading to a misleadingly optimistic reliability risk assessment in the lab. EM risk can also emerge in actual products due to the stringent design and aggressive scaling in advanced interconnects, especially with short-length EM rules. For EM risk mitigation, it is essential to have effective methods to reveal such barrier defects. This paper presents two PFA techniques for barrier integrity evaluation. The chemical etch method is very simple yet effective, and can be readily performed in general PFA labs. The STEM tomography requires an advanced TEM but can pinpoint the defect location.

title: DNACrypt: A Secure DNA and Steganography Based Post-Quantum Framework with Content-Based Keying
authors: Md Farhan Shahriyar, Md. Abdulla Al Mamun
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Post-quantum cyber threats with their increasing complexity and pervasiveness demand security solutions that go beyond conventional, isolated defenses. Cryptography protects the content of a message but reveals that secret communication is taking place. Steganography hides the existence of communication but does not protect the content itself. This paper proposes a novel hybrid security model that synergistically integrates bio-inspired DNA cryptography, AES encryption and Least Significant Bit (LSB) steganography. The framework introduces a deterministic key generation mechanism derived from the cover image's intrinsic content eliminating key exchange overhead and enhancing security. The secret message undergoes a dual-phase transformation: first, it is encrypted using a DNA-based XOR cipher conditioned by the content-based key and second, the resulting DNA sequence is further encrypted using AES in CBC mode. The final ciphertext is covertly embedded into the cover image using LSB substitution. Empirical evaluation demonstrates the model's efficacy achieving high visual imperceptibility (PSNR <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$>50 \text{dB}$</tex>, SSIM 1.00) and low Bit Error Rate (BER 0) confirming its resilience against detection and cryptanalysis.

title: Enabling Smart Radio Environments in the Frequency Domain With Movable Signals
authors: Matteo Nerini, Bruno Clerckx
abstract: Smart radio environments (SREs) enhance wireless communications by allowing control over the channel. They have been enabled through surfaces with reconfigurable electromagnetic (EM) properties, known as reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (RISs), and through flexible antennas, which can be viewed as realizations of SREs in the EM domain and space domain, respectively. However, these technologies rely on electronically reconfigurable or movable components, introducing implementation challenges that could hinder commercialization. To overcome these challenges, we propose a new domain to enable SREs, the frequency domain, through the concept of movable signals, where the signal spectrum can be dynamically moved along the frequency axis. We first analyze movable signals in multiple-input single-output (MISO) systems under line-of-sight (LoS) conditions, showing that they can achieve higher average received power than quantized equal gain transmission (EGT). We then study movable signals under non-line-of-sight (NLoS) conditions, showing that they remain effective by leveraging reflections from surfaces made of uniformly spaced elements with fixed EM properties, denoted as fixed intelligent surfaces (FISs). Analytical results reveal that a FIS-aided system using movable signals can achieve up to four times the received power of a RIS-aided system using fixed-frequency signals.

title: A Deep Learning Based Ensemble Approach for Digital Image Forgery Detection
authors: Anik Deb Nath, Sourav Adhikary
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: The rapid proliferation of digital media has raised significant concerns regarding the authenticity of images shared across online platforms. Image forgery, which involves tampering with visual content, can result in misinformation, legal disputes, and security threats. In this paper, we propose a deep learning–based system for image forgery detection that not only classifies images as authentic or tampered but also localizes the manipulated regions. The proposed framework integrates multiple CNN architectures trained on benchmark datasets, including CASIA and MICC, and employs a fusion strategy based on soft voting to enhance robustness. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed method achieves reliable detection performance across various manipulation types, such as copy–move and splicing. Comparative analysis with existing approaches shows competitive accuracy while maintaining strong generalization to unseen forgeries. Overall, the proposed framework offers a scalable and effective solution for combating digital image tampering, making it highly applicable to real-world forensic and security contexts.

title: Solving Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem with Quantum Alternating Operator Ansatz and Column Generation
authors: Wei-Hao Huang, Hiromichi Matsuyama, Yu Yamashiro
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: This study proposes a hybrid quantum-classical approach to solving the Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem (CVRP) by integrating the Column Generation (CG) method with the Quantum Alternating Operator Ansatz (QAOAnsatz). The CG method divides the CVRP into the reduced master problem, which finds the best combination of the routes under the route set, and one or more subproblems, which generate the routes that would be beneficial to add to the route set. This method is iteratively refined by adding new routes identified via subproblems and continues until no improving route can be found. We leverage the QAOAnsatz to solve the subproblems. Our algorithm restricts the search space by designing the QAOAnsatz mixer Hamiltonian to enforce one-hot constraints. Moreover, to handle capacity constraints in QAOAnsatz, we employ an Augmented Lagrangian-inspired method that obviates the need for additional slack variables, reducing the required number of qubits. Experimental results on small-scale CVRP instances (up to 6 customers) show that QAOAnsatz converges more quickly to optimal routes than the standard QAOA approach, demonstrating the potential of this hybrid framework in tackling realworld logistical optimization problems on near-term quantum hardware.

title: Burst Mode Triangular Pulse CV System for Reliability Analysis of Two-Terminal Multilevel Memory Devices
authors: Himanshu Marothya, Sandip Mondal
abstract: Reliable characterization of two-terminal multilevel charge-trapping memory devices requires fast readout, large-signal voltage sweeps, and integrated high-voltage program/erase capability. In this work, a burst-mode triangular capacitance-voltage (BMTCV) measurement system is presented for large-signal characterization of such devices. The system captures a complete CV curve within <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2 \mu$</tex> s using a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm 12 \mathrm{V}$</tex> triangular ramp and incorporates a high-voltage stress module capable of applying program and erase pulses up to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm \mathbf{3 0 ~ V}$</tex>. Unlike conventional small-signal CV techniques, the proposed approach enables ultrafast readout immediately after electrical stress, minimizing read-induced disturbance. The BMTCV system is applied to an eight-level CdTe quantum-dot-based two-terminal memory device. Clear separation of all programmed states is achieved, and systematic retention and endurance measurements are performed using the same measurement platform. The results demonstrate that the BMTCV system provides a practical and reliable solution for large-signal, low-disturb characterization of multilevel charge-trapping memories under realistic operating conditions.

title: Topology-Hiding Connectivity-Assurance for QKD Inter-Networking
authors: Margherita Cozzolino, Stephan Krenn, Thomas Lorünser
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: While QKD ensures information-theoretic security at the link level, real-world deployments depend on trusted repeaters, creating potential vulnerabilities. In this paper, we thus introduce a topology-hiding connectivity assurance protocol to enhance trust in quantum key distribution (QKD) network infrastructures. Our protocol allows network providers to jointly prove the existence of a secure connection between endpoints without revealing internal topology details. By extending graph-signature techniques to support multi-graphs and hidden endpoints, we enable zero-knowledge proofs of connectivity that ensure both soundness and topology hiding. We further discuss how our approach can certify, e.g., multiple disjoint paths, supporting multi-path QKD scenarios. This work bridges cryptographic assurance methods with the operational requirements of QKD networks, promoting verifiable and privacy-preserving inter-network connectivity.

title: Dependency-Aware Circuit Scheduling for Multi-Core Quantum Systems to Minimize Makespan
authors: Rajeswari Suance P S, Ruchika Gupta, Maurizio Palesi, John Jose
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Multi-core quantum computing architectures have emerged as a promising solution to the qubit scalability limitations of monolithic NISQ devices. Quantum algorithms are expressed as quantum circuits composed of single- and twoqubit gates. However, circuit scheduling in multi-core quantum systems remains largely unexplored. Reducing overall execution time (makespan), increasing core utilization, and hiding communication latency behind computation depends on effective scheduling. In this paper, we first introduce a layered scheduling approach as a baseline where quantum gates within the same layer are executed in parallel, while layers themselves are executed sequentially. We then propose a greedy scheduling strategy which schedules each gate as soon as all its dependencies and required resources are available. This allows finegrained parallelism across cores. Our evaluation shows that on real benchmarks, greedy scheduling achieves an average 40% reduction in makespan and improvement in core utilization. The results suggest that the use of intelligent circuit scheduling to exploit parallelism can greatly enhance the speed of circuit execution in multi-core quantum architectures.

title: End-to-End QKD using LEO Satellite Networks
authors: Sumit Chaudhary, Baqir Kazmi, Janis Nötzel
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: We propose a satellite-based Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) network that enables global-scale, end-to-end secure key exchange without relying on trusted intermediate nodes. The network is formed by a ring constellation of satellites that maintain persistent inter-satellite connectivity and support two configurations: a polar Type-I constellation providing global coverage, and an equatorial Type-II constellation offering continuous, terrestrial-like operation. End-to-end secrecy is achieved through the use of Twin-field Quantum Key Distribution (TFQKD) and a redundant XOR-based key-forwarding protocol, in which each forwarding step incorporates independently generated QKD keys from ground–satellite and inter-satellite links. As a result, the final secret key is never exposed to any intermediate satellite, eliminating the single-point vulnerabilities inherent in trusted-node networks. Scaling the network offers two benefits: improved security and higher key rates. Increasing the constellation size enhances security by forcing an adversary to compromise a larger number of nodes to break the protocol, while simultaneously improving link availability and key throughput. Using realistic uplink and Inter-Satellite Link (ISL) models, we compute finite-size secret-key lengths based on the Sending-or-not-sending (SNS)–TF-QKD protocol. Our results show that the achievable key rates scale favourably with constellation size, with Type-II constellations reaching operational continuity and generating multi-gigabit secret keys per day, demonstrating a practical route toward secure global quantum communication.

title: Relative Range-Doppler Map for Multipath Ghost Suppression in FMCW Harmonic Radar
authors: Feng Zhao, Yuanzhe Li, Tao Yuan, Zhenhua Liang, Weidong Hu, Hongqi Fan
abstract: Harmonic radar is particularly valuable in clutter-rich environments owing to its excellent clutter rejection capability. However, in frequency-modulated continuous wave harmonic radar systems, multipath propagation caused by strong linear scatterers generates ghosts on the range-Doppler map (RDM), which significantly increases the false alarm rate. To address this issue, we first establish a signal propagation model that explicitly incorporates the intermodulation effect between multi-path signals. This model reveals that the true targets and ghosts can be categorized into four distinct types, exhibiting midpoint-symmetric relationships (MSRs) on the RDM. To leverage this property for ghost suppression, we propose a new range-Doppler domain termed the relative range-Doppler map, defined with reference to the specific range and velocity. The proposed signal model and ghost suppression algorithm are validated by simulation and experimental results. Compared with the comparison methods, the proposed algorithm demonstrates a significant advantage in ghost suppression effectiveness. Furthermore, this study can provide a feasible solution for multipath interference mitigation in other radar systems where targets show MSRs.

title: Leveraging Ensemble Learning Models to Forecast Agricultural Yield in Bangladesh Under Climate Impact
authors: Shovan Samanta Turzo, Shafin Ibnul Mohasin, Mahamudul Hasan Sourov, Md Jahidul Islam Moon, Taifur Rahman, Md Muhasin Ali
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: The precise forecasting of agricultural productivity under climate change is essential for food security where many areas are sensitive. In this work, a multiclass classification problem is shown for categorizing crop yield as Low, Medium and High using climate and environment variables of Bangladesh. We apply the proposed method to a dataset with features such as rainfall, average temperature, drought severity, flood impact, cyclone frequency and air quality comparing the performance of SAINT (self-attention for tabular data), NODE (Neural Oblivious Decision Ensembles) and Support Vector Machine (SVM). Furthermore, we also consider a stacked ensemble. Results indicate that 99% accuracy is achieved by the ensemble model, which is superior to three single models: SAINT (95.33%), NODE (96.83%) and SVM (95.50%). The feature importance analysis indicates that rainfall and average temperature are the most influential features for predicting agricultural yield. This study indicates that hybrid models are effective in monitoring the climate impacts on agriculture, which can provide decision-making supports for stakeholders, policy makers, and agronomists.

title: Optimizing Resource Allocation in a Distributed Quantum Computing Cloud: a Game-Theoretic Approach
authors: Bernard Ousmane Sane, Michal Hajdušek, Rodney Van Meter
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Quantum cloud computing will play a vital role in achieving quantum supremacy by providing flexible allocation of resources for applications that demand extensive computational power. Such a platform allows various clients to run their quantum jobs (quantum circuits) without managing the quantum hardware and to pay based on resource usage. Therefore, defining optimal quantum resource allocation is essential to prevent overcharging clients and to help quantum cloud providers maximize resource utilization. Our approach involves examining the problem from a game theory perspective. We introduce a quantum circuit partitioning resource allocation game model (QC-PRAGM) designed to minimize client costs and inter-node communication. This is achieved by solving a convex optimization problem that finds the system's optimal cost while maximizing the number of local gates within a partition through the selection of the best qubit combinations. We demonstrate analytically that clients are charged fairly. Additionally, our simulations show that our solutions outperform traditional methods.

title: Quantum Approximate Optimization for Targeted Weight Removal in Large Language Models
authors: Don Roosan, Rubayat Khan, Avik Mahata, Mazharul Karim
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Large language models enable powerful text understanding and generation but can inadvertently memorize sensitive or disallowed information. We present a practical pipeline for targeted removal of such knowledge by operating directly at the parameter level. The approach combines classical attribution to nominate influential weights with quantum-assisted refinement and selection. Quantum Gradient Descent refines gradient signals to prioritize parameters most responsible for the hazardous snippet, and the Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm frames final pruning as a discrete subset selection problem that balances thorough unlearning with preservation of general capability. In experiments on a model intentionally imbued with a hazardous snippet, the hybrid method sharply reduced adversarial recovery of the removed content while maintaining near-baseline fluency after brief clean fine-tuning. The pipeline also showed greater resistance to re-learning the snippet under subsequent exposure, suggesting deeper disruption of the underlying representation. These results indicate that quantum-assisted pruning offers a viable path for robust, auditable unlearning in safety- and privacy-critical deployments of large language models.

title: Discontinuous Integro-Differential Equations and Sliding Mode Control
authors: Andrey Polyakov
abstract: The paper deals with analysis and design of sliding mode control systems modeled by finite-dimensional integro-differential equations. Filippov method and equivalent control approach are extended to a class of nonlinear discontinuous integro-differential equations and to a class of control systems modeled by infinite-dimensional differential equations in Banach spaces. Sliding mode control algorithms are designed for distributed input delay systems and for a heat control system.

title: Reinforcement Learning for Decoupling Capacitor Optimization in Post-Layout PDNs Using Transformer Architecture
authors: Haran Manoharan, Hanfeng Wang, Chulsoon Hwang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Signal and Power Integrity
abstract: In this paper, a novel transformer-based reinforcement learning framework is proposed for post-layout PDN (Power Distribution Network) decoupling capacitor (decap) optimization. The model is designed to be scalable and generalizable to any number of impedance observation ports, decap placement ports, and candidate capacitors. The proposed approach uses a sequential transformer decoder that learns to assign capacitors port-by-port, guided by impedance deviation and port priority features derived from the PDN's frequency-dependent impedance response. At each step, the model embeds the current system state, including the delta between observed and target impedance, and uses attention to select the most suitable decap from a candidate library. The model is trained via reinforcement learning, where the policy is optimized to minimize impedance violations and reduce capacitor usage. The architecture supports rapid adaptation to new decap libraries. Compared to traditional optimization techniques such as Genetic Algorithms (GA), which may require hours, the proposed model produces high-quality results in seconds, while maintaining adaptability across varying PDN configurations. The results demonstrate the model's robustness in handling real-world PDN scenarios with diverse port and component configurations.

title: A KAN-Based Decoupled Equalization Framework Towards Latency-Optimized 6G Optical Transport Systems
authors: Tianqi Zheng, Shen Hu, Kaihui Wang, Jiayi Li, Yifan Chen, Yuxiang Liu, Mingjie Zhu, Yujie Gong, Chengzhen Bian, Jianjun Yu
abstract: The demand for high throughput and low latency in 6G optical networks requires efficient equalization of complex impairments. Although neural network (NN) equalizers perform well, their computational complexity hinders real-time field programmable gate array (FPGA) deployment. This paper presents a novel equalization structure that decouples network updating from real-time inference by compressing layer computations into lookup table (LUT) mappings. Based on Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN) theory, the design achieves effective nonlinear equalization with low overhead. Implemented on FPGA via parallel LUT mapping, it attains ultra-low latency while improving sensitivity. Experimental results on a terahertz-over-fiber testbed show a 1.2 dB sensitivity gain at the HD-FEC threshold with only 8.53 ns latency, demonstrating strong potential for high-speed, latency-sensitive optical systems.

title: mmHSense: Multi-Modal and Distributed mmWave ISAC Datasets for Human Sensing
authors: Nabeel Nisar Bhat, Maksim Karnaukh, Stein Vandenbroeke, Wouter Lemoine, Jakob Struye, Siddhartha Kumar, Mohammad Hossein Moghaddam, Jesus Omar Lacruz, Joerg Widmer, Rafael Berkvens, Jeroen Famaey
abstract: This article presents mmHSense, a set of six labeled mmWave datasets to support human sensing research within Integrated Sensing and Communication (ISAC) systems. The datasets can be used to explore mmWave ISAC for various end applications such as gesture recognition, person identification, pose estimation, and localization. Moreover, the datasets can be used to develop and advance signal processing and deep learning research on mmWave ISAC. This article describes the testbed, experimental settings, and signal features for each dataset. The datasets cover both Wi-Fi and 5G signals. Furthermore, the utility of the datasets is demonstrated through validation on different downstream tasks such as pose estimation, gesture recognition, localization, and person identification. In addition, we demonstrate the use of parameter-efficient fine-tuning to adapt ISAC models to different tasks, significantly reducing computational complexity while maintaining performance on prior tasks.
