title: Power Quality Detection Based on AT-CoSaMP
authors: Lang Wu, Zeying Zhi
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: This paper proposes AT-CoSaMP (Adaptive Threshold Compressive Sampling Matching Pursuit), an improved version of CoSaMP incorporating an adaptive threshold selection strategy to enhance reconstruction accuracy in power quality signal detection. Unlike conventional CoSaMP, AT-CoSaMP dynamically adjusts the threshold during iteration to refine the support set, improving identification accuracy when signal sparsity is unknown or variable. To address the non-sparsity of time-domain signals, Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) is applied to enable frequency-domain sparsity, where both algorithms perform denoising and reconstruction. The denoised signal is then transformed back to the time domain via Inverse FFT (IFFT). Simulation results demonstrate that under identical noise conditions, AT-CoSaMP achieves superior reconstruction accuracy compared to CoSaMP.

title: Dual-Subspace Robust Predictive Control of Marine Propulsion Six-Phase PMSM Based on Composite Virtual Vectors
authors: Xiangjun Wang, Yancheng Liu, Haohao Guo, Fengkui Zhang, Shi You, Qiaofen Zhang
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: This paper presents a model-free robust predictive control strategy for a shipboard six-phase permanent magnet synchronous motor (six-phase PMSM) propulsion system, addressing challenges such as parameter uncertainties, load disturbances, and nonlinear harmonic currents under long-term heavy-load operations. The proposed approach utilizes a dual-subspace composite virtual vector set to simultaneously achieve precise current tracking in the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\alpha-\beta$</tex> subspace and harmonic suppression in the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$x-y$</tex> subspace, effectively reducing current distortion and energy losses in heavy-load conditions. By integrating nonlinear disturbance estimation with an ultra-local modeling framework, a model-free current controller is developed to enhance robustness against parameter variations. Furthermore, an independent model-free speed loop is incorporated to improve system adaptability in plug-and-play and variable-load scenarios. Simulation results under typical ship propeller load conditions demonstrate the superior performance of the proposed method in harmonic suppression, robustness, and dynamic response compared to conventional approaches.

title: Explainable Sentiment Analysis of E-Commerce Product Reviews in Bangla Using Attention-Based BiLSTM
authors: M. Humayet Islam, Rejowan Arifin Nayeem, Neha Khan, Sumiya Rahman, Faria Afsana Rimu, Abdullah Raian
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: In the age of digitization, customer reviews on ecommerce websites contain valuable insights on consumer satisfaction, product quality, and effectiveness of services. Sentiment analysis, a subfield of natural language processing (NLP), enables automatic classification of reviews to determine public opinion. In this work, a sentiment analysis system is presented for e-commerce product reviews in Bangla using both traditional machine learning models and deep learning approaches. A manually labeled dataset of 1,000 reviews and an augmented dataset of 5,000 reviews were used for training and evaluation. Word embeddings were applied for feature representation, and Explainable AI (XAI) with attention scores was used to identify key tokens influencing predictions. Among all models, BiLSTM with Attention achieved the best performance, with 94.00% accuracy on the original dataset and 98.80% on the augmented dataset. This study highlights the effectiveness of combining augmentation, deep learning, and attention mechanisms in enhancing automatic review analysis for e-commerce platforms.

title: Overshoot-Free Decoupling Control Strategy for Steer-by-Wire Systems based on Asymmetric Cost Function and Dual Neural Networks
authors: Yiting Huang, Zheng Wu, Bole Su, Yuan Yao
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: Four wheel independent steering (4WIS) could improve the stability and active safety of electric vehicles. Its steer-by-wire system needs permanent magnet synchronous motors (PMSMs) with high position tracking accuracy and fast dynamic response. However, a conventional proportional-integral-derivative (PID) controller faces a trade-off between speed and overshoot in high-dynamic scenarios, further causing trajectory deviation and degraded performance, especially considering the tire-road nonlinear disturbances. To address these challenges, an overshoot-free decoupling control strategy for PMSMs in 4WIS systems is proposed based on a modified dual-neural network (DNN) PID, which employs a DNN cascade structure and innovatively designs an asymmetric cost function for offline training. Besides, the particle swarm optimization (PSO) algorithm is involved to solve extremum trap problem caused by asymmetric constraints in the solution space. Simulations show that PSO effectively escapes local optima under step loads, which is different from conventional gradient-free methods. Furthermore, the proposed strategy significantly improves the dynamic response speed while strictly maintaining the zero-overshoot constraint, enhancing the handling stability of the 4WIS system.

title: Product Reliability of Commercial Vertical SiC Power MOSFETs Achieved by Optimized Screening
authors: Ayan K. Biswas, Daniel J. Lichtenwalner, Callie Woods, Shadi Sabri, Sei-Hyung Ryu, Brett Hull, Adam Barkley, Donald A. Gajewski, Elif Balkas
abstract: This paper discusses gate oxide reliability in SiC vertical power MOSFETs under gate and drain bias stresses. Excellent intrinsic and extrinsic reliability has been achieved via optimized processing and screening methodologies. Time-dependent dielectric breakdown (TDDB) and accelerated reverse bias (ARB) tests demonstrate that the reliability of the gate oxide in SiC MOSFETs is comparable to that of silicon devices.

title: A Multi-Die Self-Consistent Framework for Thermal Stability Analysis in Nanosheet 3D SiPs
authors: Yukai Chen, Moritz Brunion, Matthew Walker, Dwaipayan Biswas, Julien Ryckaert, James Myers
abstract: In nanosheet-era 2.5D/3D chiplet-based systems, strong temperature-dependent power introduces pronounced power-temperature feedback, rendering conventional fixed-power thermal analysis insufficient to assess thermal feasibility. In vertically integrated architectures, such temperature-induced power feedback can eliminate the existence of a stable steady-state thermal operating point, leading to thermal instability and runaway behavior that remain invisible in one-pass steady-state simulations. This work presents a multi-die self-consistent thermal analysis framework that explicitly couples temperature-dependent leakage with system-level thermal solving across heterogeneous 3D Systems-in-Package (SiPs), enabling direct evaluation of thermal stability rather than peak temperature alone. The framework is applied to a representative HPC-class SiPs integrating an N7 FinFET I/O chiplet and an A10 nanosheet compute chiplet with multiple 3D integrations, including face-to-back, face-to-face, and face-to-face with backside power delivery network (BSPDN). The results show that configurations deemed thermally feasible under fixed-power analysis can become thermally unstable once temperature-induced power feedback is considered, while enhanced package-level cooling restores convergence and exposes clear stability boundaries. These results highlight the necessity of self-consistent thermal stability analysis for nanosheet-era 3D integration and provide a practical basis for reliability-aware early-stage system-technology co-optimization.

title: Radiation Reliability of BEOL Compatible Ga-Doped Indium Oxide MOSFETs for eDRAM Applications
authors: Sharadindu Gopal Kirtania, Chengyang Zhang, Stuart E. Wodzro, Faaiq G. Waqar, Hyun Jae Lee, Dyutimoy Chakraborty, Jason Dean Yeager, Douglas E. Wolfe, Asif Khan, Shimeng Yu, Suman Datta
abstract: This work addresses a key bottleneck in radiationhardened computing: achieving high-density embedded memory in monolithically stacked (M3D) systems without relying on areainefficient SRAM or radiation-sensitive scaled Si access devices. We evaluate back-end-of-the-line (BEOL) compatible Ga-doped indium oxide (IGO) MOSFETs as access transistors for vertically integrated 1T-1C embedded DRAM (eDRAM) under both ionizing and displacement-damage radiation. Devices are subjected to<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">60</sup> Co gamma total-ionizing-dose (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\gamma$</tex>-TID) up to 10 MRad(air) and neutron fluence up to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0}^{\mathbf{1 5}} \mathbf{n} / \mathbf{c m}^{\mathbf{2}}$</tex>. IGO MOSFETs exhibit exceptional <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\gamma$</tex>-TID tolerance: transfer characteristics, threshold voltage (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{V}_{\mathbf{T h}}$</tex>), and effective mobility remain statistically invariant up to 10 MRad, and sub-gap density-of-states (DOS) extraction from multi-frequency CV confirms negligible ionization-induced defect generation. In contrast, neutron irradiation produces pronounced displacement damage, manifested as positive <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{V}_{\text{th}}$</tex> shifts, mobility degradation, and increased mid-gap and band-tail defect densities. Bias-temperature-instability (BTI) measurements show radiationaccelerated parameter drift; enhanced PBTI is consistent with activation of deep traps in the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{HfO}_{2}$</tex> gate dielectric, while increased NBTI is consistent with radiation-assisted hydrogen diffusion. Calibrated TCAD reproduces these trends, linking <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{\gamma}$</tex> TID primarily to modest interface-state increases and neutrons to broader/deeper defect spectra. System-level analysis indicates IGO-based 1T-1C eDRAM achieves <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{\sim} \mathbf{5 0 0} \boldsymbol{\times}$</tex> higher retention than silicon baselines and only a 2% retention penalty at 10 MRad (vs <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{\sim} \mathbf{4 2 \%}$</tex> for Si FinFETs), positioning BEOL IGO as a scalable access-device platform for dense, radiation-hardened monolithic eDRAM.

title: A Hybrid Deep Learning Approach for Mango Leaf Disease Classification: Integrating InceptionV3 and Vision Transformer
authors: Md. Faysal Hossan, Rayan Alam, Tarifuzzaman Riyad, Tahira Taposhi, Zarin Tasnim Prionty, Hasibul Hasan
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Early and correct identification of plant diseases is highly vital for sustainable agriculture, especially in highvalue crops such as mangoes. This research presents a Hybrid InceptionV3 plus Vision Transformer (ViT) model to automate mango leaf disease classification with high accuracy. Capitalizing on a large dataset of high-resolution images of the Rajbari and Pabna districts in Bangladesh, the model categorizes mango leaves into six disease classes: Anthracnose, Dried, Gall Midge, Healthy, Powdery Mildew, and Senescent. The hybrid model outperforms standalone models like MobileNetV2, EfficientNetV2B0, and InceptionV3 through the combination of local feature extraction abilities of InceptionV3 with global context sensitivity of ViT, rendering a whopping 98.89% accuracy. The model's effectiveness is also proven by ROC curve analysis with AUC values getting near 1.0000 for all but one class. The approach shows the power of merging CNN and transformer models, providing a robust solution to machine learning-based detection of mango leaf disease, which could have a significant effect on crop management and help advance precision agriculture.

title: A 1-kV HBM/250-V CDM Low-Leakage Back-Drive Protection I/O ESD Network Design in Nanosheet/Gate-All-Around Technology
authors: I-Cheng Lin, Ping-Chang Lin, Chun-Cheng Chen, Wen-Hsiang Huang, Jie-Ting Chen, Bo-Shih Huang, Che-Yuan Jao
abstract: A low-leakage back-drive-protection I/O (BDPIO) ESD network in leading-edge nanosheet/gate-all-around (GAA) technology is presented. The I/O leakage issue in the process with transistors of only thin-oxide devices has been successfully solved by ESD design of this work. The internal ESD bus (EBUS) active clamp is replaced with a properly sized two-stage diode string, by which pin leakage is reduced to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$<5 \mu \mathrm{A}$</tex> at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 2 5}^{\boldsymbol{\circ}} \mathbf{C}$</tex> and comparable to conventional FinFET designs using thick-oxide devices. Component-level ESD robustness of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1-\text{kV}$</tex> HBM and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$250-\mathrm{V}$</tex> CDM was achieved and further validated by TLP/vfTLP characterization results.

title: Entanglement Throughput Measurements and Capacity Estimates for Aerial-Inground Fiber
authors: Nageswara S. V. Rao, Muneer Alshowkan, Aneesh Ramaswamy, Joseph C. Chapman, Nicholas A. Peters, Hsuan-Hao Lu, Joe Lukens, Saikat Guha
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: The throughput of entangled qubit pairs per second (eqps) is a basic performance metric of quantum networks that provide the entanglement distribution capability. Over fiber connections, it is measured using specialized instruments, including photonic entanglement sources and single-photon detectors. Extensive theory has been developed to estimate the capacity of a generic quantum channel, which in turn is applied to estimate the maximum achievable eqps over a fiber connection. However, there is a gap in relating these two performance metrics, in part due to their disparate nature, namely, mathematical formulae of the channel capacity and specialized instrumentation for eqps measurements. We describe eqps measurements collected over testbed connections composed of aerial and inground fiber of lengths up to 45 km. We estimate the normalized capacity using the transmissivity parameter derived from single photon detector measurements. These estimates are then converted to capacity bounds on the connection's eqps using the source eqps rate derived from co-located detector measurements. The results indicate a consistency between eqps measurements and their capacity estimates, and provide insights into relating the parameters of analytic capacity estimates to physical measurements.

title: Quantum Advantage Without Real-Time Classical Communication in Networks with Tripartite Entangled Sources
authors: Leonardo Oleynik, Seid Koudia, Symeon Chatzinotas
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: The distribution of multipartite entanglement is a key requirement for scalable quantum networks. While LOCC networks are theoretically universal, their reliance on quantum memories limits practical deployment. LOSR networks, which avoid real-time classical communication, offer a more feasible alternative but are fundamentally constrained when limited to bipartite sources. We conjecture that these limitations can be overcome with higher-dimensional entangled resources. Focusing on a square LOSR network with tripartite sources, we demonstrate a fidelity of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1 / 2$</tex> with GHZ states, surpassing the bound established in the literature. This result provides a concrete example of quantum advantage in LOSR networks and opens new directions for entanglement distribution under realistic constraints.

title: Design and Implementation of a Dual-Mode Floor Mopping Robot
authors: Mohammad Al Hosan Kanon, Ilias Ahmad, Dipto Archishman Saha, Md. Al Amin, Nahian Tasnim, Puspita Mobarak
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Maintaining clean floors is essential for a hygienic environment. However, relying solely on manual mopping is labor-intensive and time-consuming. This paper presents a dualmode floor mopping robot, which is capable of both automatic and manual operation. Four DC motors are used for the robot's movement, and two separate motors rotate mop pads for floor cleaning purposes. In automatic mode, the robot uses two ultrasonic sensors on the sides and a front sensor mounted on a servo to detect obstacles or walls. For obstacle bypassing or wall avoidance with systematic navigation, it uses a gyrosensor to make accurate 90° turns based on a position-dependent decision algorithm. In manual mode, the robot is controlled via a Bluetooth module using a Bluetooth-enabled Android app, which allows the user to clean specific spots when needed. During mopping, a timed water pump reduces water waste, and a waterlevel sensor with an alert system notifies the user when a refill is needed. Tests in a 10×16ft room showed a 100% success rate in obstacle and wall detection. Additionally, the robot was able to complete four to five cleaning cycles on a single battery charge. Overall, the system provides an efficient and flexible solution for indoor floor-cleaning applications.

title: Cryogenic Mismatch and Variability in 22nm FDSOI MOSFETs for Digital Circuits
authors: Yihong Qing, Jinghao Zhao, Xue Fan, Valentijn De Smedt, Jeffrey Prinzie
abstract: This work evaluates mismatch and variability in 22nm fully depleted silicon-on-insulator (FDSOI) MOSFETs at cryogenic temperatures (CT). By measuring 3200 devices in total (LVTNFETs and LVTPFETs), mismatch data for the threshold voltage (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$V_{t h}$</tex>), the subthreshold swing (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$S S$</tex>), and the current ratio (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\beta$</tex>) were extracted using Pelgrom's scaling law, showing a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0 - 5 0 \%}$</tex> increase in the Pelgrom scaling factors at 4.5 K compared to 300 K. The drain-current variability increases at CT and is accurately predicted by the Croon model based on extracted parameters. At the circuit level, the static noise margin (SNM) for inverter-based SRAMs decreases at 4.5 K, degrading the inverter stability. The variability of SNM under normal supply voltage increases by 2 times at 4.5 K compared to that at 300 K. The overall inverter stability is worse, which potentially lead to functional failure of the inverter at CT. This work provides empirical data and validated models for advanced FDSOI-based digital circuits at CT.

title: Comprehensive Analysis of Hot Carrier Injection Reliability in FinFET LDMOS Devices with Various Trench Structures and Doping Configurations
authors: Sangku Park, Kwanyeol You, Jinwoo Kim, Wooyeol Maeng, Jaehyun Yoo, Taiki Uemura, Myoungsoo Yeo, Shinyoung Chuong
abstract: In this study, hot carrier injection (HCI) degradation test and simulation were conducted for fin-based laterally diffused MOS (LDMOS) devices with varying process conditions. Trench structure, channel length, and doping profile significantly influence HCI degradation. In particular, the interface doping profile in the drift region induces HCI degradation trends that differ from those observed in CMOS devices. Since these device process characteristics have a critical impact on both reliability and performance, careful device design that considers both performance and reliability under various process conditions is essential to provide optimal high-performance computing capabilities. Finally, an on/off HCI degradation model was provided to predict fin LDMOS HCI degradation under circuit operating conditions.

title: Integrating Deep Reinforcement Learning with a Hybrid GNN Model to Optimize Neighborhood Selection for Link Prediction
authors: Salman Monir, Sagor Hossain, Md. Rafiquzzaman
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Link prediction in knowledge graphs is a critical task that has numerous applications such as knowledge base completion, recommender system and question answering. One major challenge in link prediction using graph neural networks (GNNs) is selecting the appropriate neighborhood around each node. Existing models tend to have fixed scopes of aggregation, which results in inadequate capture of contexts and sub-optimality. We present a novel link prediction framework, which incorporates reinforcement learning (RL) into a hybrid model of Relational Graph Convolutional Networks (R-GCN) and Graph Attention Networks (GAT). The RL agent dynamically selects the size of the neighborhood around the node, and the hybrid model leverages relation-aware message passing and an attention mechanism to assign weights to the neighbors in order to prioritize the neighbors. We test the suggested model on various real-world data sets and also compare the performance of the model to other already existing state-of-the-art models, such as GCN, GAT, GraphSAGE and R-GCN. The findings show that our approach outperformed the baselines with regard to AUC, Precision, Recall, and F1 Score, confirming the validity of using adaptive neighborhood selection to enhance the accuracy of link prediction.

title: ES-YOLO: An Edge-Guided and Scale-Aware Model for Infrared Substation Equipment Detection
authors: Zheng Zhu, Jingcheng Wang, Qiong Wu, Junlu Xia, Rui Xia, Bo Zhang, Bo Xu, Wei Yang, Junjie Qian
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: Infrared object detection plays a critical role in overheating fault diagnosis and operational safety assurance for substation equipment. However, substation infrared imagery often exhibits cluttered backgrounds, blurred target boundaries, and substantial scale variation, which degrade detection and localization accuracy. To address these challenges, this paper proposes ES-YOLO, an oriented detector for substation infrared equipment in thermal imagery. Built on the YOLO11-OBB framework, the proposed method introduces an Edge Fusion Attention (EFA) module, which integrates structural edge cues into intermediate feature learning to strengthen contour-aware representation, alleviate background interference, and improve the characterization of weak-boundary targets. In addition, a Scale-Aware Refinement (SAR) module is designed to enhance multi-scale feature representation through progressive receptivefield expansion and dynamic scale weighting. Experiments on a real-world substation operation-and-maintenance infrared dataset show that ES-YOLO improves mAP@50 and mAP@50:95 by 2.8 and 3.4 percentage points, respectively, over the YOLO11 baseline, while achieving 118.2 FPS. These results indicate that the proposed method provides an effective and efficient solution for practical substation infrared equipment detection.

title: Design Principles for Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Structures for Vertical GaN Power Devices (Invited)
authors: T. Narita, K. Ito, H. Iguchi, D. Kikuta, S. Iwasaki, K. Kataoka, M. Kanechika, E. Kano, N. Ikarashi, K. Tomita, M. Horita, J. Suda, T. Kachi
abstract: High channel mobility of a metal-oxide-semiconductor (MOS) channel is the greatest advantage of a GaN power device. To attain high performance with reliable operation, traps and charges at the oxide/nitride interface should be controlled. Interface traps at the oxide/nitride junction reduce free electrons as well as the channel mobility. Electron injection into oxide traps under a positive gate bias causes a shift of the threshold voltage <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(V_{\text{th}})$</tex>, referred to as positive bias instability. Electron capture at interface and oxide traps can be controlled by a thin crystalline AIN interfacial layer (AIN-IL) deposited by plasma-enhanced atomic layer deposition. The quality and thickness of AIN-ILs are important design parameters that affect the channel mobility and the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$V_{\text{th.}}$</tex> In addition, their roles differ between <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$c$</tex>-plane and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$m$</tex>-plane channels. Hole traps near the valence band of GaN cause negative bias instability and can be controlled by an AIN-IL and Mg doping in the body layer. The design principles for a vertical GaN MOS field-effect transistor that exhibits a good balance between low channel resistance and high reliability are discussed.

title: Transient Short-Channel Effects Induced by Si $\delta$ Doping in Buffer-Free $\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{G}}=0.15 \mu \mathrm{m}$ AlGaN/GaN HEMTs
authors: F. De Pieri, C. De Santi, I. Rossetto, A. Carlotto, S. Roy Chowdhury, M. Saro, F. Rampazzo, H. Stieglauer, J. Grünenpütt, D. Sommer, Jr-Tai Chen, M. Meneghini, G. Meneghesso, E. Zanoni
abstract: The impact of deep-levels on the control and charge confinement of the channel has been studied in RF buffer-free AlGaN/GaN HEMTs on SiC. The adoption of Si <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\delta$</tex>-doping to compensate 2DHG, under high lateral electric field, leads to temporary degradation of the OFF-state and subthreshold characteristics. The degradation of the parameters is affected by the Si concentration and process condition, finding that thermal annealing could drastically improve the device behavior.

title: Research on Loss Assessment and Risk Identification of Natural Disaster Power Outages Based on Multi-dimensional User Portraits
authors: Wei Liu, Shenglong Zhu, Jian Ge, Chao Zhen, Zhengkai Zhang
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: To address challenges in disaster-induced outage assessment-coarse classification, static loss functions, and lagging risk identification-this paper proposes a framework integrating multi-dimensional user portraits with machine learning. We construct a “Disaster-Grid-User” portrait system and employ Feature-Weighted Adaptive Clustering (FWAC) to refine user segmentation. A library of dynamic, multi-variable loss models replaces static equations for diverse user types and scenarios, while <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$5 \text{km} \times 5 \text{km}$</tex> grid technology enables a four-level risk early warning mechanism. Validated on 34 disasters in Anhui Province, the framework reduces MAPE for industrial and commercial users to 9.7% and 11.9%, outperforming Logistic Regression and SVR. By precisely identifying high-risk zones, this study offers robust tools for enhancing grid resilience, optimizing resource allocation, and guiding rapid recovery.

title: Interpretable and Explainable AI for Predicting Systems Thinking Skills
authors: Saroj Raut, Fazla Rabbi, Niamat Ullah Ibne Hossain, Raed Jaradat, Alex Gorod, Randy Buchanan
abstract: Rapid technological advancements in recent years have led to escalating complexity in the modern systems environment. As a result, many contemporary challenges may exceed engineers’ ability to operate effectively within today’s highly complex systems landscape, where systems thinking skills are particularly important. This study aimed at applying Interpretable Machine Learning (ML) algorithms to assess individual systems thinking (ST) ability in light of seven essential ST dimensions, including Level of - Complexity (CMP), Independence (IND), Interaction (INT), Change (CNG), Uncertainty (UNCR), Flexibility (FLEX), and Systems Worldview (SYSW). Consequently, several ML algorithms, including Logistic Regression (LR), AdaBoost, Artificial Neural Network (ANN), Random Forest (RF), and K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) were trained and evaluated to classify participants’ ST levels across these dimensions. In addition, all the models underwent hyperparameter tuning to improve predicting capabilities, with an optimal model selected based on comparison analysis that can better forecast an individual’s ST skills. Further, a set of explainable ML analysis (XAI) tools, such as Brier Score, Youden Index, LIME, SHAP force, and summary plots, has been generated to offer further insights into the underlying ML models. The comparative analysis indicates that LR outperformed other models with respect to all four performance metrics (Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1 score). The study’s model can assist industries in adopting systems thinking principles for both hiring candidates and workforce development. Moreover, integration of XAI enhances the validity of the analysis and enables more intelligent decision-making.

title: Development and Validation of a Freehand 3D Ultrasound System for Real-Time Intraoral Image Reconstruction
authors: Hongbo Chen, Logiraj Kumaralingam, Javaneh Alavi, Kim-Cuong T. Nguyen, Thanh-Giang La, Rui Zheng, Trang H. Hoang, Dean Ta, Kumaradevan Punithakumar, Michelle Noga, Paul W. Major, Edmond H. M. Lou, Lawrence H. Le
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Two-dimensional (2D) ultrasonography has shown great potential for evaluating periodontal structures due to its portability, flexibility, and non-ionizing nature. However, for clinical applications, three-dimensional (3D) ultrasound imaging of oral anatomy is essential. This study presents the development of a freehand 3D intraoral imaging system using high-frequency ultrasound for reconstructing the 3D anatomical structures of teeth. The proposed hand-held system integrates an in-house designed ultrasound transducer with positioning markers and four optical cameras to acquire 2D ultrasound images along with corresponding 3D tracking poses. A forward-mapping algorithm was employed for 3D data fusion to enable real-time imaging. Both dimensional measurements and 3D visualized difference colormaps were generated for a total of 24 teeth from maxillary and mandibular phantoms to compare the proposed system with an optical scanner and cone-beam computed tomography (CBCT). The mean distance differences ranged from -0.13 to 0.18 mm versus the optical scanner and from -0.18 to 0.15 mm versus CBCT. The overall mean absolute difference across all dimensional measurements of two raters were 0.17 mm for optical scanner and 0.16 mm for CBCT, respectively. The correlation coefficient with both reference modalities exceeded 0.98. The average model-to-model distance between the 3D volumes acquired from the proposed system and the corresponding reference volumes was approximately 0.20 mm for the optical scanner and 0.28 mm for CBCT. The proposed 3D intraoral imaging system demonstrated the feasibility of achieving high-quality 3D intraoral imaging in real time, showing the potential to significantly accelerate the clinical adoption of ultrasound technology in dentistry.

title: Experimental Validation of Precision Control in Low-Friction Seal-Type and Polymer-Coated Seal-Less Hydraulic Actuators
authors: Jaeyoung Kim, Kyung-Rok Kim, Dongwoo Kang, Hyunchang Kim
abstract: This paper presents an experimental investigation of frequency-response characteristics and precision motion control performance in low-friction seal-type and polymer-coated seal-less hydraulic actuators operating under high-pressure conditions. Instead of relying on detailed parametric modeling, frequency-response-function (FRF) analysis is utilized to identify dominant resonance characteristics and bandwidth constraints arising from coupled hydraulic–mechanical dynamics under realistic operating conditions. Based on the measured FRFs, a feedback–feedforward control strategy with resonance attenuation is implemented to achieve stable and repeatable precision motion within practically achievable closed-loop bandwidths. Precision control performance is evaluated through sinusoidal tracking and micro-stepping control experiments under closed-loop bandwidths (CBW) of 10, 20, and 50 Hz. In sinusoidal tracking, the polymer-coated seal-less actuator reduces tracking error near velocity reversal points under identical operating conditions. Across all cases of micro-stepping tests, steady-state positioning accuracy is governed by the selected control bandwidth rather than by actuator structure. Dual-actuator gap control experiments further demonstrate micrometer-level dynamic gap tracking and sub-micrometer-level steady-state gap control, confirming the applicability of the proposed framework to synchronized multi-actuator hydraulic systems.

title: Decentralized Parametric Stability Certificates for Grid-Forming Converter Control
authors: Verena Häberle, Xiuqiang He, Linbin Huang, Florian Dörfler, Steven Low
publish: IEEE Transactions on Control of Network Systems
abstract: We propose a decentralized framework to analytically guarantee the small-signal stability of future power systems with grid-forming converters. Our approach leverages dynamic loop-shifting techniques to compensate for the lack of passivity in the network dynamics and establishes decentralized parametric stability certificates, depending on the local device-level controls and incorporating the effects of the network. By following practical tuning rules, we are able to ensure plug-and-play operation without centralized coordination. Unlike prior works, our approach accommodates coupled frequency and voltage dynamics, incorporates network dynamics, and does not rely on specific network configurations, offering a general and scalable solution for the integration of power-electronics-based devices into future power systems. We validate our theoretical stability results through numerical case studies in a high-fidelity simulation model.

title: Automated Iterative Analytical Modeling of Transient Electromagnetic Disturbance Coupling to Multiconductor Transmission Lines
authors: Ming-yue Gou, Yan-zhao Xie, Jun Guo, Ning Dong, Qing Dou
abstract: Building upon the distributed analytical representation and iterative technique (DARIT-field) derived from frequency-domain waveform relaxation, this article presents DARIT-auto: an efficient automated iterative technique for transient electromagnetic disturbance (TED) radiated field coupling to multiconductor transmission lines (MTLs). While the original DARIT-field method yields analytical solutions through a four-step iteration and demonstrates superior efficiency compared to the conventional chain parameter matrix method for a large number of conductors, strongly coupled scenarios may require more iteration steps to meet prescribed accuracy requirements. Furthermore, although the DARIT-field framework theoretically allows for additional iterations to enhance accuracy, manual derivation of voltage and current expressions along the lines beyond four iterations becomes impractical due to the rapid escalation of algebraic complexity. To address these limitations, the DARIT-auto method automatically derives analytical equations for arbitrary iteration steps without the need for manual derivation. This work implements a unified treatment using Taylor expansion to resolve propagation constant challenges, guaranteeing both analytical consistency and numerical stability regardless of the line configuration. A key contribution of this work is the development of an automated framework that systematically generates analytical expressions for MTLs voltages and currents across arbitrary iteration steps. Extensive validation demonstrates that the DARIT-auto method could efficiently handle TED coupling to MTLs with large conductor counts and provides the flexibility to balance computational overhead and accuracy by adjusting the number of iterations.

title: Autoencoder-Based Constellation Redesign for Joint Operation of Dual Geostationary Earth Orbit Satellites
authors: Seungcheol Ha, Seongmin Pyo, Dong-Hyo Lee, Taehoon Kim, Inkyu Bang
abstract: In satellite communication systems, efficient utilization of limited frequency resources has become a critical challenge as the number of satellites sharing orbital and spectral domains increases. To address this issue, this paper proposes an autoencoder-based constellation redesign framework that enables the joint operation of dual geostationary Earth orbit (GEO) satellites with a single ground base station. Our proposed framework leverages an autoencoder to jointly learn end-to-end signal encoding and decoding processes, thereby enabling adaptive reconstruction of superimposed signals under satellite channel conditions. Accordingly, our proposed framework allows multiple GEO satellites to transmit simultaneously over a shared frequency band, while a single ground base station adaptively reconfigures the learned constellations to achieve precise separation and reconstruction of overlapped signals. Simulation results demonstrate that simultaneous operation of dual GEO satellites can be feasible with only a single ground station, while achieving superior symbol error rate performance compared to conventional receivers that employ joint maximum likelihood detection or successive interference cancellation.

title: An Explainable KAN&#x2013;Shapley Framework for Many-to-Many Electric Vehicle Charging Dispatch in Intelligent Transportation Systems_supp1-3688187.pdf
authors: Chih-Yung Chang
abstract: Mobile Charging Services (MCS) can alleviate the shortage of fixed charging infrastructure, but real-world dispatching in dense cities must jointly handle time-varying traffic and grid-price signals, hard feasibility constraints such as SOC safety, service time windows, and service regions, and the need for auditable decisions. Existing optimization-based schedulers rely on hand-crafted costs and provide limited decision transparency, whereas end-to-end learning-based dispatchers are often opaque and may violate constraints at inference time. This paper proposes an Explainable KAN–Shapley Dispatch Network (XKSDN) for many-to-many dispatch between EV charging request clusters and multiple Mobile Charging Vehicles (MCVs). XKSDN first abstracts massive requests via spatiotemporal–energy clustering, and then learns a structured utility function with a Kolmogorov–Arnold Network (KAN), whose edge-wise one-dimensional spline functions expose nonlinear effects such as distance decay and SOC-urgency transitions. To turn learned utilities into executable schedules, a constraint-aware matching layer integrates feasibility masking (Big-<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$M$</tex-math> </inline-formula>), virtual nodes for partial matching, and node replication for limited MCV capacity, and solves the resulting assignment with an improved Hungarian algorithm. For accountability, XKSDN computes local Shapley-value attributions within clusters, quantifying the marginal contributions of distance, SOC, congestion, and price to each dispatch command. Experiments on New York City trip data with SUMO simulations show that XKSDN improves service success rate and response delay while reducing operational cost against representative optimization and deep learning baselines, and provides consistent, auditable explanations for safety-critical deployments.

title: OccSora: 4D Occupancy Generation Models as World Simulators for Autonomous Driving
authors: Lening Wang, Wenzhao Zheng, Yilong Ren, Han Jiang, Zhiyong Cui, Haiyang Yu, Jiwen Lu
abstract: Understanding the evolution of 3D scenes is crucial for autonomous driving. While conventional methods describe scene development through individual instance motions, world models provide a generative framework for modeling overall scene dynamics. However, most existing approaches rely on autoregressive next-token prediction, which suffers from error accumulation and limited global spatiotemporal reasoning, leading to degraded long-term consistency. To address these issues, we propose a diffusion-based 4D occupancy generation model, OccSora, to simulate 3D world evolution for autonomous driving. A 4D scene tokenizer is introduced to obtain compact spatiotemporal representations and enable high-quality reconstruction of long occupancy sequences. We then train a diffusion transformer on these representations to generate 4D occupancy conditioned on trajectory prompts. Experiments on the nuScenes dataset with Occ3D annotations show that OccSora can generate 16s videos with authentic 3D layout and strong temporal consistency. With trajectory-aware 4D generation, OccSora has the potential to serve as a world simulator for autonomous driving decisionmaking.

title: Addressing Class Imbalance in CNN-LSTM Intrusion Detection Systems
authors: Mohammod Ehsanul Haque, Lydia Bouzar-Benlabiod
abstract: Machine learning-based intrusion detection systems (IDS) play a critical role in modern cybersecurity; however, their effectiveness is often limited by severe class imbalance in network traffic datasets. In datasets such as UNSW-NB15, normal traffic dominates the data, while several attack categories occur only rarely. As a result, classifiers trained on such data may achieve high overall accuracy while failing to detect minority-class attacks. This paper investigates the impact of data balancing on IDS performance using a hybrid CNN-LSTM architecture designed to effectively capture both spatial features and temporal dependencies within network flows. To mitigate class imbalance, the Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (SMOTE) is applied during preprocessing to generate synthetic samples for under-represented attack classes. Model performance is evaluated using imbalance-aware metrics to ensure reliability. Results show substantial improvements in minority-class detection, particularly for Worms $(+43.24 \%)$, Backdoor $(+38.30 \%)$, Shellcode $(+29.06 \%)$, and Analysis $(+16.91 \%)$, while maintaining competitive overall accuracy.

title: Automatic 0 to 3150kVA Distribution Transformer Winding Machine using PLC, VFD, HMI
authors: Md. Ashiqueuzzaman Rupom, Anik Sarker, Rakesh Karmaker, Srimanti Roychoudhury
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Automatic <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{0 - 3 1 5 0 k V A}$</tex> Distribution Transformer Winding Machine is an invention in the manufacture of transformers. It involves smart automation during the winding of the transformers. The manual and semi-automatic methods of winding that have been used traditionally may be labor intensive and time consuming and may not be consistent in winding that affects quality and reliability of transformers. To overcome these shortcomings, the proposed system has been established to automate the winding process of large-scale distribution transformers of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0-3150 \text{kVA}$</tex>. The system is a combination of a Programmable Logic Controller (PLC), Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs), a Human-Machine Interface (HMI), encoder feedback and gear motors to provide synchronized, accurate, and efficient control of the winding operations. The PLC is the main controller and it connects with the HMI and VFDs via the Modbus protocols (RS232 and RS485) so that operators can adjust several critical parameters like the motor speed, total coils turn, tapping settings, and can monitor performance in real time. The system includes the important functions like automatic coil layering, the number of turns, the control of speed and tension, reducing the number of human errors and improving the winding consistency. The control logic is designed to guarantee proper alignment of the wire feeding and bobbin motors to achieve consistency in tension and produce smooth coils. The prototype has been experimentally tested and the results have shown that the system can maintain good quality winding and remain stable in different load conditions. The productivity was enhanced to about <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$8-15$</tex> coils per day as opposed to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2-3$</tex> coils in manual processes that uses more manpower and total production costs. In addition to that, operational reliability is guaranteed by inbuilt safety measures such as proximity and limit switches, encoder feedback and emergency stop features. To sum it up, this automated PLC-winding solution provides an efficient, costeffective and scalable manufacturing with the Industry 4.0 standards by guaranteeing the precision, minimizing manpower and optimized production in the transformer manufacturing environment.

title: Contracts over Clusters: From Mission Guarantees to System Verification
authors: Loïc Lagadec, Ciprian Teodorov, Loïc Plassart, Jannik Laval, Charbel Geryes Aoun
abstract: We present a three-level framework for autonomous UAV management-architecture (nodes, clusters, fleets), process (mission coordination, data-to-knowledge), and business (mis-sion/KPIs/decisions)-implemented with an MBSE pipeline that derives metrics for verification bases on simulation. A key contribution is a two-tier assurance scheme: mission-level discrete-event simulation establishes local guarantees (continuous coverage, redundancy, bounded drift), which are lifted via assume-guarantee contracts to fleet-level geometric checks (inter-cluster hull disjointness), serialized by a simple lock/barrier protocol. We provide a tool environment for visualization, simulation, and dynamic reconfiguration (e.g., weight updates, cluster recomposition). A surface-coverage case study demonstrates feasibility and measurable properties; we discuss limitations and extensions.

title: Lackadaisical Quantum Walk Search on Dicyclic Cayley Graphs
authors: Rou Yan Ng, Yong Qing Tiong, Kai Lin Ong
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Lackadaisical quantum walks are coined quantum walk models in which each vertex is associated with a weighted self-loop, allowing the walker to remain at its current position. They have been shown to effectively enhance spatial search on various graphs under different oracle models, with the cyclic graph being the most fundamental instance. In this paper, lackadaisical quantum walk search is studied on Cayley graphs of dicyclic groups, a non-abelian extension of cyclic groups, under three different oracle models. A preliminary analysis of the search performance is first conducted on dicyclic Cayley graphs with respect to the minimal generating set of the underlying dicyclic group. By expanding the generating sets to enable symmetric exploration and applying an appropriate oracle, the quantum walk search achieves a near-one success probability. Under our proposed configurations, the expected runtime to search on dicyclic Cayley graphs outperforms both quantum walk search on the cycle graph and classical unstructured search.

title: LAUREL: Logic Dependency-Aware Qubit Routing via Reinforcement Learning
authors: Ming-Bang Fan, Cheng-Yun Hsieh, Tzu Hsuan Chiu, Chun-Yi Lee, James Chien-Mo Li
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Traditional routing methods in quantum computing often fail to simultaneously optimize circuit depth and the number of gates. This study introduces LAUREL, a novel framework that addresses these challenges with four innovative techniques. First, we incorporate a dependency graph to model RL states and enable the exploration of different gate execution orders. Second, we introduce a dependency-graph-based lookahead window to enhance action selection. Third, we adapt the concept of move and introduce a 'chain lock' mechanism to manage the vast action space. Fourth, we implement a reward factorization approach to mitigate sparse reward issues. Our experiments on modern devices and realistic circuits show that LAUREL optimizes circuit depth more effectively than state-of-the-art approaches while preserving competitive gate numbers. LAUREL outperforms QRoute in terms of gate count and depth for random circuit deployment on Google Sycamore by approximately 90 %.

title: Demonstration and Quantification of Trap State Relaxation in Sic Mosfets
authors: Yanghao Wang, Hang Xu, Jianbing Guo, Peng Liao, Dong Zou, Zhen Wang, Qingqing Sun, David Wei Zhang
abstract: We report the first systematic investigation of trap relaxation in SiC MOSFETs. Wafer-level amplifier testing and pulsed I-V characterization demonstrate impacts from circuit to device. Modified three-terminal charge pumping measurements (T-CP) and Constant-capacitance deep-level transient spectroscopy (CC-DLTS) pinpoint deep-level traps, with firstprinciples calculations revealing defect atomic structures. Furthermore, stress degradation on commercial planar devices uncovers trap relaxation evolution, while trench-gate comparison demonstrates pronounced structure-dependent susceptibility. Our findings establish the first linkage between atomistic defect mechanisms and circuit-level performance, constructing a systematic framework for defect research. This advancement propels high-frequency operation in applications like onboard chargers (OBC), facilitating more compact designs and enhanced system efficiency.

title: Modular Reconfigurable Wheel-Track-Leg Hybrid Robot: Structural Design and Simulation Analysis
authors: Yaoyao Chen, Liheng Zhang, Huashan Zuo, Zhizhong Wang, Wenkai Zhang, Zhenglong Zhao, Wenhao Wang
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: To address the terrain-adaptability challenges of ground mobile robots in complex environments, we propose a modular reconfigurable wheel-track-leg hybrid robot that employs a wheel-track composite deformable wheel that enables rapid switching between wheeled and tracked modes. Integrated with a leg mechanism, it supports wheeled, tracked, and legged locomotion modes. A modular reconfigurable coupling mechanism based on tapered wedge pair and guided push-pull rods is designed to realize fast docking and separation among multiple robot modules. Using multibody dynamics theory, a full-scale multibody model is built in RecurDyn. By defining joints, contact parameters, and actuation, we simulate the robot operating in wheeled mode on paved roads, in tracked mode on soft soil, while climbing slopes, and while negotiating trenches and obstacles. Simulation results demonstrate that the robot's motion stability and propulsion performance under diverse terrains satisfy the design requirements, validating its multimodal locomotion capabilities and terrain adaptability.

title: Comprehensive Strategies for Imprint Recovery in Hafnia-Based Ferroelectric Capacitors and Mechanism Clarification: Experiment and Modeling
authors: Guan Feng, Yu Li, Hao Jiang, Xiaodong Wang, Shucheng Zhang, Xianzhe Chen, Yingfen Wei, Qi Liu, Ming Liu
abstract: In this work, we comprehensively investigate the imprint recovery strategies and their underlying mechanisms in <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{Hf}_{\mathrm{x}} \text{Zr}_{1-\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{O}_{2}$</tex> (HZO)-based ferroelectric (FE) capacitors (FeCaps). Our electrical measurements evaluated a wide range of pulse conditions and operation schemes, revealing that (1) recovery of imprint-induced <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$E_{\mathrm{c}}$</tex> shift is significantly more effective under bipolar pulses than unipolar ones, and (2) the recovery efficiency can be further enhanced by moderately extending the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$T_{d+}$</tex> delay time during bipolar schemes. By leveraging on our dynamic TCAD model, the above observations can be explained. By identifying the respective contributions and interactions of ion migration (oxygen vacancy, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{o}}{}^{2+}$</tex>) and charge trapping (CT) effects, the simulation results confirm that <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{Vo}^{2+}$</tex> migration plays a dominant role in <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$E_{\mathrm{c}}$</tex> shift recovery, and the efficiency is closely correlated with the concentration gradient across the FeCap. This work presents an effective electrical strategy for alleviating imprint-related reliability issues in Hafnia-based ferroelectric memories.

title: Numerical Simulation Analysis of Electromagnetic Characteristics and AC Loss of STARS Conductors with Different Structures
authors: Wenye Li, Qianjun Zhang, Mengchao Dong, Qin Li
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: The HTS STARS conductor, developed by the National Institute for Fusion Science (NIFS) for the FFHR-d1 fusion device, exhibits a straightforward structure with the potential to lower production expenses, making it an attractive option for various fusion magnets. Nonetheless, the suitability of STARS conductors with diverse structures for FFHR-d1 remains inadequately validated. Hence, this study employs a multiphysics simulation tool to assess the electromagnetic characteristics distribution and AC losses variation of S1 (comprising three strands of 4 mm-wide tapes) and S2 (comprising one strand of 12 mm-wide tapes) under self-field conditions at 77 K and an 8T external magnetic field at 20 K. The findings indicate that S 1 exhibits lower cumulative AC losses than S2 under self-field conditions; however, in a high magnetic field setting, S2 generates a larger reverse shielding current, leading to greater cumulative hysteresis losses in S1 compared to S2.

title: Differential Resistance Lowering Effect in High-Speed Ge Photodetectors During Nanosecond ESD Events
authors: Po-Yen Lin, Marko Simicic, Ping-Yi Hsieh, Wen-Chieh Chen, Bjorn Vermeersch, Conor Coughlan, Mathias Berciano, Javad Rahimi Vaskasi, Swetanshu Bipul, Maumita Chakrabarti, Dimitrios Velenis, Yoojin Ban, Filippo Ferraro, Joris Van Campenhout, Kristof Croes, Piet Wambacq
abstract: An electrothermally induced resistance modulation in advanced high-speed Ge photodetectors under nanosecond electrostatic discharge (ESD) events is investigated through measurements and physics-based models. By employing a very-fast transmission line pulsing (VF-TLP) test system, the measured I-V characteristics reveal that the differential resistance-lowering (DRL) effect is triggered under a 1-ns pulse duration, consistent with temperature-dependent intrinsic carrier density behavior and TCAD simulation results. Among the evaluated devices, the vertical Ge photodetector (VPIN) exhibits the most pronounced DRL behavior, attributed to its device geometry. Understanding these mechanisms can help improve ESD robustness and enhance the reliability of next-generation high-speed silicon photonic technologies.

title: Thermal Fatigue in Copper Wire Bonds Under Power Cycling
authors: Daniele Bini, Alberto Mancaleoni, Carlo Neva
abstract: In the microelectronics industry, copper wire bonding is today the dominant technology for plastic-packaged Integrated Circuits. The reliability of copper wire under key environmental stress factors like temperature, humidity and thermal cycles has been extensively studied and demonstrated. However, among the failure mechanisms that deserve deeper attention, wire thermal fatigue caused by the active cycling of power stages is gaining an increasing relevance for smart power products. The market demand for miniaturization and upgraded performance drives a growing power density and sharper temperature gradients to be managed without compromises on lifetime, so a more accurate reliability characterization is becoming necessary. This paper presents the experimental results on <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1. 2}$</tex> mil copper wires tested in three different stress conditions. Three copper wire types, 4 N, 2 N, and PCC have been compared regarding thermal fatigue effects. In addition to read-out functional testing, physical analysis has been performed on both virgin and failed wires. The main outcomes include a discussion about the physics of failure, the proposal of Norris-Landzberg model for the acceleration factor, and the identification of the most robust wire type against thermal fatigue.

title: KDM-RRT: A Bidirectional RRT with KD-Tree and Multi-Candidate Evaluation
authors: Wei Wang
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: Traditional RRT algorithms often suffer from poor convergence, low search efficiency, and suboptimal path quality. To address these issues, this paper introduces KDM-RRT, an improved bidirectional RRT algorithm integrating a KD-tree with a novel multi-candidate point evaluation mechanism. KDM-RRT significantly reduces nearest neighbor query time complexity through KD-tree maintenance and employs multi-candidate evaluation, combining distance and geometric margin for optimal expansion direction selection, thereby improving path quality and avoiding local optima. A bidirectional expansion strategy further accelerates convergence. Experimental results demonstrate that KDM-RRT achieves superior efficiency and quality, significantly reducing planning time while generating high-quality paths. This makes KDM-RRT highly advantageous for robust path planning in complex environments.

title: Research on a Transformer Fault Diagnosis Model Based on RF-SSA-SVM
authors: Jiaqi Shi
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: Multidimensional feature inputs in dissolved gas analysis (DGA) often introduce redundant interference during transformer fault diagnosis. Additionally, selecting optimal kernel functions and penalty factors for support vector machines (SVM) remains a significant challenge. To address these issues, this study proposes an RF-based feature selection method combined with SSA-optimized SVM. This method generates a 22-dimensional feature vector by analyzing five characteristic dissolved gases in transformer oil. It employs RF to rank feature importance and remove redundant components, resulting in an optimal 13dimensional feature vector. Subsequently, the SSA algorithm performs global optimization of the SVM's penalty factor and kernel parameters to construct the RF-SSA-SVM diagnostic model. Results show the proposed method achieves 96.67% accuracy, an improvement of 8.34% over the baseline SVM. Its recognition accuracy surpasses that of both the RF-PSO-SVM and RF-ABC-SVM models, enabling accurate assessment of transformer faults.

title: Transitional-State Architectures for Hybrid Mainframe to Cloud Modernization With AI-Derived Code Intelligence
authors: Ankur Kalohia
abstract: Most mainframe modernization programs fail or stall not in the target state, but during transitional phases where legacy and cloud workloads coexist. In parallel, artificial intelligence (AI)-enabled code understanding tools increasingly produce rich metadata—call graphs, data flows, domain clusters, and inferred business capabilities—that are rarely exploited to design transitional architectures and to evaluate them empirically. This article proposes a reference framework for AI-informed transitional-state architectures in mainframe-to-cloud modernization. Using multiwave evidence from two multiyear programs (anonymized), we show how AI-generated dependency graphs and domain clusters can guide strangler patterns, carve-out sequencing, and network segmentation. We define a key performance indicator set for transitional-state health that extends beyond on-time/on-budget delivery to include cutover risk, batch window compliance, incident rates, and cross-domain coupling. The contributions include: 1) taxonomy of AI-informed transitional patterns; 2) decision heuristics that map code-level insights to architectural moves; and 3) reusable evaluation blueprint.

title: Quantum Computational Simulation of CRISPR-Cas9 Edits Targeting the Salt Bridge in Amyloid- $\beta$
authors: Avik Mahata, Rubayat Khan, Mazharul Karim, Don Roosan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: We present a hybrid quantum classical framework to prioritize CRISPR Cas9 edits that disrupt the salt bridge between aspartate at position twenty three and lysine at position twenty eight in the amyloid beta peptide, a motif implicated in aggregation and neurotoxicity in Alzheimer disease. The pipeline unifies four components. First, it performs precise mapping from genomic coordinates at the amyloid precursor protein locus to the codon window that encodes the target motif. Second, it computes residue level profiles of hydropathy, charge under physiological conditions, and intrinsic disorder in the motif neighborhood. Third, it models candidate substitutions in the same region and estimates their local feature impact. Fourth, it includes a quantum optimization module that selects an edit set by solving a constrained subset selection problem. Edit prioritization is formulated as a quadratic binary objective that rewards predicted salt bridge disruption while penalizing feasibility risks such as guide availability, editor window constraints, and off target propensity. A quantum approximate optimization routine is then applied on the filtered candidate set to produce discrete edit recommendations that can be validated experimentally. We report the objective construction, resource requirements in terms of qubits and circuit depth, and how the approach generalizes to motif targeted editing problems in other proteinopathies.

title: Cardiovascular Risk Factors in Ischemic Heart Disease: A Study in South-Western Bangladesh
authors: Md Mahmudul Haque, M. Raihan, Kamrul Golder, Anindya Biswas, Abu Naim Khan
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Assessment of heart risk has become increasingly important as climate change increasingly impacts human health and the environment. Accurate predictive modeling can help mitigate risks and support timely decision-making for vulnerable populations. In this study, multiple machine learning and deep learning approaches have been employed to evaluate and predict heart-related risks using a comprehensive dataset. Feature selection was conducted through a combination of statistical and model-driven techniques to identify the most influential factors contributing to heart exposure. Six deep learning models: Artificial Neural Network (ANN), Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU), Bidirectional GRU (Bi-GRU), Long Short Term Memory (LSTM), and Bidirectional LSTM (Bi-LSTM) were implemented and compared. The results demonstrate that recurrent architectures, particularly LSTM, outperform traditional models by effectively capturing temporal dependencies in the data. The LSTM model achieved an accuracy of 98%, confirming its robustness and suitability for heart risk prediction. Based on this best performing model, a web application was developed to provide real-time, user friendly heart risk predictions, demonstrating the practical applicability of this model for decision support in heart attack risk management. These findings highlight the potential of deep learning, and especially LSTM, in developing reliable decision-support systems for climate risk management.

title: Q-DIVER: Integrated Quantum Transfer Learning and Differentiable Quantum Architecture Search with EEG Data
authors: Junghoon Justin Park, Yeonghyeon Park, Jiook Cha
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Integrating quantum circuits into deep learning pipelines remains challenging due to heuristic design limitations. We propose Q-DIVER, a hybrid framework combining a largescale pretrained EEG encoder (DIVER-1) with a differentiable quantum classifier. Unlike fixed-ansatz approaches, we employ Differentiable Quantum Architecture Search to autonomously discover task-optimal circuit topologies during end-to-end finetuning. On the PhysioNet Motor Imagery dataset, our quantum classifier achieves predictive performance comparable to classical multi-layer perceptrons (Test F1: 63.49 %) while using approximately 50 × fewer task-specific head parameters (2.10 M vs. 105.02M). These results validate quantum transfer learning as a parameter-efficient strategy for high-dimensional biological signal processing.

title: Distributing Quantum Circuits using Pre-Distributed Entanglement Pairs Over Quantum Networks
authors: Yukun Yang, Ranjani G. Sundaram, Himanshu Gupta
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Distributing quantum circuits across a network of quantum devices is a recent approach to improve the scalability of quantum computation. Entangled pairs of qubits distributed across network nodes are used as a communication resource for executing inter-node (remote) gates. However, establishing these entanglement pairs incurs significant latency, and doing so during circuit execution may lead to decoherence of logical qubits. Instead, it may be beneficial to proactively distribute certain entanglement pairs a priori (before execution begins). Thus, we consider the problem of distributing a quantum circuit over a quantum network with pre-distributed entanglement pairs, and seek to minimize the circuit's execution time. We develop algorithms for (i) allocating a limited predistribution budget across memory pairs (optionally guided by a training-circuit distribution) and (ii) computing a static qubit-to-memory allocation that exploits the resulting pre-distributed resources to reduce execution time. We evaluate the proposed methods in NetSquid under a telegate-based execution model on standard benchmark circuit families.

title: BiBiEQ: Bivariate Bicycle Codes on Erasure Qubits
authors: Ameya S. Bhave, Navnil Choudhury, Andrew Nemec, Kanad Basu
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Erasure qubits reduce overhead in fault-tolerant quantum error correction (QEC) by converting dominant faults into detectable errors known as erasures. They have demonstrated notable improvements in thresholds and scaling in surface and Floquet code memories. In this work, we use erasure qubits on Bivariate Bicycle (BB) codes from the quantum low-density parity-check (QLDPC) regime. Owing to their sparse structure and favorable rate-distance trade-offs, BB codes are practical candidates for QEC. We introduce BiBiEQ, a novel framework that compiles a given BB code into an erasure-aware memory circuit <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$C_{E}$</tex>. This erasure circuit <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$C_{E}$</tex> comprises erasure checks (ECs), resets, and erasures spread over a user-specified erasure check schedule (2EC, 4EC). BiBiEQ converts this erasure circuit <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$C_{E}$</tex> into the stabilizer circuit <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$C$</tex> for general-purpose decoding. BiBiEQ provides two engines for this conversion, BiBiEQ-Exact and BiBiEQ-Approx. BiBiEQ-Exact preserves the joint-erasure correlations and serves as our accuracy benchmark, while BiBiEQ-Approx uses an independence approximation to accelerate large sweeps and expose accuracy-throughput tradeoffs. Using BiBiEQ, we decode the stabilizer circuits to get a per-round logical error rate (LER) for the BB codes and quantify the effect of the EC schedules on the correctable operating region below the pseudo-threshold. The <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$4 E C$</tex> schedule keeps the accuracy of both engines close to one another, making BiBiEQ-Approx a reliable proxy for BiBiEQ-Exact for faster sweeps. Below the pseudo-threshold, the code distance (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$d$</tex>) hop from <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$d: 6 \rightarrow 10$</tex> yields a drop in LER by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\approx 10-17 \times$</tex> larger than <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$d: 10 \rightarrow 12$</tex>, showing that most gains are realized by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$d=10$</tex>.

title: A Mixed-Mode Sampling Control Strategy for Capacitor-Current-Feedback Active Damping to Enhance Performance of LCL-Type Grid-Connected Inverters
authors: Yunzhe Li, Jingjie Xu, Jian Yang, Yuhao Wang, Tianxiang Yin, Lei Lin
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: LCL filters are widely utilized in grid-connected inverters, where capacitor current feedback is commonly employed for active resonance damping. However, computational delays and pulse-width modulation (PWM) delays in digital control significantly impact the performance of active damping methods, particularly when the resonant frequency <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(f_{\mathrm{r}})$</tex> lies near one-sixth of the sampling frequency <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(f_{\mathrm{s}} / 6)$</tex>. Although doubling the system's sampling frequency can address this issue, it doubles the computational burden. This paper proposes a control strategy that doubles only the sampling frequency of the capacitor current. By doing so, the performance of the system is enhanced when <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$f_{r}$</tex> is close to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">${f}_{s} / {6}$</tex>, without significantly increasing the computational burden compared with the conventional method. Bode diagrams are used to compare how a system performs when different sampling methods are applied. Finally, simulation results are presented to validate the feasibility of the control strategy and the corresponding performance improvements of the system.

title: ML-based Medical Diagnostics System for Infectious Diseases (Case Study: COVID-19)
authors: Sanjay Manda, Siddhartha Vinnakota, Manohar Adapa, Bindu Chandra Shekar Reddy, Akanksha Ojha, Bonaventure Chidube Molokwu
abstract: Early and reliable identification of COVID-19 cases is important for timely public-health response, especially in settings where laboratory resources are limited. This study develops a supervised Machine Learning (ML) model to predict COVID19 infection status using the Mexico National Epidemiological Surveillance Dataset [1], which contains demographic information, comorbidities, basic clinical indicators, and symptomrelated dates. The dataset includes a large amount of noise, government-coded missing values, and class imbalance which requires careful preprocessing. In the original surveillance system, several categorical fields use special numeric codes (such as 97, 98, and 99) to indicate non-standard responses rather than real clinical states. These codes typically correspond to categories such as “not applicable”, “unknown”, or “no information provided"; and therefore, they must be treated as missing values rather than genuine patient attributes. Additional features such as a comorbidity count, age-group categories, and symptom-toadmission intervals were created (i.e. feature engineered) to improve predictive signal. Several machine-learning models were evaluated, and the XGBoost classifier achieved the strongest performance with an Accuracy of 0.72, a positive-class Recall of 0.73, a macro F1-Score of 0.72, and a ROC-AUC of 0.80. These results show that a well-designed preprocessing pipeline, combined with gradient-boosting methods, can provide reliable COVID-19 predictions using large-scale epidemiological data.

title: IRPSFF: Improved Recovery-Path Structured Flip-Flop for Enhanced Soft-Error Tolerance with Low Performance Overhead in a 65-nm FD-SOI Process
authors: Ryuichi Nakajima, Shuhei Mandai, Hikaru Nakamoto, Arata Matsumoto, Sotaro Taniguchi, Shotaro Sugitani, Jun Furuta, Kazutoshi Kobayashi
abstract: We propose a radiation-hardened flip-flop (FF) suitable for FD-SOI processes. It integrates a reduce-sensitive-range (RSR)-based secondary latch and a pass-transistor delay in the feedback-recovery path to improve soft-error tolerance without increasing performance overheads. The target delay for the recovery path was determined based on a sigmoid approximation of prior experimental data to ensure robustness against long Single-Event Transient pulses. Post-layout simulation and Xeion irradiation results show that the proposed FF achieves approximately <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2 x}$</tex> higher soft-error tolerance than the conventional stacked FF, while maintaining low area, delay, and power overheads comparable to those of the Dual Feedback Recovery FF.

title: Dynamic Optimization Method of DSO-IESs Based on DOE Guidance in P2P Energy Trading
authors: Zhiqing Lu, Lei Dong
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: To solve the problem that it is difficult to optimize the operation safety of distribution network and the economy of integrated energy system under the background of double carbon. This paper proposes a dynamic optimization framework of DSOIESs based on dynamic operating envelope (DOE) guidance. Firstly, the upper-level uses the Distflow model to improve the distribution network available capacity under the premise of ensuring the security of the distribution network, and then the lower-level establishes the operation cost minimization model of the multi-agent alliance. On this basis, a multi-agent cooperative planning model based on Nash negotiation is constructed, which is decomposed into two sub-problems: social cost minimization and payment income maximization, and the augmented Lagrange function of these two sub-problems is constructed. Secondly, to protect the data privacy of all parties involved in the planning stage and accelerate the formulation of solutions, the alternating direction multiplier method is used to solve the Nash equilibrium solution of these two sub-problems. The final results of the example show that the total operating cost is reduced by 16.43% after the cooperative planning of each participant, and the reliable operation of each system is guaranteed, which verifies the effectiveness of the proposed cooperative planning model.

title: Contrails over Sweden: Patterns in the Sky
authors: Tobias Elneros, Tatiana Polishchuk, Lucie Smetanová
abstract: Aviation industry is facing multiple climate and weather challenges, one of which is the impact of condensation trails, or contrails, which are ice clouds that form as a result of the mixing of cold, humid air with aircraft engine exhaust plumes. Contrails trap outgoing terrestrial radiation and counteract emission reduction benefits from emission-optimized flight routes. The formation of persistent contrails has been recognized as a potential threat to the global climate. While carbon dioxide emitted today impacts global warming within $20-40$ years, the warming effect of contrails is immediate. In this study, we present a regional analysis of contrail formation in Swedish airspace, focusing on seasonality and geographical variability. The study utilizes high-resolution atmospheric data from the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts (ECMWF) alongside detailed flight traffic data from the historical trajectory database Opensky Network. Two contrail estimation models are tested and compared, and vertical trajectory optimization is evaluated as a mitigation strategy. The results reveal distinct seasonal and diurnal patterns, with contrail radiative forcing generally higher in winter and lower in summer. Furthermore, the trajectory optimization approach demonstrates significant potential for reducing contrail-induced warming, underscoring the value of operational measures as part of aviation climate mitigation efforts.

title: An Intelligent Detection and Assessment Method for the Safety Status of Transmission Towers Based on Multi-Source UAV Data Fusion
