authors: Hong Huo, Qiang Zhang, Chunlei Zhao, Jian Du, Junliang Liu
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: This article addresses the prominent issues of low efficiency, high subjectivity, and difficulty in achieving systematic condition assessment in traditional safety inspection methods for transmission line towers. It proposes a safety status detection and evaluation framework for towers based on multi-source data fusion and intelligent analysis. The framework integrates UAV LiDAR scanning and visible light image acquisition to construct high-precision 3D point clouds and visual fusion models. By designing a multimodal feature extraction and defect recognition algorithm for key structural components of towers, it achieves automatic identification and localization of typical hazards such as corrosion, deformation, and missing bolts. Furthermore, by combining 3D models with structural mechanical characteristics, a quantitative defect safety risk assessment model is established. Experiments and applications show that the proposed method can significantly improve the automation of inspections and the objective consistency of evaluations, providing an effective technical solution for intelligent operation and maintenance and preventive maintenance of transmission lines.

title: Dynamic $R_{\text{ON}}$ and $Q_{\text{OSS}}$ in P-GaN Power HEMTs Explained with the Hole Redistribution Model
authors: Nicolò Zagni, Alessandro Chini, Giovanni Verzellesi
abstract: There is a broad consensus in the literature about the critical role of Carbon (C) doping in determining dynamic <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$R_{O N}$</tex> and current collapse in GaN power HEMTs. The physical mechanism is the emission, redistribution, and retrapping of holes within the carbon-doped buffer (“hole redistribution” in short) that affects the 2-DEG concentration with relatively long time constants, owing to the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.9-\text{eV}$</tex> energy difference between the trap level and GaN valence band. Relatively less investigated is the role of C-traps on dynamic Qoss, that critically impacts switching losses. Here, we show by means of TCAD simulations that C-related traps are at the origin of both dynamic <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$R_{\text{ON}}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$Q_{\text{OSS}}$</tex>.

title: Physics-Constrained Denoising Autoencoders for Data-Scarce Wildfire UAV Sensing
authors: Abdelrahman Ramadan, Zahra Dorbeigi Namaghi, Emily Taylor, Lucas Edwards, Xan Giuliani, David S. McLagan, Sidney Givigi, Melissa Greeff
abstract: Wildfire monitoring requires high-resolution atmospheric measurements, yet low-cost sensors on Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) exhibit baseline drift, cross-sensitivity, and response lag that corrupt concentration estimates. Traditional deep learning denoising approaches demand large datasets impractical to obtain from limited UAV flight campaigns. We present $\mathrm{PC}^{2}$ DAE, a physics-informed denoising autoencoder that addresses data scarcity by embedding physical constraints directly into the network architecture. Non-negative concentration estimates are enforced via softplus activations and physically plausible temporal smoothing, ensuring outputs are physically admissible by construction rather than relying on loss function penalties. The architecture employs hierarchical decoder heads for Black Carbon, Gas, and CO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> sensor families, with two variants: PC<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>| DAE-Lean (21 k parameters) for edge deployment and $\mathrm{PC}^{2}$ DAE-Wide (204 k parameters) for offline processing. We evaluate on 7,894 synchronized 1 Hz samples collected from UAV flights during prescribed burns in Saskatchewan, Canada (approximately 2.2 hours of flight data), two orders of magnitude below typical deep learning requirements. PC<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> DAELean achieves 67.3% smoothness improvement and 90.7% highfrequency noise reduction with zero physics violations. Five baselines (LSTM-AE, U-Net, Transformer, CBDAE, DeSpaWN) produce $\mathbf{1 5} \boldsymbol{-} \mathbf{2 3} \boldsymbol{\%}$ negative outputs. The lean variant outperforms wide ($+5.6 \%$ smoothness), suggesting reduced capacity with strong inductive bias prevents overfitting in data-scarce regimes. Training completes in under 65 seconds on consumer hardware.

title: Fast In-Situ TDDB Prediction Method for HKMG Oxide Stacks
authors: Uma Sharma, Aaron Knoll, Mehran Samiee, Alexandre Subirats, Matthew Ulrich, Mandar S. Bhoir, Durga P. Panda, Abhishek Khanna, Shivani Srivastava, Dan Mocuta, Timothy J. Owens
abstract: Conventional TDDB (Time-Dependent Dielectric Breakdown) testing using Constant Voltage Stress (CVS) provides accurate lifetime predictions but is time-intensive, requiring up to two days per wafer. This work proposes a fast, in-situ methodology combining Voltage Ramp Stress (VRS) and Fowler-Nordheim Delta Vt (FNDVt) measurements to estimate TDDB lifetime with high confidence and significantly reduced test time. The integrated suite enables 10 X throughput improvement while maintaining prediction accuracy within <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1 \sigma$</tex> of standard TDDB. Monte Carlo simulations are performed to consider the stochastic nature of the mechanism in the lifetime distributions. This approach has been validated across multiple process conditions.

title: Disturbance-Resistant State Estimation Method for AUV Based on Bidirectional Fusion and Adaptive Noise
authors: Jiacheng Li
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: To address the issue of state estimation bias caused by high and low frequency wave disturbances during shallow-depth constant-depth cruising of autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs), a disturbance-resistant state estimation method for AUV based on bidirectional fusion and adaptive noise is proposed. First, perform frequency decoupling to establish a state model that includes both high and low frequency movements. The forward filtering of adaptive noise is used as a low pass filter to suppress high-frequency noise. Subsequently, a backward smoothing step performs global correction for low frequency errors, ultimately outputting the corrected state vector. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method effectively separates and suppresses high and low-frequency disturbances, significantly improving state estimation accuracy in both vertical and horizontal planes, this demonstrates excellent state estimation performance and robustness in both the simulation of complex wave environments and the actual sea trials.

title: A Systems Engineering Approach to a Wearable Assistive Monitoring Device for Dementia Patients
authors: Idunnuoluwa Adewolu
abstract: Dementia conditions like Alzheimer’s disease increase the risk of falls and medication non-adherence significantly, hence creating a continuous safety challenge for patients and caregivers in home-based care environments. Even though wearable assistive technologies have been put forward to lessen these risks, many of them place emphasis on device-level functionality and algorithmic performance, without deep consideration of system-level design and verification, as well as lifecycle traceability. This paper presents a systems engineering approach to the design and validation of a wearable assistive monitoring device for patients with dementia. The proposed system combines fall detection and emergency localization with medication reminder functionalities all within a deterministic finite state machine (FSM) architecture to guarantee a predictable and verifiable system behavior. System requirements are deduced formally from the safety needs of dementia patients alongside safety-critical design principles. This is followed by a modular architectural decomposition which encompasses sensing, decision, and control, human interaction, communication, and energy subsystems. A twostage computation based on a threshold fall detection algorithm combining signal magnitude vector analysis with postural confirmation through tilt angle computation is conducted to reduce false positives while also maintaining deterministic decision-making. Verification and validation are done through high-accuracy simulation to produce quantitative performance metrics before the physical hardware can be implemented. The results display deterministic state changes, bounded detection latency, and correct sequencing of emergency localization and communication actions. A requirements-to-results traceability framework is used to support early-stage system validation. The results provide initial evidence of system feasibility and safety and establish a structured foundation for subsequent physical prototyping and real-world evaluation.

title: Unbounded Length Minimal Synchronizing Words for Quantum Channels Over Qutrits*
authors: Bjørn Kjos-Hanssen, Swarnalakshmi Lakshmanan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Grudka et al. constructed quantum channels with synchronizing words of length 3 for qutrits. We extend their result to arbitrary long minimal synchronizing words.

title: A Shared Risk Link Group Approach for Multi-Path Key Relay in QKD Networks
authors: Hedwig Koerfgen, David Schatz, Guido E. Smeenk, Guenter Schaefer, David Koch
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Multi-path key reinforcement (MKR) enhances confidentiality in Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) networks by distributing key shares across multiple routes. To account for shared technological vulnerabilities, we model path selection using Shared Risk Link Groups (SRLGs) and introduce a parametric attacker model capturing correlated compromises. We propose two SRLG-aware <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$k$</tex>-path algorithms: ClassOverlap, which minimizes color overlap among candidate paths, and AttackerEffort, a hierarchical optimization maximizing color-disjointness while reducing edge overlap. Simulations on network sizes representative of current QKD deployments show that both approaches improve resilience compared to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$k$</tex>-shortest-path routing, particularly under correlated attacks.

title: Telekinetic UI: Using Unconscious Facial Micro-motions forIntent-based Cursor Control
authors: Genki Akimoto, Shuto Takashita, Hiroto Saito, Masahiko Inami
publish: Proceedings of the Augmented Humans International Conference 2026
abstract: None

title: Sports Injury Risk Prediction and Intervention for College Students Based on Decision Tree Algorithm
authors: Rongdan Chen, Yu Zhang
publish: Proceedings of the 2nd International Conference on Digital Society, Information Science and Risk Management
abstract: None

title: Image Fusion Based on Prior Information_supp1-3690497.pdf
authors: Zhenhong Jia
abstract: Existing feature extraction-based image fusion methods have an initial output of noise and iteratively refine them toward the target through training. This approach results in slow convergence and unstable training, with suboptimal fusion quality. To address these limitations, we propose prior-based fusion (PBF), a novel paradigm that constructs the fused output using multi-source image priors as its structural foundation, unlike noise-initialized approaches. This strategy enables faster and more stable training, while allowing the model to focus on complementary information exploration and detail enhancement, ultimately achieving superior fusion quality. Building upon the PBF paradigm, we propose PBFNet, a general-purpose image fusion framework, with its unified version PBFNet-U. They use a manually designed fusion criterion as the switching function to achieve optimal prior information extraction with only a slight increase in model complexity. The novel dense residual dense block is used as a feature extraction block, and at the same time creates a continuous memory between convolutional blocks and convolutional layers, which has a stronger feature extraction capability than residual block, dense block, residual dense block, and aggregated residual dense block. Furthermore, a channel attention mechanism is incorporated to adaptively fuse encoded features, and a gradient-enhanced loss function is designed to improve gradient perception. Both quantitative metrics and visual assessments demonstrate that PBFNet and its unified version surpass state-of-the-art methods across four fusion tasks. The results of ablation studies and discussion and analysis demonstrate the significant advantages of the proposed paradigm and method in stabilizing training, accelerating convergence, and improving fusion performance. Our code is publicly available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/hcb5206/PBFNet</uri>.

title: Bayesian Deep Learning for InSAR-Based Multi-hazard Assessment with Uncertainty Quantification_supp1-3690719.docx
authors: Guanhua Zhou
abstract: Geological hazards in mountainous basins commonly arise from overlapping landslide, subsidence, and uplift processes, yet susceptibility mapping remains limited by incomplete inventories and poorly quantified predictive uncertainty. This study proposes a Bayesian deep-learning framework for probabilistic multi-hazard susceptibility mapping by integrating Small Baseline Subset Interferometric Synthetic Aperture Radar (SBAS-InSAR) deformation measurements with geological, geomorphological, edaphic, climatic, land-use, and multispectral covariates. The training inventory is strengthened through physically guided label refinement based on slope and deformation thresholds derived from SBAS-InSAR, and its reliability is independently evaluated prior to model training. Bayesian convolutional and multilayer perceptron models with Monte Carlo dropout are then used to estimate class probabilities together with predictive uncertainty. Independent validation of the integrated inventory yields an overall reliability of 79.48%. Under the original validation setting, the Bayesian CNN achieves an accuracy of 0.94 and an AUCROC of 0.99, while spatial block cross-validation confirms a more conservative but methodologically stronger estimate of generalization under clustered deformation samples. The resulting susceptibility maps show spatial agreement of 76% and 72% with independent landslide and subsidence inventories, respectively. High-susceptibility zones indicate that subsidence affects 11.6% of the basin, landslides 2.2%, and uplift 1.3%, all with strong spatial clustering (Moran’s I = 0.62–0.90). Compared with deterministic baselines, the Bayesian framework improves probability calibration and enables spatial separation of observation-driven and model-driven uncertainty. A decision-grade reliability mask derived from the validation coverage–risk frontier further allows unreliable predictions to be excluded explicitly. These results demonstrate that uncertainty-aware SBAS-InSAR susceptibility mapping can provide more reliable and operationally interpretable products for regional hazard assessment, planning, and early warning.

title: Cheerful: Hardware-Oriented Early Exits for Ubiquitous Computing
authors: Haolan Zhang, Jintao Chen, Yingtao Shen, An Zou, Yehan Ma
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: Variations in the computational capabilities of edge devices and runtime resource availability pose considerable challenges in developing efficient ubiquitous inference. Dynamic Neural Networks (DyNNs) have been developed to address device heterogeneity by adapting network structures or parameters to complex deployment environments. However, existing DyNN methods are largely insensitive to runtime resource variations and hardware-specific configurations, and their reliance on extensive profiling complicates deployment. In this work, we present Cheerful, a hardware-oriented, ubiquitous early exit framework that enables efficient inference on heterogeneous devices by combining hardware-parameterized performance modeling and optimization with online adaptation to resource variations. In the offline stage, Cheerful models key performance metrics by integrating comprehensive analytical and data-driven methods, formulates an optimization problem to achieve the trade-off between inference accuracy and energy cost, and incorporates hardware-specific parameters to accommodate device heterogeneity. In the online stage, Cheerful integrates a lightweight hardware-aware inference engine (HIE) that jointly adjusts early-exit configurations and CPU frequency based on run-time resource availability, using Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling (DVFS). Extensive experiments on heterogeneous devices demonstrate that Cheerful outperforms state-of-the-art methods on the accuracy-energy Pareto frontier in resource-constrained environments while maintaining low deployment overhead.

title: RETENTION: Resource-Efficient Tree-Based Ensemble Model Acceleration with Content-Addressable Memory
authors: Yi-Chun Liao, Chieh-Lin Tsai, Yuan-Hao Chang, Camélia Slimani, Jalil Boukhobza, Tei-Wei Kuo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: Although deep learning has demonstrated remarkable capability in learning from unstructured data, modern tree-based ensemble models remain superior in extracting relevant information and learning from structured datasets. While several efforts have been made to accelerate tree-based models, the inherent characteristics of the models pose significant challenges for conventional accelerators. Recent research leveraging content-addressable memory (CAM) offers a promising solution for accelerating tree-based models, yet existing designs suffer from excessive memory consumption and low utilization. This work addresses these challenges by introducing RETENTION, an end-to-end framework that significantly reduces CAM capacity requirement for tree-based model inference. We propose an iterative pruning algorithm with a novel pruning criterion tailored for bagging-based models (e.g., Random Forest), which minimizes model complexity while ensuring controlled accuracy degradation. Additionally, we present a tree mapping scheme that incorporates two innovative data placement strategies to alleviate the memory redundancy caused by the widespread use of don’t care states in CAM. Experimental results show that implementing the tree mapping scheme alone reduces CAM capacity requirement by 1.46× to 21.30×, while the full RETENTION framework achieves 4.35× to 207.12× reduction with less than 3% accuracy loss. These results demonstrate that RETENTION is highly effective in minimizing CAM resource demand, providing a resource-efficient direction for tree-based model acceleration.

title: Techno-Economic Assessment of Battery Energy Storage Technologies and Energy Management Strategies for Hydrogen Hosting Capacity in Microgrids
authors: Mohamed M. Refaat, Mujahed Al-Dhaifallah, Abd El-Fattah A. Omran
abstract: This paper presents a techno-economic evaluation of battery energy storage systems (BESSs) integrated into hydrogen-based microgrids (MGs). Eight BESS technologies are investigated to quantify their impact on the hydrogen energy storage hosting capacity (HC) and MG performance. A detailed techno-economic model is developed to capture the key technical and economic characteristics of BESSs. The model considers depth of discharge (DOD), round-trip efficiency, capital cost, replacement cost, operation and maintenance cost and salvage value. Two energy management strategies (EMSs), namely hydrogen-prioritized and battery-prioritized schemes, are formulated to assess their influence on hydrogen utilization, BESS sizing and system operation. An optimization framework is established to minimize the levelized cost of energy (LCOE) and the Stellar Oscillation Optimizer is employed to solve the problem. The results indicate that high-efficiency, high-DOD battery technologies significantly reduce the required number of battery units and the LCOE under variable-sizing scenarios, whereas lower-capital-cost technologies exhibit superior performance under fixed-sizing conditions. The hydrogen-prioritized EMS decreases battery capacity requirements but increases hydrogen utilization. While the battery-prioritized EMS reduces hydrogen reliance at the expense of higher battery cycling and operating frequency. Under the battery-prioritized EMS, low-cost BESS technologies require 0.8–3.3% more battery units. Whereas higher-cost technologies achieve up to a 1.6% reduction in battery count. An increase of about 50% in the HC of PEM fuel cell is achieved. Furthermore, the choice of BESS technology significantly affects environmental performance. Variations in CO₂ emissions of up to 3.11 tonnes is reported for the same MG configuration. A sensitivity analysis is also conducted to evaluate MG performance under variations in load demand, RES generation, battery capital cost, minimum DOD, and operating efficiency.

title: Prof. Yinhai Wang [ITS People]
authors: Yinhai Wang, Yisheng Lv
abstract: Provides society information that may include news, reviews or technical notes that should be of interest to practitioners and researchers.

title: Voltage Regulation under Prosumer-Grid Conflicts: An Evolutionary Learning Strategy
authors: Arman Ali, Branislav Hredzak, Chaojie Li, Shyamal S. Chand
abstract: The modern power grid experiences significant voltage violations due to evolving spatio-temporal variations in load and photovoltaic (PV) generation patterns. Although distributed energy resources (DERs) at the prosumer end help remedy this problem, prosumers' profit-seeking decisions may conflict with the distribution system operator's (DSO) technical concerns of maintaining grid voltages. This conflict makes the decision-making through existing multi-agent deep reinforcement learning (MADRL) strategies challenging, which otherwise offer a promising solution for simplistic grid-centric objectives. Therefore, to solve a potential prosumer-grid conflict, the incentive mechanism is introduced for prosumers with a multi-agent evolutionary reinforcement learning (MAERL) approach for robust decision-making. Specifically, a teaming mechanism is devised to help agents explore a large search space and escape local minima, enhancing traditional MADRL capabilities. Additionally, we explicitly integrate two key features for a realistic and scalable solution: (1) a hybrid policy network that is rightly needed for the prosumer's discrete-continuous actions and (2) a Bayesian neural network (BNN)-based surrogate model for power flow, which is selected particularly because of its scalability edge over its nonparametric counterpart, a Gaussian process regression (GPR) method. For validation and performance demonstration, a modified IEEE 33-bus system is used as a case study.

title: Identifying Crying and Screaming in Audio Recordings of Autistic Children Using Transfer Learning
authors: Marina Eni, Ilan Dinstein, Yaniv Zigel
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems
abstract: Crying and screaming (CS) events are important indicators of distress and emotional dysregulation in children with autism spectrum disorder (ASD) that are difficult for researchers and clinicians to quantify. This study aimed to develop an algorithm for identifying and quantifying crying and screaming segments in behavioral assessment audio recordings of 206 children (80% with ASD). We trained a multilayer perceptron (MLP) model to classify audio segments containing CS versus speech. We compared classification accuracy across multiple MLP models trained with different input embeddings extracted from YAMNet, VGGish, OpenL3, Whisper (tiny, base, small, and medium), and Wav2Vec2 models, as well as the eGeMAPs feature set. Integrating classification probabilities of Whisper-medium and eGeMAPs achieved the highest performance, yielding Matthew’s correlation coefficient of 0.56 ± 0.10. This demonstrates strong performance for this challenging, highly imbalanced real-world task, yielding an improvement of 1.8% over the next single-embedding model, when tested on independent recordings from children not included in the training set. The number and duration of algorithm-identified CS segments per child were highly correlated with the number [concordance correlation coefficient (CCC) = 0.79, Pearson’s r(204) = 0.83] and duration [CCC = 0.81, r(204) = 0.85] of manually annotated segments. These findings demonstrate that the accuracy of an algorithm outperforms alternative embedding-based methods and provides a scalable and objective tool for quantifying distress and emotional dysregulation in ASD children that will be of high utility for basic and clinical research purposes.

title: From Radio Waves to Wellness: Radar and Machine Learning for Noncontact Dehydration Assessment
authors: Jose Maria Sosa, Vijith Varma Kotte, Sajid Ahmed, Muhammad Mahboob Ur Rahman, Mohamed-Slim Alouini, Tareq Y. Al-Naffouri
publish: IEEE Journal of Electromagnetics, RF and Microwaves in Medicine and Biology
abstract: This paper introduces a novel, noninvasive, and contactless method for dehydration measurement using a frequency-modulated continuous wave (FMCW) radar operating at center frequencies of 10 GHz and 77 GHz. The proposed framework begins with a customized preprocessing technique designed to isolate the signal corresponding to the target of interest (ToI) from the received radar data, ensuring that the extracted features reliably reflect physiological indicators of hydration status. To capture the complex, nonlinear patterns associated with dehydration, a wavelet scattering transform (WST)-based feature extraction method was employed, which provides robust input for machine learning models. These features are then used to train and evaluate various classifiers, the best one being neural networks (NN), which accurately classify protocol sessions. A key challenge in nonclinical dehydration assessment is the absence of biochemical ground truth. To overcome the challenge, we developed and annotated a novel dataset—the Fasting Dataset—collected under controlled conditions from individuals who observed fasting. This dataset captures radar measurements at different physiological stages, offering a valuable reference for hydration-level analysis. Validation using this custom dataset demonstrates the initial feasibility of the proposed methodology for real-time, noninvasive, and contactless dehydration monitoring in remote healthcare settings.

title: Crystal Materials Design via Physics-Guided Evolutionary Multi-Objective Optimization With Knowledge Transfer
authors: Cheng Chen, Haokai Hong, Wanyu Lin, Kay Chen Tan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Emerging Topics in Computational Intelligence
abstract: The design of new crystal materials holds significant scientific value for societal advancement. Recent advancements in machine learning-based approaches have demonstrated considerable promise in the realm of crystal material design. However, their effectiveness relies heavily on the availability of high-quality and extensive training datasets, which are often challenging to collect in practice. To this end, we propose a novel physics-informed evolutionary transfer optimization framework that can design new crystal materials without necessitating extensive data. Specifically, we introduce a physics-informed encoding for materials, enabling the use of multi-objective evolutionary optimization to simultaneously optimize multiple physical objectives that are critical to the effective design of crystal materials, including the validity, properties, and energy of crystal materials. Additionally, to mitigate the slow optimization speed of evolutionary computation, we propose a physics-informed evolutionary transfer optimization technique to enhance the design speed of optimized materials. We conducted comprehensive experiments to analyze the designed crystals from the perspectives of validity, density functional theory (DFT) validation, formation energy, and energy above hull. The experimental results validate the immense potential of the proposed physics-informed multi-objective evolutionary optimization framework in crystal material design.

title: Unveiling of Clusters thin film transistor 3, 3-dimethyl pentane sensor; A biomarker linked to breast cancer_supp1-3690782.docx
authors: Basanta Bhowmik
abstract: This study demonstrated for the first time 3, 3-dimethylpentane detection using MoS2 thin film transistor (TFT) and modified Ni cluster MoS2 TFT sensor, a biomarker linked to breast cancer. The Ni clusters make the film more sensitive and selective by creating active adsorption sites and catalytic interactions. The MoS2 film was prepared by low cost hydrothermal followed by dip coating for successfully forming Ni cluster on MoS2/SiO2/Si. Electrical characterizations of the Ni-MoS2 TFT for the –1 V to 2.5 V bias window showed Ion/Ioff, sub-threshold swing (SS), threshold voltage (Vt), and mobility (μn) as 5.3×106, 67 mv/decade, 0.6 V, and 47 cm2/Vs, respectively. Under dynamic response measurements Ni-cluster modified MoS₂ surfaces demonstrated better 3, 3-dimethylpentane adsorption capability than the pristine MoS2. 3, 3-dimethylpentane sensing showed maximum response of 79% and 59.6% at 150°C and 200°C for Ni-MoS2 thin film and pristine MoS2, respectively. The sensor showed fast response time of ∼5s with lower detection limits of ∼1 ppm, and good repeatability. Electrostatic gate induced adsorption-desorption of 3, 3-dimethylpentane accelerated by spill over mechanism of Ni cluster on MoS2 surfaces were correlated considering barrier limited energy band diagram and charge transfer model.

title: Tackling a Resource-Sharing Hybrid Disassembly Line Balancing Problem Using Reinforcement Learning_supp1-3689904.pdf
authors: Liang Qi
abstract: Driven by accelerated product obsolescence and frequent consumer replacements, electronic waste is growing rapidly. Waste recycling, as a core component of resource reuse, has become an important means of alleviating resource scarcity and reducing environmental pollution. In the process of recycling discarded products, the efficiency of disassembly operations is crucial. To improve disassembly efficiency and maximize resource utilization, this work proposes a hybrid disassembly line structure that incorporates both linear and U-shaped workstations. Shared labor is introduced between adjacent disassembly lines, allowing workers to flexibly execute tasks across lines. This resource-sharing mechanism enhances task coordination and reduces idle time, contributing to improved system efficiency. Using a precedence relationship graph to model dependencies among tasks, we develop a mathematical model aimed at maximizing profit. We use an exact solver to verify the model and adopt a variant of dueling deep Q-network, called PER-Dueling DQN (PDDQN), which incorporates prioritized experience replay to enhance sampling efficiency and solve the model optimally. A simulation environment aligned with this problem is constructed for the reinforcement learning agent. We compare the proposed method with other reinforcement learning approaches, including advantage actor-critic, proximal policy optimization, and trust region policy optimization. Through experiments on disassembling products of different sizes, the feasibility and effectiveness of PDDQN are demonstrated, exhibiting significant advantages over other methods.

title: Ensemble Model for EEG-Based Emotion Recognition with Non-Linear Fractal Features in Smart Healthcare Automation
authors: Rabita Hasan, Sheikh Md. Rabiul Islam
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Emotional state recognition is essential for mental health assessment and the development of intelligent healthcare systems. This study proposes an EEG-based emotion recognition framework for smart healthcare automation, utilizing non-linear fractal features from the DEAP dataset. Two descriptors: Higuchi Fractal Dimension (HFD) and Petrosian Fractal Dimension (PFD) were extracted from 32-channel EEG signals to capture complex brain dynamics. Ensemble models, including Light Gradient Boosting Machine (Light-GBM) and Random Forest, were applied for binary classification of valence and arousal. After preprocessing (bandpass filtering, 3 -second segmentation, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{Z}$</tex> score normalization and binary labeling), results showed that HFD outperformed PFD, achieving 82.47% and 80.62% accuracy for valence and arousal with Light-GBM. Independent-samples t-tests confirmed the statistical significance of these differences. The findings highlight HFD's superior ability to model EEG non-linearity, offering a robust approach for emotion-aware healthcare applications such as mental health monitoring, neurofeedback and personalized smart systems.

title: Leakage Fault Diagnosis in Low.Voltage Distribution Areas with Photovoltaic Integration Based on the SOGI Algorithm
authors: Rongxiang Su
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: The increasing integration of solar photovoltaic (PV) systems into low-voltage distribution networks presents significant challenges to conventional leakage protection schemes. To address these issues, this paper proposes a leakage fault diagnosis method based on a second-order generalized integrator (SOGI) algorithm. The introduction of the SOGI algorithm effectively mitigates the impact of PV generation on the original residual current characteristics and reduces high-frequency harmonics in the system. Meanwhile, considering that using the SOGI algorithm to process historical data requires a certain convergence time, this paper proposes a globally optimized SOGI algorithm. This method solves all states of the historical data at once, making the processed results closely match those obtained from real-time data acquisition using the SOGI algorithm in practical engineering. Experimental results show that this method can significantly improve the accuracy of leakage fault diagnosis for low-voltage distribution areas with PV integration.

title: Formal Verification of Line-Following Robot Control Using Linear Temporal Logic
authors: Md Khurram Monir Rabby, Md. Fahad Jubayer, Asiful Islam Bhuiyan, Zakaria Mahmud
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: This paper addresses the challenge of ensuring formally verified behaviors in autonomous line-following robots by integrating Linear Temporal Logic (LTL) with sensor-based control. A control synthesis methodology is proposed that transforms temporal logic specifications into executable control strategies for differential drive robots. The approach utilizes an observation map that converts continuous sensor readings into discrete propositions, allowing real-time evaluation of LTL formulas. The implementation features a dual-mode simulation environment, supporting both physics-based dynamic simulation and simplified kinematic analysis. Key contributions include: a formal problem formulation for temporal logic-based line following, a control framework that guaranties satisfaction of complex temporal specifications, and validation demonstrating robust performance under the given track configurations. The results show that the proposed method maintains the accuracy of the tracking while providing formal guaranties for recovery behaviors that meet specifications such as coverage and sequencing requirements. In this work, the integration of formal methods with robot control allows a foundation for developing autonomous systems with verifiable safety and performance characteristics.

title: Quantum Annealing for Staff Scheduling in Educational Environments
authors: Alessia Ciacco, Francesca Guerriero, Eneko Osaba
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: We address a novel staff allocation problem that arises in the organization of collaborators among multiple school sites and educational levels. The problem emerges from a real case study in a public school in Calabria, Italy, where staff members must be distributed across kindergartens, primary, and secondary schools under constraints of availability, competencies, and fairness. To tackle this problem, we develop an optimization model and investigate a solution approach based on quantum annealing. Our computational experiments on real-world data show that quantum annealing is capable of producing balanced assignments in short runtimes. These results provide evidence of the practical applicability of quantum optimization methods in educational scheduling and, more broadly, in complex resource allocation tasks.

title: MADQRL: Distributed Quantum Reinforcement Learning Framework for Multi-Agent Environments
authors: Abhishek Sawaika, Samuel Yen-Chi Chen, Udaya Parampalli, Rajkumar Buyya
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) is one of the most practical ways to learn from real-life use-cases. Motivated from the cognitive methods used by humans makes it a widely acceptable strategy in the field of artificial intelligence. Most of the environments used for <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R L}$</tex> are often high-dimensional, and traditional RL algorithms becomes computationally expensive and challenging to effectively learn from such systems. Recent advancements in practical demonstration of quantum computing (QC) theories, such as compact encoding, enhanced representation and learning algorithms, random sampling, or the inherent stochastic nature of quantum systems, have opened up new directions to tackle these challenges. Quantum reinforcement learning (QRL) is seeking significant traction over the past few years. However, the current state of quantum hardware is not enough to cater for such highdimensional environments with complex multi-agent setup. To tackle this issue, we propose a distributed framework for QRL where multiple agents learn independently, distributing the load of joint training from individual machines. Our method works well for environments with disjoint sets of action and observation spaces, but can also be extended to other systems with reasonable approximations. We analyze the proposed method on cooperativepong environment and our results indicate <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sim 10 \%$</tex> improvement from other distribution strategies, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sim 5 \%$</tex> improvement from classical models of policy representation.

title: Adaptable City Planning
authors: Haifeng Zhu, Sirasak Tepjit, Ray Barton
abstract: Modern cities are huge and complex systems. Traditional planning methods are hard to support such complexities, especially related to smart cities. Many new elements, such as UAVs (Unmanned Aerial Vehicles), the Internet of Things and electric vehicles, have emerged that are outside of urban planners’ expertise. Adaptability in systems engineering has been researched and developed significantly during the past 20 years, as Adaptive Systems Engineering (ASE), and successfully used in areas of aerospace, defense and communication, etc. Urban planning is one of the areas that adaptability is critically important for sustainability and livability of the city. This paper presents the Adaptable City Planning (ACP) method by applying ASE theory and methods to ensure planning a sustainable city, leveraging comprehensive computational power. We executed the ACP process using real-world data from Khon Kaen city, the $4^{\text {th }}$ largest city in Thailand, and demonstrated that ACP is able to produce a better outcome in 2030 than existing plans. ACP brings in systems thinking and supports policymakers and city planners with many technological components, which enables data-driven decisions and provides significant advantages over current planning methods. Its success also attracted standard organizations’ attentions, and was collected into the current draft of the IEEE P2784 Smart Cities standard by its committee.

title: Quantum Network Simulation and Emulation: A Roadmap for Quantum Internet Design
authors: Brian Doolittle, Michael Cubeddu
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Quantum networks are advancing the information technology infrastructure of society. Simulation and emulation software tools have emerged to support the design, development, and deployment of quantum networks, however, classical simulation and emulation methods have major bottlenecks in the error, latency, and cost that they can achieve at scale. In this work, we review quantum network simulation and emulation tools, including foundational principles, state-of-the-art tools, and bottlenecks. We then discuss how quantum technologies can address these challenges, and we construct a roadmap for the adoption of quantum simulation and emulation tools, emphasizing codesign with quantum network testbeds.

title: Temporal Framework for Causality-Preserving Scheduling of Measurements in Quantum Networks
authors: Jakob Kaltoft Søndergaard, René Bødker Christensen, Petar Popovski
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Distributed quantum protocols rely on classical feedforward information to process measurement outcomes, but heterogeneous hardware and uncertain local timing can make the causal order of measurements ambiguous when inferred solely from arrival times. Even in simple line networks with only Pauli measurements, end nodes cannot distinguish whether a missing outcome is caused by slow measurement or by delayed classical propagation. To resolve this ambiguity, we propose a time-division architecture for quantum networks in which nodes perform measurements in pre-assigned slots, ensuring a unique causal interpretation of outcomes. We formalize this temporal framework and derive the feedforward and adjacency constraints required to preserve measurement causality. For simple network topologies, we present an algorithm that yields optimal measurement schedules. Overall, the proposed time-division model provides a practical coordination layer that bridges the classical network timing with quantum measurement processing, enabling reliable and scalable measurement-based quantum networking.

title: Effects of Redundancy and Current Nonuniformity on Multi-Ball Electromigration in Advanced Packaging
authors: Howard Gan, Jeffrey Zhang
abstract: To meet ever-increasing power carrying demands in high performance products, the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$I_{\text {max }}$</tex> limited by BGA electromigration in advanced packaging was analyzed considering the redundancy effect and nonuniform current re-distribution by a Monte Carlo model. Design guidelines and Calculator were developed to optimize the current carrying capacity to meet reliability requirements and avoid over-design simultaneously.

title: PCGNet: A CNN-Based Real-Time Cardiac Auscultation Classification System
authors: Faizul Hakim, Jahin Ahmed, Ananya Anower, Manosh Sur Choudhury, Aishwariya Dutta, Akid Ornob
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Cardiac auscultation is a cost-effective, accessible method for early detection of cardiac diseases. However, its diagnostic effectiveness is limited due to its low signal-to-noise ratio and high subject dependency. To address these problems, a novel CNN model, PCGNet, is proposed for cardiac sound classification in real-time. The real-time system uses a CNN-based classification system and digital stethoscope and is trained on a reference dataset from PhysioNet 2016. In this study, the 1D phonocardiogram signals were initially denoised and subsequently transformed into 2D scalograms, which served as the input to the PCGNet model. Both audio-level and scalogram-level augmentation were performed to address class imbalance. The PCGNet was then optimized through hyperparameter tuning and ultimately employed for real-time cardiac sound classification via digital stethoscope. PCGNet outperformed pre-trained AlexNet and VGG16 models in accuracy (94.2%), sensitivity (94.3%), and specificity (94%), with reduced computational complexity. It accurately evaluated heart sounds from four healthy individuals and two individuals with disease, demonstrating the technique's predictive capabilities and diagnostic relevance.

title: Wafer-Level Cryogenic CMOS Variability and its Impact on 6T SRAM Performance with Low Temperature Design-Technology Co-Optimization Considerations
authors: Keyang Zhu, Yuanxi Liao, Ran Tao, Dong Liu, Kai Xu, Xiaoli Hu, Qing Li, Hao Yang, Jiabao Ye, Xiao Yu, Bing Chen, Yuxuan Luo, Genquan Han, Dawei Gao, Ran Cheng
abstract: In this work, we report a wafer-level device-to-circuit variability study for 6T SRAM bitcells at low temperature (LT). Paired pull-up and pull-down transistors are characterized down to 15 K to extract device-to-device (DDV) and cycle-to-cycle (CCV) variations, while experimental calibrated SPICE models enable accurate evaluation of SRAM static noise margin (SNM) across temperatures. Although DDV and CCV increase at cryogenic temperatures, the nominal SNM improves by 23% from 300 to 77 K due to enhanced subthreshold behaviour. Moreover, the secondary impact of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$V_{\mathrm{T}}$</tex> modulation, as an essential task within LT design-technology co-optimization (DTCO), is quantitatively analyzed, revealing improved uniformity but reduced nominal SNM. This study establishes a systematic link between cryogenic device physics and memory-level stability, offering practical DTCO guidelines for high performance computing and reliable memory designs for LT CMOS computing platforms.

title: A Semantically Integrated Approach for MBSEMDAO Model Transformation
authors: Tianxiao Xu, Néjib Moalla, Mohand-Lounes Bentaha, Giuseppe Cereda, Claudia Agostinelli
abstract: In the automotive domain, Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) has been widely adopted as an effective approach to managing system complexity, enabling the establishment of traceability between system requirements and various design elements. Integrating Multidisciplinary Design Analysis and Optimization (MDAO) into MBSE provides a quantitative verification mechanism that supports multidisciplinary trade-off analysis. Similarly, MBSE also enhances MDAO’s capability to manage complex problems. However, a large number of MBSE and MDAO integration applications are tool-dependent, which means that when modeling languages or software tools are upgraded, the model transformation applications require significant time to modify or even complete redesign. Developing a reusable, scalable, and tool-independent integration approach that enables model transformation between MBSE and MDAO remains a significant challenge. This research proposes an MBSE and MDAO integrated workflow based on a semantic integration approach. It provides metamodel mapping between system models created in System Modelling Language (SysML) v1 and v2 and multidisciplinary models written in Python, thus achieving model transformation as well as guaranteeing digital continuity between the complex system design and analysis processes. The proposed approach is validated through a multidisciplinary trade-off case study in the automotive domain and is implemented by using a specific SysML tool together with an open-source MDAO tool.

title: Design of a Microcontroller-Based Heart-Rate Monitor
authors: Deshen Lv, Qi Chen, Mengting Zeng, Yongjian Chen, Renhe Zuo
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: To achieve low-cost and efficient heart-rate measurement, this research designs a microcontroller-based heart-rate monitor. Regarding the system hardware, the designed heart-rate monitor is composed of several components, including a microcontroller control module, a heart-rate detection module, a liquid crystal display (LCD) module, a power supply module, an audible and visual alarm module, and a button module. With the AT89S52 microcomputer serving as the control core, the proposed heart-rate monitor is capable of converting heart-rate information into digital signals that can be recognized by the microcontroller through the heart-rate detection module. By using its internal timer and counter to perform timing and counting, the microcontroller can detect the number of pulses per minute. The above buttons are utilized to set the upper limit of pulse rate. In the case of detecting that the pulse rate per minute exceeds the set value, the microcontroller drives the audible and visual alarm circuit to emit sound and light, thereby reminding people of an abnormal health status. During the foregoing process, the microcontroller continuously drives the LCD screen to display the measured pulse rate and the upper limit set by the button module in real-time. Simultaneously, the power supply module supplies power for the entire system. Through physical validation, the proposed heart-rate monitor can accurately detect heart rate, while presenting a variety of advantages, such as over-limit alarm function, simple operation, practicality, and low cost.

title: Towards Real-World Solutions: QSVM with NSGA-II Feature Map Search for Imbalanced Classification
authors: Jay Patel, Yaqi Han, Erin Li, Jamal Kawach
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: We assess whether quantum models can be a practical choice for imbalanced datasets where good minority class performance is required. The proposed pipeline uses a multi-objective genetic search (NSGA-II) to discover compact feature maps under fixed qubit and depth budgets and pre-computes Gram matrices on GPU for efficiency. We compare against classical baselines on a financial dataset and measure the effect of decision-threshold selection on F1. Using QSVM as a base model of the pipeline, we obtain an F1 within about one hundredth of strong classical baselines at the default threshold and observe a stable precision-recall trade-off across a broad range of thresholds. Taken together, these results suggest that compact feature-map search and efficient circuit evaluation are key ingredients for deploying competitive quantum models for minority-class classification at this data scale.

title: Modeling Energy-Performance-Aware IoT Networks Using Hierarchical Colored Petri Nets
authors: Diogo Lages, Marcos Falcão, Andson Balieiro, Jamilson Dantas, Dalton Valadares
abstract: Modern Internet of Things (IoT) networks comprise a vast number of interconnected devices capable of seamless communication and cooperation, thereby enhancing the intelligence and efficiency of different environments. They are growing in scale and complexity, making it difficult to assess energy-performance trade-offs before deployment. Yet robust pre-deployment evaluation is critical to ensure energy efficiency and compliance with performance requirements. To address this challenge, we introduce a hierarchical Colored Petri Net (CPN) formal model that jointly captures both device-level energy usage and network performance metrics. The proposed model integrates CPU power states, transceiver operations, communication delays, and unreliable channel conditions into a unified framework. Key innovations include support for heterogeneous CPU architectures and multiple transceivers per node, bidirectional communication, and fine-grained interactions between CPU and transceiver components. This fine-grained formal approach enables combined evaluation of energy consumption and throughput under realistic conditions before physical implementation. We validate the model against real hardware, comprising an ARM7TDMI CPU and an SX1278 LoRa transceiver, demonstrating high accuracy with relative errors below $0.4 \%$ for CPU energy, under 2% for transceiver energy, and near-zero for throughput. These results highlight the model’s accuracy and practical relevance. Overall, the work contributes a unified, modular, and formally verifiable modeling framework to enhance energy-performance analysis for complex, realistic IoT systems.

title: Channel-Side Interlayer Engineering in Laminated Fe-FETs: Trap-Driven Optimization of Memory Window, Retention, and Disturb in Ferroelectric NAND Flash
authors: Lance Fernandes, Minji Shon, Prasanna Venkatesan, Priyankka Ravikumar, TaeYoung Song, Mengkun Tian, Kijoon Kim, Woohyun Hwang, Kwangyou Seo, Suhwan Lim, Kwangsoo Kim, Wanki Kim, Daewon Ha, Andrea Padovani, Suman Datta, Shimeng Yu, Asif Khan
abstract: In this work, we demonstrate for the first time that laminated Fe-FETs for Fe-NAND exhibit a distinct behavior: despite the common expectation that higher-<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$k$</tex> channel-side interlayers (C-ILs) enhance reliability by lowering the equivalent oxide thickness (EOT), lower-<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$k$</tex> C-ILs instead deliver superior memory-window (MW), retention, and pass-disturb performance. We systematically compare three different pure-oxide C-ILs <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\text{SiO}_{2}, \text{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}, \text{HfO}_{2})$</tex> through experiments and TCAD modeling to elucidate trap-driven reliability. The results reveal that although high- <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$k$</tex> C-IL layer reduce EOT, their higher trap densities cause MW narrowing, faster retention loss, and enhanced charge trapping. Devices with an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$8 ~\text{nm} \text{Hf}_{0.5} \text{Zr}_{0.5} \mathrm{O}_{2} / 3 ~\text{nm} \text{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3} / 8 ~\text{nm} \text{Hf}_{0.5} \text{Zr}_{0.5} \mathrm{O}_{2}$</tex> stack and 1.5 nm C-ILs confirm that the low <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$k$</tex> oxide achieves the most stable operation, exhibiting 15% larger MW, 1% retention loss at 1 ks, and 44% lower electron-trapping-induced disturb degradation after 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">6</sup> passdisturb cycles. This behavior arises from trap-induced charges that outweighs the expected benefit of high dielectric constant, as confirmed by TCAD simulations. These findings highlight C-IL trap engineering as a key enabler for reliable, high-density FeNAND integration and clarify how channel IL-dependent traps govern reliability in laminated Fe-FETs.

title: Evaluating Few-Shot Large Language Models for Intrusion Detection in Smart Grid Communication Systems
authors: Nourhan Ibrahim, Rasha Kashef
abstract: Efficient Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) in smart grids are thus required due to the substantial cybersecurity risks brought by the growing digitization of communication networks, given changing attack behaviors and limited labeled data. This paper provides a framework for comparing large language model (LLM)-based approaches and conventional machine learning (ML) classifiers for intrusion detection across three benchmark datasets: TON-IoT, UNSW-NB15, and CICIDS2017, under both binary and multiclass classification tasks. We investigate three complementary configurations: (1) baseline machine learning models trained on structured features, (2) LLMs employed as semantic feature extractors through MiniLM embeddings, and (3) few-shot LLM classification with prompt-based reasoning and GPT-4o-mini. Experimental results show that LLM-based approaches are more flexible and generalizable than traditional ML models, which attain near-perfect accuracy when trained on large labeled datasets. Specifically, with just ten labeled samples per class, the few-shot GPT-4o-mini configuration achieved accuracies of up to 0.78 (binary) and 0.73 (multiclass), significantly lowering data requirements without sacrificing competitive detection performance. These results demonstrate how few-shot LLMs may be used to provide lightweight, scalable, and semantically aware intrusion detection systems for communication networks in smart grids.

title: An Optimized Machine Learning Approach for Polycystic Ovary Syndrome Detection Using Feature Selection and Hyperparameter Tuning
authors: Rubaiya Hafiz, Lubaba Binta Abu Bakar, Tasnim Choudhury
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS) is a prevalent condition affecting women of all ages globally. It is a real challenge for doctors to identify it early and accurately because of the wide variety of symptoms. Here, we propose a model for the early prediction of PCOS using clinical data so that the chances of long-term complications can be reduced. We began with a Kaggle dataset containing information on 541 patients, each with 44 distinct clinical features. After carefully cleaning and organizing this data, we applied 12 baseline algorithms—including Random Forest, Logistic Regression, SVM, Decision Tree, KNN, XGBoost, CatBoost, AdaBoost, Gradient Boosting, LightGBM, etc.—on the dataset to obtain the prediction results. Among them, the highest accuracies were achieved by Random Forest, XGBoost, Gradient Boosting, and LightGBM. These four models were then selected for further experiments involving feature selection and hyperparameter optimization. After testing various techniques, the best performance in terms of Accuracy (0.92638), Precision (0.926887), Recall (0.92638), and F1-score (0.925149) was achieved by the Gradient Boosting model combined with the MutualInfo feature selector and the TPOT optimizer. Additionally, the highest ROC_AUC value was obtained using the Random Forest model with the Variance feature selection method and the TPOT optimizer.

title: A New Model for Reliability Design Rules Against Well Charging Damage in IC Process
authors: Mingte Lin, T. C. Chen, W. C. Chang, S. N. Kuo, R. Hsiao
abstract: The effect of well charging damage (WCD) on transistor in deep N-well (DNW) structures with varying areas of metal antennas, DNW and gates is evaluated. WCD is shown to depend on the areas of the antenna, DNW, protection diode (PD), and gate. A larger well antenna area will enhance WCD, while increasing DNW, gate and PD areas will reduce WCD. The position of gate relative to the charging antenna contact also impacts the WCD effect. A new well charging model is proposed to encompass all the charging factors and quantitatively predict the degree of well charging effect. Functions for voltage and current of gate and DNW as a function of charging time are derived to explain the WCD results. The electric voltages and currents of gate and DNW in various structures are simulated to demonstrate the WCD evaluation results from our experiments. With the new model, design rules and a safe operation area of device structure against the WCD in the DNW configuration can be established with clear guidelines.

title: A Parallel BiGRU-FEDformer Framework for Power Load Forecasting
authors: Jinyang Lv
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: Precise power load forecasting is a key step in ensuring stable and efficient grid operation, laying the foundation for supply-demand balance and energy planning. This study proposes a parallel forecasting framework based on BiGRUFEDformer to solve the problems of non-stationarity and multiscale complexity in load data. First, the original load sequence was decomposed using variational mode decomposition, with the penalty factor and the number of modes optimized through the particle swarm optimization algorithm. Then, this study used the K-means clustering algorithm based on sample entropy to reconstruct the decomposed components, which reduces noisy data due to the process of data collection and processing. Lastly, this study used an adaptive weighted sum mechanism to fuse the FEDformer and BiGRU neural networks, which constructed a BiGRU-FEDformer parallel model to improve its ability to capture local bidirectional temporal dynamics and long-term global dependencies for power load forecasting. The results show that the proposed parallel BiGRU-FEDformer model possesses outstanding predictive capability. Particularly in the 336 steps prediction, the model achieves an MSE of 0.2098, representing an accuracy improvement of at least approximately 10% compared to all baseline models.

title: RF Characterization of a Large-Diameter Embedded Reflective Half-Wave Plate
authors: Andrea Occhiuzzi, Giampaolo Pisano, Barbieri Ripamonti, P. de Bernardis, F. Columbro, A. Coppolecchia, I. Di Nitto, L. Lamagna, S. Masi, F. Piacentini, R. Sudiwala, C. Tucker, Marteen van der Vorst
abstract: Many of the current and future polarization experiments require the quasi-optical modulation of linear polarization at millimeter and sub-millimeter wavelengths. The need for large-aperture telescopes (diameters larger than 500mm) combined with broad frequency coverage has made half-wave plate technologies based on birefringent crystals often not compliant with other mission requirements, such as weight allocation. The Embedded Reflective Half-Wave Plate (ER-HWP) is a new device developed to achieve the required phase-shifts across a wide range of frequencies, in particular a multi-octave bandwidth of approximately 150%[1]. A device with a diameter of 650mm and a thickness of 750μm has been successfully manufactured at Cardiff University. In this work, we present the room-temperature experimental characterization of this ER-HWP. A coherent test system, capable of transmitting and receiving S- and P- polarizations, was employed to measure the differential phase-shift, the axes' reflection coefficients, the modulation efficiency, and the beam pattern distortion introduced by the device. We measured a modulation efficiency and axes' reflection coefficient greater than 90% within the band of interest, together with an acceptable level of the beam pattern deformation. A subset of these measurements is planned to be repeated at approximately 10K using a large-diameter cryogenic testbed, currently under development at Sapienza University, capable of testing devices with an effective diameter of up to 1m mounted at the coldest stage.

title: Automatic Prompt Generation via Contrastive Self-feedback
authors: Lib Kim, Haelim Namgung, Namgyu Kim
abstract: The rapid expansion of large language model (LLM) applications across diverse domains has spurred increasing interest in automatic prompt generation, a technique focused on the efficient design of task-specific prompts. Among such approaches, the automatic prompt engineer has gained attention as a representative method for automating prompt engineering by enabling models to generate instructions based on few-shot examples. However, studies report inconsistent instruction quality, and that replacing weaker prompts with semantically similar versions of stronger ones restricts semantic diversity. To address these limitations, this study proposes a novel method, automatic prompt generation via contrastive self-feedback, which enables the model to revise its own low-quality outputs autonomously. Instead of replacing low-quality instructions, the proposed method refines them through feedback-driven iteration. Specifically, the model constructs contrastive examples by pairing each low-quality instruction’s generated response with its corresponding ground-truth output and applies self-feedback to identify reasoning errors and revise the original instruction. Experimental evaluations conducted on the IELTS Writing Essay, ASAP++ and ELLIPSE dataset demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms existing automatic prompt generation techniques with respect to instruction quality and scoring accuracy, as measured by the quadratic weighted kappa metric. Code will be made available at https://github.com/rachelnamgung/Automatic-Prompt-Generation-via-Contrastive-Self-feedback.

title: Synchronous Models and Fundamental Systems in Observer Design
authors: Pieter van Goor, Robert Mahony
abstract: This paper introduces the concept of a synchronous model as an extension of the internal model concept used in observer design for dynamical systems. A system is said to contain a synchronous model of another if there is a suitable error function between the two systems that remains stationary for all of the trajectories of the two systems. For any given system, we provide necessary and sufficient conditions that there exists another system that contains a synchronous model of the first, and a method to construct a (there may be many) model should one exist. We characterise the class of all systems for which synchronous models can be constructed by showing that they consist of fundamental vector fields induced by a Lie group action, a class of systems we term fundamental systems. For fundamental systems we propose a simple synchronous observer design methodology, for which we show how correction terms can be discretised and combined easily, facilitating global characterisation of convergence and performance. Finally, we provide three examples to demonstrate the key concepts of synchrony, symmetry construction, and observer design for a fundamental system.

title: A Segmentation-Guided Feature Alignment and Fusion Network for Glioma IDH Genotyping
authors: Minghui Chen, Guohua Zhao, Lei Yang, Haowen Zhu, Hongwei Xu, Huiqin Jiang, Ling Ma
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Isocitrate dehydrogenase (IDH) is a pivotal molecular marker for glioma diagnosis, prognosis, and treatment planning. Multi-modal deep learning methods, which integrate features from multiple magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) sequences, have become a powerful solution for non-invasive IDH genotyping. However, existing methods still have limitations in feature extraction and fusion, which constrains their robustness. In this work, we propose a novel segmentation-guided feature alignment and fusion network (SFAF-Net) for glioma IDH genotyping, with three key innovations: 1) The Segmentation-guided Feature Alignment (SFA) module leverages tumor segmentation supervision to facilitate cross-modal feature alignment; 2) The Redundancy-Attenuated Fusion (RAF) module implements similarity-based selective fusion of modality pairs to reduce feature redundancy; 3) A randomized modality dropout mechanism within RAF enhances model robustness against input variations. Comprehensive experiments conducted on public and private datasets demonstrate that SFAF-Net outperforms state-of-the-art methods across diverse MRI sequences. Moreover, SFAF-Net supports an arbitrary number of input sequences, enabling flexible adaptation to diverse clinical scanning protocols in personalized diagnosis.

title: R2C-MGR2L: Enhancing Multi-granularity Relational Representation in Dual-temporal Optical Remote Sensing Images through LLM-based Scene Graph Generation
authors: Lilu Zhu, Xiaolu Su, Jiaxuan Tang, Yang Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Recent advances in Earth observations have enabled the acquisition of high-precision optical remote sensing imagery (ORSI) with sub-meter spatial resolution and hourly revisit periods. While early neural network-based methods have demonstrated notable limitations in visual perception, more recent multimodal fusion approaches still struggle to effectively recognize and reason over geospatial object relations. To fully promote the collaborative analysis of very-high-resolution (VHR) dual-temporal ORSIs, this paper presents a novel framework named R2C-MGR2L that is designed to enhance multi-granularity relational representation in dual-temporal optical remote sensing images through LLM-based scene graph generation (SGG). This framework presents a two-stage learning paradigm—knowledge alignment and cross-modal fusion—that incorporates structured knowledge representations and a hierarchical attention mechanism to improve the refinement and reasoning of fine-grained relational semantics. First, we investigate the types of geospatial object relations and assess the feasibility of enhancing relational semantic representation through multimodal knowledge extraction and embedding. We subsequently employ a pre-trained code large language model (Code-LLM) to transform multigranularity relation triplets from extracted optical remote sensing (ORS) scene graphs into semantically equivalent programs, thereby alleviating the scarcity of domain-specific ORSI knowledge. Moreover, a hybrid-structured asymmetric contrastive learning network is developed to further refine the semantic representations derived from complex scenes. By hierarchically fusing dual-temporal ORSIs and corresponding scene graphs, an integrated representation of visual targets and structural knowledge across multiple depths is achieved. We evaluate our proposed framework on three relation-aware ORS scene understanding tasks. The experimental results suggest that the R2C-MGR2L outperforms the state-of-the-art generic methods, resulting in consistent improvements in accuracy, completeness and timeliness for relational semantic recognition.

title: Ka-Band Bandwidth-Enhanced Antenna Array Based on Low-Profile High-Efficiency Dielectric-Free Radiator with UV-LIGA Technology
authors: Ai Hu Song, Jian Dai, Yu Jian Cheng
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: This letter proposes a Ka-band bandwidth-enhanced beam-scanning antenna array with a dielectric-free dual-slot radiator with high efficiency. An asymmetric feeding position is adapted in the radiation element to improve the impedance bandwidth and to maintain a 0.13λh low-profile without the additional matching structure. To prevent the abnormal side lobe of the 16 × 16 antenna array, a metal strip is loaded on the assembly gap to eliminate the resonance mode. The antenna array is fabricated with ultraviolet lithographie, galvanoformung, and abformung (UV-LIGA) technology to guarantee the radiation efficiency and the machining accuracy in Ka band. The 17% relative bandwidth is realized to cover 32–38 GHz. The measured radiation efficiency is over 84%. The antenna array covers the scanning range of ±60° in E- and H-plane without the grating lobe and the scanning blindness.

title: Estimating Patient-specific Tooth Reference Model from CBCT Images for Precise Dental Implant Planning via Point Cloud Diffusion Model
authors: Lei Ma, Zongrui Ji, Yi Jiang, Yunbi Liu, Guangming Zhang, Na Li, Ningyu Wang, Peng Qi, Yi Dong, Peng Xue
publish: IEEE Transactions on Radiation and Plasma Medical Sciences
abstract: Dental implantation necessitates comprehensive preoperative planning to achieve optimal implant positioning and surgical efficiency, owing to the intricate three-dimensional (3D) oral anatomy and confined surgical field. A fundamental challenge in current implant planning is the absence of patient-specific 3D reference models for missing teeth. This compels dentists to rely on empirical decisions and labor-intensive manual adjustments during virtual planning, resulting in significant variability in positioning accuracy and planning efficiency. To address this limitation, we propose a point cloud deep learning framework that generates patient-specific 3D tooth reference models of missing teeth from cone-beam computed tomography (CBCT) images. Our approach begins by accurately reconstructing a complete dentition model from CBCT image and automatically identifying the adjacent teeth of a given missing tooth. We then introduce a conditional shape generation network based on point cloud diffusion model, leveraging geometric features of neighboring teeth to synthesize the missing tooth’s shape in point cloud. Finally, a three-stage post-processing pipeline converts the predicted tooth in point cloud into a clinical-grade surface mesh. Experimental results demonstrate significant superiority over competing volumetric methods, achieving a mean Chamfer distance of 1.16 mm, Hausdorff distance of 2.15 mm, and angular deviation of 3.63°. Qualitative validation on actual tooth-loss cases confirms seamless anatomical integration, with predicted teeth exhibiting natural continuity in crown morphology and occlusal alignment with adjacent dentition.

title: EdgeSAC: Graph Neural Soft Actor-Critic for Hierarchical IoV Resource Management
authors: Arif Raza, Uddin Md. Borhan, Yueling Che, Chen Jie, Lu Wang
abstract: Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS) rely on the Internet of Vehicles (IoV) to sustain high data rates and low latency under dynamic and heterogeneous conditions. Joint power and spectrum control across macro and micro tiers remains challenging due to mobility, interference coupling, and large continuous action spaces. EdgeSAC is a graph-aware Soft Actor Critic (SAC) framework executed at the edge for power control in hierarchical Fifth-Generation New Radio (5G NR) Multiple-Input Multiple-Output (MIMO) networks. A permutation-equivariant Graph Neural Network (GNN) with edge updates encodes co-channel interference among Base Stations (BSs) and outputs node-level power fractions under tier budgets. An on-demand scheduler activates fixed-size channels and assigns at most one macro and one micro resource per user to realize dual connectivity. Signal-to-Interference-plus-Noise Ratio (SINR) is mapped to rate using a Shannon with gap model with rank adaptive MIMO, enabling tier aggregation without action discretization. In simulation with Third Generation Partnership Project (3GPP) TR 38.901 path loss and Manhattan mobility, EdgeSAC increases throughput over SAC and Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) and reduces power relative to Twin Delayed Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (TD3), which raises energy efficiency and fairness. The findings indicate that interference-aware graph embeddings combined with entropy regularized continuous control provide a scalable and power-efficient solution for hierarchical IoV resource management.

title: Electronic Field Effect Transistor Biosensors: Fundamentals To Major Breakthroughs
authors: Usha Rani Dash, Mirza Rohan Ahamed, Piyali Mukherjee, Chirasree RoyChaudhuri
abstract: Field-effect transistor (FET) based biosensors have been established as robust analytical devices that integrate biological recognition elements with semiconductor transducers to enable label-free, rapid, and highly sensitive detection of chemical and biological analytes. Since the pioneering demonstration of the ion-sensitive field-effect transistor (ISFET) by Bergveld in the 1970s, extensive research has advanced device architectures, biofunctionalization strategies, and nanomaterial integration to enhance sensitivity, selectivity, and scalability. While numerous research to address the Debye screening effect have strengthened the understanding of interfacial electrostatics governing signal transduction, the incorporation of nanomaterials has significantly improved surface-to-volume ratios and charge sensitivity. Simultaneously, recent work has increasingly focused on improving translational readiness by reducing device-to-device variability and developing user-friendly fluidic interfaces for healthcare and environmental applications. Despite substantial progress and numerous comprehensive reviews, the multidisciplinary sensing mechanisms of FET biosensors, spanning semiconductor physics, electrostatics, surface chemistry and molecular biology, remain non-trivial for new researchers to the field. This tutorial provides a unified framework covering the fundamental operating principles of ISFET biosensors, critical surface functionalization strategies, and the physicochemical mechanisms underlying the sensing phenomena in bioreceptor-immobilized interfaces. Key technological limitations, including screening effects and reproducibility challenges, are systematically analyzed alongside emerging solutions. By consolidating foundational concepts with recent breakthroughs, this work aims to clarify design considerations, identify persistent research gaps, and guide the development of next-generation FET biosensors with enhanced performance and translational potential.
