title: UrbanTrunkSegNet: An Enhanced Deep Learning Approach to Individual Tree Trunk Segmentation for Urban Forest Inventory
authors: Irfan Abbas, Muhammad Abdullah Sarwar, Ali Amini, Rytis Maskeliūnas, Izabela Karsznia, Robertas Damaševičius
abstract: Urban tree trunk segmentation is a crucial part of accurate and scalable urban forest inventories. It allows for various downstream tasks such as tree health monitoring, trunk diameter estimates, and species identification. Segmenting trunks in street-level images presents challenges ranging from complex urban backgrounds to varying illumination conditions, occlusions, and structural diversity. To improve or mitigate these challenges for tree trunk segmentation, we developed UrbanTrunkSegNet, a hybrid deep learning model combining global feature modeling capabilities of a Vision Transformer (ViT), a portion of the U-Net++ decoder architecture to improve spatial detail, and leveraging multi-scale feature fusion and skip connections to improve boundary accuracy in complex urban settings. UrbanTrunkSegNet was evaluated using the Tree Binary Segmentation (TBS) and Urban Street Segmentation Trunk (USST) benchmark datasets. Across 8,007 images, UrbanTrunkSegNet achieved: Dice $=0.96$, IoU $=0.90$, and Accuracy $=0.95$ on TBS; and Dice $=0.90$, $\mathrm{IoU}=0.87$, and Accuracy $=0.89$ on USST. High segmentation performance and ability to generalize to complex urban scenes were confirmed with these results. Visual analysis confirmed high robustness against occlusions and background interference during segmentations and the ability to reliably segment multi-trunk structures with few false positives. These results confirm that UrbanTrunkSegNet is a reliable solution to automatically segment tree trunks within street-level images, with very good segmentation performance.

title: Reliability of Large Scale (width~1000 mm) Submicron $L_{g}$ Enhancement Mode High-K Gallium Nitride Transistors on 300Mm Gan-On-Silicon
authors: Ahmad Zubair, Kirby Maxey, Samuel Bader, Prafful Golani, Michael Beumer, Pratik Koirala, Heli Vora, Thomas Hoff, Curtis Hoffman, Mustafa Jamil, Marko Radosavljevic, Leonard Shtargot, Han Wui Then
abstract: We report, for the first time, reliability studies of large scale (width, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{w} \sim \mathbf{1 0 0 0 ~ m m}, \mathbf{R}_{\text {on }}<\mathbf{1 ~ m} \boldsymbol{\Omega}$</tex>) sub-micron Power GaN MOSHEMT Transistors on 300 mm GaN-on-silicon wafer with record low <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$R_{\text {dson }}$</tex> of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1.39 ~\mathrm{m} \Omega-\text{mm}^{2}$</tex>. We demonstrate, for a 10 -year lifetime at 90° C, (a) stabilization of PBTI with saturated <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\Delta V_{T}$</tex> of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2 0 0 ~ m V}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 1 \%}$</tex> shift in transistor <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}_{\text {on }}$</tex>; (b) TDDB <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{V}_{\text {gs, } \max }=\mathbf{2. 1} \mathrm{V}$</tex> with projected failure rate of 1 PPM; (c) stable transistor operation up to 30 V HTRB (reverse bias) stress with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$<20 \%$</tex> shift in Ron; and (d) HCI (hot carrier) Vds, max <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=15.6 ~\mathrm{V}$</tex>. Thermal resistance as low as <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$18 ~\mathrm{K} \cdot ~\text{mm} / \mathrm{W}$</tex> is characterized for the first time for a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{3 0 0 m m}$</tex> GaN-on-silicon wafer. These results are promising reliability indicators for <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{3 0 0 m m}$</tex> GaN-on-silicon MOSHEMT technology for <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 2 V}$</tex> power electronics for datacenter and highperformance computing applications.

title: Two-Dimensional Materials and Wide Bandgap Semiconductors: Integration Challenges and Novel Device Applications (Invited)
authors: F. Giannazzo, S. E. Panasci, E. Schilirò, G. Greco, M. Vivona, R. Lo Nigro, P. Fiorenza, F. Roccaforte
abstract: The integration of two dimensional (2D) materials, like graphene and MoS<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>, with wide bandgap (WBG) semiconductors (SiC, GaN and III-N alloys) is currently explored to enhance the performances of state-of-the-art WBG-based devices and to demonstrate innovative devices for applications in high frequency electronics and optoelectronics, including band-to-band tunneling diodes, hot electron transistors and UV photodetectors. This paper provides an overview of the state-of-the-art approaches for scalable integration of 2D materials on SiC and GaN, as well as examples of relevant device applications demonstrated so far. Open challenges to be addressed and future perspectives are discussed.

title: Electrical Pulse Signal Identification Incorporating Artificial Intelligence Pattern Recognition, Material Analyses, Microstructural Investigation, and Protective Configuration for AlInGaP Light Emitting Diode Deterioration in Saline Vapor
authors: Wei-Cheng Chen, Chun-Yen Yang, Yu-Hao Qiu, Zi-Han Wang, Wei-Kai Wang, Jian-Wei Liu, Chia-Chi Chang, Yaw-Wen Kuo, Raymond Wang, Jeih-Weih Hung, Wei-Han Hsiao, Hsin-Hung Chou, Chia-Feng Lin, Yung-Hui Li, Hsiang Chen, Jung Han
abstract: This study systematically investigates the degradation behavior of AlInGaP orange-red light-emitting diodes (LEDs) subjected to saline vapor exposure under reverse-bias electrical stress, and establishes a comprehensive reliability diagnosis framework integrating electrical, optical, material analyses, and artificial intelligence (AI). The experimental results indicate that saline vapor induces NaCl accumulation and downward elemental diffusion, leading to surface crack formation, internal layer erosion, and pronounced nonuniform electroluminescence. The corrosion-induced surface roughness was observed to reach several tens of micrometers. From an electrical perspective, two dominant failure modes—short-circuit and open-circuit degradation—were identified, and their evolution was clearly reflected by characteristic one-dimensional current–time (I–t) pulse waveforms. These waveforms exhibit distinct temporal features corresponding to different health states of the devices. By employing a one-dimensional convolutional neural network for waveform classification, various degradation stages were successfully distinguished with an identification accuracy exceeding 80%. In addition, AI-assisted analysis of two-dimensional electroluminescence images enabled noninvasive, automatic localization and labeling of abnormal emission regions, including hot spots and dark defects. Overall, the proposed AI-assisted diagnostic approach, which combines one-dimensional electrical pulse signal recognition with two-dimensional optical image analysis, provides an effective and practical solution for real-time degradation monitoring and protective design of LEDs operating in harsh saline environments.

title: The Carbon Footprint Grading Evaluation Method for Electrical Equipment: A Case Study of Transformers
authors: Ran Zhuo, Mengsi Wang, Meng Gao, Qiulin Chen, Sicheng Zhao, Ning Ding
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: To promote the low-carbon transformation of the electrical equipment industry, this study proposes a carbon footprint (CF) grading evaluation method for electrical equipment based on “CF + life cycle indicators.” This method quantifies the carbon emissions of electrical equipment throughout its entire life cycle and integrates low-carbon indicators from each life cycle stage to form a comprehensive CF evaluation system. Using transformers as a case study, the research analyzes the CF and low-carbon performance of transformers at different voltage levels. The results show that there are differences in CF and life cycle low-carbon indicators among different types of transformers, with the 330 kV transformer performing exceptionally well, receiving an overall CF grade of Level I + 89.57, indicating its excellent low-carbon performance. However, some transformers, despite having low carbon emissions, still have significant room for improvement in low-carbon design during their life cycle stages. The CF grading evaluation method proposed in this study provides scientific support for the low-carbon design optimization and green supply chain management of electrical equipment, helping to enhance the industry's overall carbon reduction benefits.

title: Autonomous Business System via Neuro-symbolic AI
authors: Cecil Pang, Hiroki Sayama
abstract: Modern business environments demand continuous reconfiguration of cross-functional processes, yet most enterprise systems remain organized around siloed departments, rigid workflows, and hard-coded automation. Meanwhile, large language models (LLMs) demonstrate strong capabilities in interpreting natural language and synthesizing unstructured information, but they lack deterministic, auditable execution of complex business logic. We introduce Autonomous Business System (AUTOBUS), a system that integrates LLM-based AI agents, predicate-logic programming, and business-semanticscentric enterprise data into a unified neuro-symbolic architecture for executing end-to-end business initiatives. AUTOBUS models a business initiative as a network of interrelated tasks with explicit pre- and post-conditions, required data, evaluation rules, and API-level actions. Enterprise data is organized as a knowledge graph, whose entities, relationships, and constraints are translated into logic facts and foundational rules that ground reasoning and ensure semantic consistency. Core AI agents synthesize task instructions, enterprise semantics, and available tools into taskspecific logic programs, which are executed by a logic engine that enforces constraints, coordinates auxiliary tools, and produces deterministic outcomes. Humans specify task instructions, define and maintain business semantics and policies, curate tools, and supervise high-impact or ambiguous decisions, ensuring accountability and adaptability. We detail the AUTOBUS architecture, the structure of AI-generated logic programs, and the human-AI collaboration model and present a case study that demonstrates accelerated time to market in a data-rich organization. A reference implementation of the case study is available at https://github.com/cecilpang/autobus-paper.

title: A2G-QFL: Adaptive Aggregation with Two Gains in Quantum Federated Learning
authors: Shanika Iroshi Nanayakkara, Shiva Raj Pokhrel
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Federated learning (FL) deployed over quantumenabled and heterogeneous classical networks can degrade under uneven client quality, modeled teleportation/link fidelity, and communication instability, while standard aggregation rules assume Euclidean topology and uniform reliability, limiting their suitability for emerging quantum federated systems. We introduce A2G (Adaptive Aggregation with Two Gains). This dualgain framework jointly regulates server-side relaxation through a geometry gain. It modulates client importance using a QoS gain derived from teleportation/link fidelity, latency, and instability (variance of the QoS signal). The proposed A2G update rule shows that A2G recovers baseline approaches such as FedAvg, Latency-only, QoS-aware averaging, and manifold-inspired aggregation as special cases. Experiments on a Qiskit Aer-based quantum-classical simulation testbed demonstrate improved stability and accuracy under heterogeneous, realistic, and simulated quantum-noisy conditions.

title: Explainable Multi-Agent Distributed Optimization for Integrated Power Grid Outage Scheduling
authors: Yang Zhang, Yue Zhu, Liu Feng, Jiantie Xu, Guan Wang
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: Outage scheduling in renewable-dominant power systems must jointly ensure operational security, supply adequacy, and renewable accommodation under strong topology coupling and strict <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{N}-\mathbf{1}$</tex> screening. This paper proposes an explainable multi-agent distributed optimization framework, termed X-MAADMM, for integrated outage planning. The problem is modeled as a multi-period security-constrained mixed-integer program with maintenance continuity and crew limits. A <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\varepsilon$</tex>-constraint strategy is used to generate a Pareto set by minimizing operating cost and security risk while bounding supply deficiency and curtailment. To improve scalability, the system is partitioned into regional agents that solve local subproblems in parallel, while inter-area couplings are enforced through ADMM-based consensus updates. Explainability is achieved by interpreting ADMM dual variables and binding constraints, which identify the dominant interfaces, limiting branches, and critical contingencies driving each maintenance decision. A case-study protocol and benchmark comparisons are provided to demonstrate feasibility, fast convergence, and audit-ready decision rationales for dispatcher adoption.

title: Wafer to Wafer Hybrid Bonding Pitch Scaling Challenges (Invited)
authors: Stefaan Van Huylenbroeck, Soon Aik Chew, Boyao Zhang, Shuo Kang, Lieve Bogaerts, Joke De Messemaeker, Emmanuel Chery, Serena Iacovo, Koen D'havé, Sven Dewilde, Joeri De Vos, Eric Beyne, Zsolt Tőkei
abstract: The past years technology aspects of wafer-to-wafer hybrid bonding pitch scaling down to 200nm was documented extensively. This paper discusses specific yield improvement challenges and solutions related to sub-micron scaling, such as wafer-to-wafer bond alignment accuracy, global and local wafer topography control by CMP ensuring void free hybrid bonding, electro-chemical copper corrosion of hybrid pads connected to long metal lines, surface-diffusion-driven copper bulge-out mechanisms, dielectric breakdown and copper diffusion along the bonding interface.

title: Thermal Robustness of a CMOS-Compatible GaN-on-Si MIS-HEMT Technology
authors: L. Nyssens, I. Charlet, A. Lassenberger, A. Machmach, A. Barboura, F. Morisot, Y. Gobil, E. Morvan, A. Divay
abstract: This paper investigates the reliability of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.15 \mu \mathrm{m}$</tex> CMOS-compatible, Au-free SiN/InAIN/GaN MIS-HEMTs under 1400 -hour, up to 375° C thermal storage tests with no bias. DC parameters monitoring, pulsed-IV measurements, HR-STEM, and EDX analyses demonstrate great transistor robustness to long-term 375° C storage stress without sidewall interdiffusion at the gate thanks to refractory-alloy-based gate metallization. A~200 mV V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">th</inf> shift, ~ 20% R_c increase, 7% and 5% reductions in I_d, S and g_m, \textmax, respectively, are the main degradations observed affecting transistor operation. Pulsed-IV measurements indicate little if any variation in trapping states. HR-STEM and EDX analyses under the gate and at the contacts show high morphological and chemical integrity even after thermal storage stress. Taking 5% degradation in I_d, S as failure criterion, an activation energy from 1.5 to 2 eV is extracted, and the predicted time to failure is above 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">7</sup> hours for a junction temperature of 200° C.

title: SE-Induced Parasitic BJTs at Advanced Bulk FinFET Nodes
authors: J. B. Kronenberg, Y. Xiong, N. J. Pieper, S. L. Tolson, X. Zhao, D. R. Ball, B. L. Bhuva
abstract: Experimental results, as well as Technology Computer-Aided Design (TCAD) simulations, are used to elucidate the activity of the single-event (SE) induced parasitic bipolar junction transistors (BJTs) at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$7-\text{nm}, 5-\text{nm}$</tex>, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3-\text{nm}$</tex> bulk FinFET technologies. Changes to well potential following an ion strike turn on parasitic BJTs. In the case of SE vulnerability, parasitic BJTs enhance charge collection by amplifying the initial current created by the ion strike. This work uses experimental single-event upset (SEU) results of D-FFs fabricated with triple-well and dual-well options, as well as circuits that compare the SE vulnerability of NMOS and PMOS transistors, to isolate the behavior of parasitic BJTs. Under nominal operating conditions, the contribution of parasitic BJTs to SE charge collection is shown to be significantly reduced at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$5-\text{nm}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3-\text{nm}$</tex> technology nodes compared to the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$7-\text{nm}$</tex> technology node.

title: Modeling the Impact of High-$\kappa$ Thickness Scaling (Down to 1.1 nm) on Gate Leakage and PBTI in Advanced FDSOI Devices
authors: Elhadji Alhousseyni Diallo, Tadeu Mota Frutuoso, William Vandendaele, Laurent Brévard, Claire Fenouillet-Beranger, Marie-Claire Cyrille, Xavier Garros
abstract: In this study we address the impact of high- <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\kappa$</tex> (HK) thickness scaling on both leakage current and PBTI. Modeling the gate current with a direct tunneling model reveals a higher HK tunnel mass than in former studies suggesting a microstructure change in these ultra-thin oxides <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(<2 n m)$</tex>. PBTI also improves when HK thickness decreases. This is well modeled with Comphy simulations, and explained by a different distribution of filled traps within the HK layer.

title: Research on Sensorless Low-Speed Control of Automotive Electronic Water Pumps Based on an Improved Active Flux Observer
authors: Pandeng Meng, Xin Cheng, Yan Deng, Hui Pan
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: Sensorless control is critical for the automotive electronic water pump, or EWP. However, traditional voltage models suffer from integral drift at low speeds. This paper proposes an improved active flux observer based on a secondorder generalized integrator, denoted as SOGI. By utilizing SOGI to replace pure integration, this method eliminates direct current drift issues. Experimental results demonstrate that the algorithm significantly enhances position estimation accuracy and disturbance rejection in the low-speed region.

title: Simulating Life Paths with Digital Twins: AI-Generated Future Selves Influence Decision-Making and Expand Human Choice
authors: Rachel Poonsiriwong, Chayapatr Archiwaranguprok, Constanze Albrecht, Peggy Yin, Nattavudh Powdthavee, Hal Hershfield, Monchai Lertsutthiwong, Kavin Winson, Pat Pataranutaporn
publish: Proceedings of the Augmented Humans International Conference 2026
abstract: None

title: Rethinking 5G Core for Performance, Fairness, and Flexibility
authors: Yang-Zhe Lin, Chun-Ting Lin, Po-Yi Lin, Shixiong Qi, Jyh-Cheng Chen, K. K. Ramakrishnan
publish: Proceedings of the 1st free5GC World Forum 2025
abstract: None

title: Image-Voiceprint Multi-Channel Bandwidth Allocation for Power Distribution Equipment Monitoring
authors: Hongfa Li, Aiting Qiu, Deihui Zhang, Lang Cheng, Man Xia, Jiamu Yang, Linjun Wang, Ziqing Qu
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: With the increase of power distribution equipment monitoring terminals and data acquisition rates, concurrent multichannel transmissions of image and voiceprint data under limited bandwidth led to large congestion and latency. To address this issue, this paper proposes an image-voiceprint multi-channel bandwidth allocation method for power distribution equipment monitoring. A bandwidth allocation model is first established by jointly considering channel transmission data disparity and total transmitted data volume, followed by a dynamic bandwidth allocation algorithm based on an improved Deep Q-Network (DQN) with a differentiated experience sampling mechanism. Simulation results demonstrate that, compared with baseline algorithms, the proposed method reduces channel transmission data disparity by 37.1% and 56.7%, and improves data processing throughput by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{7. 3 {\%}}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{4 7. 7 {\%}}$</tex>.

title: Learning Gaussian Processes with Randomized Quantum Local Kernels
authors: Abdallah Aaraba, Soumaya Cherkaoui, Ola Ahmad, Jean-Frédéric Laprade, Shengrui Wang
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Gaussian processes (GPs) are flexible nonparametric Bayesian priors over functions and are widely used in regression, time-series modelling, and Bayesian optimisation. Yet their performance is often bottlenecked by the choice of covariance kernel, especially when the relevant correlations are unknown or inherently non-stationary. In this work, we put forward a NISQ-compatible framework that uses a quantum device as a generator of local quantum kernels for GP learning. At a high level, shallow, geometry-aware randomly parametrized circuits with area-law entanglement encode inputs into many-qubit states, and few-body Pauli measurements-estimated efficiently via classical shadows-produce quantum feature vectors that are subsequently wrapped into classical scalar kernels. A multiplekernel learning layer then fits a convex mixture of these quantum kernels by maximizing the GP marginal likelihood, so that all heavy optimisation remains on the classical side, while the quantum hardware is used solely for kernel estimation. On synthetic regression tasks, we find that the learned mixture can approximate both stationary and non-stationary covariances, achieving high kernel alignment and accurate predictions. Taken together, these results suggest that local quantum kernels can in principle form a family of flexible GP covariance learners within realistic NISQ resource budgets.

title: Q2NS: A Modular Framework for Quantum Network Simulation in ns-3
authors: Adam Pearson, Francesco Mazza, Marcello Caleffi, Angela Sara Cacciapuoti
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Quantum network simulators are essential for the design and evaluation of quantum network architectures and protocols. However, developing an effective simulator is particularly challenging due to the stateful and non-local nature of quantum entanglement, which requires persistent state awareness and tight in-network coordination. Moreover, because quantum communication protocols inherently rely on classical signaling, a realistic simulator must seamlessly integrate classical and quantum communication primitives within a unified framework. In this work, we present <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$Q 2 N S$</tex>, a modular and extensible quantum network simulation framework built on top of ns-3. The architectural design of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$Q 2 N S$</tex> enforces a clear separation of concerns between the operational functionalities of modules and entities and the overarching control logic, enabling easy adaptation to heterogeneous and rapidly evolving Quantum Internet paradigms while leveraging ns-3's mature classical networking stack. Additionally, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{Q} 2 N S$</tex> includes a dedicated visualization tool to facilitate developing an intuitive understanding of entanglement-based quantum networking. This design enables accurate modeling of entanglement dynamics and protocol control logic, by providing a flexible, open, and scalable platform for advancing the Quantum Internet research.

title: Study on the Decision-Making and Stability of Power Users' Demand Response Participation Under Multi-Attribute Cost Impacts
authors: Yixin Hou, Lin Zhao, Haiwei Jiang, Xing Ji, Jingzhi Zhao
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: This paper addresses the limitations of traditional demand response research, which often focuses on economic incentives, and innovatively approaches the issue from a multiattribute cost perspective. It constructs a comprehensive evaluation framework for electricity users' participation decisions and stability, encompassing economic costs, knowledge costs, and attention costs. By integrating quantitative analysis with qualitative assessment methods, the study examines how these multidimensional cost factors differently affect the willingness to respond among industrial, commercial, and residential users, and scientifically evaluates the stability of their long-term participation behaviors. The results indicate that non-economic costs, such as knowledge barriers and attention burdens, are key factors restricting continuous user engagement. The findings can provide theoretical support and practical guidance for refined power system operations and the design of differentiated incentive strategies.

title: Angle-Dependent Magnetic Field Immunity of 16nm Embedded STT-MRAM
authors: Hsin-I Chan, Kuan-Cheng Chiu, Chih-Hui Weng, Chih-Lin Cheng, C.Y. Wang, Chun-Yu Wu, Allen Wang, Yi Ching Ong, Harry Chuang, Yuan-Jen Lee, Chih-Hsin Ko
abstract: We experimentally evaluate both data retention and active write performance of 16nm embedded STTMRAM cells under external magnetic fields ranging from 300G to 3000G and applied at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0^{\circ}-90^{\circ}$</tex> in the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{X Z}$</tex> plane. Retention error measurements as a function of field angle are captured by a modified Néel-Brown model, from which the chip level maximum tolerable field <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\left(H_{\max}\right)$</tex> is extracted. In the active write mode, error rates show clear angular dependence–<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{P} \rightarrow \mathbf{A P}$</tex> errors peak near 30° and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{A P} \rightarrow \mathbf{P}$</tex> errors near <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 0}^{{\circ}}$</tex>. Micromagnetic simulations with Mumax3 reproduce <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{P} \rightarrow \mathbf{A P}$</tex> angular trends and reveal the switching dynamics that cause failures during and after the current pulse. This integrated experimental and simulation study provides predictive guidance for designing robust STTMRAM in magnetically noisy environments.

title: Scalable Counterfactual Explanations for Large-Scale Automated Decision Systems
authors: T V Priya, Shrisha Rao
abstract: Counterfactual explanations have emerged as a prominent approach in explainable AI, providing intuitive insights into model decisions by identifying minimal changes that alter predictions. These explanations are especially valuable in highstakes domains such as finance and healthcare, where transparency and accountability are essential. However, many existing counterfactual generation methods lack a formal logical foundation, which limits their ability to guarantee consistency, rigorously verify constraints, and ensure meaningful interpretability. In addition, current approaches often struggle with efficiency and may produce redundant or unrealistic feature changes. To address these limitations, we introduce the Logical Counterfactual Framework (LCF), a logic-based framework that generates counterfactual explanations via set-theoretic analysis of discriminative features. LCF systematically identifies minimal and sufficient feature-value pairs that distinguish a target outcome from the original prediction, and it generates counterfactuals by modifying only these critical features. This design promotes sparsity, interpretability, and adherence to logical constraints. Additionally, we define Counterfactual (CF) fairness based on logical neutrality, enabling explicit bias detection without causal graphs. Experimental results on four real-world datasets, including applications in finance and healthcare, demonstrate that LCF is significantly more efficient, produces sparser counterfactual explanations, and maintains close proximity to original instances, making it well-suited for highstakes decision-making scenarios.

title: Enhanced Intermediate State in Magnetic Tunnel Junctions at Cryogenic Temperatures
authors: Jiajia Jian, Fangyi Zhao, Yongjie Luo, Zheng Chai, Xue Zhou, Xihui Yuan, Xin Yue, Jian Fu Zhang, Weidong Zhang, Yumeng Yang, Tai Min
abstract: The emergence of pronounced intermediate state (IM state) in magnetic tunnel junctions (MTJs) at cryogenic temperatures presents a critical challenge for the reliability of Magnetoresistive Random-Access Memory (MRAM). This work systematically investigates this phenomenon, characterizing the IM state as an intrinsic metastable energy minimum during switching process of MTJ. We reveal its non-monotonic dependence on write voltage and an exponential increase in lifetime with decreasing temperature. While these properties introduce a write voltage penalty and stochastic delay for binary operation, they also establish the IM state as additional resistance levels. This discovery provides a framework for reliability concerns and present a potential for multi-level cell (MLC) storage within standard binary MTJs for advanced cryogenic applications.

title: Accelerating Physics-Based BTI Modeling for Arbitrary Workloads Using Defect Ensemble and Time Compression Techniques
authors: Jonas Eerdekens, Stanislav Tyaginov, Robin Degraeve, David Coenen, Pablo Sarazá-Canflanca, Ben Kaczer, Michiel Vandemaele, Dieter Claes, Siegfried Mercelis
abstract: We present a methodology to simulate bias temperature instability (BTI) for long-term reliability predictions and alternating current (AC) conditions. Our focus is on repetitive structure within the applied gate voltage (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$V_{\mathrm{g}}$</tex>) pulse. Throughout this work, we develop a set of different methodologies to simplify and substantially reduce computational demand for predictive BTI modeling on longer times scales than previously numerically feasible. To achieve this goal, we employ a compact representation of transition rates for complex sequences of subsequently applied gate voltages. This simplification enables efficient simulation of the threshold voltage shift <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\Delta V_{\text{th }}$</tex> for periodical (with a possible extension to arbitrary) workloads. Besides this compression over time, we develop compression techniques over the defect ensemble, further accelerating simulation for periodical <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$V_{\mathrm{g}}$</tex> signals. Using commercial quality planar nFETs subjected to BTI stress with a periodical gate voltage signal, we reproduce experimental <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\Delta V_{\text{th}}(t)$</tex> traces (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$t$</tex> is time), and achieve good agreement between experimental and simulation data with a significant speed-up compared to conventional BTI modeling. As a consequence, these methods are suitable for both practical, large-scale reliability simulations involving realistic lifetime workloads.

title: PCBDefect-Net: An Efficient Deep Learning-Based Algorithm for PCB Defect Detection
authors: Junxi Hu
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: Printed Circuit Board (PCB) defect detection is essential for ensuring product quality in electronic manufacturing. However, challenges such as small defect sizes, diverse defect patterns, and complex backgrounds hinder accurate and real-time inspection. To address these issues, this paper proposes PCBDefect-Net, an improved YOLOv12-based framework for PCB defect detection. The model incorporates a Multi-Scale Defect Feature Perception (MDFP) module to enhance small-defect recognition, a PCB Context-aware Attention (PCB-CA) mechanism to suppress background interference, and a RemoteDistance Micro Defect Enhancer (RDMDE) to improve detection of tiny and distant defects. Experiments on a public PCB defect dataset show that PCBDefect-Net achieves a mean Average Precision (mAP) of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 6. 2 {\%}}$</tex> while maintaining real-time performance, significantly outperforming the baseline YOLOv12 model.

title: Experimental Validation of AUX Scheme for Quantum Homomorphic Encryption on IBM Quantum Platforms
authors: Gia Phat Dang, Weisheng Si, Belal Alsinglawi, Jim Basilakis
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Quantum Homomorphic Encryption (QHE) addresses Quantum Cloud Computing (QCC) security concerns by ensuring the privacy of a client's data and algorithms when outsourced to untrusted third-party quantum servers. However, current QHE schemes face significant challenges: scaling computational resources introduces overhead and hardware noise, degrading accuracy and compromising security. This paper implements and analyses a non-interactive AUX-QHE scheme that employs pre-generated auxiliary states for universal computation. We identify three critical computational bottlenecks: exponential growth in auxiliary state count, complex homomorphic evaluation, and extensive symbolic key updates. Through experimental evaluation on IBM Quantum hardware, we quantify the impact of NISQ noise on AUX-QHE performance and establish practical resource thresholds for deployment. Our results bridge the gap between theoretical QHE frameworks and their practical implementation on noisy quantum devices, providing concrete benchmarks for future noise mitigation efforts.

title: Interpretable Machine Learning Framework for Patient Risk Prediction Using XAI
authors: Aditya Das Gupta, Shihab Al-Rashid, Md. Nahin Irtiza
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: An interpretable patient risk stratification framework is a systematic approach that integrates highaccuracy predictive modeling with Explainable AI (XAI) techniques to forecast clinical deterioration from critical events, like In-Hospital Cardiac Arrest (IHCA) and sepsis. Accurate machine learning models are often complex to adopt due to their “black box” nature, which limits clinical trust and actionability. Promptly predicting patient deterioration remains a significant clinical challenge. This work introduces a systematic framework for developing a highly accurate and interpretable risk stratification tool for high-acuity patients. Using a large, publicly available dataset of 200,020 patient snapshots, the methodology evaluated six supervised learning algorithms and selected eXtreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) as the best performer due to its outstanding results. A comprehensive suite of Explainable AI (XAI) techniques, such as SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP), Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations (LIME), and Permutation Feature Importance, was carefully integrated to analyze the model's logic and ensure transparency. With a 99.74% accuracy and an Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC) of 1.0000, the final XGBoost model exhibited nearly perfect predictive performance. The XAI analysis identified physiological indicators, such as Mean Arterial Pressure and Pulse Pressure, as the top predictors of patient risk, surpassing standard vital signs in predictive influence. A web-based, interactive proof-of-concept was created to showcase a practical step toward clinical use, offering real-time risk predictions based on patient vital signs.

title: Research on Autonomous Ramp Climbing Control Strategy of Electric Tractors Based on Improved Pid Algorithm
authors: Junwei Li, Bo Li, Qianlong Zhang, Jiachang Song
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: To address the issues of initial response lag and speed overshoot (“over-climbing”) at the crest during slope-climbing scenarios for electric autonomous luggage tractors at airports, this paper proposes an improved PID control strategy that integrates intelligent optimization and dynamic feedforward compensation. The strategy employs the Cuckoo Search Optimized Improved Grey Wolf Optimizer (CSO-IGWO) for online tuning of controller parameters and introduces an adaptive feedforward compensation module based on real-time slope estimation to actively counteract disturbances caused by gravitational components <sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">[3]</sup>. Field tests conducted on a typical composite route (flat-slope-flat) at Capital Airport demonstrate that, compared with conventional PID control, the proposed approach increases the minimum climbing speed maintenance by 46.9%, reduces the speed overshoot at the slope crest by 81.1%, and improves the root-mean-square value of the speed tracking error throughout the journey by approximately 78.9%. The research outcomes significantly enhance the dynamic performance and ride smoothness of the unmanned tractor during slope climbing, providing a feasible technical solution for high-precision longitudinal motion control of unmanned special-purpose vehicles operating on complex terrains.

title: AutoChemo: An Intelligent Deep Learning Pipeline for Personalized Chemotherapy Planning from Raw Clinical Features
authors: Al Mamun, Farhan Tanvir, Effat Ara, Md. Parvezur Rahman Mahin, Raihan Ul Islam
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: The adaptation of chemotherapy to individual patient profiles remains a crucial challenge in improving the effectiveness of cancer therapy. In this work, we introduce AutoChemo a deep learning framework designed to recommend personalized chemotherapy options for breast cancer patients by analyzing raw clinical and biospecimen information. The system employs a multi-target neural network combined with interpretable machine learning tools to deliver both high predictive accuracy and transparency. Training and validated in the TCGA-BRCA cohort, the model shows notable improvements over conventional benchmarks, reinforcing its potential for clinical decision support.

title: Statistical Analysis of an Entanglement-Based Quantum Key Distribution Protocol and Validation of its Anti-Tampering Measures Under Detector Efficiency Attacks
authors: David Koch, Fabian Farina, Hedwig Körfgen, Hanns Zimmermann, Nils Gentschen Felde, Arno Wacker
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) utilizes the principles of quantum mechanics for secure communication. Despite its theoretical robustness, practical implementations of QKD systems contain imperfections enabling loopholes. This paper presents a practical validation of the security of a commercially available entanglement-based QKD system. We conduct an attack where we imitate Detector Efficiency Mismatch (DEM) behavior. By investigating the bitstreams over all intermediate key generation steps, we find that Privacy Amplification (PA) restores statistical randomness in the final key, even when intermediate material shows significant bias. However, statistical uniformity does not necessarily imply full information-theoretic security as residual correlations may persist beyond the scope of statistical testing. Our findings indicate that while the manufacturer's anti-tampering mechanisms effectively prevent observable bias, further analysis is required to assess hidden leakage channels.

title: Obfuscated Quantum and Post-Quantum Cryptography
authors: Anju Rani, Xiaoyu Ai, Aman Gupta, Ravi Singh Adhikari, Robert Malaney
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: In this work, we present an experimental deployment of a new design for combined quantum key distribution (QKD) and post-quantum cryptography (PQC). Novel to our system is the dynamic obfuscation of the QKD-PQC sequence of operations, the number of operations, and parameters related to the operations; coupled to the integration of a GPS-free quantum synchronization protocol within the QKD process. We compare the performance and overhead of our QKD-PQC system relative to a standard QKD system with one-time pad encryption, demonstrating that our design can operate in real time with little additional overhead caused by the new security features. Since our system can offer additional defensive strategies against a wide spectrum of practical attacks that undermine deployed QKD, PQC, and certain combinations of these two primitives, we suggest that our design represents one of the most secure communication systems currently available. Given the dynamic nature of its obfuscation attributes, our new system can also be adapted in the field to defeat yet-to-be-discovered practical attacks.

title: Insight into the Impact of Trap and Hydrogen on the PBTI in IGZO TFT by 3D KMC
authors: Yongjia Wang, Jinghan Xu, Yuanzhao Hu, Xiaoyan Liu
abstract: This work investigates PBTI in IGZO TFTs using a 3D KMC framework capturing trap- and hydrogen-related mechanisms. Two coupled pathways are identified: charge trapping/detrapping in the dielectric and hydrogen diffusion-reaction in the channel. Hydrogen accumulates at the interface before diffusing into the channel, reacting with metal atoms or oxygen vacancies to generate defects and release carriers. The framework reproduces thickness-dependent degradation and elucidates the roles of hydrogen precursor and reactive site densities in PBTI optimization. Variability analysis shows trap-induced degradation follows defect-centric statistics while hydrogen-induced degradation exhibits Gaussian behavior.

title: Validation of Supply Chains AI/ML Model with Synthetic Data for Embedded Semiconductors
authors: Matthew C. Gunn, Davis C. Loose, Megan E. Gunn, Megan C. Marcellin, Negin Moghadasi, Thomas L. Polmateer, Zachary A. Collier, Igor Linkov, James H. Lambert
abstract: Validation is a critical step in systems evaluation of supply chain models. Semiconductor supply chains are complex, globally distributed, and prone to disruption including from technological advances, counterfeiting, geopolitical tensions, and natural hazards causing cascading impacts. The decentralized supply chains face a data shortage that limits the ability to perform modeling and simulation of resilience to disruptions. This paper introduces a framework for generating synthetic data for semiconductor supply chains using large language models, knowledge graphs, validation, and prompt engineering. A knowledge graph is used to define the supply chain structure and data format by specifying entities and relationships within the supply chain. Two stages of validation are applied sequentially. Structural validation is applied to ensure that the generated data follow the knowledge graph, focusing on data formatting and type. Semantic validation enforces internal consistency within the dataset, requiring coherent relationships within supply chain entities and plausible parameter values. A full-factorial experiment compares the performance of six large language models, utilizing three different prompting strategies, in generating synthetic semiconductor supply chain data through successful validation rates, cost measures, and error correction rates. Results of the experiment include: (i) refining the model sophistication of structural and semantic validation, (ii) prompting strategies and agentic model performance, and (iii) recommended best practices for the generation and use of synthetic data in this application.

title: Applied Quantum Machine Learning on PCB Multi-Class Defect Detection
authors: Ching-Wen Huang, Kuan-Cheng Chen, Ching-Jui Lai
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: This work establishes a systematic benchmark for hybrid quantum-classical learning applied to industrial visual inspection. A hybrid framework integrating ResNet-based feature extraction with Quantum Support Vector Machines (QSVMs) is proposed to evaluate quantum kernel performance in multi-class printed circuit board (PCB) defect classification. Feature embeddings extracted from a ResNet-18 backbone are reduced via principal component analysis and classified using both classical SVMs and multiple quantum kernel variants simulated on a 9 qubit system. Results show that well-designed quantum kernels achieve accuracy comparable to the classical RBF SVM baseline. These findings highlight the importance of circuit design and provide one of the first systematic benchmarks of QSVMs in a PCB defect classification setting, offering practical insights for future quantum-classical applications in manufacturing inspection.

title: Stepwise Breakdown-Induced Degradation Analysis of 1200V SiC MOSFETs with Different Gate Structures
authors: Peng Liao, Jianbing Guo, Yanghao Wang, Dong Zou, Qingqing Sun, David Wei Zhang, Hang Xu
abstract: This study compares the degradation behavior of three commercial 1200V SiC MOSFETs with planar-gate (PG), asymmetric-trench (AT), and double-trench (DT) structures under stepwise high drain voltage stress. Results reveal distinct degradation modes: PG and AT show hard breakdown behavior, with abrupt parameter shifts after breakdown, while DT exhibit soft breakdown characterized by progressive degradation. TCAD simulations reveal that degradation originates from hole injection and trapping in the gate oxide at the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{SiO}_{2} / \text{SiC}$</tex> interface, driven by the combined effects of interfacial electric field and hole current. In PG and AT, degradation results from avalanche-generated holes injected into the oxide after breakdown, while in DT, persistent leakage and hole injection due to base region punch-through cause severe and progressive pre-breakdown degradation. Hole trapping above the channel reduces threshold voltage, while hole trapping above the JFET increases <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{C}_{\mathbf{G D}}$</tex>. Capacitance degradation is more pronounced due to stronger hole current and electric field intensity through the JFET region. The deep P+ shield structure of AT effectively suppresses interface electric field and limits the active JFET area, achieving superior reliability under high drain bias stress. These findings underscore the importance of interfacial field mitigation and punch-through suppression in enhancing the robustness of SiC MOSFETs for high-voltage applications.

title: Change-Aware Test Suite Prioritization for CPS using Multi-Domain Dependency Graphs
authors: Maximilian Beck, Jonas Boll, Vitus Lüntzel, Eric Sax
abstract: Cyber-Physical Systems integrate mechanical, electrical, and software components, resulting in tightly coupled development processes and extensive regression-testing demands. As agile and over-the-air update strategies increase the number of system versions, executing full regression test suites becomes infeasible due to resource and time constraints. Existing test case prioritization techniques, developed largely within the software domain, do not account for cross-domain dependencies and therefore overlook faults that arise only through interactions between heterogeneous engineering artifacts. This work presents a change-aware, cross-domain test case prioritization method that builds on Multi-Domain Dependency Graphs (MDDGs) as a unified representation of CPS. By propagating modifications via graph-based relations across all engineering domains, change impact is estimated and used to derive execution orders that reflect the system’s structural and semantic dependencies. The approach is instantiated via a set of graph-based metrics that score test cases along paths connecting changed artifacts and test-relevant elements. A modular implementation with interfaces to existing engineering tools enables integration into modelbased development environments without requiring changes to underlying domain-specific models. The method is evaluated in representative scenarios from an embedded vehicle platform. Across different datasets, the approach achieves Average Percentage of Faults Detected (APFD) values between 60% and 90%, exceeding the 50% expected from random test ordering, indicating improved fault detection relative to random test execution while preserving the full test suite and thus avoiding coverage losses associated with minimization techniques.

title: Equity-Aware Multi-Objective Optimization for Renewable Microgrids
authors: Augusto Magalhães Pinto de Mendonça, Filipe Pessôa Sousa, Igor Machado Coelho, Laura Silva de Assis
abstract: Renewable microgrid design typically addresses cost, reliability, and emissions without incorporating spatial equity as an optimization objective. Yet equitable access to clean energy is essential for social acceptance and long-term sustainability, especially in remote communities where unequal power distribution compounds existing disparities. This work integrates the Gini coefficient as a fourth objective alongside net present cost, loss of power supply probability, and carbon emissions, enabling proactive design for fair power distribution rather than retrospective evaluation. Using NSGA-III with 30 independent optimization runs, we identify trade-offs between equity and system performance: high-equity configurations require approximately 29 percent higher capital investment but enable substantially greater renewable penetration and reduce lifetime emissions by 30 percent on average compared to low-equity designs, demonstrating that equity and environmental goals are complementary rather than competing. We validate the framework using hourly load and meteorological data from Inuvik, Canada, an Arctic community at 68 degrees north latitude with complete open-access records from the CASES Partnership. The framework addresses three socio-technical challenges: off-grid communities requiring electricity access, Indigenous populations facing energy inequity, and grid-connected regions preparing for climate-induced islanding capability. Real-world validation confirms alignment with Inuvik’s 2023 wind turbine deployment. Source code and datasets are publicly available for replication.

title: A Unified Adversarially-Robust and Privacy-Preserving Federated Learning Framework for Multimodal Healthcare IoT
authors: Md. Awinul Hoque Utsha, Farshid Rafiq, Yeasir Ramim, Ezaz Mahmud Jim, Anuradha Choudhury, Mohammad Akidul Hoque
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Federated learning (FL) is aneffective tool in cooperative healthcare IoT systems for distributed model training without any revealing of private medical data. Nonetheless, the vast majority of the existing methods merely concentrate on one single aspect, privacy-preserving or robustness, which therefore inevitably bring host of serious side effects including: data leakage, attack by adversarial perturbations and/or model poisoning for applications with medical related. In practical IoT settings, such as those for multimodal physiological and motion data collected from resource-limited wearable sensors, it is critical to guarantee privacy andadversarial resilience, which however are very challenging. In this paper, we address a joint adversarially robust andprivacy-preserving FL framework for multimodal healthcare IoT. Contrary to previous works that consider these goals in isolation, we adopt a federated learning strategy in which homomorphic encryption (HE) secure gradient sharing, adversarial training for local robustness, and cross-sensor consistency validation mechanism to filter out the inconsistent clientupdates. A strong aggregation module helps to further block poisoning or noisy data contributed from globalmodel updates. Extensive studies on three widely used datasets namely WISDM, MHEALTHand UCI-HAR verify the superiority of the proposed model. The approach improves 6-10 percent robustnessto FGSM and PGD attacks over the baseline FL models while being competitive with clean accuracy. Furthermore, the system only introduces marginal computational and communication overheads, which verifies its feasibility ofdeployment in edge networks. The results verifythat the joint design of encryption, robustness and multimodal consistency is effective for privacy-preserving intelligence in healthcare IoT. This work offers a flexible basis forsecure and dependable medical analysis over distributed sensor networks.

title: Security of a Secret Sharing Protocol on the Qline
authors: Alex B. Grilo, Lucas Hanouz, Anne Marin
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Secret sharing is a fundamental primitive in cryptography, and it can be achieved even with perfect security. However, the distribution of shares requires computational assumptions, which can compromise the overall security of the protocol. While traditional Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) can maintain security, its widespread deployment in general networks would incur prohibitive costs. In this work, we present a quantum protocol for distributing additive secret sharing of 0, which we prove to be composably secure within the Abstract Cryptography framework. Moreover, our protocol targets the Qline, a recently proposed quantum network architecture designed to simplify and reduce the cost of quantum communication. Once the shares are distributed, they can be used to securely perform a wide range of cryptographic tasks, including standard additive secret sharing, anonymous veto, and symmetric key establishment.

title: Spot Electricity Market Clearing Coordinating Carbon-Energy Linked Pricing and Demand Response
authors: Jiayi Lu
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: To address market deficiencies caused by neglecting carbon emissions and demand response, this paper proposes a spot electricity market clearing model integrating carbon-energy linked pricing and demand response coordination. First, a pricing model based on static carbon emission factors couples generation costs with carbon characteristics. Second, demand response is incorporated into the clearing process. Finally, from a consumer rights perspective, total end-user expenditure evaluates clearing effectiveness. Results on the PJM 5-bus system show the coordinated model balances economic efficiency, environmental protection, and stability while enhancing consumer welfare. Sensitivity analysis on carbon prices and response participation rates confirms robustness.

title: New Insight Into the Process Variations of Advanced Nanosheet Technology: A N/PMOS Correlation Perspective
authors: Yewei Zhang, Shuying Wang, Chenyang Zhang, Yong Liu, Pengpeng Ren, Zhigang Ji
abstract: The advancement of nanosheet technology increases circuit sensitivity to process variations, including global and local sources like work-function variation (WFV) and line-edge roughness (LER). While N/PMOS correlation affects the circuit performance, the local correlation is underexplored. This study introduces a new insight considering local correlation and analyzes the impact of varying WFV and LER proportions. Results show performance variations across circuit cases, improving evaluation accuracy and enabling more reliable circuit optimization under process variations.

title: Integrating Neuromorphic Sensors, Digital Twins, and MBSE Interfaces for System Validation
authors: Emi Aoki, Flore Norcéide, Gayathri Boopathy, Charles Thompson, Kavitha Chandra
abstract: The rationale for digital transformation is to manage the increasing complexity of systems by breaking down silos between units of the organization and forming a community with shared governance of the digital artifacts of the system throughout its lifecycle. In this context, digital engineering, model-based systems engineering (MBSE), and digital twins are expected to be processes and products that support collaborative design, analysis, testing, and validation of systems across the traditional systems engineering phases. However, a significant challenge in the adoption of MBSE is in the varied design approaches taken by practitioners and often the lack of crossdomain experts that can use these models to bridge the left and right sides of the V-model of systems engineering. This work presents an architecture for conceptual modeling of a digital twin (DT) representing a dynamical system, driven by data from a neuromorphic vision sensor (NVS). The MagicGrid methodology, which structures problem, solution, and implementation domains across the pillars of requirements, structure, behavior, and parameters, is applied to demonstrate its potential to engage personnel involved in validating the physical system driving the design phase. The black box and white box stages of the problem domain provide interfaces to define needs and contexts from the validation perspective. The case study of designing a DT for a one degree-of-freedom pendulum with its dynamics recorded by the NVS is presented. A particle filter serves as a candidate DT model for estimating and tracking the system states of the physical system. The interaction between the physical system and its DT is illustrated using black box and white box models that describe potential roles of the DT for system validation.

title: Hybrid Deep Embedding and Ensemble Models with SHAP-Based Interpretability for Heart Disease Prediction
authors: Khushnur Binte Jahangir, Farhan Anan Himu
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Heart disease remains one of the leading causes of mortality worldwide, motivating the development of reliable predictive models. In this work, we study heart disease prediction on the UCI Cleveland dataset using a calibrated hybrid pipeline. We compare classical baselines (Random Forest, shallow Multilayer Perceptron) against a hybrid approach where embeddings learned by an MLP are used as inputs to a Random Forest, with tuned XGBoost as an additional benchmark. Repeated stratified cross-validation with SMOTE ensures robust model selection, while isotonic calibration and threshold optimization improve deployment readiness. To address interpretability, we relate embedding dimensions back to clinically meaningful variables using SHAP and correlation analysis. Our best model achieves competitive performance (Accuracy <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\approx 0.82$</tex>, ROC-AUC <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\approx 0.91$</tex>), while also offering calibrated and interpretable predictions suitable for decision support.

title: Scalable Quantum Device Authentication Using Hardware-Efficient Quantum Physical Unclonable Functions
authors: Franco Cirillo, Christian Esposito
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: The rapid development of quantum computing has increased the need for reliable methods to authenticate quantum devices and ensure that only genuine systems participate in computations. Existing approaches often rely on quantum memory or are not easily adaptable to different hardware platforms, which limits their practicality as quantum processors continue to grow in size and complexity. We propose a new Quantum Physical Unclonable Function scheme that exploits the intrinsic noise of Noisy Intermediate Scale Quantum devices while avoiding the need for quantum memory. The method employs a quantum circuit designed to capture device-specific noise efficiently without adding unnecessary complexity, making it suitable for larger processors and a variety of quantum hardware. The scheme implements a challenge-response protocol and is evaluated on real IBM quantum processors using up to 32 qubits, significantly more than the small systems typically considered in previous studies. We measure instability and uniqueness, showing a clear separation between responses from the same device and those from different devices. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach is scalable, practical, and capable of providing robust authentication for current and future quantum devices.

title: 8KUHD Forward Facing Video for Rail Safety: An Exploratory Study of Practitioner Perception
authors: Anthony Adeyemi-Ejeye, Gregory Howell, Mikołaj Leszczuk
abstract: This paper presents the first study quantifying how practitioner experience affects perceived quality and usability of compressed Forward-Facing Video (FFV). We captured a bespoke 8K UHD forward-facing dataset and encoded each sequence using H.264/AVC and HEVC at three bitrates (30,50, and $\mathbf{6 0 ~ M b p s}$). 18 rail practitioners were recruited and stratified by operational experience. Participants provided subjective ratings of video quality and usability and completed a taskbased recognition assessment with associated decision-confidence ratings. FFV is increasingly integral to railway safety assurance and incident investigation, providing visual evidence for events such as Signals Passed at Danger (SPAD), near-misses, and collisions. In operational deployments, FFV acts as a human-in-the-loop decision-support resource that must be transmitted and archived under bandwidth and storage constraints, making compression unavoidable. However, compression artefacts can reduce the visibility of decision-critical cues and potentially undermine confidence in operational judgements. While expertise has been shown to shape video interpretation in medical and surveillance contexts, its influence on rail FFV interpretation has received limited empirical attention. Initial results indicate a clear effect of experience: more experienced practitioners appeared more tolerant of visible artefacts, rated compressed FFV as more usable, and reported higher confidence when making task judgements under degraded conditions. A three-way mixed-design ANOVA confirmed a significant effect of experience, while codec and bitrate effects were non-significant, with performance approaching a ceiling at 60 Mbps. These preliminary findings motivate practitioner-aware evaluation protocols and system requirements for safety-critical rail FFV, complementing fidelity metrics with interpretability-driven acceptance criteria. We outline implications for codec selection, bitrate provisioning, and role-based quality thresholds in railway video systems engineering.

title: Automated Black-Box Detection of Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR) Vulnerabilities in Web Applications
authors: Ayemun Hossain, Najmus Sakib Sizan, Bibi Kawsar, Tanusree Saha Tanny
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Broken authentication and access control are among the most critical vulnerabilities in web applications, often enabling attackers to bypass security mechanisms and gain unauthorized access. A notable instance of this class of weakness is the Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR) vulnerability, where object identifiers in client requests can be manipulated to expose or modify restricted resources. Despite its significance, IDOR remains difficult to detect automatically. Existing scanners are limited in scope, focusing mainly on HTTP GET and POST methods, requiring manual intervention, and often producing high false positive rates with lengthy information-gathering phases. To address these limitations, we present IDORD, a fully automated black-box tool for IDOR detection. IDORD incorporates a crawler for automated URL extraction, applies parameter mutation to craft unauthorized requests, and analyzes response behaviors to identify violations of access control policies. Unlike conventional scanners, our approach systematically evaluates all major HTTP methods, including GET, POST, PUT, and DELETE. Validation on deliberately vulnerable applications shows that IDORD improves efficiency, reduces detection time, and provides broader coverage of IDOR vulnerabilities compared to existing approaches.

title: Electrochemical Performance and Stability of a Neutral Hydrophilic Silicone Oil/Zinc Acetate Mixed Electrolyte from 20 to 80 °C
authors: Maosong Xia, Xingyun Ma, Weidong Peng, Yunqi Guo
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: To address the limitations of conventional strongly alkaline zinc-air battery electrolytes, such as severe volatilization at elevated temperatures, serious zinc anode corrosion, and poor cycling stability, a neutral binary mixed electrolyte based on hydrophilic silicone oil and zinc acetate was developed. Volatilization tests, in situ morphology observations, and wide temperature range electrochemical measurements were carried out to evaluate its liquid-retention ability, interfacial stability, and electrochemical kinetics over 20-80 °C. The results show that this electrolyte exhibits low volatility and reduced side reactions at high temperatures, effectively suppressing zinc corrosion, hydrogen evolution, and carbonate precipitation. The battery achieved a cycle life of 1650 h at 20 °C, 8.25 times that of the 6 M KOH system, and still operated stably for 145 h at 80 °C. In addition, the ionic conductivity increased from 39.4 to 86.2 <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{mS} \cdot \text{cm}^{-1}$</tex> with rising temperature, while the apparent activation energy remained as low as <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$16.4 \text{kJ} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1}$</tex>. Overall, this neutral mixed electrolyte significantly enhances the wide-temperature adaptability and cycling stability of zinc-air batteries.

title: Swept-frequency Eddy Current Method for Multi-Parameter Characterization of Ferromagnetic Metal Tube
authors: Yuedong Xie, Keyu Du, Guoyuan Wang, Pu Huang
abstract: As critical components in demanding industrial sectors such as petrochemical processing and nuclear power, the accurate characterization of key parameters in ferromagnetic metal tubes—namely inner radius, electrical conductivity, and magnetic permeability—is essential for guaranteeing their structural integrity and operational safety. This article presents a swept-frequency eddy current method for multiparameter characterization of ferromagnetic metal tubes. An absolute-type internal eddy current sensor is designed with its coil concentrically aligned with the central axis of tube, forming an axially symmetric configuration in which the excitation coil induces eddy currents within the tube wall. A simplified analytical model for ferromagnetic tube eddy current detection is established to enhance computational efficiency, achieving a 44.3% reduction in frequency-sweep computation time while maintaining less than 1% discrepancy from the original Dodd–Deeds model. Sensitivity analysis of the swept-frequency inductance response indicates that the inner radius exhibits the strongest influence over a wide frequency range, whereas electrical conductivity and magnetic permeability show complementary frequency-dependent sensitivities, which enables reliable simultaneous inversion. Using detection signals from ferromagnetic metal tubes, the simplified analytical model serves as the forward calculation method, transforming the parameter inversion problem into a least squares optimization problem. An improved Levenberg–Marquardt iterative algorithm is employed to solve this optimization problem, enabling simultaneous determination of three critical parameters: inner diameter, electrical conductivity, and magnetic permeability of ferromagnetic metal tubes. Experimental validation on 12 specimens demonstrates that measurement errors for all three parameters remain below 2%, confirming the effectiveness and accuracy of the proposed method.

title: Descriptor: Large-Scale Campus WLAN Flow-Level Traffic Dataset (CampusFlow-WLAN)
authors: Archana Das, Aditya Dubey, M. P. R. Sai Kiran, Arun Kumar Singh
abstract: Publicly available network traffic datasets are essential for reproducible research in traffic characterization, intelligent network management, and data-driven optimization of next-generation communication systems. However, many existing datasets are limited in scale, duration, or operational realism. To address this gap, this paper presents a comprehensive flow-level network traffic dataset collected from a large-scale campus WLAN deployment at the Indian Institute of Technology Jodhpur, India, by the Wireless and Navigation Laboratory, Department of Electrical Engineering. The dataset is collected using a distributed traffic monitoring framework in which traffic from different campus buildings and operational zones including academic departments, administrative offices, staff residences, and student hostels is exported as independent streams and stored separately. This structure resembles the Distributed Unit (DU) and Centralized Unit (CU) architecture used in modern 5G networks, enabling realistic evaluation of centralized monitoring and analytics. The dataset includes continuous 24-hour monitoring over multiple months and high-resolution flow traces captured for two minutes every 15 minutes, enabling analysis of temporal traffic dynamics. The flow-level attributes enable mapping to key 5G service categories-enhanced Mobile Broadband (eMBB), ultra-reliable low-latency communications (uRLLC), and massive machine-type communications (mMTC), making the dataset valuable for research in traffic analysis, network slicing, load balancing, and AI/ML-based traffic prediction in large-scale operational networks.

title: Self-Supervised Denoising via Pulse Separation for Distributed Acoustic Sensing in Large-Scale Infrastructures
authors: Chen Li, Zheng Zhou, Yang Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: The fidelity of Distributed Acoustic Sensing (DAS) data is critical for the structural health monitoring of large-scale infrastructures yet remains susceptible to complex background noise. To overcome the limitations of traditional filters at low signal-to-noise ratios (SNR) and the "ground truth" scarcity inherent in supervised learning, this paper introduces a self-supervised denoising framework using pulse separation. Distinct from conventional approaches, our method uses the independence of noise across separated pulse intervals to formulate a self-supervised learning objective, eliminating the need for manual labeling or clean reference labels. We further incorporate a residual deep network integrated with an attention mechanism to adaptively distinguish key strain events from random noise. Laboratory experiments and field tests demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms traditional filters and state-of-the-art self-supervised models in terms of noise suppression and signal preservation, highlighting its significant potential for stability monitoring in complex engineering environments.

title: Beyond the Job Description: How women quietly carry the culture in tech [Career Advisor]
authors: Parul Jain

title: UltraUNet: Real-Time Ultrasound Tongue Segmentation for Diverse Linguistic and Imaging Conditions
authors: Alisher Myrgyyassov, Zhen Song, Yu Sun, Bruce Xiao Wang, Min Ney Wong, Yongping Zheng
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Ultrasound tongue imaging (UTI) provides a non-invasive, cost-effective modality for investigating speech articulation, speech motor control, and speech-related disorders. However, real-time tongue contour segmentation remains a significant challenge due to the inherently low signal-to-noise ratio, variability in imaging conditions, and computational demands of real-time performance. In this study, we proposed UltraUNet, a lightweight and efficient encoder-decoder architecture specifically optimized for real-time segmentation of tongue contours in ultrasound images. UltraUNet introduces several domain-informed innovations, including lightweight Squeeze-and-Excitation blocks for channel-wise feature recalibration in deeper layers, Group Normalization for enhanced stability in small-batch training, and summation-based skip connections to minimize memory and computational overhead. These architectural refinements enabled UltraUNet to achieve a high segmentation accuracy while maintaining an exceptional processing speed of 250 frames per second, making it suitable for real-time clinical workflows. UltraUNet integrates ultrasound-specific augmentation techniques, including denoising and blur simulation using point spread function. Additionally, we annotated UTI images from 8 different datasets with various imaging conditions. Comprehensive evaluations demonstrated the model's robustness and precision, with superior segmentation metrics on single-dataset testing (Dice = 0.855, MSD = 0.993px) compared to established architectures. Furthermore, cross-dataset testing on 7 unseen datasets with 1 train dataset revealed UltraUNet's generalization capabilities and high accuracy, achieving average Dice Scores of 0.734 and 0.761, respectively, in Experiments 1 and 2. The proposed framework offers a competitive solution for time-critical applications in speech research, speech motor disorder analysis, and clinical diagnostics, with real-time performance in tongue functional analysis in diverse medical and research settings.
