title: Out-of-Distribution Detection with Overlap Index
authors: Hao Fu, Prashanth Krishnamurthy, Siddharth Garg, Farshad Khorrami
abstract: Out-of-distribution (OOD) detection is crucial for the deployment of machine learning models in the open world. While existing OOD detectors are effective in identifying OOD samples that deviate significantly from in-distribution (ID) data, they often come with trade-offs. For instance, deep OOD detectors usually suffer from high computational costs, require tuning hyperparameters, and have limited interpretability, whereas traditional OOD detectors may have a low accuracy on large high-dimensional datasets. To address these limitations, we propose a novel effective OOD detection approach that employs an overlap index (OI)-based confidence score function to evaluate the likelihood of a given input belonging to the same distribution as the available ID samples. The proposed OI-based confidence score function is non-parametric, lightweight, and easy to interpret, hence providing strong flexibility and generality. Extensive empirical evaluations indicate that our OI-based OOD detector is competitive with state-of-the-art OOD detectors in terms of detection accuracy on a wide range of datasets while requiring less computation and memory costs. Lastly, we show that the proposed OI-based confidence score function inherits nice properties from OI (e.g., insensitivity to small distributional variations and robustness against Huber ϵ-contamination) and is a versatile tool for estimating OI and model accuracy in specific contexts.

title: Finite-Sample System Identification of Linear Systems Under Random Packet Loss
authors: Tianyang Tian, Zhenning Zhang, Liang Xu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Control of Network Systems
abstract: This paper investigates finite-sample system identification for discrete-time linear systems subject to random data packet loss. Random data packet loss destroys the usual linear structure of least squares identification, which makes classical non-asymptotic analyses inapplicable. We address this difficulty by viewing the resulting nonlinear least squares estimator as an empirical loss minimizer and developing a statistical learning based framework to study its performance under packet loss. Under mild assumptions, we derive high-probability finite-sample guarantees for the generalization error that reveal how identification performance improves as more samples are collected, but tends to deteriorate for higher-dimensional systems and for longer bursts of packet loss, especially when the underlying dynamics are only weakly stable. By further imposing a local identifiability and strong-convexity condition around the true system matrices, we translate these generalization guarantees into non-asymptotic bounds on the parameter estimation error, which exhibit the same qualitative trends and show that longer packet loss worsen the constants in the bounds, so that more data are required to achieve a given accuracy. The numerical simulation verified the conclusion.

title: Sparse Chirp-Rate Modulated GST for High-Resolution Time-Frequency Analysis with Adaptive Low-Rank Regularization in Seismic Denoising_supp1-3689318.pdf
authors: Amin Roshandel Kahoo
abstract: Time–frequency representation (TFR) is fundamental for analyzing the nonstationary characteristics of seismic signals. This study introduces the Sparse Chirp-Rate Modulated Generalized S-Transform (SCRM-GST), a sparsity-promoting framework that combines the Chirp-Rate Modulated Generalized S-Transform (CRM-GST) with Structured Orthogonal Matching Pursuit (S-OMP) to produce adaptive, high-resolution, and low-rank TFRs. By employing chirp-rate–dependent Gaussian windowing and block-sparse recovery, the method effectively captures both harmonic and transient features while maintaining accurate signal reconstruction. Integrated with Sparse and Low-Rank Regularization with Local Adaptive Parameter Mapping (SD-SpaLR-LAPM), it adaptively tunes parameters for enhanced denoising. Tests on synthetic and field seismic data demonstrate that SCRM-GST outperforms conventional approaches, such as CRM-GST, Sparse-STFT, and MSST, in terms of Rényi entropy, matrix rank, and SNR, achieving superior sparsity, energy concentration, and noise suppression without compromising structural fidelity.

title: Tactile Sensing-Based Measurement and Correlation Analysis for Peach Ripeness Assessment
authors: Chaoyue Han, Shuo Kang, Lipengcheng Wan, Dongdong Du, Yuxuan Zhang, Yuchen Lu, Jun Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Accurate and non-destructive measurement of fruit ripeness is a critical requirement for intelligent agricultural systems, yet existing approaches rely heavily on visual cues and often fail to capture texture-related properties that are more directly linked to fruit quality. In this work, we present an instrumented parallel gripper with integrated tactile sensing for the quantitative assessment of peach ripeness. The system records force–displacement responses during controlled grasping, from which compressive stiffness is extracted as a tactile feature. To establish its physical relevance, the stiffness values were systematically compared with elastic modulus obtained through three classical mechanical models, namely Hooke’s, Hertz’s, and Boussinesq’s formulations. Experimental studies were conducted on two peach varieties (”Jinxiu” and ”Baifeng”) under different storage durations to capture progressive ripening effects. Results showed that compressive stiffness declined significantly with storage time for ”Jinxiu”, while remaining relatively stable for ”Baifeng”. Analysis of variance confirmed that fruit variety, storage duration, and measurement method all exerted statistically significant effects on elastic modulus (p < 0.001). Moreover, strong correlations were observed between compressive stiffness and elastic modulus, with Hooke’s model yielding the closest agreement (r = 0.9234 for ”Jinxiu” and r = 0.8199 for ”Baifeng”). These findings confirm that tactile stiffness features acquired by the parallel gripper provide a reliable proxy for elasticity-based ripeness characterization. Beyond demonstrating the feasibility of non-destructive ripeness measurement, this work highlights the potential of tactile-enabled robotic grippers to complement vision-based approaches in intelligent harvesting and postharvest quality monitoring, thereby advancing the development of practical and adaptive agricultural automation systems.

title: Hypothesis Testing for Adversarial Channels: Chernoff-Stein Exponents
authors: Eeshan Modak, Neha Sangwan, Mayank Bakshi, Bikash Kumar Dey, Vinod M. Prabhakaran
abstract: Consider the following binary hypothesis testing problem: Associated with each hypothesis is a set of channels. A transmitter, without knowledge of the hypothesis, chooses the inputs to the channel. Given the hypothesis, from the set associated with the hypothesis, an adversary chooses channels, one for each element of the input vector. Based on the channel outputs, a detector attempts to distinguish between the hypotheses. For the fixed-length setting, we study the Chernoff-Stein exponent for the cases where the transmitter (i) is deterministic, (ii) may privately randomize, and (iii) shares randomness with the detector that is unavailable to the adversary. It turns out that while a memoryless transmission strategy is optimal under shared randomness, it may be strictly suboptimal when the transmitter only has private randomness. We also study the sequential version of this problem in each of the three settings and show that both the Chernoff-Stein exponents can be simultaneously achieved.

title: RF Aging Extensive Characterization & Modeling for Reliability-Aware Power Amplifier Design
authors: T. Daher, A. Divay, B. Duriez, X. Garros, J. Antonijevic, X. Federspiel, D. Roy
abstract: A novel methodology has been developed to evaluate Power Amplifier HCI lifetime under RF stress. This approach addresses the limitations of conventional DC-to-RF reliability projections, which often fail to capture the full extent of degradation under RF stress. In this work, Stand-Alone SOI PAs were subjected to RF HCI stress across a range of bias conditions and load impedance mismatches. An empirical model has then been developed to generate Time-to-failure (TTF) contour maps, which provide a practical tool for PA designers to assess the performance-reliability trade-off under varying load mismatch scenarios during early design stages.

title: Ultra-Fast TLP Characterization of Gate-All-Around (GAA) ESD Diodes for Low-Capacitance Optimized I/Os
authors: Anish K Kumar, Krzysztof Domanski, Raj S Dua, Reshma R Menon, Umair Ishfaq, Salukazi Shamutete, Florian Klotz
abstract: This paper presents the characterization and modeling of FINFET fabricated diodes using bulk and gate-all-around (GAA) technologies, highlighting the enhanced electrostatic discharge (ESD) robustness observed in the GAAFINFET node. Ultra-fast Transmission Line Pulse (ufTLP) measurements with rise times of 40ps, and veryfast TLP (vf-TLP) measurements with rise times of 100ps were conducted, targeting both diode structures and gate oxide (GOX) victims. Diodes using nonplanar FINs with bulk node exhibited slower response and higher forward recovery (FR) overshoot, whereas GAA diodes demonstrated faster switching behavior without FR overshoot. GOX structures in the bulk node failed prematurely due to FR-induced stress, in contrast to the more robust performance observed with GAA diodes. The overshoot-free switching of ESD diodes processed in GAA-FINFET Intel technology allows for improvement of several key performance indicators (KPI) in the High Speed (HS) interfaces, as shown on the example of DDR.

title: Toward Quantum-Resilient Software Supply Chains: A DevSecOps Case Study with Hybrid Post-Quantum Artifact Signing
authors: Rafael Lucas Silva
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Software supply-chain security has become a central concern due to the pervasive reuse of third-party dependencies, automated CI/CD pipelines, and the increasing number of attacks targeting build systems and artifact distribution channels. In parallel, emerging quantum computing capabilities threaten the long-term security of widely deployed public-key signatures (e.g., RSA/ECDSA) used to establish artifact authenticity. This paper reports a practitioner-led case study of a GitLab CI/CD pipeline that operationalizes DevSecOps with Shift-Left controls across linting, software composition analysis (SCA), dynamic application security testing (DAST), centralized quality governance, security orchestration, artifact packaging, and a hybrid signing workflow combining a classical Vault Transit signature and a post-quantum signature (CRYSTALS-Dilithium). The pipeline culminates in a signed bundle published to a secure store, enabling verifiable integrity under both classical and post-quantum verification regimes. The case study demonstrates how a future-resilient supply chain can be incrementally adopted without breaking existing verification flows while increasing early detection capability and improving traceability and auditability.

title: Real-Time Review Analysis with a Sentiment-Integrated Hybrid LDA Approach
authors: Afzalul Abid Nazir, Islam Saiful, Md. Faruk Abdullah Al Sohan
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: In the era of rapid digital communication, real-time understanding of consumer sentiment is essential for businesses seeking to enhance their products and services. This study proposes a Sentiment-Integrated Hybrid Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) model that improves traditional topic modeling by embedding emotional context directly into the topic generation process. Conventional LDA models often struggle with short, dynamic texts commonly found in social media and online reviews. To address this, the proposed approach incorporates sentiment filtering via a BERT-based classifier, which refines topic distributions and enhances thematic coherence. The model is evaluated on a diverse consumer review dataset, demonstrating improved performance in terms of topic coherence (0.497) and perplexity (-5.58) compared to standard LDA. The results indicate that the model effectively identifies sentiment-rich and contextually relevant themes, offering a deeper insight into consumer opinions. Due to its scalability and adaptability, the model is well-suited for applications such as social media monitoring, customer feedback analysis, and decision support systems. This work contributes to the advancement of sentiment-aware artificial intelligence and provides a practical framework for extracting actionable insights from large-scale textual data.

title: A Hybrid Self-Supervised Approach with Radiomics for Accurate Brain Tumor Classification
authors: Sinthiya Tabashoum, Joy Debnath
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Clinical brain-tumor MRI suffers from scarce expert labels despite large archives of unlabeled scans. We tackle this with a hybrid self-supervised objective that merges four complementary signals: (i) masked image modeling (MIM) to capture anatomical context, (ii) InfoNCE-based contrastive alignment for invariance, (iii) radiomics prediction to inject texture/intensity priors, and (iv) latent-space consistency for stable embeddings. After hybrid pretraining, we fine-tune with MixUp, label smoothing, EMA, and rotation-based test-time augmentation, and convert probabilities to decisions via validationonly per-class thresholds. On the Nickparvar Kaggle dataset, the best configuration reaches test accuracy 0.9146 and macro-F1 0.9103 (vs. 0.9039/0.8994 with argmax), with macro-ROC AUC <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\approx 0.986$</tex>. To avoid ad-hoc loss weighting, we evaluate four prespecified combinations for (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\lambda_{\text{mim }}, \lambda_{\text{align }}, \lambda_{\text{rad }}, \lambda_{\text{cons }}$</tex>) and select on validation only; the strongest overall is (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1.0,0.7,0.20,0.15$</tex>). We provide per-class ROC/PR curves, disable-one ablations (every objective helps), and a weight-sensitivity study. The approach is architecture-flexible (ResNet-18, EfficientNet-B0, MobileNetV3S/L) and label-free during pretraining.

title: From Classroom to Enterprise: Architectural Patterns for LLM Integration
authors: Brian Moriarty
abstract: Large language models are rapidly diffusing into academic and enterprise knowledge work, yet most deployments lack explicit collaboration architectures, leading to inconsistent outcomes, unclear accountability, and unresolved risk exposure. Organizations respond with opposing approaches: academic institutions impose prohibitions or detection mechanisms to preserve the integrity of the author, while enterprises mandate usage to accelerate productivity. Both approaches fail because they regulate behavior without defining how humans and LLMs should collaborate within actual workflows.This paper proposes centaur and cyborg frameworks as distinct human-LLM interaction patterns that differ in authority allocation, control boundaries, and failure modes. Centaur architectures maintain explicit decision boundaries with human authority over validation and final decisions, while cyborg architectures enable continuous collaboration with distributed oversight and supervisory control. We ground these frameworks in parallel longitudinal observations from academic (graduate data visualization course, seven semesters 2022-2025) and enterprise (software engineering and business operations) contexts, documenting remarkably similar evolution across three phases: novelty-driven atomic usage, stark shift to delegated patterns, and eventual differentiation into stable collaboration architectures. Both environments show atomic usage as the dominant pattern, with substantial subsets exhibiting centaur-aligned and degradedcentaur patterns, and small minorities demonstrating emergent cyborg usage.The convergence of patterns across contexts with opposing governance mechanisms suggests that these architectures represent recurring patterns observed in contrasting contexts rather than context-specific adaptations. We translate these observations into practical deployment guidance that emphasizes maturity-gated adoption, selection of risk-tiered architecture, and lightweight embedded controls over enforcement-based policies. Contribution is an architectural framework for designing, evaluating, and governing LLM-enabled workflows grounded in observed collaboration behaviors.

title: Explainable Deep Learning for Real-Time Power Quality Event Detection and Diagnosis in Smart Grids: A SHAP-Enhanced CNN-LSTM Framework with PYPOWER Simulation Validation
authors: Mahmoud Kiasari, Hamed Aly
abstract: Power quality (PQ) monitoring is critical to modern smart grids that are moving towards high renewable penetration; however, current PQ detection techniques have difficulty handling the complexity and volume of smart meter data. Although deep learning facilitates automatic event detection, its black box nature makes it difficult for operators to feel confident and ensure compliance with regulations. This manuscript presents an end-to-end explainable AI framework built using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for waveform feature extraction in combination with Long ShortTerm Memory (LSTMs) networks for temporal pattern recognition augmented with SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) to provide model transparency. A novel simulationbased methodology using PYPOWER along with three standard test cases of the interconnection of power systems (IEEE 39 and 57 bus test cases) was used to obtain realistic power quality events, thus creating a large set of $\mathbf{8 5 0, 0 0 0}$ labeled events in nine categories of the standards (IEEE 1159), namely, voltage sags, voltage swells, harmonics, flicker, transients, interruptions, notches, spikes, and noise. The proposed CNN-LSTM architecture offers a high classification accuracy of 96.3 percent with an inference latency of 11 ms, which can be deployed at the edge using the proposed architecture on the Raspberry Pi 4 board. SHAP analysis shows that harmonic magnitude (3rd, 5th, and 7th order) is the major factor for the classification of harmonic distortion occurrences, whereas sag depth and duration are the major factors in voltage sag diagnosis. Extensive ablation studies have shown that temporal features improve the accuracy by 4.2%, and frequency-domain features improve the accuracy by 6.8%. The framework includes perturbation validity tests, sanity checks, and domain consistency validation to ensure that the explanations are reliable. Comparative analysis revealed better performance than conventional SVM (78.4%), Random Forest (83.6%), and standalone CNN/LSTM architectures. The open-source implementation with pretrained models allows utilities to implement AI-enabled PQ management without losing operator control or being out of compliance with the requirements of IEC 61850 standards (IEEE 1159).

title: Unraveling Intricate Switching Dynamics of FeFETs with Gate Side Injection Toward Reliable Disturb-Immune 3D NAND Storage
authors: Sizhe Ma, Saikat Chakraborty, Yixin Qin, Jiahui Duan, Giuk Kim, Stefany Gutiérrez Galvis, Zijian Zhao, Xingtian Wang, Moonyoung Jung, Kijoon Kim, Suhwan Lim, Kwangyou Seo, Kwangsoo Kim, Wanki Kim, Daewon Ha, Halid Mulaosmanovic, Stefan Duenkel, Sven Beyer, Vijaykrishnan Narayanan, Jaydeep P. Kulkarni, Kai Ni
abstract: In this work, gate metal work function <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\Phi_{\mathrm{M}}$</tex>) engineering is employed to probe the switching dynamics in hafnium zirconium oxide (HZO)-based n-type ferroelectric field-effect transistors (FeFETs) with gate-side injection (GSI), targeting reliable 3D NAND storage. We show that switching behavior is governed by charge trapping during program/erase and post-pulse detrapping, both strongly modulated by the gate metal <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\Phi_{\mathrm{M}}$</tex>. Four key observations are identified: (i) switching is jointly determined by charge trapping and subsequent detrapping; (ii) without detrapping, lower <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\Phi_{\mathrm{M}}$</tex> gates enhance GSI, resulting in larger threshold voltage <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\mathrm{V}_{\text{TH}}$</tex>) shifts or earlier switching onset at a given write voltage; (iii) after erase, lower <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\Phi_{\mathrm{M}}$</tex> gates experience increases GS electron detrapping during the delay, requiring higher write voltages to compensate charge loss; (iv) after programming, enhanced injection and polarization from the lower <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\Phi_{\mathrm{M}}$</tex> gates increase the electric field at the channel-side (CS) interlayer, suppressing CS electron detrapping. The results show that GSI FeFETs with evaporated aluminum gates exhibit higher onset voltages <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\mathrm{V}_{\text{ERS }}=-9 ~\mathrm{V}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{V}_{\text{PGM }}=10 ~\mathrm{V}$</tex>), higher DC breakdown voltages (-11.5 V), and superior disturb immunity <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\Delta \mathbf{V}_{\mathbf{T H}} \approx 0$</tex> after 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">5</sup> cycles at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{V}_{\text{PASS }}=\mathbf{7 V}$</tex>).

title: Scaling a Quantum Network to Real-World Applications: MadQCI and its Ecosystem
authors: Alberto Sebastián-Lombraña, David Rincón, Laura Ortiz, Juan P. Brito, Jaime S. de Buruaga, Juan Morales, Antonio Melgar, Javier Faba, Juan José Romero, Adrián Sacks-Nogal, Luis Cortés, V.B. Gil-Rubio, Marta I. Garcćia-Cid, Raul D. Bonete, Sofia Ángel-Carlón, Alejandro Guzmán-Huerta, Carlos Castró-Sánchez, Alejandro Fernandez-Ibañez, José Luis Bejarano, Rubén B. Méndez, Rafael J. Vicente, Luis Mengual, Julio Setien, Carmen Escribano, Pedro Salas, José M. Rivas-Moscoso, Antonio Pastor, Diego R. López, Rafael Canto-Palancar, Jesus Folgueira, César Sánchez, Vicente Martin
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: The Madrid quantum network, namely MadQCI and its ecosystem, is testing the scalability and adaptability of the network to multiple application niches. To achieve this, it is essential adapting the quantum communications infrastructure (QCI) to the specific requirements of each niche, as well as having versatile and adaptative key management system. In addition, collaboration from the industrial ecosystem is necessary, so several open calls were articulated as public procurement files, as well as multiple demonstrators, besides the usual collaboration of the industrial partners in the related R&D projects. This work presents these multiple applications; the design decisions made to tailor the quantum network to them in a scalable and adaptable manner; and some preliminary results.

title: Monocular 3D Visual Homing for UAV Based on Photometric Potential Field
authors: Jiawen Liu, Yixiang Xu, Shaowei Gan, Liangming Chen
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: Autonomous visual navigation is critical for the operation of Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) in complex environments. However, traditional navigation frameworks, such as visual SLAM, impose heavy computational loads that accelerate energy depletion and restrict flight endurance. To address this, we propose a lightweight 3D visual homing algorithm specifically designed for monocular drones. Unlike traditional feature-based geometric approaches, our method employs a direct visual servoing framework. By constructing a photometric potential function and incorporating an iso-depth assumption, the system directly derives a 3D translational control vector from image gradients, entirely bypassing explicit depth estimation and complex backend optimization. Simulation and real-world homing tests validate that the proposed algorithm forms a globally convergent 3D attraction area around the target pose. Despite inherent monocular observability constraints along the optical axis, the system maintains high lateral accuracy during the final approach with extremely low processing latency, significantly alleviating the computational burden. The proposed algorithm provides a computationally efficient solution for the autonomous navigation of resource-constrained UAVs.

title: Enhancing Diagnostic Accuracy in Multi-Modal Medical Imaging Through Targeted Data Processing and Customized Ensemble Learning
authors: Symum Ahmed, Tanvir Ahmed Palok, Nabuat Zaman Nahim
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Misdiagnosis in medical imaging poses critical patient health risks due to limited cross-validation mechanisms for radiological decisions. This study introduces a structured data-processing workflow and a multi-stage prediction system aimed at improving disease detection accuracy across X-ray, MRI, and CT images. The system employs transfer-learning-based Convolutional Neural Networks, specifically EfficientNetV2S and Inception-ResNetV2, supported by modality-appropriate preprocessing. Histogram equalization, adaptive histogram equalization (AHE), and contrast-limited adaptive histogram equalization (CLAHE) are applied to enhance image consistency, while extensive augmentation—including rotation, Gaussian filtering, scaling, shearing, jittering, flipping, and sharpening—mitigates dataset variability. The ensemble framework combines five models through three decision-fusion strategies: majority voting, selective class-wise voting, and customized weighted voting. These fusion mechanisms reduce model-specific biases and strengthen the stability of predictions across 18 disease classes. Validation using confusion matrices shows that selective class-wise voting achieved 95.27% accuracy, while customized weighted voting achieved 94.07%. Grad-CAM visualization is included to support interpretability of the overall decision process. The findings demonstrate the contribution of structured preprocessing and decision-level fusion to improved diagnostic reliability and highlight the system's potential as a second-opinion tool for radiologists.
