title: Distributed Tracking Control of Multiagent Systems with Actuator Saturation: A Dwell-Time-Based Cycle-Mirroring Stabilization Approach
authors: Qi Chen, Ruicheng Ma, Chun-Yi Su
abstract: This paper addresses the distributed consensus tracking control problem for multiagent systems (MASs) with actuator saturation using a dwell-time cycle-switching strategy, cycle-mirroring gain feedback protocol, and multiple time-varying cycle-mirroring Lyapunov function (MTVCMLF). A novel cycle-mirroring gain feedback controller is designed for each subsystem with a lower bound on the dwell-time, ensuring distributed consensus tracking. By integrating the MTVCMLF with the feedback controller and dwell-time cycle-switching signal, global stability is guaranteed. Sufficient conditions for distributed consensus tracking are proposed within the dwell-time cycle-switching framework. Finally, two numerical examples validate the effectiveness of the approach.

title:
Modulating Structure and Properties of P(VDF-HFP)/Co
                    <sub>3</sub>
                    O
                    <sub>4</sub>
                    Nanocomposites with 2D Nanofillers
authors: Khatri Nirajan, Wang Feipeng, Muhammad Abdul Rahim, Ruta W. Deusdedith, Mahmoud A. Ali
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: Poly (vinylidene fluoride-co-hexafluoropropylene) (P(VDF-HFP)) is a strongly promising fluoropolymer because of outstanding flexibility, electrochemical consistency, and excellent dielectric property, making it promising material for dielectric and electronic applications. Nanocomposite films composed of P(VDF-HFP) and varying-concentration of 2D Co<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</sub> nano mesh are synthesized via spin-coating process. Mechanical, structural, morphological, dielectric, electrical characteristics of the nanocomposites are comprehensively studied. The FTIR and XRD analysis confirms the retention of electroactive β-phase and successful incorporation of Co<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</sub> nano mesh into polymer matrix with strong interfacial interactions. SEM study reveals a uniform dispersion at lower loadings and agglomeration at higher loadings. Mechanical testing shows that Young’s modulus increases from 123.05 MPa (0.0 wt.%) to 959.92 MPA (4.0 wt.%). Dielectric analysis reveals significant enhanced permittivity as the temperature increases for all nanocomposite films. AC conductivity also improves significantly with filler content, frequency and temperature. At 30 °C σ<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">ac</sub> rises from 1.65 x 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-8</sup> S/cm for 0.0 wt.% to 6.99 x10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-3</sup> S/cm for 7.0 wt.% composite at 107 Hz. The 4.0 wt.% sample shows a peak σ<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">ac</sub> of 1.11 x 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-2</sup> S/cm, indicating enhanced charge transport due to interfacial polarization and filler-induced conduction pathways. These results indicate that Co<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</sub> nanomesh effectively tunes the mechanical and dielectric response of P(VDF-HFP), although excessive loading leads to aggregation and higher dielectric loss.

title: Blind Estimation of Always-On and On-Demand 5G Signals for Opportunistic Positioning
authors: Shaghayegh Shahcheraghi, Zaher M. Kassas
abstract: This letter presents the first stationary user equipment (UE)-based positioning framework that blindly estimates both always-on and on-demand 5G downlink signals with multiple 5G gNBs, transmitting on the same channel. The framework opportunistically exploits the full bandwidth of downlink 5G signals. In the acquisition stage, the maximum likelihood function of the beamformed signal is computed to differentiate the gNBs in both carrier frequency offset and angular domains, followed by blind beacon estimation of each gNB. Carrier-aided code delay tracking loops are developed to track the carrier phase and code delay to extract navigation observables. The Cram´ er–Rao lower bounds (CRLBs) for carrier frequency offset, azimuth, and elevation angles are derived. Experimental results are presented demonstrating that the estimated beacon exhibits a narrower autocorrelation function compared to that of always-on signals, due to the exploitation of on-demand signals, leading to higher time resolution estimation. Monte Carlo (MC)–based positioning results for a stationary UE using pseudorange and azimuth angle measurements from two co-channel 5G gNBs are presented. With initial positions randomly located within a 99% confidence circle of 2.1 km radius, the mean final position estimate across MC realizations was within 8.6 m of the true UE position.

title: A Distributionally Robust Data-Driven Approach to Active Fault Detection for Stochastic Dynamic Systems
authors: Ting Xue, Linlin Li, Qinqin Fan, Dong Zhao, Yueyang Li, Maiying Zhong
abstract: Practically inaccessible precise probability distribution for disturbance poses significant challenges to stochastic active fault detection (AFD) in achieving satisfactory detection accuracy. In this paper, without making specific distribution assumption on disturbance, a distributionally robust data-driven approach is proposed to AFD for stochastic linear dynamic systems. On the basis of constructing a data-driven stable kernel representation-based residual generator, the distributional uncertainty of disturbance is characterized by the mean-covariance-based ambiguity set of residual both for the fault-free and faulty cases. To minimize the energy of input while guarantee tolerable false alarm rate (FAR) and missed detection rate (MDR), the design of AFD system is formulated as an optimization problem subject to distributionally robust chance constraints (DRCCs). By bridging the DRCCs with deterministic constraints in the probabilistic context, the targeting optimization problem is then converted into a generalized eigenvalue-eigenvector problem, by solving which analytical solutions of the input and separating hyperplane for online detection are derived. Hence, the developed AFD system can not only ensure the FAR and MDR criteria not exceeding predefined levels, but also improve the robustness of the system against distributional uncertainties of disturbance. Besides, a batch-wise realization algorithm is developed for continuous online fault detection. A simulation study based on a four-tank system is demonstrated to validate the effectiveness of the proposed approach.

title: Understanding Uncertain Spatial Terms for Manipulating Objects on Irregular Plane Surfaces
authors: H.A. Harindu Y. Sarathchandra, A.G.B.P. Jayasekara, R.A.R.C. Gopura
abstract: Understanding and interpreting spatial geometric terms in relation to human cognition remain a challenging and intriguing area of research. It is difficult to comprehend theway humans generate definitions. Although current state-of-the-art vision-language models can associate terms with text inputs, identifying spatial regions according to human preferences remains challenging. Nevertheless, this multimodal understanding is crucial for making robots more autonomous and reducing the cognitive load on users. Initially, a preliminary human study was conducted to explore human preferences for grounding spatial terms “Left/Right” and “Center”. Insights from this study helped develop a framework with two main branches to ground these terms. Three key features Circular level, Symmetrical level, and Edge Linearity level were identified to define the “Left/Right” regions relative to a given spatial area. By combining these features, fuzzy activations were created to accurately determine the borderline orientation for the left and right regions. All memberships were carefully tuned based on insights from the preliminary study, and the entire grounding process was evaluated through a human subject-based assessment, which ensured alignment with human preferences throughout the framework. Simultaneously, to ground the term “Center”, a fuzzy-based attention map was introduced, along with an evaluation process involving human subjects to verify the accuracy of the proposed framework. The evaluations revealed an overall accuracy of 91.67% for defining “Left/Right” and 65.52% for “Center”. Moreover, grounding these uncertain spatial terms effectively supports human-like decision-making in task execution with open vocabulary inputs, ultimately enhancing the robot’s intelligence and precision.

title: Accelerated Ageing and Modeling of Electrical Insulation in Electric Aircraft: A State-of-the-Art Review
authors: Divya Velu, Pablo Alvarez Santos, Maxime Tolu, Ibon Elosegui Simón
abstract: Electric aircraft operate under extreme environmental conditions that accelerate insulation degradation, particularly through partial discharges (PD). Conventional ageing models, largely developed for terrestrial steady-state environments, do not explicitly account for altitude-dependent pressure variation, inverter-fed excitation, and mission-profile stress coupling. This review systematically analyzes recent advances in PD behavior, accelerated ageing methodologies and insulation lifetime models, classifying them into single- and multi-stress categories. The limitations of existing IEC and aviation standards in addressing integrated multi-stress aerospace conditions are critically examined. A key gap is identified in the absence of lifetime formulations that intrinsically incorporate pressure-dependent PD behavior and waveform effects. To address this, a structured model-selection decision framework tailored to electric aircraft applications is proposed. This work provides a comprehensive reference for future experimental validation and the design of high-reliability insulation systems in next-generation electric aircraft.

title: Prompting Rain Off: Evolving Compact Dual Prompts for Continual De-Raining
authors: Minghao Liu, Wenhan Yang, Jiaying Liu
abstract: In recent years, there has been notable progress in single-image rain removal, particularly focusing on static data distributions in these approaches. When dealing with data that constantly changes, the challenge of catastrophic forgetting arises, which is quite common and critical in real-world scenarios. To address this, we propose Evolving COmpact Dual Prompt Learning (EcoDPL), an efficient rehearsal-free continual learning deraining framework designed specifically for low-level vision tasks. Specifically, we design two prompt pools at both image and feature levels and insert these prompts into images and embedding tokens, for better knowledge transfer across tasks. Our adaptive weight generation module, P-Fuser, attaches an attention map to each prompt, to adaptively pay attention to different inputs, and get different weights to fuse prompts, making the inserted prompts more flexible with various inputs. Also, we introduce Grad-Tuner, a dictionary learning strategy, to compress knowledge into fewer prompts. This makes the knowledge more compact and provides more space for new prompts to learn new tasks. Our method stands out by leveraging small, learnable prompts for efficient knowledge retention across tasks, not increasing training time or parameters. Furthermore, we present an augmented method that upgrades the distance function γ from simple cosine distance to a more advanced weight generation network. We also employ a fine-tuned dictionary learning technique, compressing knowledge into a more compact form, and enhancing the ability of prompts to learn new tasks. With our new designs, the model becomes more flexible with various inputs and it compresses knowledge into fewer prompts to free up spaces to learn new tasks. Through extensive experiments on various rain removal datasets, our EcoDPL method consistently outperforms previous continual learning techniques. Notably, although EcoDPL is designed for continual learning with changing data, it also performs well with stationary data, proving its robustness and versatility.

title: MIST: A Benchmark and Baseline for Multi-frame Infrared Small Target Detection in Complex Motion_supp1-3689420.pdf
authors: Dan Zeng
abstract: Motion cues play a vital role in multi-frame infrared small target detection (MISTD). However, most targets in existing datasets exhibit regular and slow motion, which cannot reflect the complex and diverse motion patterns in real-world scenarios. This biased data distribution makes recent data-driven methods highly rely on simplified motion assumptions that tend to fail in irregular or fast motion, resulting in noisy feature representations cluttered with target-irrelevant factors. Hence, we stress that methods for MISTD should also work when targets are in complex motion. To enable this research, we propose a large-scale dataset called MIST for airborne infrared detection scenarios. The dataset is built on a synthetic data engine that models variations in pose, size, and intensity of moving targets while seamlessly blending them into real backgrounds for physical, geometric, and visual realism. Targets in MIST exhibit low signal-to-clutter ratios and complex motion, making it a promising yet challenging benchmark for developing algorithms focused on motion analysis. To tackle the challenges of MIST, we develop MISTNet, a robust baseline based on the Information Bottleneck theory. To handle irregular and fast motion, we propose a shifted neighborhood compensation block to efficiently model multi-scale correspondences for implicit motion compensation. To distill compact representations free from irrelevant cues, we design a progressive distillation decoder to hierarchically filter out redundancy while preserving target-relevant information. We benchmark 31 state-of-the-art methods and find that their performance on MIST drops significantly compared with that on the widely used NUDT-MIRSDT dataset. Our MISTNet outperforms all other methods by a large margin, with an over 6% gain in the IoU metric, demonstrating its superiority. The dataset, code, and model weights are available at https://github.com/GR-ray/MIST.

title: Automated Volumetric FAZ Measurement for Enhanced OCTA Sensing Using Global–Local Attention-Based 3D Segmentation
authors: Mane Pooja, Samarendra Dandapat, Manish Bhatt
abstract: The foveal avascular zone (FAZ) is a key biomarker for retinal microvascular alterations in diabetic retinopathy (DR). However, conventional optical coherence tomography angiography (OCTA) sensing pipelines rely on two-dimensional projections that fail to capture depth-dependent FAZ morphology, limiting measurement sensitivity. This work introduces an automated volumetric analysis framework that enhances OCTA sensing capability by enabling direct three-dimensional quantification of FAZ structure from OCTA volumes. A Global-Local Squeeze-and-Excitation enhanced 3D network is proposed to accurately segment FAZ volumes while preserving axial structural variations. Volumetric morphological descriptors derived from the segmented FAZ enabled reliable DR discrimination with an Area Under the Curve (AUC) of 0.9031 and a Dice score of 81.92 %, demonstrating that automated volumetric FAZ measurement provides clinically meaningful biomarkers beyond conventional 2D analysis. These results indicate that the proposed framework advances OCTA sensor functionality by enabling practical extraction of depth-resolved vascular biomarkers for diagnostic sensing applications.

title: Radar Target Classification Based on Wavelet Analysis and Dual-Stream Neural Network
authors: Qi Dai, Peilong Hu, Liyu Tian
abstract: Radar target classification in battlefield environments faces two primary challenges: severe clutter interference and environmental noise. To address these issues, this paper proposes a novel method that combines wavelet transform with a Dual-Stream Heterogeneous Neural Network (DS-HNN). First, a wavelet basis selection strategy utilizing signal entropy and spectral kinematics is developed for clutter suppression, followed by Doppler spectrum shifting for velocity normalization and spectral alignment. For feature extraction, Daubechies 7 (db7) wavelet packet decomposition is applied to extract time-frequency coefficients, which are then integrated with signal entropy and key physical parameters, to construct a multidimensional feature vector. The proposed DS-HNN addresses the limitations of single-domain classifiers by employing a heterogeneous architecture: a Time-Frequency Stream extracts latent spectral features via deep mapping layers, while a Physical Parameter Stream encodes global kinematic constraints. A Physics-Aware Cross-Attention mechanism is introduced to bridge these domains. By utilizing physical embeddings to weight spectral features, this mechanism not only resolves the dimensional disparity between the two feature sets but also enforces robust feature complementarity. Experimental validation using real X-band radar echo data demonstrates that the proposed method achieves an overall recognition accuracy of 98.11% across three target types: human, vehicle, and unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV), confirming its effectiveness.

title: Influence of Coating, Voltage, and Temperature on Surface Conductivity Properties of Epoxy-Resin-Impregnated Paper
authors: Qian Zeng, Xize Dai, Jian Hao, Hao Tang, Yi Zhang, Dingqian Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: Surface coating provides an effective approach to modulate the surface charge and conductivity properties of epoxy resin-impregnated paper (ERIP). In this study, the influence of coating thicknesses (0, 20, 40, and 60 μm each side), DC voltages (1 kV and 3 kV), and temperatures (25 °C, 55 °C, and 80 °C) on surface conductivity characteristics of ERIP were systematically investigated using polarization and depolarization current (PDC) measurements. The charge dynamics of both uncoated and coated ERIP were quantitatively evaluated by the software-assisted integrated charge method. Results indicate that coating thickness significantly affects the time-dependent PDC profiles. Specifically, increasing coating thickness can reduce surface conductivity, whereas higher applied voltage and elevated temperature enhance the mobility of charge carriers. Integrated charge analysis further reveals a near-linear decrease of the total charge at t = 600 s with increasing coating thickness, which may be attributed to the formation of deep traps induced by the coating. These findings provide mechanistic insights into the surface charge and conductivity behaviors in uncoated and coated ERIP and demonstrate the potential of surface engineering for reliability enhancement of valve-side bushings.

title: From Motion to Energy: Health-Aware Motion Planning for Enhanced Predictive Energy Management in Hybrid Fuel Cell/Battery Autonomous Mobile Robots
authors: Ghofrane Benarfa, Mohammad Mohammadpour, Marie Hébert, Sousso Kelouwani, Ali Amamou
abstract: Autonomous mobile robots (AMRs) operating in industrial and urban environments must function efficiently under dynamic and energy-constrained conditions. Conventionally, motion planning and energy management are designed separately: planners generate feasible trajectories, while the energy management system (EMS) reactively supplies power without anticipating future demand. In hybrid robots powered by batteries and fuel cells (FCs), this separation reduces long-term efficiency and accelerates component degradation. This work introduces a health-aware planning and predictive EMS architecture in which the motion planner explicitly incorporates battery state-of-charge (SoC) and FC state-of-health (SoH) predictions into trajectory optimization, while the EMS performs anticipative power allocation. By shaping motion to avoid stress-inducing load patterns, the framework promotes stable operation and durability. Long-duration evaluations across warehouse, urban delivery, and industrial forklift scenarios demonstrate reduced hydrogen consumption, smoother FC operation, and controlled battery cycling.

title: Community’s Impact on Self-Worth: Building Resilience as Women in Engineering [Career Advisor]
authors: Attrayee Chakraborty, Varshitha Manjunath

title: Bottom-Up Generation of Verilog Designs for Testing EDA Tools
authors: João Victor Amorim Vieira, Luiza de Melo Gomes, Rafael Sumitani, Raissa Maciel, Augusto Mafra, Mirlaine Crepalde, Fernando Magno Quintão Pereira
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: Testing Electronic Design Automation (EDA) tools rely on benchmarks: designs written in Hardware Description Languages (HDLs) such as Verilog, SystemVerilog, or VHDL. Collections of benchmarks for these languages are typically limited in size. This scarcity has recently drawn more attention due to the increasing need for training large language models in this domain. To deal with such limitation, this paper presents a methodology and a corresponding tool for generating realistic Verilog designs. The tool, ChiGen, was originally developed to test the Jasper® Formal Verification Platform, a product by Cadence Design Systems. Now, released as open-source software, ChiGen has been able to identify zero-day bugs in a range of tools, including Verible, Verilator, and Yosys. This paper outlines the principles behind ChiGen’s design, focusing on three aspects of it: (i) generation guided by probabilistic grammars, (ii) type inference via the Hindley-Milner algorithm, and (iii) code injection enabled by data-flow analysis. Once deployed on standard hardware, ChiGen outperforms existing Verilog Fuzzers such as Verismith, TransFuzz, and VlogHammer regarding structural diversity, code coverage, and bug-finding ability.

title: Smart Attention-Aware Vision and Language-Based Agentic Framework for Enhanced Workplace Engagement
authors: Md. Shahriar Hossain Apu, Bitta Boibhov Barmon, MD. Nur-E Ferdaus, Saurabh Joardar, Md. Ashiqussalehin, Md. Toukir Ahmed
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: The application of digital technologies and smart offices has made workplace productivity and monitoring of the employees more relevant. The old supervision procedures tend to be ineffective, subjective and incapable of offering real time feedback. The proposed paper introduces a hybrid YOLO-CBAM and Large Language Model (LLM) system of smart workplace surveillance. The proposed system is based on the YOLOv12 with the Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM) that helps to recognize minor actions of an employee like working, idling, or using a mobile phone. An LLM processes the detected activities further to make contextual reasoning, summarize and generate automated, human-like feedback. In order to improve the usability, the text-to-speech service is used to convert the warnings to speech so that corrective measures are taken in time with a minimum involvement of human factor. The accuracy of classification in activity is high, the low latency is appropriate to real-time supervision, and the context-related alerts are also successful in experimental assessment on a custom dataset. The findings suggest the possibility of using attention-based computer vision models together with LLM reasoning to establish scalable, intelligent, and autonomous systems to monitor workplace productivity.

title: An Explainable Machine Learning Framework for Crop Production Prediction and Recommendation System in Bangladesh
authors: Rakib Ahammed Diptho, Pial Ghosh, Sarnali Basak
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: As an agricultural country growing through developing processes, it is necessary to have good crop yield predictions as well as good crop recommendations to ensure the long-term growth of agriculture in Bangladesh. In this study, we are presenting a machine learning-based system for crop production prediction and crop recommendation by implementing Decision Tree, Random Forest, and XGBoost models on the SPAS-DatasetBD. In addition to many valuable pieces of information for our analysis, the dataset included the area, production, season, minimum humidity, maximum humidity, minimum temperature, maximum temperature, district, and crop name. As a result of the comparison among the models implemented, the XGBoost model gave us the most suitable <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{2}$</tex> value (0.9230) for crop production prediction, whereas the Random Forest and XGBoost models showed the most suitable performance for crop recommendation with precision and F1-score values of 0.94, respectively. To enhance the explainability of the results, we have incorporated the SHAP-based Explainable AI method into the developed framework. It is anticipated that the proposed framework will enable farmers and decision-makers to make informed decisions about crop choice, thus contributing to the sustainable development of agriculture in Bangladesh.

title: Raytheon Company - Approved for Public Disclosure: System for Adaptable, Scalable, and Autonomous Protection Verification and Decision Support (ASAP VDS) During Mission Planning and Execution
authors: Paul C. Hershey
abstract: In order to protect critical assets, military defense departments need an adaptable, scalable, and autonomous protection decision support capability to objectively verify that their critical personnel and assets are protected before, during, and after mission engagements. Present methods to accomplish this are subjective, based primarily on subject matter expertise. This greatly reduces the commander’s ability to accurately predict successful personnel and asset protection, limits the number of protectable assets, and does not adapt to changing mission conditions. What is required is an easy-to-use capability through which mission analysts and engineers can communicate to the commander current or predictive impacts to the military force from the protection warfighting function during Course of Action (COA) generation, mission planning, and mission execution. This paper presents a Adaptable, Scalable, and Autonomous Protection Verification and Decision Support (ASAP VDS) system that objectively predicts and verifies protection effectiveness across all mission phases.

title: Active Sampling Sample-Based Quantum Diagonalization from Finite-Shot Measurements
authors: Rinka Miura
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Near-term quantum devices provide only finite-shot computational-basis measurement outcomes and typically prepare imperfect, contaminated states rather than exact ground states. This practical constraint motivates algorithms that can convert samples into reliable estimates of low-energy properties without full state tomography or exhaustive Hamiltonian measurement. In this work we propose Active Sampling Sample-based Quantum Diagonalization (AS-SQD), an approach that frames Sample-based Quantum Diagonalization (SQD) as an active learning problem: given a finite multiset of measured bitstrings, which additional basis states should be included in the effective subspace to most efficiently recover the true ground-state energy? SQD constructs an effective low-dimensional eigenvalue problem by restricting the Hamiltonian to the span of a selected set of computational basis states and then classically diagonalizing the restricted matrix. However, naive SQD that only uses the sampled subspace often suffers from severe bias under finite-shot sampling and excited-state contamination, while blind subspace expansion (e.g., random exploration of connected basis states) is inefficient and unstable as system size grows. We introduce a perturbation-theoretic acquisition function based on Epstein-Nesbet second-order energy corrections to rank candidate basis states that are connected to the current subspace by the Hamiltonian. At each iteration, AS-SQD (i) diagonalizes the restricted Hamiltonian to obtain an approximate ground state, (ii) generates a candidate set of connected basis states, and (iii) adds the most valuable candidates according to a scoring function derived from perturbation theory. We evaluate AS-SQD on disordered Heisenberg and Transverse-Field Ising (TFIM) spin chains up to 16 qubits under a realistic preparation model that mixes 80% ground state and 20% first excited state. Furthermore, we validate the inherent robustness of our approach against real-world state preparation and measurement (SPAM) errors using physical samples directly from an IBM Quantum processor. Across both simulated and physical evaluations, AS-SQD consistently reduces energy errors by orders of magnitude compared with standard SQD and random expansion. Detailed ablation studies isolate the driving mechanisms of the perturbation score, demonstrating that physics-guided basis acquisition effectively concentrates computation on energetically relevant directions and bypasses exponential combinatorial bottlenecks.

title: Physics Based Modeling of Space Charge Modulation Snapback Phenomenon in the TLP Characteristics of HV LDMOS Devices
authors: Harihar Nath, Shreenidhaa K K, Raju Kumar, Mayank Shrivastava
abstract: A physics-based analytical model is developed for capturing the phenomenon of space charge modulation in the intrinsic NPN of the LDMOS device for a 100 ns TLP stress. Reverse-biased base–collector avalanche, current partitioning through the emitter diode and base resistance, and space charge modulation by injected electrons are coupled self-consistently to reproduce the snapback phenomenon and to form the basis for current filament formation. Model Validation was also done by varying the n-well region's doping.

title: Automated Derivation of Formal Properties from Requirements
authors: Bastien Sultan, Ludovic Apvrille, Pierre de Saqui-Sannes
abstract: Early detection of design errors in the life cycle of systems is one of the many benefits one may expect from using an model-based systems engineering approach. The formal verification of models is thus an issue. Among verification techniques, the paper focuses on model checking. Taking as input a set of properties and a model, model checking outputs a “yes/no” answer for each property saying whether the property is verified or not. The relevance of this proof relies on the relevance of the defined properties. The paper proposes solutions for automated generation of properties from a SysML model and, more specifically, from the requirements expressed in the SysML model. We introduce an AI- and syntactic rule-based process that assists systems engineers in the generation of syntactically correct CTL properties, along with complementary contributions for computing and propagating requirement coverage from formal verification results, backtracing them directly in the SysML requirement diagram. The approach is mathematically defined, with explanatory comments accompanying the formalism to support comprehension. The proposed approach is further implemented by the free software TTool-AI. The latter is an open source SysML toolkit extended with an LLM-based assistant. A satellite system and an embedded maritime system serve as case studies.

title: Research on Heat Transfer Characteristics for Reactor Vessel Based on FBG Sensing and Thermal Simulation
authors: Juan Wang, Zhentao Zhang, Xin Zhang, Mengge Xu, Siqi Ma
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: Intricate temperature distribution presents a significant challenge for reactor vessels, as conventional thermocouples are fixed in place without considering how fluid level and heating conditions influence their monitoring performance. This approach leads to compromised measurement accuracy, weak anti-interference capability, and uncertainty in defining the ideal measuring position for temperature detection. This work combines fiber Bragg grating (FBG) sensing technology with ANSYS steady-state thermal simulation to explore the heat transfer characteristics and temperature field distribution within the reactor vessel. Experimental results demonstrate that the central wavelength of the FBG sensor shifts by 0.026 nm as the fluid level increases from 20% to 50%. When the fluid level continues to rise to 70%, the wavelength changes by an additional 0.061 nm. The pronounced shift in wavelength directly indicates a rapid temperature increase in the internal medium of the reactor vessel, with a linear correlation between fluid level and FBG wavelength confirmed by a coefficient of determination of 0.887 from the fitted data. In this experimental setting, both simulation and experiment confirm that the temperature field inside the reactor vessel achieves the highest uniformity under the condition of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$100{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$</tex> heat source and 50% fluid level. Based on these confirmations, it is valuable to define optimal temperature measurement points and implement safe operation monitoring for reactor vessels.

title: Online Optimal Probe Allocation for Quantum Network Tomography
authors: Xuchuang Wang, Yu-Zhen Janice Chen, Matheus Guedes de Andrade, Mohammad Hajiesmaili, John C.S. Lui, Ting He, Don Towsley
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: How to efficiently perform network tomography is a fundamental problem in network management and monitoring. A network tomography task usually consists of applying multiple probing experiments, e.g., across different paths or via different casts (e.g., unicast and multicast). We study how to optimize the network tomography process through online sequential decisionmaking. From the methodology perspective, we introduce an online probe allocation algorithm that sequentially performs network tomography based on the principles of optimal experimental design and the maximum likelihood estimation. We rigorously analyze the regret of the algorithm under the conditions that <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$i)$</tex> the optimal allocation is Lipschitz continuous in the parameters being estimated and ii) the parameter estimators satisfy a concentration property. From the application perspective, we demonstrate that the quantum bit-flip network fulfills the two theoretical conditions and provide their corresponding regrets when deploying our proposed online probe allocation algorithm. Besides case studies with theoretical guarantees, we also conduct simulations to compare our proposed algorithm with existing methods and demonstrate our algorithm's effectiveness.

title: Language-Agnostic Reverse Engineering of Software into SysML v2 via LLM Reasoner
authors: Richard A. Qualis, Carlos Otero, Adrian Peter
abstract: We present a language-agnostic Model-Driven Reverse Engineering (MDRE) framework that uses large language models (LLMs) to transform heterogeneous software codebases into formal SysML v2 models. For each source file, the pipeline detects the language, parses to an Syntax Tree (AST), serializes to a normalized XML intermediary, and applies rule-structured prompting that maps programming constructs (e.g., classes, resources, variables) to SysML v2 elements (e.g., package, part def, attribute def). Unsupported or ambiguous constructs trigger iterative repair, and artifacts are validated using the official SysML v2 conformance tools, with detected issues injected into error-aware prompts to close the loop. To our knowledge, this is the first end-to-end, LLMdriven pipeline for code → SysML v2 with validator-in-the-loop conformance. We evaluate the framework across Java, C/C++, and Terraform using macro- and micro-precision, recall, and $F_{\beta}$, along with a coverage metric capturing the proportion of source constructs represented. Results show that normalization and validator-in-the-loop checks consistently increase precision and improve macro $F_{1}$, while micro $F_{1}$ remains recall-limited, indicating effective structural cleanup but limited semantic completion at instance scale. We outline concrete paths to lift recall without sacrificing precision and note ongoing integration of a multilayered, knowledge-graph-enabled LLM reasoner to support targeted completions and closed-loop, agentic system-software co-engineering.

title: Reliability Qualification of 1700V Lateral GaN HEMTs for Data Center, Industrial and Automotive Applications
authors: Karthick Murukesan, Robert Yang, Kamal Varadarajan, Alexei Ankoudinov, Bhawani Shankar, Alexey Kudymov, Sorin Georgescu
abstract: In this work, we describe high-voltage reliability qualification for the industry's first 1700 V-rated lateral GaN HEMT with significant transient overvoltage capability making it ideal for high-reliability industrial, automotive and AI data center applications. Based on the PowiGaN<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">TM</sup> technology platform of Power Integrations, the 1700 V GaN cascode device shows stable off-state leakage beyond 2.5 kV with a typical breakdown of 3 kV. Results from high voltage off-state reliability stress tests - HTRB, H3TRB along with switching DHTOL stress are discussed in detail. We have also shown the intrinsic reliability by TDDB predicting a cumulative failure rate of 1 ppm in 900 years of operation at 1360 V/100° C, indicating a significant built-in reliability margin for the 1700 V GaN power device. Additionally, we also describe an isolated high-efficiency power supply, utilizing the 1700 V GaN flyback switcher IC, benchmarked against an equivalent 1700 V SiC switcher IC. We show for the first time that lateral GaN HEMTs can be a realistic wide band-gap solution at this voltage level.

title: Actor-Critic-Based Event-Triggered Adaptive Dynamic Programming for Nonlinear Multi-Agent Systems
authors: Ruijia Sun
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: To address excessive communication consumption and optimal control challenges in nonlinear multi-agent systems (MASs), an event-triggered adaptive dynamic programming (ADP) method based on the actor-critic network is proposed. The critic network approximates the optimal value function, and the actor network optimizes the control strategy, with a relative threshold event-triggered condition reducing redundant communication. Lyapunov stability theory verifies closed-loop signal boundedness and Zeno behavior exclusion. Simulation results show the method outperforms traditional time-triggered ADP, improving control accuracy by 13% and balancing control performance with resource consumption.

title: A Hybrid Machine Learning Approach with Explainable AI for Prostate Cancer Diagnosis
authors: Sumaiya Rahman Liza, Ashfaqul Islam, Nazim Uddin, Abdul Kadar Muhammad Masum, Khandaker Mohammad Mohi Uddin
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Prostate cancer is the most common nonskin cancer in the human body. Early-stage prostate cancer frequently has no symptoms and progresses slowly, necessitating only vigilant surveillance. One of the most prevalent malignancies in males is prostate cancer, and successful treatment planning depends on early detection. The stacking-based ensemble learning framework proposed in this article combines several fine-tuned machine learning models, such as Random Forest (RF), XGBoost (XGB), Gradient Boosting (GB), and LightGBM (LGBM), SVM, Logistic Regression (LR) etc. The dataset was normalized using MinMax scaling and outlier elimination using the interquartile range approach. GridSearchCV with stratified cross-validation was used to optimize the hyperparameters. The system leveraged complementary capabilities of individual classifiers by combining the optimized base models in a stacking ensemble with RF, XGB, GB, LGBM with soft voting. In comparison to single models, ensemble models give experimental evaluation on the prostate cancer dataset, achieving accuracies in range of 85% to 96%. Key predictive biomarkers were found by interpreting the models using XAI approaches (SHAP and LIME). According to the findings, the suggested framework is a potential decision-support tool for prostate cancer risk prediction since it combines PCA, ensemble learning, and XAI to give both strong accuracy and transparency.

title: Online Scheduling and Distribution of Quantum Circuits Over Quantum Networks
authors: Yukun Yang, Himanshu Gupta
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Emerging quantum networks enable distributed quantum computing (DQC) by interconnecting modest, noisy quantum processors. However, practical deployments face tight memory budgets, probabilistic entanglement generation, and decoherence, all while serving multiple users. In realistic settings—cloud execution, iterative/interactive workloads, and concurrent jobs-circuits do not arrive in a single batch but as a stream. This creates a fundamentally online problem: the system must react to stochastic arrivals, balance latency and throughput, and remain stable under varying loads without foresight of future jobs. We study Online Scheduling and Distribution of Quantum Circuits (OS-DQC) and formalize the online scheduling problem for quantum networks: circuits arrive over time and must be executed without knowledge of future arrivals. At each event time (arrival or completion), the system decides which waiting circuits to launch and how to place their qubits, while accounting for congestion from in-flight circuits and constraints due to finite memories and decoherence. The objective is to minimize the mean completion time and the makespan subject to these physical and resource constraints.

title: FPGA-NAND Flash Reliability Measurement System to Probe Layer-Dependent Neurocomputing Efficiency
authors: Asifa Khatun, Himanshu Marothya, Dharmendra Kumar Singh, Sandip Mondal
abstract: Reliability remains a key concern when using NAND flash memory for neuromorphic computing applications. In this work, we investigate the storage capabilities of 3D NAND flash for neural network weights using an FPGA-based experimental platform. An ANN trained on the MNIST dataset was implemented on the DDR3 memory of a Zybo Z7-10 FPGA, and the quantised network weights were stored across 32 vertical layers of the NAND flash. To examine long-term data retention, the device was subjected to high-temperature baking at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$150{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$</tex> for 5 hours, with periodic readouts. We observed that the bottom layers were the most vulnerable, showing higher fail bit counts (ranging from 20 to 2000) and a corresponding drop in inference accuracy from 99% to 96%, while the middle layers remained relatively stable. To address this non-uniform degradation, a layer-aware ECC scheme with variable code lengths was employed, resulting in a nearly 80% reduction in bit errors. This adaptive approach significantly improved inference stability while keeping redundancy overhead low, demonstrating that layer-aware reliability management is an effective strategy for enabling efficient and robust neuromorphic operation using 3D NAND flash.

title: Intelligent Commutation Optimization Method Based on Improved Multi-Objective Genetic Algorithm
authors: Tao Zhang, Hongbin Liu, Yunguang Gao, Dongyao Wang
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: Aiming at the three-phase unbalance problem caused by different load sizes and power consumption periods of each phase in the three-phase 380 V distribution network dominated by single-phase loads, an intelligent commutation optimization method based on improved multi-objective genetic algorithm is proposed. This scheme adopts intelligent phaseswitching switch technology to design a real-time load adjustment strategy for the low-voltage distribution area of the distribution network. It focuses on constructing an optimal configuration mathematical model with three-phase current unbalance degree, number of commutations, line loss and transformer loss as objective functions. An improved multi-objective genetic algorithm optimization mode commutation strategy is adopted, which adds the catfish effect and merging effect, and completes the solution of the commutation model to obtain the optimal commutation scheme. Finally, the effectiveness, superiority and universality of the control strategy are verified through experimental simulation. The results show that the proposed method is feasible and verify the effectiveness and practicability of the optimized commutation scheme in reducing the three-phase unbalance of the distribution network.

title: Scalable Quantum Molecular Generation via GPU-Accelerated Tensor-Network Simulation
authors: Yu-Cheng Xiao, Jen-Yu Chang, Tzu-Ling Kuo, Aninda Astuti, Shu-Chi Wu, Ka-Lok Ng, Yun-Yuan Wang, Yu-Ze Chen, Nan-Yow Chen, Tai-Yu Li
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: We propose Scalable Quantum Molecular Generation (SQMG), a variational quantum-circuit for sampling molecular graphs using chemical priors on atoms and bonds. SQMG assigns a fixed 3-qubit register to each heavy atom and reuses a single 2-qubit bond register to generate bonds sequentially, yielding an “atom no-reuse, bond reuse” architecture with linear qubit scaling. Measurement results are mapped to molecular graphs via lightweight classical decoding with structural constraints. In CUDA-Q, we benchmark the statevector simulation (CPU/GPU) and the tensor-network simulation (GPU). At N = 8 heavy atoms, the state-vector simulator (GPU) and the tensor-network simulator (GPU) achieve speeds of up to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$4.5 \times 10^{4}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2.2 \times 10^{3}$</tex> over the state-vector (CPU) baseline, respectively. Crucially, tensor-network simulation extends exact simulation to N = 40 heavy atoms, where state-vector methods become memory-limited. For training, Bayesian optimization outperforms COBYLA on a Validity×Uniqueness objective, and the same architecture supports de novo generation, scaffold decoration, and linker design. Overall, SQMG provides a scalable, reproducible testbed for evaluating accelerated tensor-network simulation and future quantum molecular generation algorithms.

title: Blockchain-Based Relay Protection Setting Storage Using Dynamic Rational Interval Encoding
authors: Congcong Li, Yi Tang, Yudun Li, Jiayin Jiang, Qingfa Chai, Wei Cong
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: To address the characteristics of relay protection XML setting files—deep nesting, strong coupling, and frequent updates—and to enable efficient storage, dynamic updates, and trustworthy evidence preservation, this paper proposes a mapping-based storage model founded on Dynamic Rational Interval Encoding (DRIE). Unlike traditional path- or vector-based encoding schemes, DRIE encodes structural relationships by assigning each XML tree node a unique and ordered rational interval (left,right), where each child interval is strictly contained within its parent interval. This encoding allows ancestor-descendant relationships to be determined in constant time and supports localized dynamic updates by inserting new rational values between adjacent ones, thereby avoiding global re-encoding. Based on DRIE, a three-table relational mapping schema (document table, node table, and hash table) is designed, together with a corresponding algorithm for translating XPath queries into SQL. Furthermore, a Merkle tree is constructed from node content hashes to support chunked blockchain-based notarization of setting data and rapid auditing. Experiments on a public dataset and real-world setting files demonstrate that, compared with classical methods such as XRel and XVector, DRIE achieves competitive storage efficiency while effectively supporting dynamic insertions, offering a new solution for building secure and traceable relay protection setting management systems.

title: Enhanced Heavy-Ion Radiation Hardness in ZnO Thin-Film Transistors via Crystal Structure Engineering
authors: Yongsu Lee, Hae-Won Lee, Su Jin Kim, Jeong Min Park, Byoung Hun Lee, Chang Goo Kang
abstract: The influence of crystal structure on the radiation hardness of ZnO thin-film transistors (TFTs) under heavy-ion irradiation is investigated through a comparative analysis between conventional polycrystalline ZnO (Poly-ZnO) and phase-composite <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{Z n O}(\mathbf{P h C}-\mathbf{Z n O})$</tex>. To isolate the intrinsic effects of crystallinity from surface-driven instabilities, an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$</tex> passivation layer was employed to stabilize the channel interface. While Poly-ZnO TFTs suffer significant degradation in on-current, field-effect mobility, and subthreshold swing due to atomic displacement damage within polycrystalline grains, PhC-ZnO TFTs exhibit remarkable stability with negligible changes. This robustness is attributed to the unique micro-topology of PhC-ZnO-comprising nanocrystalline domains dispersed within an amorphous matrix-which acts as a structural buffer to preserve carrier transport integrity. This study provides a new design rule for engineering intrinsically radiation-hardened metal-oxide semiconductors via precise crystallinity control.

title: A Portable Hybrid Quantum-Classical Neural Network for Continual Learning
authors: Zeyu Yang, Yunpeng Bai, Changyi Li, Yu Xiao
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Quantum continual learning (QCL) has recently emerged as a promising paradigm, enabling quantum models to incrementally acquire knowledge across tasks while mitigating catastrophic forgetting. Yet, existing approaches face two persistent obstacles: (i) quantum architectures scale poorly to datasets of practical size, and (ii) continuous learning strategies are often entangled with specific quantum models, reducing their adaptability. To address these challenges, we introduce a twofold design. First, we propose hybrid quantum-classical (HQC) networks tailored to different data regimes: HQC1, optimized for Split-MNIST, achieves a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 3 3. 5 \%}$</tex> average accuracy improvement over pure quantum baselines, while HQC2 is the first to demonstrate QCL on CIFAR-100, boosting accuracy by 14.66% over a comparable ResNet-18. Second, we present a decoupled QCL framework that separates model architecture from learning strategy, enabling flexible alignment and yielding more than double the average accuracy relative to existing pairings. Together, these contributions establish a scalable and adaptable foundation for practical quantum continuous learning on large, complex datasets.

title: Comparative Evaluation of Machine Learning and Hybrid Models for Stock Price Forecasting: A Case Study on Grameenphone Ltd. in Bangladesh's Emerging Market
authors: Md Mitul Hossain, Faisol Rahman, Md. Saon Sikder, Ratri Khanom, Mohammad Shamsul Islam
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Forecasting stock prices is a difficult task because financial data is highly dynamic, nonlinear, and often unpredictable. This challenge is even greater in emerging markets such as Bangladesh, where there are fewer research studies and reliable prediction tools. In this work, we focused on predicting the stock prices of Grameenphone Ltd., one of the largest companies listed on the Dhaka Stock Exchange. We collected four years of historical stock data (2021-2025), along with technical indicators and economic features such as volume, moving averages, sector index, and inflation rates. To improve prediction, we applied feature engineering techniques, including lag values and rolling statistics. Several forecasting models were tested, ranging from traditional statistical methods (ARIMA, Prophet) to machine learning (Random Forest, XGBoost, SVR) and deep learning (LSTM, GRU). We also proposed a hybrid SARIMA-LSTM model that combines the strengths of statistical and deep learning approaches. All models were evaluated using walk-forward validation and standard performance metrics such as MAE, RMSE, MAPE, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{2}$</tex>. The experimental results show that the hybrid SARIMA-LSTM model outperformed all other methods, achieving an accuracy of 97.95% with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{2}=0.7242$</tex>. This proves that combining linear and nonlinear modeling can capture the complex behavior of stock prices better than individual models. Our study is one of the first systematic applications of hybrid deep learning for the Bangladeshi stock market, and it provides valuable insights for investors and researchers. In future, we plan to expand the study to multiple companies, integrate sentiment analysis, and explore real-time prediction systems.

title: Stacking Ensemble Approach for AI Generated Text Detection with Appropriate Feature Selection
authors: Md. Siam Ansary
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: With the growing prominence of artificial intelligence, its integration into everyday life has become increasingly widespread, often in ways once thought unimaginable. Today, AI tools are routinely employed to generate diverse forms of text, such as academic articles and business reports. While these systems demonstrate impressive capabilities, the ability to distinguish between human-written and AI-generated content has become crucial to prevent misuse and deception. In this study, we propose a Stacking Ensemble Approach that leverages seven strong base learners and uses a meta-model. We utilized publicly available datasets from Kaggle and conducted extensive feature engineering to extract key indicators relevant to the detection task. Our methodology has achieved near-perfect performance across standard evaluation metrics. To support transparency and reproducibility, the code used in our experiments will be made publicly accessible.

title: Modelling the value of space project ecologies through systems architecting
authors: Alessandro Paravano, Paolo Trucco
abstract: In the Space Economy, complex space projects are increasingly expected to generate long-term value that extends beyond traditional space stakeholders, reaching new terrestrial domains such as insurance, energy, transportation, and healthcare. While the space sector has decades of Systems Engineering experience in defining and validating project performance, existing methodologies remain insufficient for modeling and designing the value of complex space projects. We argue that this gap stems from a system-architectural failure: projects are conceived as closed systems rather than as nested systems within the broader project ecology (systems of systems). Our work addresses this shortcoming by 1) framing complex space projects as nested systems and 2) applying systems architecting to model the value created by space projects in their ecology. The empirical setting of the study is Copernicus, the European flagship Earth Observation programme. The research proceeds through three main stages. 1) Ecology Modeling - Based on secondary data, we mapped and modeled the Copernicus ecology architecture (entities and functions). 2) Value Perception Analysis - We conducted an assessment of value perception among the entities in Copernicus ecology, examining how different entities define and pursue value functions, and how end-users interpret and derive value from Copernicus outputs. Data were gathered through interviews with engineers and managers in the ecology, to perceived value misalignments and architectural bottlenecks. 3) Value Network Modeling - We developed a value network model, quantifying the flows of value among Copernicus ecology entities. The resulting value network model reveals how and why Copernicus’s value lies in its ecology. Findings indicate that Copernicus, as currently architected, does not fully satisfy the expected value functions of its intended end users, with value often being propagated to unintended or weakly connected domains. This misalignment reflects a lack of architectural coherence and systemic feedback mechanisms, underscoring the need for new frameworks for architectural design governance that integrate ecological considerations into project design. Our research advances the systems architecture and project management literature by demonstrating the relevance of broadening the level of analysis to the ecology (systems of systems) for designing next-generation projects.

title: Systems Optimization and Risk Management of Sensor Networks for Detection of Wildfires
authors: Megan E. Gunn, R. Ranger Dorn, Matthew C. Gunn, Davis C. Loose, Bilal M. Ayyub, William A. Barletta, John F. Organek, Marco Piras, S. Fabrizio Zichichi, James H. Lambert
abstract: An urgent topic of systems engineering and engineering management is the design and evaluation of sensor networks for societal resilience. Wildfires are threatening communities with impacts on landscapes, health, and economies. Early detection of new wildfires is essential for effective responses and improving risk mitigation. In this application, cameras are used to monitor landscapes and identify anomalies. However, line-of-sight, financial, jurisdictional, and other factors complicate efforts to deploy cameras across a region. Camera and other sensor deployments are typically chosen to maximize the coverage area subject to limited resources. However, wildfire risk varies by location and terrain. Existing camera networks can be expanded over time as more resources are available, and once-optimal solutions may become sub-optimal with the addition of new cameras, potentially requiring expensive relocation of existing camera towers. The complexity of camera network design thus calls for an approach that incorporates risk metrics and supports decision-making over time from a perspective of large-scale systems. This paper formulates the network design as a weighted maximum coverage problem and describes a greedy algorithm for sequential system design. The approach provides insights into system design, balancing coverage of high-risk and low-risk areas, and cost-coverage tradeoffs. The approach is applied to a case study in Erice, Trapani, Italy.

title:
Enhancing Endurance of Ferroelectric Tunnel Junctions with Dual Al
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    O
                    <sub>3</sub>
                    Layers
authors: Jiajun Qiu, Min Zeng, Wen-Feng Li, Guang-Hua Xu, Qincheng Yang, Ning Feng, Yue-Yang Liu, Runsheng Wang, Yanqing Wu, Lining Zhang
abstract: This work demonstrates a sandwiched insulator-ferroelectric-insulator (IFI) structure using dual Al<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> O<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf> interlayers to enhance the FTJ endurance. The device achieves an excellent endurance exceeding 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">11</sup> cycles, with a tunneling electro-resistance (TER) ratio of ~9. Mechanisms contributing to this significant endurance improvement are revealed with electron energy loss spectroscopy (EELS) and density functional theory (DFT) analyses. It is shown that the Al<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> O<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf> interlayer suppresses oxygen vacancy formation and facilitates vacancy migration away from the interface, thereby mitigating defect accumulation and the performance degradation. These results highlight interface engineering as a critical pathway to improve FTJs' reliability and pave the way for their practical application in non-volatile memories.

title:
Physical Understanding of Short Circuit Stress (SCS) Effect on R
                    <sub>ON</sub>
                    and I
                    <sub>MAX</sub>
                    Drift of on-Wafer 100 V p-GaN Gate AlGaN/GaN HEMTs
authors: M. Cioni, G. Giorgino, G. Cappellini, S. Strano, A. Chini, A. Constant, F. Iucolano
abstract: The physical understanding of Short-Circuit-Stress (SCS) effect on Ron and Imax drifts of 100 V GaN-based HEMTs is provided in this work. Particularly, the ionization of buffer traps and hot-carrier trapping at the devices surface are identified as the physical mechanisms responsible for Ron and Imax drifts after SCS. To pursue this study, the AM3200 Pulsed-IV system is used to apply SCS at different Ids, stress and Vds, stress, while measuring the parameters drift from the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{I}_{\mathbf{D}}-\mathbf{V}_{\text{DS }}$</tex> curves acquired before/after SCS. An increase in Ids, stress yields an increase in the Ron and Imax drifts, suggesting the involvement of hot-carriers trapping. A similar trend is observed with increasing Vdd, stress, consistently to electric field acceleration. To discern buffer and surface related trapping, the Id-Vds recovery after SCS is investigated. To this end, three samples featuring different combinations of buffer and passivation type are compared, yielding differences in the short- and long-term drifts. Finally, the impact of temperature is studied, confirming the involvement of hot-carriers.

title: Analysis of SDN Control Protocols in Industrial-Grade QKD Networks
authors: Ruben B. Mendez, Jesus Ballesta, Ramón Querol, Carmen Escribano, Luis Mengual, Juan Robles, Antonio Pastor, Rafael Cantó, Jesus Folgueira, Diego Lopez, Vicente Martin, Juan P. Brito
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Quantum Key Distribution is emerging as a promising technology to increase the robustness of current communications. Quantum Computing promises substantial benefits for society, particularly through its ability to solve complex mathematical problems far more efficiently than classical systems. However, these emerging computational capabilities also introduce significant security risks that must be mitigated to preserve the integrity of worldwide communication infrastructures. A primary concern is the potential for quantum algorithms to break widely deployed cryptographic schemes within operationally feasible timeframes. To counter this threat, industry and academia are investing considerable effort and resources into advancing quantum communication technologies and accelerating their adoption across world wide networks. Software-Defined Networking (SDN) is increasingly becoming the de-facto architectural paradigm for enabling the deployment and operation of QKD networks. Also the adoption of emerging standards that build upon this new network model, such as ETSI GS QKD 015, plays a crucial role in the adoption of this new technology. In this paper, we present a qualitative analysis of key SDN communication protocols, including NETCONF, RESTCONF, and gNMI/gRPC, evaluated within a real, industrial-grade QKD network. This assessment provides a realistic comparison of their capabilities, limitations, and practical suitability for managing quantum-safe communication infrastructures.

title: Research on Optimization of Reactive Power Compensation in Power Grids Based on Intelligent Optimization Algorithms
authors: Jiaxuan Dai, Shiping Su
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: Addressing the challenges of traditional reactive power compensation strategies in adapting to dynamic fluctuations in power grid reactive power demand and the tendency of optimization algorithms to fall into local optima, this study proposes a Hybrid Intelligent Differential Evolution with Simulated Annealing (HDESA) algorithm. It integrates the global search capability of Differential Evolution (DE) with the local optimization advantage of Simulated Annealing (SA), effectively overcoming the limitations of single algorithms. With the objectives of minimizing network losses and maximizing voltage compliance rates, an optimization model incorporating constraints such as equipment capacity and voltage magnitude is constructed, fully considering the actual operating characteristics of the power grid. A hierarchical coding scheme is employed to achieve collaborative decision-making regarding the siting and capacity configuration of compensation nodes, ensuring the rationality and feasibility of the optimization results. Simulations on the IEEE 33-node system demonstrate that HDESA achieves a network loss reduction rate of 28.6% and improves the voltage compliance rate to 100%. Compared to standard Genetic Algorithms (GA) and Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO), HDESA enhances optimal solution precision by 33% and 20%, respectively, and accelerates convergence by 22%, providing efficient technical support for power grid reactive power optimization and laying a solid foundation for practical engineering application.

title: QuIC: A Quantum-Inspired Interaction Classifier for Revitalizing Shallow Convolutional Neural Networks in Fine-Grained Recognition
authors: Cheng Ying Wu, Yen Jui Chang
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Deploying deep learning models for Fine-Grained Visual Classification (FGVC) on resource-constrained edge devices remains a significant challenge. While deep architectures achieve high accuracy on benchmarks like CUB-200-2011, their computational cost is often prohibitive. Conversely, shallow networks (e.g., AlexNet, VGG) offer efficiency but fail to distinguish visually similar sub-categories. This is because standard Global Average Pooling (GAP) heads capture only first-order statistics, missing the subtle high-order feature interactions required for FGVC. While Bilinear Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) address this, they suffer from high feature dimensionality and instability during training. To bridge this gap, we propose the Quantum-inspired Interaction Classifier (QuIC). Drawing inspiration from quantum mechanics, QuIC models feature channels as interacting quantum states and captures secondorder feature covariance via a learnable observable operator. Designed as a lightweight, plug-and-play module, QuIC supports stable, single-stage end-to-end training without exploding feature dimensions. Experimental results demonstrate that QuIC significantly revitalizes shallow backbones: it boosts the Top1 accuracy of VGG16 by nearly 20% and outperforms state-of-the-art attention mechanisms (SE) on ResNet18. Qualitative analysis, including t-SNE visualization, further confirms that QuIC resolves ambiguous cases by explicitly attending to finegrained discriminative features and enforcing compact intra-class clustering.

title: PSO-Consensus Optimization based Distributed Generator Dispatch Considering Cyber-Physical Coupling
authors: Hongshuo Zhang, Ziming Li, Haodong Liu, Haijun Liao, Zhenyu Zhou
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: Cyber-physical coupling plays an essential supporting role in achieving low-carbon operation of microgrid. Distributed consensus algorithm is the core principle to facilitate cost-efficient operation of distributed generator output and load. However, current distributed consensus algorithms cannot satisfy the requirements of cyber-physical coupling scenarios for the speed and flexibility of dispatching. This paper proposed particle swarm optimization (PSO) -consensus optimization based distributed generator dispatch considering cyber-physical coupling. First, a PSO-consensus optimization based distributed generator dispatch framework considering cyber-physical coupling is constructed. Secondly, an optimization model is constructed with the objective of minimizing dispatch cost and convergence iteration weighted value, balancing the convergence speed with cost. Then, a PSO-consensus optimization based distributed generator dispatch algorithm is proposed to determine the optimal feedback gain, and adapt to the needs of different microgrid operating scenarios. Finally, this paper conducts simulation verification using an improved IEEE-15 node distribution network model. The proposed algorithm achieves convergence speeds that are <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{7 0. 0 0 \%}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 9. 4 5 \%}$</tex> faster than Baseline1 and Baseline2, respectively, effectively improving the efficiency and economic viability of microgrid dispatching.

title: Competing Failure Mechanisms Impacting Long-Term Drift of a Bandgap Voltage Reference During Operational Life Tests
authors: E.G. Marina Alonzo, R.T.H. Rongen, P. Turpin, P. Tounsi, S. Ben-Dhia
abstract: Electrical drifts observed in embedded Bandgap Voltage References during stress tests are difficult to assess and reproduce as all the composing materials of the semiconductor device (silicon, interconnects, dielectrics and package) degrade over time, preventing a straightforward attribution to a single source and complexifying predictions for device aging. Lifetime assessments based solely on silicon wear-out mechanisms are insufficient to explain these drifts. Whereas models that incorporate the interaction of the silicon and its encapsulation provide a more accurate explanation. To better understand these drifts, this study evaluates and compares both perspectives. Silicon wear-out impact was investigated through two major degradation mechanisms: Bias Temperature Instability, assessed under High Temperature Operating Life test conditions for 6500 h at a junction temperature of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$165^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$</tex>. And Hot Carrier Injection, assessed under Low Temperature Operating Life test conditions for 2000 h at a junction temperature of 0° C. Correlation of tests data with aging simulations based on these silicon degradation mechanisms confirmed that these degradations account for only a small fraction of the measured drift. In contrast, the silicon-encapsulation based approach, also assessed in the same High Temperature Operating Life test conditions, analyzed through thermomechanical simulations, shows that progressive package molding compound oxidation is the dominant source of electrical drift. When this stress evolution is translated into piezoelectric expressions of the constituent components of this circuit, the resulting model accurately reproduces the test measurements.

title: Economic Optimal Scheduling of a Renewable Energy–Data Center Considering Time-Flexible Loads
authors: Jianrui Wang, Maoran Xia, Bingsi Wang, Yinong Wang
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: To address the economic operation and renewable energy absorption synergy of integrated renewable energy–data center systems under electricity price volatility and uncertainty in photovoltaic and wind power output, an economic optimal dispatch method is proposed that incorporates time-flexible computational loads. On the basis of traditional energy-side constraints, time-inflexible and time-flexible computational tasks are explicitly distinguished, and the waiting computational demand and computational demand delay are introduced to construct mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) constraints, thereby enabling unified optimization of service quality, electricity purchasing and selling, energy storage charging and discharging, and renewable energy accommodation. For the formulated MILP, a two-level iterative solution framework based on Benders decomposition is developed, and after convergence, CPLEX is employed to refine the original problem, thereby improving computational efficiency and solution optimality. Simulation studies compare two scenarios, namely without time-flexible computational loads and with time-flexible computational loads, and the results show that under tolerable delay constraints, time-flexible computational loads enable inter-temporal task shifting and effectively avoid high-price electricity purchases, reducing the total operating cost significantly, while simultaneously improving peak–valley load characteristics and enhancing renewable energy absorption capability.
