title: Learnable Affine Transformation Guided Multi-Feature Aggregation for Remote Sensing Scene Classification
authors: Qirui Bai, Yang Xu, Kun Yu, Shangdong Zheng
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Although remote sensing scene classification has advanced significantly, accurately identifying diminutive targets remains challenging due to extreme scale variations and severe background interference. The stacked confusion between complex backgrounds and target morphologies always leads to perceptual imbalance in networks. To address these persisting issues, we propose a learnable affine transformation guided multi-feature aggregation network, a lightweight and efficient architecture based on YOLOv8. Specifically, we parameterize the affine transformation learning that incorporates an adaptive spatial dropout mechanism to capture critical image cues.By doing so, we construct a multi-feature extraction branch to capture the latent representations from the original and dropped features. Through the inverse transformation, the dropped feature is recovered into the correct mode and then fused with the original feature for the following classification task. The proposed method is validated on three public remote sensing datasets, which exhibits competitive performance compared to current popular classification methods.

title: Micro Surface Defect Inspection of Aero-Engine Blades via Dynamic Cross-Scale Semantic Aggregation
authors: Kaijie Li, Shuai Jiang, Yao Lin, Jingyu Zhou, Hao Qu, Xiangfei Meng, Yaonan Wang, Min Liu
abstract: Automated surface inspection of aero-engine blades is crucial for aviation safety and faces challenges from metallic reflections, texture interference, and extreme scale imbalance of microdefects. To address these problems, we present DCSA-Net, a two-stage framework designed for reliable blade defect detection. DCSA-Net includes a dynamic perception attention module that adaptively suppresses high-frequency texture artifacts using dual-dimension feature recalibration. To resolve semantic discrepancies in feature fusion, we introduce an adaptive pooling fusion mechanism that aligns cross-layer distributions with global semantic calibration. We also implement a 3-D cross-scale semantic aggregation module, reformulating multiscale features into a scale-space manifold and employing 3-D convolutions to capture spatio-scale correlations for precise microdefect localization. Evaluation on the Aero-Engine Blade Dataset and NEU-DET benchmark shows DCSA-Net achieves state-of-the-art accuracy. The system has also been integrated into a real-world inspection platform with a six-degree-of-freedom robotic arm, demonstrating its robustness and efficiency in industrial manufacturing environments.

title: When Customer Support Becomes an Attack Surface: How Support Workflows Act as Shadow APIs to Bypass Authentication
authors: Luis Marquez-Carpintero, Ernesto Canales-Pereña, Miguel Cazorla
abstract: We show how customer support and compliance workflows act as shadow APIs that can bypass authentication and lock out users. We analyze process-based denial-of-service risks in large-scale platforms, highlight incidents, and provide a checklist to treat support as a first-class security surface.

title: Welcome Message from the General Chair
authors: N/A
abstract: On behalf of the IRPS Management Committee and the IRPS Board of Directors, it is my pleasure to welcome you to the 2026 IEEE International Reliability Physics Symposium (IRPS), taking place March 22-26, 2026, in Tucson, Arizona—our first time meeting in this remarkable city. Now in its 64th year, IRPS remains the premier venue for presenting and discussing advances in the reliability of semiconductor devices, integrated circuits, and microelectronic systems. Our global community spans industry, academia, and research institutions, united by a commitment to deepening our understanding of the physics of failure and real-world reliability challenges.

title: DSDRN: A Dual-Stream Dual-Refinement Network with Superpixel and Location Semantics for Building Damage Classification
authors: Dawei Wen, Hengyu Wang, Wenxia Gan, Deng Chen, Xinghua Li
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Building damage assessment aims to rapidly and safely localize buildings in disaster-affected areas and classify damage severity using high-resolution remote sensing imagery acquired by satellites or unmanned aerial vehicles. Although convolutional neural networks provide strong feature extraction capability, they often suffer from insufficient change modeling and semantic inconsistency within building regions. Meanwhile, existing object-based methods primarily address semantic inconsistency through post-processing, which is prone to errors in dense urban scenes where nearby small buildings may be merged due to inaccurate boundary delineation. In this paper, we propose DSDRN, a Dual-Stream Dual-Refinement Network with Superpixel and Location Semantics, built upon a two-stage Building Damage Assessment framework, the proposed network adopts a dual-stream structure in the encoding stage, where both post-disaster images and concatenated bi-temporal images are used as inputs. This design enables the model to simultaneously capture complementary deep single-temporal features and multi-scale bi-temporal change information. To alleviate intra-building semantic inconsistency and boundary fragmentation caused by locally dominant damage patterns, we introduce dual-refinement modules, including Superpixel Semantic Refine (SSR) and Location Semantic Refine (LSR). The SSR module models semantic relationships among superpixels at the regional level, encouraging semantically similar regions to converge toward consistent representations, thereby enhancing structural coherence across building areas. The LSR module incorporates building mask priors to suppress background noise and preserves accurate building boundaries in the final predictions. Extensive experiments on the xBD dataset demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed network and its individual components.

title: AI-Driven Energy Management of a Multi-Port Hybrid Renewable Microgrid with Hybrid Energy Storage
authors: Birand Erdogan
abstract: This paper proposes a novel intelligent energy management framework for a hybrid DC microgrid integrating photovoltaic (PV), wind, hydro, and fuel cell sources with a hybrid energy storage system (battery and supercapacitor). To overcome the efficiency and complexity limitations of conventional multi-converter architectures, a unified system was developed using two coordinated multi-port converters (MPC) connected to a common DC bus. A Particle Swarm Optimization-tuned Adaptive Neuro-Fuzzy Inference System (PSO-ANFIS) was implemented to determine the optimal fuel cell reference power by processing real-time renewable generation, storage states of charge (SOC), and load demands. The proposed architecture was rigorously validated in MATLAB/Simulink using an extensive dataset comprising 25,000 operating scenarios. The results demonstrate that the PSO-ANFIS controller achieves a high prediction accuracy with a testing root mean square error (RMSE) of 0.0201 pu, representing a 12% performance improvement over traditional methods within only 10 optimization iterations. The integrated MPC framework effectively coordinates diverse energy sources; the supercapacitor manages fast transients up to -4.5 kW, while the battery stabilizes steady-state power variations. This system ensures an uninterrupted supply to a 6-kW critical load and adaptive management of non-critical loads under highly fluctuating environmental conditions. By combining hardware-level multi-port integration with an AI-driven supervisory strategy, this work significantly enhances renewable energy utilization and microgrid reliability, offering a robust and scalable solution for modern decentralized power systems.

title: QazNLP: Constraint-Aware Multi-Task Sequence Labeling for Morphologically Rich Low-Resource Languages
authors: Aigerim Aitim, Graduate
abstract: Automatic processing of morphologically rich, agglutinative, and low-resource languages remains challenging because productive affixation increases lexical sparsity, weakens statistical generalization, and often produces inconsistent predictions across related linguistic annotation tasks. This study presents QazNLP, a constraint-aware multi-task framework for Kazakh that jointly performs morphological tagging, part-of-speech tagging, and named entity recognition using a shared transformer encoder with task-specific prediction heads. To improve structural reliability, the framework introduces differentiable cross-task compatibility penalties that discourage linguistically invalid label combinations during training and constrained decoding. The study further provides a reproducible evaluation setting based on a cleaned Kazakh news corpus with fixed data splits and robustness diagnostics for out-of-vocabulary tokens, long agglutinative word forms, reduced-data regimes, and cross-task contradiction analysis. In addition, the manuscript explicitly documents the encoder and tokenization setup, justifies the choice of pretrained encoder, reports decoding complexity, and situates the NER component with respect to the KazNERD benchmark. Experimental results show that the proposed model consistently outperforms competitive single-task and shared multi-task baselines in joint average F1, robustness under sparse-data conditions, and structural consistency of predicted labels. The findings indicate that explicit compatibility-aware optimization offers a practical and extensible direction for sequence labeling in morphologically rich low-resource languages.

title: Digital Tides: A Fluid-Dynamic Framework for Flux-Aware Infrastructure Provisioning in UAV Logistics Networks_supp1-3688690.pdf
authors: Wen-Yu Dong
abstract: The emergence of high-frequency pulsating logistics unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) swarms gives rise to “Digital Tides”, i.e., complex traffic dynamics that challenge sustainable resource provisioning in mobile computing networks. Conventional infrastructure provisioning strategies, which typically rely on static snapshot-based analysis and localized density estimation, fail to capture the macroscopic advection of computational workloads. As a result, reactive resource activation suffers from inherent hysteresis, yielding nominal efficiency gains at the cost of mission-critical service loss at the advancing wavefront. To address this issue, we develop a fluid-based spatiotemporal framework by explicitly solving the continuity equation to characterize the macroscopic velocity field of the workload flow. Building on this framework, we propose a flux-aware asymmetric activation strategy that leverages the derived information flux vector as a kinematic precursor of demand propagation. Unlike symmetric thresholding, the proposed control logic decouples activation and deactivation dynamics. Theoretical analysis confirms the intrinsic spatial phase-lead of the flux signal and shows that the proposed strategy generates a proactive guard ring to compensate for service setup latency, including delays caused by mobile edge computing container cold-starts. We further derive closed-form expressions for instantaneous service availability and period-average energy efficiency. In addition, we formulate a quality-of-service-penalized metric to evaluate effective energy efficiency under strict outage constraints. Numerical results show that the proposed flux-driven strategy enables zero-latency tracking of the mobile wavefront and achieves a Pareto-optimal trade-off between service reliability and energy consumption, outperforming reactive baselines in dynamic logistics corridors.

title: SMem-Diff: A Simple Memory-Augmented Diffusion Model for Effective Video Deblurring on Cloud-Edge Servers
authors: Qichuang Liu, Hui Li, Li-Ying Hao, Fa Zhu, Xingchi Chen, Qing Li, Moustafa Youssef, Giancarlo Fortino
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Deep learning–based video deblurring can exploit inter-frame information to remove blur artifacts in videos captured by cameras on edge devices, thereby enhancing video quality. Most existing methods adopt sliding window mechanism, recurrent feature propagation, transformers, or their combinations to leverage inter-frame information in video sequences. However, these methods typically process only a subset of adjacent blurry frames at a time and cannot effectively extract useful information from distant frames, thus failing to fully leverage the available information from the entire input video. Moreover, most of them are based on regression models, where the network fundamentally relies on highly degraded blurry input frames, causing the quality of the output frames to degrade as the input frames deteriorate. To address these issues and achieve finer-grained deblurring, we construct a simple yet effective memory bank for edge servers to filter and store temporal frame information, and design a memory update algorithm based on usage frequency to further exploit the advantages of memory networks. Additionally, we introduce the diffusion priors by combining a diffusion network with the designed hierarchical dense attention module to further alleviate the model’s high sensitivity to degraded input frames. Qualitative and quantitative experiments show that our proposed SMem-Diff exhibits superior performance on the GOPRO, DVD, and BSD datasets, with quantitative comparison result on the GOPRO dataset exceeding STCT+ by over 0.4dB in PSNR.

title: Topology Optimization of 3-D NoC for High-Speed Data Acquisition Systems via Deep Reinforcement Learning
authors: Yunhui Deng, Chuanpei Xu, Wei Mo, Chunting Wan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) Systems
abstract: The deployment of high-speed data acquisition systems (HS-DAQs) on 3-D network-on-chip (3-D NoC) requires the real-time transmission of large volumes of data across the network. However, conventional generic topology structures are difficult to adapt effectively to the specific communication characteristics of such systems, leading to degraded overall transmission efficiency. This article proposes a topology optimization methodology for 3-D NoC oriented toward HS-DAQ systems. The topology adjustment process is formulated as a sequential optimization task, and deep reinforcement learning is employed to progressively refine the network connections to better match the system communication load. A pruning–regrowth mechanism is introduced to reconstruct critical links, thereby achieving a balance between latency performance and area overhead. In addition, an action candidate set combined with a dual-level <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\varepsilon $</tex-math> </inline-formula>-greedy strategy is adopted to enhance training stability and search efficiency in large-scale action spaces. Experimental results on HS-DAQ systems of different scales (16–48 nodes) demonstrate that, compared with the 3-D Mesh NoC and 3-D PC-NoC, the optimized network topology reduces the average latency by approximately 4.2%–14.3% and 11.7%–30.3%, the maximum path latency by about 13.6%–19.7%, and the edge area by 27.8%–33.3% and 5.6%–14.7%, respectively.

title: Robustness Certificates for Neural networks Against Data poisoning and Evasion attacks
authors: Sara Taheri, Mahalakshmi Sabanayagam, Debarghya Ghoshdastidar, Majid Zamani
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Control Systems
abstract: The increasing use of machine learning in safety-critical domains amplifies the risk of adversarial threats, especially data poisoning attacks that corrupt training data to degrade performance or induce unsafe behavior. Most existing defenses lack formal guarantees or rely on restrictive assumptions about the model class, attack type, extent of poisoning, or point-wise certification, limiting their practical reliability. This paper introduces a principled formal robustness certification framework that models gradient-based training as a discrete-time dynamical system (dt-DS) and formulates poisoning robustness as a formal safety verification problem. By adapting the concept of barrier certificates (BCs) from control theory, we introduce sufficient conditions to certify a robust radius ensuring that the terminal model remains safe under worst-case <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">${\ell }_{p}$</tex-math></inline-formula>-norm based poisoning. To make this practical, we parameterize BCs as neural networks trained on finite sets of poisoned trajectories. We further derive probably approximately correct (PAC) bounds by solving a scenario convex program (SCP), which yields a confidence lower bound on the certified robustness radius generalizing beyond the training set. Importantly, our framework also extends to certification against test-time attacks, making it the first unified framework to provide formal guarantees in both training and test-time attack settings. Experiments on MNIST, SVHN, CIFAR-10, and CIFAR-100 show that our approach certifies non-trivial perturbation budgets while being model-agnostic and requiring no prior knowledge of the attack or contamination level.

title: Why is Mandatory Governance Insufficient?Heterogeneous Governance Effects of Compliance Information Disclosure: Evidence from Regression and Machine Learning Analyses in China
authors: Tianyu Zhang, Weiping Liu, Zixun Liu, Yunhan Wang, Yunxi Wang
publish: Proceedings of the 2nd International Conference on Digital Society, Information Science and Risk Management
abstract: None

title: Integrating Machine Learning into Additive Manufacturing through Service-Oriented and Low-Code Architectures
authors: Cathal Hoare, Stephen Ryan
publish: Proceedings of the 2026 2nd International Workshop on Low-Code Development and Software Architecture
abstract: None

title: Entanglement Fidelity in Standard Quantum Channels
authors: Niccolò Zanieri, Marios Kountouris
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Entanglement fidelity quantifies how well a quantum channel preserves the correlations between a transmitted system and an inaccessible reference system. We derive closed-form expressions for the entanglement fidelity of several standard quantum noise models, including the random Pauli-<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$X$</tex>, dephasing, depolarizing, Werner-Holevo, generalized Pauli (Weyl), and amplitude-damping channels. For each model, we express the entanglement fidelity in terms of a general input density operator <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">${\rho}$</tex>, using Schumacher's Kraus-operator approach, which provides a channel-agnostic recipe for any completely positive trace-preserving map with a finite Kraus representation. We then specialize to a communication scenario in which the source emits a two-letter parametric alphabet, thereby making explicit the dependence of entanglement preservation on both channel and source parameters. The resulting expressions enable direct comparisons of channel performance and rankings for representative families of input states.

title: Energy Planning Strategy of Smart Community Microgrid Based on Improved Gray Wolf Algorithm
authors: Xiaorong Xia, Yansong Huang, Pengfei Hu, Fei Wang, Wenjie Xie, Jiangzhou Cheng
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: In order to meet the user demand response ability of intelligent community, the stability and economy of community microgrid are further strengthened. Building the intelligent community energy management system framework, this paper expounds the operation mode of the energy management system and information collection and management process, put forward the community and individual price demand response strategy, by the user controllable load and energy storage equipment to participate in demand response and rewarded, intelligent community power coordination scheduling model is established, The verification is carried out with the goal of economic consumption. The results show that when considering individual price incentive, users who actively transfer the load will get more revenue, and when considering community price incentive, users' return rate is equal, belonging to the collective benefit, which verifies the feasibility of the coordinated scheduling strategy.

title: Performance Enhancement in Quantum Repeater Chains via Simultaneous Entanglement Swappings
authors: Javier Faba, Andrés Agustí Casado, Luis Robledo, Vicente Martin
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: In this work, we study the performance of simultaneous—also referred to as parallel—entanglement swapping strategies through the average entanglement of formation of the resulting Werner states shared between the end nodes. As an application of our analytical extension of the quantum network model, we analyze a simple four-node quantum repeater chain to compare both parallel and non-parallel strategies. Although parallel entanglement swapping strategies exhibit lower success probabilities, they outperform non-parallel ones when decoherence and entanglement measures are taken into account. These results indicate that parallel entanglement swapping processes can significantly enhance the performance of entanglement distribution networks, suggesting that they could play a key role in the design of future quantum network architectures.

title: QFiLa: Entanglement-Assured Link Metric Design for Long-Distance Quantum Networks
authors: Thu Trang Nguyen, Yuto Lim, Ruidong Li
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Efficient entanglement routing in the quantum Internet requires balancing multiple link metrics to satisfy diverse application demands. Existing routing schemes primarily focus on fidelity, without explicitly incorporating latency into path selection. This paper proposes QFiLa, a fidelity-latency composite link metric that extends Q-LEAP to jointly optimize both metrics. Simulation results on random network topologies and the Japan Photonic Network Model (JPNM) show that QFiLa effectively captures the fidelity-latency tradeoff while maintaining highly stable throughput, with variations below 1% across weighting configurations. On the JPNM topology, different weight settings enable controllable performance shifts, achieving up to 2-3% reduction in path computation time under fidelity-oriented routing, while preserving overall routing efficiency. These results demonstrate that QFiLa provides flexible and scalable entanglement routing for heterogeneous quantum network scenarios.

title: Mitigation of Retention Failure Based on Conduction Mechanism Reversal in 40nm RRAM
authors: Yuhang Yang, Yingchen Ji, Zongwei Wang, Yimao Cai
abstract: Resistive random-access memory (RRAM) faces a critical retention-reliability challenge, especially for tail-bit failures that limit high-density and automotive-grade applications. While conventional approaches rely on statistical characterizations, they often lack the physical insight needed to address the root cause. In this work, we uncover a previously unreported “conduction-mechanism reversal” in 40-nm TaO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">x</inf>-based RRAM devices: after accelerated baking, tail bits exhibit opposite conduction trends-low-resistance state (LRS) cells become dominated by trap-assisted tunneling (nonlinear), whereas high-resistance state (HRS) cells shift toward Ohmic behavior (linear). This physical understanding enables us to propose a low-overhead, two-step read scheme that exploits the intrinsic nonlinearity difference between LRS and HRS tail bits. Experimental results demonstrate a 90% reduction in bit-error rate while reducing read power by 78%, together satisfying stringent reliability targets with minimal latency and area overhead. The work shifts the paradigm from statistical tail-bit management to physics-aware reliability design, offering a general methodology for emerging memory technologies.

title: In-Place Backup: A Lightweight Strategy for Ondemand Reliability Enhancement in Multi-Bit-Per-Cell Solid-State Drives
authors: Yuhan Wang, Jian Huang, Longbin Chen, Ruibin Zhou
abstract: Different types of data exhibit distinct access patterns and corresponding reliability requirements. However, conventional solid-state drives (SSDs) typically apply uniform and stringent management strategies to all data, regardless of their actual reliability needs. With the increasing storage density in Triple-level Cell (TLC) and Quad-Level Cell (QLC) 3D-NAND flash, the overall reliability of memory cells has degraded, making it increasingly difficult to ensure high reliability across heterogeneous data types. Reliability-aware management strategies, which classify data into multiple tiers and apply differentiated protection mechanisms, have emerged as an effective solution and a focus of recent research. Nevertheless, reducing hardware resource consumption, alleviating controller overhead, and minimizing modifications to existing SSD architectures continue to pose significant challenges. In this study, we propose a lightweight and flexible framework for reliability-aware data management in 3D-NAND SSDs. Leveraging the Gray code property of full sequence programming (FSP), we first introduce a same-WL duplication method to enhance the reliability of critical data. On this basis, we design a reliability enhancement module using a Reliability Enhancement Bit (REB) stored in the out-of-band (OOB) area to support tiered reliability management. This design supports dynamic, fine-grained reliability control based on runtime data characteristics and extensible for SSD lifetime enhancement. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed strategy significantly reduces the raw bit error rate (RBER) caused by read disturb and data retention effects. Compared to conventional Redundant Array of Independent Disks (RAID) methods, the data failure rate is reduced by several orders of magnitude. Moreover, when 50% of the drive's data is subject to enhanced reliability protection, the average write latency increases by less than 4%, and the read latency increases by less than <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2 \%}$</tex> in most cases.

title: Benchmark of Test Structures and Measurements Gaining Most Sensitive PID Well Charging Evaluation Methodology
authors: Andreas Martin, Johannes Berger, Tam Lyn Tan, Elodie Ebrard
abstract: The sensitivity of PID (plasma induced damage) well charging test structures and stress measurements is investigated with two independent test chips and measurement installations. The worst case structural set up is identified. Precisely scaled measurement biases are found to be essential to detect extrinsic gate oxide characteristics, otherwise PID weaknesses are overlooked. Two MOS oxide thicknesses are characterized for dielectric strength and MOS parameter drifts. The findings will introduce a fundamental new approach for measuring PID and designing structures which will be followed up in the JEDEC standard currently developed for a PID well charging assessment.

title: TNO-QA: Tensor-Network Optimized Quantum Attention
authors: Yaswitha Gujju, Romain Harang, Tetsuo Shibuya
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: We propose Tensor-Network Optimized Quantum Attention (TNO-QA), a hybrid quantum-classical framework that reduces the parameter overhead of classical attention mechanisms. TNO-QA combines low-rank tensor parameterization with a Quantum Train module to form a compact yet expressive attention block. In this design, Tensor-Train (TT) cores generate parameters for a fixed Parameterized Quantum Circuit (PQC) within the quantum train, whose outputs are processed by the associated Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) to produce the attention weights. This approach enables fully differentiable optimization in the classical domain, while the quantum circuit operates only in the forward pass, mitigating barren plateaus and quantum noise. A batched parameter generation scheme further reduces qubit requirements logarithmically, enhancing scalability. We integrate TNO-QA into U-Net architectures for diffusion models and evaluate on CIFAR-10, MNIST, and FMNIST. Experiments demonstrate <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$57-65 \%$</tex> reduction in attention parameters while maintaining comparable reconstruction quality and improving Fréchet Inception Distance relative to classical attention. TNO-QA is architecture-agnostic and supports multi-scale attention hierarchies, providing a scalable, stable, and hardware-efficient pathway toward practical quantum-assisted deep learning.

title: Swarm Intelligence-Driven Dung Beetle Optimizer for Accurate Photovoltaic Model Parameter Estimation
authors: Krishnapada Mondal, Md. Abu Salman, Disha Hasan, Md. Akter Hossen, Md. Parvej, Md. Shariful Islam
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Precise determination of PV parameters is vital as it could enhance the performance, efficiency, and reliability of solar energy devices. In this paper, a new swarm intelligencebased metaheuristic technique called Dung Beetle Optimizer (DBO) is proposed for accurate parameter estimation of the PV model. Inspired by the distinct foraging and survival tactics of dung beetles, DBO combines ball-rolling, brood ball, small beetle, and thief modes forging an equitable between exploring and exploiting. This algorithm seeks the elements of the DDM based on Newton-Raphson ideas. Finally, the Wilcoxon signedrank test (WSRT) was utilized to analyze the positive impact of DBO with respect to different two-optimization process. From the statistical results, DBO performs significantly better than those of WOA and HHO with p-values attached to 2.00 × 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-6</sup> and with the most favorable test statistics. Simulation results also indicate that DBO has a better convergence rate, robustness, and lower RMSE than the other methods. In the case of the R.T.C. France solar cell dataset in MATLAB, it had the minimum RMSE for DDM of 7.47 × 1 0<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-4</sup>. These findings indicate that DBO is a valid and efficient modality for the estimation of PV variables. Indeed, they demonstrate that it may enhance the precision and reliability of solar energy simulation.

title: Electromigration Performance and Underlying Mechanism in CoWoS Packaging
authors: David T. Chu, Sherwin Tang, Jui-Shen Chang, Chung-Yu Chiu, Chen-Nan Chiu, M.H. Lin, Jyun-Lin Wu, Yao-Chun Chuang, Ryan Lu
abstract: Electromigration (EM) has become one of the dominant reliability concerns in advanced packaging using interposers. As current demand in high-performance computing continues to rise, the interconnect hierarchy—comprising redistribution layers (RDLs), through-silicon vias (TSVs), microbumps, and C4 bumps—faces increasing current density and thermal stress. This work analyzes EM diffusion behavior, failure mechanisms, and improvement strategies across each interconnect tier within the interposer. Sn-based bumps remain the EM bottleneck, whereas Cu and Al interconnects demonstrate higher endurance. Relevant design strategies are discussed to improve the EM performance.

title: Demonstration of Fundamental Weight Accuracy Limits Using Bi-Directional Tuning of SuperFlash ESF3 Cells for Neuromorphic Computing
authors: Louisa Schneider, Steven Lemke, Parviz Ghazavi, Nhan Do, Yuri Tkachev
abstract: In this study, three analog in-memory compute cell options and two weight tuning algorithms are presented to precisely set the SuperFlash ESF3 split-gate cells to different target levels in the sub-threshold current range (1 to 100 nA). While the program-only algorithm (uni-directional) is subject to possible programming overshoot, the program-erase algorithm (bi-directional) makes it possible to selectively retune the overshot cells back to their target weights. Due to the tighter final distribution, the bi-directional tuning method results in higher post tuning accuracy of analog weight setting. It was demonstrated that each cell option can be set to their target weights within their respective fundamental elementary charge limit. The post tuning error is minimized with program-erase operations combined with the optimized cell floating gate capacitance.

title: Performance and Viability Analysis of Deploying Cloud-native 5G Autoscaling Platforms
authors: Amber Yucebas, Ruoting Xiong, Yi Ren, Xu Zhang, Gerard Parr
publish: Proceedings of the 1st free5GC World Forum 2025
abstract: None

title: A Multi-Criteria Decision Model for Renewable Microgrid Operation Using AHP and Metaheuristic Weight Optimization
authors: Filipe P. Sousa, Augusto M. P. De Mendonça, Igor M. Coelho, Cristiane O. De Faria
abstract: The increasing penetration of renewable-based microgrids introduces trade-offs among economic cost, environmental sustainability, technical reliability, and social performance (proxied by load coverage). Conventional approaches often treat these objectives in isolation, limiting transparency in planning decisions. We propose a hybrid multi-criteria decision-making (MCDM) framework that combines an AHP-inspired hierarchy with objective weighting (Entropy, CRITIC, and Bayesian aggregation) and metaheuristic weight optimization. The framework is evaluated on an open-access dataset from the U.S. Department of Energy, comparing three configurations: dieselonly, PV plus battery, and diesel-PV-battery hybrid. A two-stage procedure refines literature-initialized baseline weights: (i) global exploration with Artificial Bee Colony, Particle Swarm Optimization, Simulated Annealing, and a PSO-SA hybrid; and (ii) neighborhood-based refinement adapted for continuous weight vectors (inspired by I2PLS, MTS, and WILB). Candidate weights are assessed against Entropy, CRITIC, and Bayesian reference distributions and by rank-agreement diagnostics. Four MCDM methods (Fuzzy-TOPSIS, VIKOR, COPRAS, and MOORA) then rank alternatives under economic, environmental, technical, and social criteria, where load coverage (percent load served) proxies energy access and service adequacy. PV plus battery consistently ranks first across all weighting models and ranking methods. Combined global-local strategies achieve minimax regret below $6 \%$ and inter-method Spearman correlation above 0.88. The workflow is reproducible and supports data-driven selection of resilient, low-carbon microgrid configurations.

title: Multi-Objective Collaborative Optimization of Power System Maintenance Scheduling Using a Proximal Policy Optimization Approach
authors: Yue Zhu, Yang Zhang, Rui Liu, Jiutai Jin, Yu Xing
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: High renewable penetration increases volatility and complicates power grid outage scheduling under security and operational constraints. This paper proposes a deep reinforcement learning framework that formulates maintenance scheduling as an MDP and solves it using Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO). A hierarchical reward function balances operational security, supply adequacy, and renewable accommodation, while a security-constraint embedding layer filters unsafe actions to prevent physical limit violations during exploration. Case studies on a modified IEEE 39-bus system show that the proposed method outperforms MILP and genetic algorithms, achieving 35.1 s online inference (97% faster than MILP), zero <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{N}-1$</tex> violations, and 94.8% renewable utilization. Sensitivity analysis identifies a balanced weight setting that delivers strong performance without sacrificing robustness, providing a scalable basis for intelligent outage scheduling in modern power systems.

title: Quantum Dominating Set Problem: An Unbalanced Penalization Analysis
authors: Freddy Alejandro Chaurra-Gutierrez, Claudia Feregrino-Uribe, Guohua Sun
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: The Dominating Set Problem (DSP) is a fundamental, NP-hard, combinatorial problem with applications in logistics, security, and networks. However, quantum solutions to optimization problems face the challenge of limited quantum resources, which require careful design and formulation. Therefore, we present a detailed analysis of the impact of penalty parameters on the performance of the Variational Quantum Eigensolver (VQE) when applied to the DSP using an unbalanced Penalization QUBO formulation. Our analysis shows that these parameters play a decisive role in switching between distinct failure modes infeasibility, feasibility, and near-optimality - rather than being mere tuning elements. Our theoretical and numerical analyses reveal that the range of penalty values enabling a high probability of finding the true optimum is narrow and highly dependent on the specific structure of the graph. These findings suggest that the unbalanced penalty formalism itself poses a fundamental limitation to VQE's ability to solve the DSP. This motivates the future exploration of alternative algorithms, such as QAOA, or more advanced constraint-embedding techniques, for practical quantum optimization.

title: Quantum MIMO Diversity Framework Using Approximate Cloning and Probabilistic Purification
authors: Shehbaz Tariq, Junaid ur Rehman, Symeon Chatzinotas
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Quantum communication serves as a foundational technology for distributed quantum computing and quantum sensing. This work presents a quantum multiple-input multipleoutput (MIMO) diversity framework based on approximate cloning and probabilistic purification for discrete-variable (DV) quantum channels. In the proposed framework, approximate quantum cloning is employed at the transmitter to distribute quantum information across multiple noisy channels, while a probabilistic purification process at the receiver coherently combines the received states to reconstruct a higher-fidelity output. The communication channels are modeled with both depolarizing noise and crosstalk effects using controlled-SWAP operators. To enhance end-to-end fidelity, a semi-definite programmingbased purification strategy is developed, jointly optimizing the cloning asymmetry and the purification map. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed cloning-purification approach achieves superior fidelity compared to best-channel selection and other baseline schemes when full channel state information (CSI) is available, and that symmetric configurations remain robust under limited or no CSI conditions.

title: Explainable Deep Learning Framework for Multi-Species Mushroom Classification Using DenseNet121
authors: Al Arian Ahmad, Md. Abu Johab, Nakib Aman
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Deep learning has shown remarkable potential in fine-grained image classification tasks by significantly improving recognition accuracy while reducing manual effort. However, the black-box nature of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) often limits their interpretability in real-world decision-making scenarios. This study introduces an Explainable AI (XAI)-driven framework that combines high predictive performance with transparent model reasoning for mushroom species classification. Four state-of-the-art CNN architectures—AlexNet, MobileNetV2, ResNet50 and DenseNet121—are evaluated on a curated and augmented mushroom image dataset. Among these, DenseNet121 integrated with XAI techniques pulled off the highest classification accuracy of 99.01%. Model performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score and confusion matrix analysis. To enhance interpretability, Local Interpretable Model-Agnostic Explanations (LIME) and Grad-CAM are employed to provide localized and feature-based explanations of predictions. The integration of XAI not only ensures high classification performance but also offers transparent decision-making, thereby enabling greater trust and reliability in AI-assisted mushroom identification systems.

title: A Plausible Explanation for the P-Metal Phenomenon in the Electromigration Degradation of VLSI Interconnects
authors: Rajarshi Sarkar, Cher Ming Tan, Debraj Banerjee
abstract: Electromigration (EM) remains a critical reliability concern in advanced VLSI interconnects, and copper is now being replaced by tungsten silicide (W-Si, which could be WSi2, W5Si3, or its variation) and ruthenium (Ru). Recent experimental studies have reported EM-induced void formation in W-Si and Ru at locations opposite to those predicted by the conventional electron wind force (EWF), leading to the proposal of hole-dominated transport and the concept of p-metal behavior. However, the physical origin of this phenomenon remains debated. In this work, we present an alternative and physically consistent explanation by systematically evaluating the driving forces governing EM. A multiscale modeling framework is employed, combining density functional theory (DFT) calculations with atomic flux divergence-based (AFD) finite element analysis. The results show that the hydrostatic stress gradient force (SGF) dominates AFD in both W<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">5</inf> Si<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf> and Ru, independent of the magnitude or sign of the effective charge number (Z*). Even when a hypothetical hole wind force is assumed, void formation remains hydrostatic stress-driven.

title: Enhancing Nature-Inspired Metaheuristic Algorithm Using Quantum and Pseudo Randomness-Based Initialization
authors: Krit Klongwithee, Boonyarit Samran, Rajchawit Sarochawikasit, Prapong Prechaprapranwong, Ruchipas Bavontaweepanya, Ekkarat Pongophas
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: The growing complexity of computational tasks presents significant challenges for engineers, underscoring the critical role of optimization techniques. Many optimization strategies are inspired by natural phenomena, including natural selection, neural networks in the human brain, and electron spins in atoms. Initialization schemes in metaheuristic algorithms can improve convergence speed or solution quality when starting near optimal solutions, but they typically depend on synthetic entropy sources rather than natural entropy. This study investigates the impact of different random number generators (RNGs) on metaheuristic algorithms, comparing pseudo RNGs with quantum RNGs, which were developed for this purpose. The research evaluates eight widely used nature-inspired metaheuristic algorithms applied to three optimization problems. Experimental results demonstrate in most cases that quantum RNGs yield superior population quality in the final generation and higher diversity in the initial generation compared to pseudo-RNGs. However, no clear relationship was found between the initial population's diversity and the final generation's global fitness. These findings highlight the potential advantages of quantum RNGs in metaheuristic algorithms while raising questions about the role of initial population diversity in achieving optimal solutions.

title: Quantum Circuit Watermarking: Design and Evaluation of a Quantum Watermarking Framework Using Qiskit
authors: Nathanael Ian Gunadi, Yoshito Tobe
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: We introduce a barrier-free watermarking framework for quantum circuits that embeds authorship as a controlled measurement bias on dedicated ancilla qubits while preserving the functional behavior of the original computation when the ancillas are ignored. Each watermark block prepares an ancilla and couples it once to a selected work qubit using an interaction that preserves the ancilla's measurement statistics, and blocks are placed before measurement to avoid compiler reordering across readout. Because the watermark is defined by a statistical property rather than a specific gate pattern, it is designed to remain verifiable after standard transpilation and hardware mapping. We provide a Qiskit implementation and an evaluation protocol that measures watermark detectability, functional impact on the circuit output distribution, and performance overhead. Experiments on RevLib benchmarks, simulator backends, and IBM quantum hardware show that the watermark can be detected reliably while introducing only modest degradation under typical NISQ noise levels, with the tradeoff becoming more pronounced as circuit depth increases.

title: Enhancing Bone Fracture Identification with YOLOv11 and Robust K-Fold Cross-Validation
authors: Akash Roy, Md. Istiak Hasan, Mst. Shikha Moni, Aminun Nahar, Shahnaj Parvin
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Bones are essential for the body's structure and function, but the frequency of fractures is steadily rising due to accidents. Since manual inspection is often time-consuming and prone to human error. To address this challenge, we propose a deep learning based framework for fracture detection and classification using improved YOLOv11, which offers real-time detection capabilities and enhanced efficiency to support radiologists in clinical practice. We utilized the Human Bone Fracture Multi-Modal Image Dataset (HBFMID), consisting of 641 raw images (510 X-rays and 131 MRIs). To minimize model bias, we employed K-fold cross-validation. The experimental results demonstrate that the model achieves 97.2% mAP50, and a 95.1% F1-score after cross-validation. Furthermore, we conducted a comparative performance analysis of improved YOLOv11 against other versions, including YOLOv8, YOLOv9, YOLOv10, and YOLOv12, to validate the robustness of the proposed framework. Furthermore, we compared the proposed study with existing studies, demonstrating that the proposed approach outperforms prior methods.

title: Quantum-Enhanced LSTM for Sequential Network Flow Analysis: A Hybrid Approach to DDoS Detection
authors: Adam Kadi, Hajar Moudoud, Lyes Khoukhi, Zakaria Abou El Houda
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS) attacks continue to grow at a rapid rate, making timely and reliable detection a challenge for intrusion detection systems (IDSs). Classical deep models such as Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) can capture temporal dependencies; however, they still struggle with highly nonlinear and noisy flow dynamics, especially when attack patterns shift. To address this, we propose QLSTM-E, an enhanced hybrid quantum-classical architecture for network intrusion detection that uses Variational Quantum Circuits (VQCs) and LSTM modeling to learn complex temporal dependencies in network traffic. QLSTM-E exploits quantum characteristics, including superposition and entanglement, to improve the representation of nonlinear patterns in sequential flow features. To balance expressivity and circuit cost on near-term devices, we adopt an angle-based encoding strategy and a star-topology entangling layout that reduces two-qubit gate overhead while preserving effective quantum correlations. We implement QLSTM-E using PennyLane and Qiskit and evaluate it on the CIC-DDoS2019 dataset under a fully simulated setting, requiring no access to quantum hardware. Experimental results demonstrate strong detection effectiveness, achieving 99.7% accuracy and F1-score, and show strong robustness under depolarizing noise up to p= 0.1.

title: TOM-YOLO: Tomato Leaf Disease Detection Using Enhanced YOLOv12 Framework
authors: Aminun Nahar, Mahbubul Islam, Mehedi Hasan Shuvo, Kamruddin Nur
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Tomato leaf diseases significantly impact agricultural productivity, making early and accurate detection essential for sustainable crop management. In this work, we propose TOM-YOLO, a robust and high-accuracy framework built upon YOLOv12, designed to address the challenges of tomato leaf disease detection under complex field conditions. The proposed TOM-YOLO enhances YOLOv12 for tomato leaf disease detection by replacing standard convolutions with GhostConv layers at positions 3, 15, and 19 and incorporating A2C2f blocks to improve feature representation and detection accuracy. An SPPF module at layer 9 captures multi-scale context for improved detection of small and large lesions, while C2f modules at layers 2, 6, and 21 in the backbone and neck extract fine-grained lesion patterns with attention. Finally, a custom detection head is designed to classify nine tomato leaf diseases into nine categories. Experimental results demonstrate that TOM-YOLO achieves superior performance compared to the original YOLOv12, with precision, recall, F1-score, mAP@50, and mAP@50-95 reaching 87.5%, 81.1%, 84.0%, 90.4%, and 77.9%, respectively.

title: Movable Antenna-Enhanced RIS-Assisted Over-the-Air Computation
authors: Sun Mao, Chau Yuen, Lei Liu, Jingwen Li, Yuanwei Liu, Kun Yang, Derrick Wing Kwan Ng
abstract: Movable antennas (MAs) and reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (RISs) have emerged as two promising technologies for enhancing wireless communication performance, owing to their capability to dynamically reshape and manipulate the propagation environment. Motivated by this potential, this paper investigates the joint utilization of the additional degrees of freedom introduced by MAs (through antenna repositioning) and RIS (via optimized reflection) to effectively mitigate computation distortion in over-the-air computation (AirComp) systems. Specifically, we formulate an optimization problem aimed at minimizing the mean square error (MSE) between the target function values and their estimates, through jointly optimizing the receive beamformer at the access point, RIS reflection phase shifts, and transmit coefficients as well as antenna positions of AirComp users. To address the non-convex nature of the formulated problem, we develop a computationally efficient algorithm capitalizing alternating optimization technique, the penalty-dual decomposition method, and the particle swarm optimization enhanced by a dynamic neighborhood pruning mechanism. Next, we further extend the optimization framework to a more practical case with discrete MA positions. Extensive simulation results demonstrate that the joint optimization of RIS beamforming and MA positioning substantially reduces the computation MSE, compared to the separate MA-enhanced AirComp and RIS-aided AirComp schemes. Moreover, the proposed algorithm achieves comparable performance to the penalty function-based method, while incurring significantly lower computational complexity.

title: Channel Material and Inner Spacer Impact on Gate Stack TDDB of Gate-All-Around Nanosheet Transistors
authors: Huimei Zhou, Shogo Mochizuki, Xiaoli He, Nicolas Loubet, Steven Hung, Jody Fronheiser, Lisa McGill, Benjamin Colombeau
abstract: In this paper, we present a detailed study of Time dependent dielectric breakdown (TDDB) reliability on n - and P-type stacked Gate-All-Around (GAA) Nanosheet (NS) transistors with (w/) Si channel and SiGe channel without(w/o) Inner Spacer (IS), then compared with Si channel GAA NS transistors w/ IS. Our results show robust TDDB reliability across all Design of Experiments (DOEs), with P-type transistors having a narrower TDDB margin compared to N-type transistors.

title: Reliability Assessment Methods for Medium- and Low-Voltage Distribution Networks Considering Distributed Energy Resources and Electric Vehicles
authors: Jiajun Zhang, Haifeng Zhang, Jingying Yang, Runzi Lin, Tao Liu, Yutong Liu
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: The uncertainty in output from distributed energy resources and electric vehicles connected to distribution grids significantly impacts the accuracy of reliability assessments for medium- and low-voltage distribution systems. To accurately evaluate the power supply reliability level of such grids, this paper proposes a reliability assessment method for medium- and lowvoltage distribution networks that incorporates distributed generation and electric vehicles. First, output models are established for wind power, photovoltaic systems, and electric vehicle energy storage systems. Subsequently, a reliability assessment workflow incorporating distributed energy sources and electric vehicles is constructed using sequential Monte Carlo simulation. Finally, the IEEE-RBTS Bus6 system is employed to simulate and analyze reliability variations under different integration scenarios. Results indicate that coordinated integration of wind and solar power enhances distribution network reliability, whereas disorderly integration of large-scale electric vehicles significantly degrades system supply reliability. The proposed method provides theoretical support for planning and operating distribution networks incorporating high proportions of clean energy and electric vehicles.

title: UVFusion: A Unified Framework for Voxel-Aligned LiDAR-Camera Fusion in 3D Object Detection
authors: Ajian Liu, Jun Wang, Juan Chen, Ziyang Meng
abstract: Accurate three-dimensional (3D) object detection for autonomous driving often relies on the fusion of Light Detection and Ranging (LiDAR) and camera data. However, existing multimodal detectors still suffer from two key limitations: inaccurate cross-modal feature alignment and coarse-grained region-of-interest (RoI) fusion. These limitations are particularly severe for small or distant objects. In addition, most existing methods address feature alignment and RoI fusion separately rather than in a unified framework. This separate treatment makes it difficult to fully exploit their combined benefits for detection performance. To address these problems, we propose UVFusion, a unified framework for voxel-aligned multimodal fusion that integrates image enhancement, cross-modal alignment, and 3D RoI fusion. First, an Adaptive Gated Fusion (AGF) module enhances the image branch with LiDAR-derived geometric priors to generate depth-aware features. Second, a Deformable Cross-Modal Attention (DCMA) module establishes precise correspondences between multi-scale voxel and image features via centroid-guided deformable sampling. Finally, a Grid-level Self-Attention Fusion (GSAF) module performs RoI-wise self-attentive fusion of geometry-enhanced multimodal features, yielding more discriminative 3D representations for proposal refinement. Experiments on the KITTI benchmark show that UVFusion consistently outperforms the Voxel-RCNN baseline, with a 3.06% improvement in 3D average precision (AP) for cars at the hard level, along with significant gains for pedestrians and cyclists. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method in detecting small-scale objects in challenging urban scenes.

title: Physics-Informed Autoencoder for Radiation Source Reconstruction via Phaseless Single-Plane Near-Field Scanning
authors: Ruo-Yu Liang, Tian-Qi Wang, Xu-Dong Han, Ze-Ming Wu, Xiao-Chun Li, Jun-Fa Mao
abstract: An autoencoder (AE) is a classic machine learning (ML) method that has been widely applied to feature extraction in natural language processing and computer vision. In this work, a physics-informed AE (PIAE) is proposed for efficient and flexible radiation source reconstruction (RSR). The proposed PIAE has an encoder-decoder pair, in which the encoder converts measured near-field data into equivalent dipole moments and the decoder reconstructs the near field from the dipole moments. The encoder consists of a convolutional neural network, which reduces the dimensionality of the input field data by extracting key features, and a multilayer perceptron, which maps the reduced-dimensionality features to the dipole moments. The decoder analytically calculates the dipole radiation fields from the dipole moments. Finally, the proposed PIAE is trained by minimizing the loss between measured and reconstructed fields. Compared with conventional ML-based RSR methods, which usually need dual-plane phaseless data, the proposed PIAE only requires single-plane phaseless data, which significantly reduces measurement time. Moreover, the proposed PIAE directly outputs equivalent dipoles, enabling seamless integration into electromagnetic interference prediction workflows. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed PIAE has both high accuracy and efficiency in RSR.

title: Problem-Specific Knowledge and Learning-Based Meta-Heuristics for On-Demand Food Delivery Scheduling
authors: Yaxian Ren, Kaizhou Gao, Zhongjie Lin, Naiqi Wu, Ling Wang, Liang Zhao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: With the increase of orders and riders, how to allocate orders to riders appropriately and complete delivery timely are huge challenges in on-demand food delivery (OFD). This work focuses on the multi-objective on-demand food delivery scheduling problems (OFDSP) with minimizing the total delay time of all orders and the total traveling distance of all riders simultaneously. First, both order-based mathematical model and a location-based mathematical model are established for the concerned problems, respectively Then, three basic algorithms (differential evolution (DE), particle swarm optimization (PSO), and artificial bee colony (ABC)) are improved to solve OFDSP. Combining order and rider information, a double string encoding method is designed to represent candidate solutions. A multi-constraint satisfying initialization strategy is proposed to avoid infeasible solutions. Seven problem-specific knowledge-based local search operators (KLSOs) are developed to speed up the convergence of the algorithms. Third, two reinforcement learning (RL) algorithms with two Q-table design strategies are constructed, yielding a total of four RL-based strategies for local search operator selection during iterations. Finally, 30 instances from real scenarios are solved to verify the performance of the proposed algorithms. The two developed mathematical models are verified and compared. The effectiveness of the improvement strategies is validated in the respective algorithm groups. Furthermore, the algorithms with the best performance in the respective groups are compared to the two advanced OFD algorithms. The results and comparisons show that the ABC_LS_Q1 is most competitive among all compared algorithms.

title: A Cross-Modal Consistency-Driven Quality-Aware Dynamic Fusion Framework for Emergency Damage Assessment
authors: Xinlei Hou, Zhenguo Chen, Yunfeng Shang
abstract: Emergency-oriented damage assessment is frequently challenged by multimodal heterogeneity, noise contamination, and modality missingness, which may lead to unstable fusion behavior and reduced prediction reliability during inference. To address these issues, this study proposes a tri-modal fusion framework that integrates Cross-Reconstruction Learning (CRL) and Dynamic Integration Fusion (DIF). Dedicated visual, textual, and audio encoders first map heterogeneous inputs into a shared semantic space. Cross-modal reconstruction is then used to model semantic consistency, and the resulting reconstruction errors are converted into sample-level quality cues. On this basis, a gating network jointly exploits modality confidence, logit margin, and CRL-related error information to adaptively regulate modality contributions and generate fusion weights at the sample level. Experimental results under complete-modality, missing-modality, and multi-level visual-degradation settings show that the proposed method achieves favorable performance in terms of Accuracy, Macro-F1, and prediction reliability, while mitigating the adverse influence of low-quality modalities on fusion decisions. Ablation and visualization analyses further indicate that the observed gains mainly arise from explicit modeling of cross-modal consistency and modality-quality differences, rather than from stronger unimodal representations alone. These findings support the proposed quality-aware fusion mechanism within a controlled weakly aligned pseudo-paired tri-modal testbed, rather than constituting full validation for physically synchronized tri-modal emergency sensing.

title: Microwave Broadband Circularly Polarized and Millimeter Wave MIMO Co-Aperture Antenna
authors: Hui Tang, Ruiping Liang, Li Jiang, Ding-Wen Shen, Jiawei Zhu, Jian-Xin Chen
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: This letter proposes a co-aperture design of a broadband microwave (MW) circularly polarized (CP) antenna and millimeter-wave (MMW) multiple input multiple output (MIMO) antenna. A crossed dipole is utilized as the microwave CP radiator, and a hybrid perturbation scheme using rectangular and chamfered parasitic patches is introduced to generate multiple well-spaced axial ratio (AR) nulls, thus effectively broadening the AR bandwidth. The parasitic patches are co-designed as MMW radiating elements, where dual-slot coupling excites dual modes to extend the bandwidth and the orthogonal arrangement of the elements suppresses mutual coupling. In the co-aperture design, an impedance bandwidth of 64.8% (4.28–8.38 GHz) and an AR bandwidth of 49.5% (4.28–7.1 GHz) with a peak gain of 7.4 dBic for MW CP segment, and a 6.3% impedance bandwidth (27.71–29.52 GHz) with a gain of 8.1 dBi for MMW part are obtain. The combination of spatial and polarization diversity provides the MMW MIMO part with an envelope correlation coefficient (ECC) below 0.02, meeting the high-throughput requirement. With the dual-functional patches, this design shows compact size, broadband performance, and high compatibility for wireless communication systems.

title: Comprehensive Study of Single Event Upset in TSMC N3E Logic Technology
authors: Yueh Chiang, Chun-Fu Chen, Pi-Chia Shih, Wei-Chiang Pan, Sheng-Chih Chuang, Yuan-Pin Huang
abstract: This work presents a comprehensive study on single event upset (SEU) in the TSMC N3E logic technology process, utilizing an in-house test chip to investigate the impacts of applied voltage, threshold voltage options, hardened circuit designs, deep-N-Well integration, and test facility beam quality. Our findings reveal that applied voltage and threshold voltage options can be synergistically combined into a novel metric, “Voltage Headroom,” which significantly improves the predictability of failure rates based on specific application requirements, and tests with quasimonoenergetic neutrons and protons demonstrated comparable error rates, thereby allowing users to choose facilities based on availability. Based on these insights, we provide specific guidelines for TSMC N3E users targeting data center applications, recommending lower threshold voltage options with higher applied voltages, highlighting that deep-N-Well designs significantly enhance SEU robustness in low voltage headroom applications, and emphasizing that hardened circuit designs remarkably improve robustness by over two orders of magnitude.

title: A Device-to-Circuit Methodology for HCI-Induced Variability Study Featuring High Efficiency Test Array Incorparated with Physical Model
authors: Junru Qu, Zhen Xu, Dong Liu, Keyang Zhu, Jiabao Ye, Zhikai Li, Jiajie Gao, Xiao Yu, Dawei Gao, Yong Ding, Ran Cheng, Bing Chen
abstract: This work presents a unified framework for large-scale characterization and statistical modeling of hot-carrier-induced (HCI) variability in 55 nm CMOS technology. A 16 × 16 nMOSFET testing array enables high-throughput HCI evaluation, while a compact virtual-source model (VSM) is employed to extract and analyze key transport parameters. The study identifies the HCI-induced traps are the dominant physical mechanisms responsible for aggravated parameter variation after stress. The degradation statistics of multiple parameters can be well described by Exponential-Poisson distribution, and strong inter-parameter correlations are identified and modeled. A variation-aware aging model incorporating parameter correlation is development and implemented in Verilog-A for circuit-level reliability simulation. The proposed framework provides a systematic and efficient approach for investigating HCI-induced variability and supports reliability-aware circuit design.

title: Design of Natural Gas Pressure Difference Power Generation System and its Grid-Connection Control Strategy Under Variable Operating Conditions
authors: Jingdong Zhu, Xuebo Li, Zhiqiang Yan, Yixin Zhang, Lin Jiang, Pengcheng Zhao
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: To address the challenges of power fluctuations and electricity quality encountered during natural gas pressure energy recovery under variable operating conditions, a dynamically coordinated control system for grid-connected expansion power generation has been designed. A mechanically coupled model comprising a ‘turbine expander-permanent magnet synchronous generator-dual PWM converter’ has been established, with corresponding coordinated control algorithms developed. The machine-side rectifier employs dual-closed-loop speed-current vector control to maintain constant generator speed during torque fluctuations in the expander, thereby stabilizing generation frequency at source. The grid-side inverter utilizes dual-closedloop voltage-current control integrated with phase-locked loop technology. This simultaneously stabilizes DC bus voltage while synchronizing grid-connected current with grid voltage and achieving unity power factor operation, thus ensuring gridconnected power quality. Simulation results demonstrate that under multiple disturbances such as inlet flow and pressure variations, the generator speed deviation remains below 2%, DC voltage fluctuation is less than 5%, and the total harmonic distortion (THD) of the grid-connected current is under 5%.
