title: Qiamese: Quantum-Hybrid Siamese Network for Predicting Drug-Protein Interaction
authors: Soham Pawar, Siddharth Ayathu, Naganand Y
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Accurate prediction of drug-protein interactions (DPIs) is a pivotal step in drug discovery. Classical deep learning models like BridgeDPI [3] have shown strong performance by using Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) with hyper-nodes to effectively learn protein-protein and drug-drug associations (PPAs and DDAs). Siamese-based models such as BioAct-Het use a heterogeneous siamese neural network to model the complex relationship between drugs and bioactivity classes, bringing them into a unified latent space. The final bioactivity class label is decided based on the distance between the final embeddings obtained from the classical twin networks. In our work, we propose a parameter-efficient QuantumHybrid Siamese model where the protein and drug embeddings, generated by the classical encoders are processed on by the Quantum Siamese circuit, much like the classical Siamese model. Using a switch qubit, helps us implement the idea of weight sharing across the twin instances for the pair of inputs, but by using only a single instance of the VQC block. We focus on using our proposed circuit with the classical protein and drug encoders proposed in the BridgeDPI model. Our experiments include testing the model variations on the unseen drugprotein split of the BindingDB dataset as suggested by Gao et. al. from https://github.com/IBM/InterpretableDTIP [3]. Upon experimenting with different variations of the classical encoders and the quantum circuits, we achieved the best performing hybrid model (10 MB) with performance metrics comparable to the best performing classical model <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(40 \text{MB})$</tex> along with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$4 x$</tex> size reduction and 540 trainable quantum parameters. The code for this work can be found at https://github.com/SoardRaspi/Qiamese.

title: EOS to ILD Fix: Uncovering the Hidden Culprit in High-Voltage IC Failures
authors: Vikash Kumar, Carl Iwashita
abstract: An unexpected surge in field failures of a high-voltage power IC in customer systems prompted a deep engineering investigation. Initial assumptions pointed to application-level EOS issues, but persistent analysis led to a breakthrough finding: insufficient inter-layer dielectric (ILD) thickness. Through HTOL replication, the root cause was confirmed. A predictive risk model was built using a SQRT E-based approach, and a robust process fix was qualified through a targeted DOE. This paper highlights how mechanism-driven analysis, risk modeling, and a cross-functional engineering journey converged to resolve a complex engineering challenge.

title: A Web-Based Diagnostic and Advisory Platform for Potato Leaf Diseases Using Transformer Based and NLP Chatbot
authors: Badol Shikdar Rifat, Mehedi Hasan, Puja Dey, Faisal Imran, Rakibul Haque
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Potato is a highly valued crop in Bangladesh, whose yield is regularly under threat from a variety of leaf diseases that farmers frequently are not in a position to accurately diagnose and manage effectively. This study addresses potato leaf disease diagnosis challenges in Bangladesh by developing an integrated web platform that combines deep learning-based image classification with natural language processing for farmer support. This study proposed a ConvNeXt transfer learning model to classify potato leaf diseases across seven categories (Bacteria, Fungi, Healthy, Nematode, Pest, Phytophthora, Virus) with high accuracy, while a Bangla-language chatbot powered by DeepSeek API provides farmers with detailed disease explanations, preventive measures, and treatment advice in a conversational format. This study bridges the technology gap in rural agriculture by offering real-time disease detection and actionable recommendations in the local language, potentially serving as a scalable model for AI-based plant disease identification across different crops and regions.

title: Multi-Parameter Characteristics and Rapid Detection Method of Arc Discharge in Oil Inside Bushings
authors: Jun Liu, Kun Li, Peilong Chen, Shengjun Fu, Kejie Huang, Yunfei Jia
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: This study established a multi-parameter experimental platform for bushing systems to analyze the dynamic characteristics of high-current arc faults inside transformer bushings and their multi-physical detection methods. Experimental results show that during arc discharge, the current follows a power-frequency exponentially decaying waveform, while the voltage exhibits spike characteristics at zero-crossing points due to insulation recovery and reignition. The pressure wave evolution can be divided into three stages: shock wave, oscillation decay, and bubble pulsation, revealing the complex process of arc energy conversion into pressure. Acoustic signals show explosive spikes and oscillatory decay in the time domain, with frequency domain energy concentrated in <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0-20 \text{kHz}$</tex>, synchronizing with pressure waves. Based on these characteristics, the study compares three detection methods: shock wave peak value of pressure signals, transient features of acoustic signals in the time domain, and frequency domain energy distribution, providing a theoretical basis for rapid fault diagnosis and protective design of bushings.

title: Responsible Entanglement Revocation Protocol for Quantum Networks
authors: Agi Prasetiadi, Masahiro Mambo
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Long-distance quantum networks lack a secure method to revoke entanglement once a route becomes suspicious. While prior work detects routing anomalies, no mechanism exists for accountable channel termination in a quantum network. This paper introduces Revocable Teleportation together with Responsible Entanglement Revocation Protocol, enabling fair and verifiable entanglement revocation using signature qubits and timestamped metadata. Both pre- and post-teleportation cases are supported, including recovery of valuable teleported states. Responsible Entanglement Revocation Protocol offers the first operational framework for secure and accountable entanglement cancellation in untrusted quantum networks.

title: Tube-Based Model Predictive Control for Robust Current Sharing in Interleaved DC-DC Converters
authors: Junkai Ding, Bin Liu
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: Interleaved DC-DC converters are commonly employed in applications that demand high power density and efficiency. However, these converters are susceptible to current imbalance caused by factors such as component tolerances, parasitic parameter mismatches, asymmetric layout, and uneven temperature distribution. Lack of current-sharing measures subjects a single phase to excessive electrical and thermal stress. This degrades the output voltage accuracy and shorten the lifespan of the components. Prolonged excessive current may trigger erroneous protection circuit activation. To address this problem, this paper introduces a Tube-based Model Predictive Control (MPC) method for robust current-sharing control in interleaved parallel boost converters. The Tube-based MPC employs a two-degree-of-freedom architecture: the nominal MPC utilizes an ideal converter model to compute an optimal reference trajectory for the inductor currents, designed to simultaneously stabilize the output voltage and balance phase currents. The auxiliary MPC controller generates a compensatory control signal based on real-time error between actual inductor currents and the nominal trajectory. This ensures that the phase currents remain tightly constrained within the “tube” around the nominal trajectory, thereby rejecting disturbances and improving robustness. Simulation results show that the proposed method achieves balanced current sharing, improves dynamic performance, and enhances disturbance rejection.

title: Integrated Path Planning of MASS Navigation in Non-Standard Maritime Encounters
authors: Heigo Mõlder, Karl Janson, Jüri Vain, Kristjan Tabri, Pentti Kujala, Aqsa Yaseen
abstract: This paper proposes an integrated path planning and navigation framework for Maritime Autonomous Surface Ships (MASS) operating in non-standard maritime encounters, where conventional COLREG-compliant solutions are insufficient. The approach combines sampling-based global path generation with a real-time local planner capable of handling dynamic, and ambiguous multi-vessel interactions. The framework incorporates risk estimation by constraintbased rule verification to enhance safety in uncertain encounters. Results from simulation scenarios demonstrate that the integrated method improves robustness against trajectory uncertainty, resolves atypical encounter geometries, and maintains safe passing distances under dynamic conditions. The approach is designed to support real-time implementations on next-generation autonomous robot vessels.

title: Field and Thermal Failure Transition Defining the Safe Operating Area in AlGaN/GaN MIS-HEMTs on Different Substrates
authors: Mohammad Ateeb Munshi, Mehak Ashraf Mir, Mayank Shrivastava
abstract: This work compares the safe operating area (SOA) characteristics of AlGaN/GaN MIS-HEMTs on silicon and SiC substrates under three critical operating conditions: off-state, semi-on state, and ON-state. The study reveals that GaN-on-Si HEMTs exhibit a higher breakdown voltage in the off-state compared to GaN-on-SiC devices due to trap-assisted reduction of the channel electric field. However, owing to significant self-heating, GaN-on-Si devices show a large drop in breakdown voltage as the bias regime transitions from the off-state to the ON-state. In contrast, GaN-on-SiC devices demonstrate superior ON-state robustness because of their enhanced thermal conductivity, and their breakdown voltage shows a smaller change from the off-state to the ON-state. semi-on failure is governed by the coupled effects of the channel electric field and self-heating, resulting in comparable failure behavior for both substrates. These in-sights provide new physics-based guidelines for optimizing the electrothermal design window and improving the SOA of GaN power devices.

title: Short-Term Photovoltaic Power Combined Forecasting Based on VMD-SSA-LSTM Under Meteorological-Load Coupling
authors: Xiaojie Wang
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: Short-term photovoltaic (PV) power generation exhibits significant volatility, posing challenges for accurate forecasting. To address this, we propose a hybrid forecasting framework integrating Variational Mode Decomposition (VMD), the Sparrow Search Algorithm (SSA), and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks. Using the PVODdatasets_v1.0, we first performed comprehensive data preprocessing, including outlier removal, missing value imputation, and normalization. VMD was then employed to decompose the non-stationary PV and meteorological sequences into several stable sub-modes, effectively mitigating noise interference and mode mixing. Simultaneously, a correlation analysis was conducted to select highly relevant weather and load features. To optimize prediction performance, SSA was utilized to autonomously determine the hyperparameters of the LSTM network. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed VMD-SSA-LSTM model consistently outperforms baseline LSTM and VMD-LSTM models across multiple metrics, including MAE, RMSE, and MAPE, offering superior accuracy and robustness.

title: A Two-Dimensional Cellular Automata-Based Lightweight Cryptographic Framework for IoT Application
authors: Anik Kumer Das, M. M. A. Hashem
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: In this age of pervasive computing, IoT devices are intelligent but continue to be resource-constrained in terms of their computational power, memory, communication, and bandwidth. Implementing strong security in this resource-constrained environment is quite a challenge because conventional cryptographic algorithms demand high complexity and resource consumption, which is unsuitable for IoT. This paper proposes a lightweight cryptographic system based on two-dimensional (2D) cellular automata (CA) for IoT devices. The suggested system employs linear and nonlinear CA rules under periodic boundary conditions with omega-flip permutations and substitution boxes (S-box) to facilitate diffusion, confusion, and key randomization. The encrypting transformation employs nonlinear CA transformations on key and plaintext, and linear CA rules, XOR operations, and layered S-box substitutions through a series of rounds. The decryption follows the opposite steps for exact recovery of the plaintext. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed framework outperforms conventional block ciphers, such as DES and AES, in terms of encryption and decryption speed, while requiring very low computational resources. Furthermore, the framework satisfies the randomness standards of NIST and passes all the DIEHARD statistical tests. Through its trade-off between lightweight design and strong security mechanisms, the proposed system is readily applicable to resource-limited IoT devices and can be scaled to more sophisticated applications.

title: Transistor Reliability in 18A RibbonFET Technology
authors: Steven Novak, Anupama Bowonder, David Towner, Dyan Ali, Ferando Lugo, Harshad Surdi, Jeff Hicks, Kihyun Choi, Kaustubh Joshi, Mikael Broas, Pradyumna Muralidharan, Rahul Pandey Rahul Ramamurthy, Subhadeep Mukhopadhyay, Yasin Kaya, Inanc Meric
abstract: This paper describes the front end (FE) transistor reliability for the Intel 18A process technology. In this work we discuss the reliability of RibbonFET transistors with comparisons of Bias Temperature Instability (BTI), Hot Carrier (HC) and Gate Oxide (GOX) Breakdown. It is shown that PowerVia has no negative impact on transistor reliability. We discuss how GAA transistors can be impacted by different sources of process induced mechanical strain and show that Intel 18A has optimized strain on its RibbonFET transistors to improve NBTI. We compare BTI and HC to prior technology nodes and show matched aging for AC ring oscillator stresses at improved performance for the highest performance devices. Finally, we show that variation in RibbonFET BTI is well described by defect-centric models.

title: Research on Overvoltage Reconstruction Based on Transfer Characteristics of Voltage Transformer
authors: Kaijun Du, Zhongchao Xue, Jin Yu, Qingfeng Wang
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: This paper presents an overvoltage inversion algorithm for on-board voltage transformers installed on electric multiple units (EMUs). This method measures the transfer characteristics of the voltage transformer and the time-domain signal of the secondary side voltage, multiplies them after Fourier transform to obtain the overvoltage frequency-domain signal, and then performs inverse Fourier transform to finally obtain the overvoltage time-domain signal. Experimental results show that this inversion algorithm effectively improves the reconstruction accuracy of the overvoltage waveform on the primary side compared with the traditional monitoring method, making the inversion result closer to the real overvoltage waveform, and reducing the peak-to-peak error from 83.23% to 5.87%.

title: Security analysis of Precision Time Protocol v2.1 using TLA+
authors: Antoine Lemay, Hugo Genesse
abstract: Modern power grid control technologies, such as synchrophasors, rely on precise time synchronization between machines for accurate control decisions. This is typically achieved through the Precision Time Protocol (PTP). The 2019 version of the protocol, PTPv2.1, includes an Authentication TLV that provides some integrity guarantees, but researchers have found design flaws, such as delay attacks or the ability to tamper with correction fields, that compromise the integrity of the time reference distribution.This paper describes a temporal logic model, built in TLA+, which can be used to analyze the security of PTPv2.1 with regards to both integrity and availability requirements. The different clocks are modeled as state machines, capabilities are modeled as state transitions, integrity requirements as modeled as invariants and availability requirements are modeled as temporal properties. If the execution of the model does not satisfy the temporal property where clocks are always eventually synchronized or fail to maintain the integrity invariants, a security problem has been found.Using the model, we confirm the findings from the other researchers but also identify the possibility of a replay attack which was not discussed in the literature previously, even through the replay protection mechanism provided by PTPv2.1. From the modeling, this new attack scenario is created if the sequence counter is reset after a clock loses its time source and a malicious clock replays messages from an authenticated time source and represents a new contribution to the security of PTP.

authors: Pengqi Lu, Zhengren Yan, Kaifeng Zhu, Jie Wang, Jia Hou, Yuyan Chu, Dinggui Zeng, Shikun He
abstract: Magnetoresistive random-access memory (MRAM) is a leading next-generation nonvolatile memory technology. However, its susceptibility to external magnetic fields impedes its adoption in applications such as wearable electronics, automotive, and security-related systems. In this work, we established a comprehensive framework for analyzing external magnetic field-induced failures. Data retention (DR), write error rate (WER), read error rate (RER) and read disturb rate (RDR) are systematically investigated using mechanism-based models as well as silicon validation. Leveraging high-endurance MRAM technology <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(>10^{12}$</tex> cycles), we present customized magnetic shielding solutions that deliver optimized performance metrics, seamless compatibility with standard packaging, and cost-efficient manufacturability. The innovative Bridge-on-Plate shielding architecture achieves industry-leading immunity levels of 360 mT during data retention phases and 63 mT during write operations, setting new benchmarks for magnetic immunity. These advancements facilitate MRAM deployment in the most demanding automotive environments.

title: Cognitive digital thread supporting agile reuse of knowledge in model-based systems engineering
authors: Jiaxing Qiao, Guoxin Wang, Shouxuan Wu, Jinzhi Lu, Haoxuan Zhang, Jianyu Huang
abstract: With the increasing complexity of systems, ModelBased Systems Engineering (MBSE) has become the mainstream approach for managing structured information through digital models and enabling automated verification. However, enterprises have accumulated substantial engineering documents over decades, which preserve valuable design knowledge and domain expertise. In the context of digital engineering, digital artifacts such as documents and digital models constitute complementary elements that will coexist throughout the industry’s digital transformation. Transforming existing documents into MBSE models therefore represents a sustained capability for minimizing MBSE adoption cost while preserving organizational knowledge assets. Yet, automated document to model transformation faces significant challenges due to incompatible tool formats, diverse document structures, and knowledge scattered across unstructured content. This paper presents an agile knowledge reuse framework based on the Cognitive Digital Thread (CDT) concept. The framework employs Open Service for Lifecycle Collaboration (OSLC) compliant adapters for documents and modeling tools to unify content and operations as service interfaces. Mapping rules derived from document structures guide a rule engine to automatically extract document knowledge and generate system architecture models. A case study of landing gear system design demonstrates that the generated models remain consistent with their source documents, providing a practical solution for agile MBSE transformation based on existing knowledge assets.

title: Avoiding the Knowledge-Graph-on-Hot-Path Anti-Pattern: A Separation-of-Truth Architecture for Real-Time COP in Battlefield Simulation
authors: Huy Liem Nguyen, Truong Son Thai, Hiroshi Sato, Masao Kubo, Bao The Phung, Quang Dung Tran
abstract: Real-time decision support in system-of-systems operations depends on a continuously updated Common Operational Picture (COP) that remains responsive, deterministic, and auditable under stochastic and heterogeneous inputs. In Battlefield Management Simulation Systems (BMSS), we structure the pipeline as a five-layer semantic architecture (input ingestion, knowledge, simulation, AI analysis, and visualization/human-in-the-loop), where an ontology-backed knowledge graph (KG) provides a shared semantic contract for scenario authoring, capability constraints, and provenance-aware explanation. However, placing KG persistence or reasoning on the COP hot path amplifies tail latency and destabilizes tick execution. We propose Separation-of-Truth (SoT-split), which decouples real-time COP state serving from semantic truth: the simulation loop reads/writes a low-latency COP state store, appends immutable events to an event log, and materializes KG views asynchronously for postrun audit and analytics. We validate logical outcome stability via a $\mathbf{5 0 0}$-run Monte Carlo campaign on the Forest Assault scenario, where Blue achieves 31.2% wins $(156 / 500)$ with a 95% Wilson confidence interval (CI) of [$27.3 \%, 35.4 \%$]. To quantify the KG-on-hot-path anti-pattern, we run a controlled microbenchmark that emulates inline semantic persistence and scan operations; at $\mathbf{4} \times$ workload scale, SoT-split reduces perturn p95 latency from 0.564 ms to $0.110 \mathrm{~ms}(5.1 \times)$ and p99 from 0.700 ms to $0.149 \mathrm{~ms}(4.7 \times)$, while increasing throughput from $\mathbf{2, 1 7 5}$ to $\mathbf{1 0, 7 2 4}$ turns/s. These results indicate that SoT-split preserves real-time COP responsiveness while retaining semantic rigor and replayable auditability.

title: Scalable High-Voltage Pass Transistor Strustures: Physical-based and Variation-Aware Evaluation Toward 1000 Layer 3D NAND Flash
authors: Namju Kim, Hyeun Woo Shin, Chae Eun Kim, Woo Cheol Shin, Suk-Kang Sung, Byung Chul Jang
abstract: As 3D NAND flash continues to advance beyond 1000 word-line (WL) layers, high-voltage (HV) pass transistor for WL driver circuit emerges as a key bottleneck for NAND chip scaling due to layout overhead and reliability degradation in scaled planar HV pass transistor. Here, we present a structure-aware physical analysis of four HV pass transistor structures: planar, recessed source/drain (RSD), buried channel array transistor (BCAT), and FinFET. Among them, FinFET shows superior gate controllability, body effect immunity and geometric variation tolerance. A machine-learning-assisted cell <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$V_{\text{th }}$</tex> prediction framework confirms the advantage of FinFET, showing efficient program (PGM) transfer with a minimum number of PGM loops and low power consumption during ISPP operation. These results highlight FinFET as a promising candidate for a reliable and scalable HV pass transistor in 3D NAND flash.

title: Dielectric Encapsulated Niobium Films to Achieve Higher Processing Temperatures
authors: Kirsten L. Lina, Anna E. Fox, Paul D. Dresselhaus
abstract: To avoid degradation of circuit performance, fabrication processes for niobium-based superconductive electronics are typically limited to temperatures below 150°C. In this study, we investigated protective dielectric capping layers that preserve the superconducting properties of Niobium (Nb) wiring at processing temperatures as high as 400°C. To assess the thermal stability of Nb films, 400 nm thick Nb layers were deposited on oxidized silicon wafers and were either left uncapped or were intentionally capped with selected dielectric materials. The samples were subjected to post-deposition annealing in an argon atmosphere for comparison. Samples were annealed up to 450°C, and changes in room temperature sheet resistance were used as a proxy for film degradation and checked with cryogenic measurements of samples annealed up to 400°C. A sharp increase in room temperature sheet resistance, and a resulting decrease in residual resistivity ratio was observed in pristine Nb films above 300°C. We found that the ex-situ deposition of insulating covering (“cap”) reduces the onset of degradation. Notably, silicon oxide (SiO$\_{x}$) capping shows significant improvement and silicon nitride (Si$\_{x}$N$\_{y}$) capped samples exhibit minimal changes in resistance across the full annealing range. These results suggest that Si$\_{x}$N$\_{y}$ encapsulation as a dielectric “cap” preserves superconductive properties and offers an expanded thermal budget for Nb-based superconductive electronics, with implications for multilayer integration and scalable fabrication.

title: Hierarchical Causal Learning for Face Age Synthesis
authors: Ye Wang, Pan Sun, Xuyang Zhou, Lifeng Shen, Jiaxu Leng, Guoyin Wang, Hong Yu
abstract: Face age synthesis (FAS) predicts a person’s future or past facial appearance. In FAS, modifying one facial attribute usually affects the generation of other attributes during face image generation. Current models directly learn entangled representations of age-related features, resulting in insufficient feature disentanglement, which consequently impairs their causal reasoning capability for FAS tasks. To this end, we propose a hierarchical causal learning model for face age synthesis (HCFace), which integrates hierarchical structures and causal relationships into the facial generative model. Specifically, we propose to leverage hierarchical causal relationships to align with facial features for feature disentanglement. Furthermore, we design a novel nonlinear mapping function that captures the true patterns of facial attribute changes with age, enhancing the disentanglement of these attributes. We conduct extensive experiments to validate the superiority of our proposed model. Compared to other advanced baseline methods, HCFace improves overall accuracy by 2.47%, with improvements of 9.75% and 9.69% in certain age-related attributes, such as skin and hair. Our source code is available at https://github.com/SE-hash/HCFace.

title: An Outsourced Attribute-Based Encryption Scheme with Verifiable Policy Update and Dynamic Attributes for Digital Twins
authors: Zhaofeng Ma, Jiayu Dong, Zhiquan Liu, Tian Tian, Pengfei Duan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Digital Twins (DTs) generate vast sensitive data, demanding secure, dynamic, and fine-grained access control. While Ciphertext-Policy Attribute-Based Encryption (CP-ABE) offers such control, its inherent computational overhead, static nature, and the need to trust outsourcers for complex operations often limit its practical application in evolving DT environments. In this paper, we proposed an Outsourced Attribute-Based Encryption Scheme with Verifiable Policy Update and Dynamic Attributes (OABE-VPUDA) for Digital Twins. OABE-VPUDA empowers Data Owners (DOs) and Data Users (DUs) by enabling verifiable outsourced encryption, where a Public Verifier confirms Cloud Server (CS) computations, and verifiable outsourced de cryption, validated by DUs using an inspired tag mechanism. The scheme further incorporates dynamic attribute management via on-chain expiration tags for timely, fine-grained revocation, and allows DOs to securely and verifiably modify ciphertext access policies with CS assistance. A consortium blockchain underpins these functionalities, significantly enhancing system transparency, auditability, and overall trust in collaborative DT ecosystems. Formal security analysis rigorously demonstrates that the OABE-VPUDA scheme is secure against chosen-plaintext attacks, thereby ensuring robust data confidentiality, and its de sign ensures the verifiability of all critical outsourced operations within a provable security framework. Experimental results from a prototype implementation confirm the scheme's operational efficiency and practical applicability, highlighting its suitability for secure and agile data sharing in resource-aware digital twin applications.

title: Dielectric characterization of ester transformer oils under thermo-oxidative aging supported by physicochemical analysis
authors: Alai Muniozguren, Maite Mujika, Maja Kobus, Andres Montero, Tim Granath, Juan F. Sevillano, Itsaso Artetxe
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: This study investigates how thermo-oxidative aging influences the dielectric response of ester-based transformer liquids and how dielectric properties relate to concurrent physicochemical degradation of oil. Two natural esters (soybean-based and high-oleic sunflower-based) and a synthetic ester were aged at 80, 120, and 140 °C for 0, 164, 332, and 500 hours, under oxygen atmosphere. Aging was tracked via density, viscosity, water content, acidity, Raman spectroscopy, and cellulose degree of polymerization (DPv). Dielectric properties were derived from impedance measurements over 10 Hz-1 MHz (real and imaginary parts the of relative permittivity) and under IEC 60247 conditions at 60 Hz/90 °C (real part of the relative permittivity and dissipation factor). Synthetic ester shows better physical properties but worst dielectric properties at extreme degradation conditions. Knee descriptors extracted from imaginary part of the relative permittivity evidence a progressive overlap of conduction- and polarization-dominated regimes, with conductivity-driven evolution in the synthetic ester and mobility-limited behavior in highly viscous natural esters. Correlation analysis (R²) shows that dielectric properties can act as integrated aging indicators, linking electrical response with physicochemical degradation and cellulose degree of polymerization.

title: Passband-Aligned Reconfigurable Filtering Phase Shifter Using Transmission-Zero Regulation Technique
authors: Zhentian Wu, Jin Cheng, Xin Fang, Xiaoming Chen, Zhixiang Huang, Lixia Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: A reconfigurable filtering phase shifter (RFPS) with simultaneously tunable frequency and bandwidth is proposed for the first time in this article. By independently controlling multiple transmission zeros (TZs), this design realizes a wide-range reconfiguration while ensuring a high passband alignment rate (PAR). A varactor-loaded coupled microstrip line is adopted to realize continuous phase shifting. Meanwhile, a hybrid substrate-integrated waveguide (SIW)-microstrip structure is implemented to construct the filtering network, and four band-stop open-circuited stubs are integrated onto the SIW transmission line, which successfully introduces four TZs within the passband. Independent tuning of these TZs can be achieved by adjusting the varactors at the stub terminals, which enables flexible reconfiguration of both the frequency and bandwidth. In addition, fine-tuning the relative positions of the TZs can effectively suppress phase shift errors. Notably, the structural decoupling between the open-circuited stubs and the coupled microstrip lines ensures a high PAR during the phase shift tuning process. For verification, the proposed design was fabricated and measured. The measured results demonstrate that the center frequency is tuned over 42.1% across the band from 0.90 to 1.38 GHz, with an insertion loss ranging from 0.8 to 2.1 dB. The 3-dB fractional bandwidth (FBW) is adjustable between 20% and 50%. Over the entire tuning range, the PAR remains between 90.0% and 95.6%. Benefiting from its wide-range frequency and bandwidth reconfigurability, low insertion loss, and high passband alignment, the proposed RFPS provides a reliable and innovative solution for broadband phase shifter design in reconfigurable phased-array systems.

title: A Wide Input Range FVF LDO Design for RF Energy Harvester System
authors: Jing Rong Thng, Hai Ye, Anqing Chen, Nagarajan Mahalingam, Jithish Jayarajan, Baoji Miao, Kiat Seng Yeo
abstract: This paper presents a flipped voltage follower (FVF) low-dropout (LDO) regulator with an innovative circuit architecture that significantly expands the input supply range and current sink capability. Conventional FVF LDO designs are typically constrained by restrictive input voltage operating ranges inherent in their control loop architecture, which poses significant challenges for RF energy harvester systems demanding robust performance across varying output voltage conditions. To address this limitation, the proposed design implements critical modifications to the biasing and buffer topology, introducing substantial architectural improvements that extend the input operating range while maintaining stable voltage regulation and preventing sudden regulation collapse. Fabricated in a 40 nm CMOS process with an area of 0.0089 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>, the proposed FVF LDO demonstrates exceptional performance characteristics, achieving a significantly widened input supply range and delivering a fast transient settling time of 103 ns for a load current step from 0 mA to 10 mA. The LDO also achieves a low quiescent current of 122 µA. The research contributes a robust voltage regulation solution specifically tailored for energy harvesting applications with demanding operational requirements.

title: Minimum Cost Swapping and Purification Scheduling Over Quantum Repeater Chains
authors: Jiyao Liu, Lei Fan, Yuanxiong Guo, Zhu Han, Yu Wang
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Entanglement swapping and purification are two fundamental primitives for long-distance entanglement distribution in quantum networks, enabling key applications such as quantum key distribution, quantum sensing, and distributed quantum computing. While prior work has established that purification should precede swapping in Binary systems to minimize the expected number of consumed link-level entanglements for an E2E fidelity guarantee, the critical problem of finding the optimal swapping and purification strategy remains open. To fill this gap, we study the Optimal Swapping and Purification Tree (OSPT) problem for a quantum repeater chain in Binary systems and derive the exact optimal solution. By leveraging the associativity of swapping and purification fidelity, we decompose the problem into three subproblems: optimal swapping tree construction, optimal purification scheduling, and global budget allocation. We show that although the exact solution can be obtained via brute-force search over budget distributions, its complexity grows exponentially. To address this challenge, we further propose an efficient greedy budget allocation algorithm. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that the greedy algorithm closely approximates the optimal solution and outperforms existing solutions while significantly reducing computational complexity.

title: Real-Time Waste Classification via Image Recognition in a ROS2-Gazebo Simulated Environment
authors: Rownak Subrina, Saima Jahan Mouli, Sumaya Ishrat Moyeen
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: As waste generation is increasing day by day, managing waste effectively has become a necessity, and especially in countries like Bangladesh, where manual sorting is still the norm. In this paper, we present a smart and cost-effective solution for waste classification using deep learning and robotic simulation. We developed a custom image dataset of common household waste and trained a lightweight MobileNetV2 model to classify as two main categories: organic and recyclable. The trained model was exported in ONNX format for real-time inference in ROS2 for fast and flexible deployment. We then integrated it into a ROS2 environment and tested the complete system using a TurtleBot3 robot in the Gazebo simulator. The robot captured images in real-time, classified the waste using the trained model, and displayed the results instantly. The system achieved a validation accuracy of 97.60 %, showing strong potential for real-world applications. This work demonstrates the impact of combining deep learning with open-source robotics tools to provide practical, scalable, and environment-friendly solutions for the future waste management.

title: Junction Extension Design Considerations for Low Leakage Vertical Channel DRAM Cell Array Transistor
authors: Moonyoung Jeong, Kilho Yu, Yootak Jun, Jaehyun Choi, Seungwoo Park, Junsoo Kim, Junghoon Oh, Segeun Park, Jaihyuk Song
abstract: This paper addresses the junction extension design considerations for low leakage <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{4 F}^{\mathbf{2}}$</tex> DRAM vertical channel cell array transistor. A comprehensive study was conducted on the junction extension coupling effects on the device characteristics of cell array transistors having ultra-thin body. Dual work function word line was introduced as one of the most feasible solution to cope with the junction extension coupling effects. Performance gain by DWF WL allows the junction extension to be designed in a way more resistant to the coupling effects.

title: Hybrid Predictive Quantum Feedback: Extending Qubit Lifetimes Beyond the Wiseman–Milburn Limit
authors: Ali Abu-Nada, Aryan Iliat, Russell Ceballos
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Amplitude damping sets a fundamental limit on qubit lifetimes, while standard Wiseman-Milburn (W-M) feedback offers limited improvement due to (i) single-quadrature homodyne readout and (ii) unavoidable loop latency. We propose a hybrid architecture that combines (a) an ancilla-assisted coherent feedback pathway that effectively recovers information from both field quadratures and (b) a lightweight supervised predictor that forecasts the delayed homodyne current to phase-align actuation. We derive compact effective decay-rate models for classical W-M, ancilla-assisted, and predictive (ancilla+ML) feedback, and benchmark them with numerical simulations under realistic superconducting-qubit parameters. The hybrid loop surpasses the W-M bound, yielding multi-fold extension of the effective <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$T_{1}$</tex>. The protocol is modular and compatible with existing measurement-and-feedback hardware, providing a practical control-layer primitive for suppressing amplitude damping.

title:
Proton Radiation Tolerance and Cryogenic HfZrO/Wo
                    <sub>x</sub>
                    FeCAPs for Space Electronics
authors: Eunjin Kim, Jiyong Woo, Dong-Seok Kim
abstract: We demonstrate proton (H<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">+</sup>) radiation-tolerant 10 nm HfZrO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">x</inf>(HZO)/10 nm WO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">x</inf> ferroelectric capacitors (FeCAPs). We perform an accelerated H<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">+</sup> irradiation test with energy of 5 MeV and a fluence of 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">13</sup> p/cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> considering long-term (>10 years) exposure for space missions. In HZO FeCAPs, 2 remnant polarization (P<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">r</inf>) of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$45 \mu \mathrm{C}/\text{c m}^{2}$</tex> is immediately degraded after irradiation at 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">11</sup> p/cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>. In contrast, HZO/WO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">x</inf> FeCAPs show high 2 P<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">r</inf> of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$58 \mu \mathrm{C}/\text{cm}^{2}$</tex> even after irradiation at 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">13</sup> p/cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>. This resiliency is evidenced by stable 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">7</sup> cycling endurance after 14 days post-irradiation and preserved polarization at 118 K. Switching parameter linking and transient analyses show that downward dipole rotation in HZO FeCAPs is interrupted and delayed compared with HZO/WO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">x</inf> FeCAPs. This suggests that oxygen vacancies (Vos) are mainly generated at the HZO/bottom electrode interface and are further promoted by irradiation. The introduced WO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">x</inf> suppresses Vo creation, rendering HZO/WO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">x</inf> largely immune to H<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">+</sup> induced ferroelectric degradation.

title: Comparative Study of Sequencing and Control for Aircraft Armament Release Mechanism
authors: Md. Shakib Al Sifat, Kazi Tauhid Mokbul Hussain, Saif Reza
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: End-to-end command-to-separation timing for aircraft armament release is seldom reported, yet it governs sequencing safety and performance. We implement a latencyaware supervisory architecture in which a Raspberry Pi sequencer issues mode-dependent commands (Auto, Manual, failsafe Jettison) and an Arduino actuator layer enforces positive-separation confirmation and diversion to Jettison on fault. Two representative mechanisms- a suspension latch and a geared claw were designed in SolidWorks, 3D-printed, and exercised on a five-station bench rig with inert articles. Using standardized metrics (actuation, release, and activation) with controller-side timestamping, the suspension mechanism consistently outperforms the claw across all modes: actuation and release are shorter by approximately 0.45 seconds (38-43%). Activation shows no systematic difference because it includes a deliberate 3s interlock shared by both mechanisms. A decomposition of pre-release timing attributes the claw's delay to a slower unlock phase. The resulting 0.45s gain expands sequencing margin and supports tighter inter-station spacing under the same guard times, and the fallback preserves failsafe behavior. Experiments were conducted on a laboratory bench with 3D-printed hardware and inert training articles; flight condition validation under vibration and dynamic loads is left to future work.

title: Optimizing Surgery Room Scheduling with Comparative Metaheuristic Algorithms
authors: Md.Wahiduzzaman Nejhum, Abrar Ul Karim, Zannatun Nur, Bikas Chandra Shill, Kiyas Mahmud, Md.Faruk Abdullah Al Sohan
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Efficient scheduling of surgery rooms during emergencies remains one of the most pressing issues in today's modern hospital operations. Acting quickly in an emergency context is vital to minimize patient wait times, as well as to mitigate operational bottlenecks and maximize the utilization of surgical resources. With the complexity of the problems at hand heuristic solutions are required to solve the NP-hard Job Shop Scheduling Problems (JSSP). This research compares five metaheuristic algorithms. They are Genetic Algorithm (GA), Simulated Annealing (SA), Tabu Search (TS), Ant Colony Optimization (ACO), and Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) for emergency surgery scheduling. We investigated the performance of each of these methods, in terms of execution time, memory usage, cache usage, and instruction count using <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$<\text{chrono}>$</tex>, Valgrind's Callgrind, Intel VTune Profiler, and Google gperftools. Experimental results conducted using real-life emergency datasets consistently demonstrate a clear trade-off in quality of solutions, speed of convergence, and consumption of computational resources. These findings will inspire the design of intelligent and real time scheduling systems that support decisions in favor of improved patient care and response to hospital emergencies.

title: Hybrid Quantum-Secure LEO Satellite Networks
authors: Angela Diaz-Bricio, Ivan Vidal, Francisco Valera
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: The quantum era compromises current cryptography, motivating the development of Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC) and Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) as complementary approaches to secure long-term communications. Satellite platforms are expected to extend QKD beyond terrestrial distance limits, yet their integration into broader network infrastructures introduces significant architectural and operational challenges. This work examines these challenges from a networking perspective and explores how PQC can assist early satellite-based QKD deployments. We introduce a control-plane model that combines satellite QKD for Earth-to-space key establishment with PQC-secured inter-satellite communication, enabling key delivery between ground stations when quantum keys are not yet available network-wide. A case study based on emulated keyexchange procedures using timing data derived from the Iridium constellation illustrates the feasibility of the approach.

title: Self-Heating in GAA Nanosheet FETs with Integrated Back Side Power Delivery Network: Device to Circuit Electro-Thermal Analysis and Implications
authors: Rinku Dutta, Minji Shon, Seongkwang Lim, Priyabrata Saha, Mingeun Choi, Mayur P. Singh, Satish Kumar, Saibal Mukhopadhyay, Sung Kyu Lim, Shimeng Yu, Reza Arghavani, Suman Datta
abstract: This work presents an electrothermal analysis of Gate-All-Around (GAA) Nanosheet (NS) FETs to reveal that, while Backside Power Delivery Network (BSPDN) provides logic density scaling advantages, it introduces thermal bottlenecks due to substrate thinning and the thermal insulation of backside dielectrics. These structural constraints impede heat dissipation, leading to junction temperature increases, such as a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\Delta \mathbf{T}_{\mathbf{j}}$</tex> of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sim \mathbf{1 5 0 K}$</tex> at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{V}_{\text{dd}}=0.7 \mathrm{V}$</tex>, which escalates to over 300 K under the worst condition of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{V}_{\text{Dd}}=1.3 \mathrm{V}$</tex>. Such a strong self-heating effect results in a direct performance penalty, evidenced by over 10% frequency degradation in inverter-chain ring oscillators. These findings are critical for high-performance computing (HPC) systems that rely on voltage overdrive and overclocking, as the localized thermal congestion at the nanosheet level can effectively neutralize the throughput gain at advanced node. Our insights underscore the importance of thermal-aware device-circuit cooptimization to successfully navigate the thermal limitations inherent in next-generation logic architecture.

title: Control of Fixed-Wing Flocking Coordination under Wind Speed Disturbance
authors: Fabrício C. S. Xavier, Sérgio R. Barros Dos Santos, Sidney N. Givigi
abstract: This paper addresses the stability of fixed-wing Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) swarms operating under wind disturbance environments. A critical challenge in this domain is the Inertial-Aerodynamic Conflict, where standard high-level coordination logic neglects the non-holonomic constraints of the platform or enforces a separation between high-level planning and low-level control, which can lead to a degradation in coordination performance. In strong wind fields, this decoupling leads to a scenario where commanded ground velocities are kinematically valid but aerodynamically infeasible, resulting in airspeed decay, actuator saturation, and eventual envelope violation. To resolve this, we propose the Wind-Adaptive Reference Compensation (WARC) strategy. Departing from traditional reactive feedback approaches, which fail to correct energy deficits in time, our method introduces a feedforward compensation layer. By projecting the estimated wind vector onto the agent’s velocity frame, the system adjusts the autopilot’s throttle and airspeed references, augmenting the necessary kinetic energy to maintain the flight envelope before position errors accumulate. The proposed architecture is validated through Software-in-theLoop (SIL) simulations in ROS/Gazebo, subjecting a flock of Cessna 172 models to stochastic severe wind conditions. Experimental results indicate that the WARC strategy significantly reduces formation dispersion under moderate perturbations. These results demonstrate that predictive compensation focused on energy awareness is a prerequisite for the use of fixed-wing aircraft swarms in adverse weather conditions.

title: Cryogenic Hot Carrier Degradation and Threshold-Voltage Turn-Around Effect in 5-V CMOS Transistors
authors: E. Soli, A. Vladimirescu, M. Babaie, S. Hamdioui, F. Sebastiano
abstract: This paper presents the first cryogenic characterization of Hot Carrier Degradation (HCD) in 5-V thick-oxide transistors fabricated in a 160-nm CMOS technology. HCD significantly worsens in nMOS devices at 4.2 K, leading to a more severe degradation, especially of threshold voltage and current in the linear regime. Contrary to expectations, pMOS devices exhibit a temporary performance improvement after stress, showing for the first time at 4.2 K a HCD-induced turn-around effect in threshold voltage and current. The threshold-voltage shift follows a power law with stress time, showing a much higher exponent at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$4.2 K$</tex> than at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$300 K$</tex> for nMOS, but not for pMOS devices. The threshold-voltage shift also follows a power law with stress voltage, strongly accelerated for nMOS at 4.2 K, but unchanged for pMOS.

title: DBA-Net: Dual-Bilinear Attention Network for High-Speed Railway Pantograph Arcing Detection
authors: Rong Wang, Hui Xiang, Ziyang Ling, Jiaxia Li, Yang Hu, Ganpei Zhao
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: Pantograph-catenary arcing is a critical fault phenomenon in high-speed railway systems, posing severe threats to the stable current collection and the service life of equipment. Detecting arcing accurately is challenging due to the small size of the arc, the complex background of the catenary system, and the interference from varying illumination conditions. Existing Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) based methods often struggle to segment arcing regions that lack clear contour and texture features, while Transformer-based methods may lose local details. To address these issues, we propose a novel model named DBA-Net (Dual-Bilinear Attention Network), tailored for robust arcing detection. The model incorporates a Dual-Linear Fusion Transformer (DLFT) module to extract global correlations while preserving local context, and a U-shaped Convolution-Dilated Convolution (UCDC) module to refine deep semantic features of small arc targets. Experimental results on pantograph arcing datasets demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance compared to existing segmentation methods.

title: Probabilistic Agents in Deterministic Audits: Evaluating Multi-Agent Systems for Automated Audits Based on the German IT-Grundschutz
authors: Lea Muth, Marian Margraf
abstract: The NIS-2 Directive mandates robust Risk Management from thousands of small and medium enterprises. To ensure compliance, companies rely on established standards such as the German IT-Grundschutz (IT-GS) of the Federal Office for Information Security. However, IT-GS certification is resource-intensive and requires a high level of manual effort for documentation, validation, and revision, making scalable implementation difficult and expensive.Building upon our previous conceptual framework, this paper presents the technical implementation and empirical evaluation of a Multi-Agent System (MAS) architecture combined with Hybrid Retrieval Augmented Generation (HybridRAG) for the partial automation of IT-GS certification. We introduce two novel technical contributions to the MAS architecture to enforce the compliance rigor. The Hypothesis-Verification Loop in the Structural Analysis (SA) phase that cross-references agent-inferred dependencies against the Knowledge Graph to reduce hallucinations, and a Decoupled Reasoning Pipeline that separates agentdriven semantic extraction from the deterministic protection need inheritance. We utilize the BSI’s “RecPlast GmbH” case study as a human expert-generated reference data set for end-to-end evaluation of the architecture and to quantify Precision, Recall, and F1-scores. The performance of the system is investigated across the phases of SA, Protection Needs Assessment (PNA), Modeling, and IT-GS CheckThe empirical results reveal noticeable differences throughout the different steps of IT-GS. While the MAS demonstrates high efficacy in semantic tasks (SA and Modeling), significantly reducing manual effort through automated information extraction, quantitative results reveal limitations in logical reasoning phases (PNA and IT-GS Check) as the probabilistic nature of current LLMs struggles to meet the deterministic rigor required by ITGS.

title: Task Concurrency and Compatibility in Measurement-Based Quantum Networks
authors: Jakob Kaltoft Søndergaard, René Bødker Christensen, Petar Popovski
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Measurement-Based Quantum Networks (MBQNs) rely on multipartite pre-shared entanglement resources to satisfy entanglement requests. Traditional designs optimize these resources for individual tasks, neglecting that multiple tasks may arrive concurrently and compete for the same entanglement. We introduce compatibility as a design-level metric, capturing whether concurrent tasks can be satisfied by the same entanglement resources. We define a worst-case notion of compatibility where nodes are prevented from coordinating after task arrival and illustrate why tasks may be incompatible. Furthermore, we explore compatibility extensions that account for stochastic arrivals and the capability to supplement the pre-shared entanglement with additional entanglement on-demand, and show that incompatibility differs structurally dependent on the set of concurrent tasks. We argue that compatibility should be used for resource state design, building the foundation for determining which task pairs the network should support with pre-shared entanglement and which require execution-time coordination. Numerical simulations demonstrate this potential, with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(G, 1)$</tex>-compatibility achieving a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{4 0 \% - 5 5 \%}$</tex> gain in simultaneously supported tasks relative to the single-task baseline. By incorporating compatibility as a fundamental design objective, quantum networks can move beyond single-task optimization towards scalable, robust architectures that effectively balance proactive entanglement distribution and supplemental reactive coordination.

title: A Comprehensive Study of Terrestrial Radiation Effects in Current Generation DRAM
authors: Vinod Ambrose, Sachin Dev, Preeti Kumari, David Meisner, Eric Stubbs, Melissa Uribe
abstract: Fast neutron, thermal neutron and alpha particle relative upset rates determined via accelerated measurements in multiple DRAM technology generations are presented. Technology scaling trends are explained via device simulations, and array to logic upset ratios are shown/explained. The threshold for total ionizing dose damage from terrestrial usecases is measured and shown to be inconsequential. Radiationinduced upsets in large DRAM deployments are extracted and shown to be a small fraction of other sources of upsets.

title: Electric Power Load Analysis for All-Electric Complex Ships: Methods, Challenges and Opportunities
authors: Andrea Vicenzutti, Andrea Alessia Tavagnutti, Samuele Sbuelz, Luca Braidotti, Giorgio Sulligoi
abstract: The progressive electrification of ships, driven by environmental regulations, efficiency goals, and new functions required by the owners is significantly increasing the variability and complexity of shipboard electrical demand. The integration of electric propulsion, high-power mission loads, alternative fuels, and energy storage systems makes accurate sizing of the shipboard electric power plant critical, as inadequate assessments may lead to inefficient oversizing or reduced reliability and mission effectiveness. In this context, Electric Power Load Analysis ($E P L A$) remains a cornerstone of shipboard electrical system design. However, traditional deterministic EPLA approaches, while widely adopted and certification-friendly, exhibit limitations in capturing load dynamics and transient behavior. More advanced stochastic and simulation-based methods have been proposed to overcome these limitations, at the cost of increased modeling complexity and data requirements. This paper reviews and compares existing EPLA methodologies, highlighting their benefits and limitations, evaluates their applicability to different ship design use cases, and proposes a hybrid $\boldsymbol{E} \boldsymbol{P} \boldsymbol{L} \boldsymbol{A}$ design framework as a set of best practices tailored to specific ship design scenarios.

title: Efficient Transpilation of OpenQASM 3.0 Dynamic Circuits to CUDA-Q: Performance and Expressiveness Advantages
authors: Vinooth Kulkarni, Jaehyun Lee, Adam Hutchings, Anas Albahri, Jai Nana, Shuai Xu, Vipin Chaudhary
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Dynamic quantum circuits with mid-circuit measurement and classical feedforward are essential for near-term algorithms such as error mitigation, adaptive phase estimation, and Variational Quantum Eigensolvers (VQE), yet transpiling these programs across frameworks remains challenging due to inconsistent support for control flow and measurement semantics. We present a transpilation pipeline that converts OpenQASM 3.0 programs with classical control structures (conditionals and bounded loops) into optimized CUDA-Q C++ kernels, leveraging CUDA-Q's native mid-circuit measurement and host-language control flow to translate dynamic patterns without static circuit expansion. Our open-source framework is validated on comprehensive test suites derived from IBM Quantum's classical feedforward guide—including conditional reset, if-else branching, multi-bit predicates, and sequential feedforward—and on VQE-style parameterized circuits with runtime parameter optimization. Experiments show that the resulting CUDA-Q kernels reduce circuit depth by avoiding branch duplication, improve execution efficiency via low-latency classical feedback, and enhance code readability by directly mapping OpenQASM 3.0 control structures to C++ control flow, thereby bridging OpenQASM 3.0's portable circuit specification with CUDA-Q's performance-oriented execution model for NISQ-era applications requiring dynamic circuit capabilities.

title: When AI Becomes Its Own Biggest Fan: Self-Preference Bias in AI-Assisted Peer Review
authors: Jianzheng Shi, Jungmin Lee, Larry Zhiming Xu, Terence Ow, Yue Wang
abstract: This study examined whether large language models exhibit self-preference bias, a tendency to favor texts generated by themselves over those produced by other models, in AI-assisted peer review. Using a controlled review simulation, GPT-4, GPT-3, and LLaMA were used to generate and evaluate 60 academic manuscripts under a common rubric covering clarity, relevance, originality, and methodological appropriateness. This design enabled direct comparison between self-reviews (a model evaluates its own generated manuscript) and cross-reviews (a model evaluates manuscripts generated by other models) under identical evaluation conditions. In addition, BERT was used to compute embedding-based semantic similarity as a robustness measure. The results indicated that GPT-4 and GPT-3 exhibited strong self-preference bias, assigning significantly higher scores to their own outputs than to manuscripts generated by other models. Human review scores were more closely aligned with AI cross-reviews than AI self-reviews, whereas LLaMA showed weaker and less consistent self-preference patterns, corresponding to its higher rejection rate during quality screening. These findings remained robust across prompt-sensitivity, resampling, alternative aggregation, subsample, and model-specification checks. Taken together, the findings point to a structural source of distortion in AI-assisted evaluation, suggesting that bias may arise not only from training data but also from how generation and evaluation roles are configured within a workflow. The study contributes to research on AI-enabled evaluation systems by extending current discussions of algorithmic bias toward workflow design, and it offers practical guidance on cross-model evaluation, calibration, and human-in-the-loop safeguards in high-stakes evaluative settings.
