title: Direct PET-to-CT Generation for Attenuation Correction: A Slice-to-Slice Continual Transformer Segmentation-Aware Network
authors: Rongjun Ge, Hanyuan Zheng, Yuxin Liu, Liutao Yang, Li Wang, Xu Ji, Jingtao Shen, Nan Li, Shengji He, Daoqiang Zhang, Chengyu Liu, Yang Chen, Shuo Li, Yuting He
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Direct synthetic computed tomography (CT) generation from positron emission tomography (PET) plays a crucial role in PET attenuation correction, yet providing detailed structural information to compensate for functional imaging. Compared to the widely used PET/CT and indirect PET/MR-CT, the direct PET-to-CT translation method (denoted as PET-to-CT) offers several advantages: 1) The CT required for PET-to-CT is directly obtained from PET, thereby avoiding the intermediate errors generated in the inter-step processes of multimodal scanning in PET/CT and PET/MR-CT. 2) Furthermore, direct PET-to-CT eliminates the requirement for supplementary imaging equipment, thereby reducing complexity and scan duration in contrast to PET/CT and PET/MR-CT imaging. Thus, direct PET-to-CT is highly promising for clinical applications. However, it faces challenges, including spatial resolution mismatches between PET and CT, as well as voxel-wise semantic differences arising from functional and structural imaging. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a 2D hierarchical method called S2SCT (Slice-to-Slice Continual Transformer)-SA (Segmentation-aware) Network. It uses a slice-continual network to acquire semantic transformation knowledge from each PET slice to a CT slice, facilitating the conversion between functional and structural imaging domains. Subsequently, the segmentation-aware network is designed to futher capture spatial correlations both between slices and within slice, resulting in improved CT spatial resolution. The experiment results demonstrate that our proposed method outperforms mainstream methods in both CT generation and attenuation correction, as evidenced by both visual results and metric values.

title: Understanding Near-Field Wireless Power Transfer Systems with Complex Mutual Inductance and Negative Mutual Resistance
authors: Shu Yuen Ron Hui, Kerui Li, Jinfeng Yu, Siew Chong Tan
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: Mutual inductance traditionally appears as a scalar quantity in electric circuit theory. However, in wireless power transfer (WPT) systems with dissipative media such seawater and human tissues, complex nature of mutual inductance has been observed. Another phenomenon of negative mutual resistance also puzzles many engineers in the WPT community. While complex mutual inductance (CMI) and negative mutual resistance (NMR) have been studied separately in the literature, they cannot be separated in many WPT systems. This paper reviews and examines CMI and NMR in a holistic manner for WPT. Based on electric circuit theory, this paper presents a generalized analysis and circuit model of WPT systems to explain the physical nature and co-existence of CMI and NMR in a tutorial style. This extended WPT theory shows that these two elements not only co-exist in WPT systems with dissipative media but also in any WPT system with at least one extra induced dissipative current loop in intended relay resonators and unintended conducting paths such as unintended metallic objects and electromagnetic shielding materials. A methodology for optimizing the WPT systems using the CMI and NMR through re-compensation of the resonator design to enhance mutual coupling is provided. The theory is illustrated with several WPT examples with practical measurements and simulations.

title: Multi-Link Turbulence Sensing and Mitigation for FSO Diversity Reception With Attention-Based Neural Combining
authors: Ruizhe Jin, Mingfeng Xu, Mengjie Zhou, Jiazheng Ding, Shuangcheng Chen, Yong Yu, Mingbo Pu, Nan Chi, Xiangang Luo
abstract: With the rapid development of applications such as sixth-generation (6G) communications, the low-altitude economy, and low-earth-orbit (LEO) satellite systems, the demand for high-capacity wireless communications is becoming increasingly urgent. Free-space optical (FSO) communication is a key enabling technology due to its large bandwidth potential, yet FSO links are highly vulnerable to atmospheric turbulence, which severely constrains system capacity and reliability. This paper proposes a multi-link FSO closed-loop sensing-assisted communication framework. Reverse-propagating probing beams are used for turbulence sensing, and a spatial light modulator (SLM)-based optical phased-array independently controls the beam on each link to suppress turbulence. At the receiver, a self-attention neural network is employed for signal combining to improve diversity reception performance. Two independent turbulence-impaired links with different propagation directions and lengths of 1.2 m and 1.3 m are implemented in the experiment. Nonuniform turbulence with intensity fluctuations up to 10 dB is emulated using SLMs, and the sensing error is below 0.5 dB. With multi-beam control, the average received optical power increases by 2.92 dB for the 1.2-m link and 2.85 dB for the 1.3-m link, and the scintillation index is reduced by 82.7% and 81.3%, respectively. In a four-wavelength wavelength-division-multiplexed (WDM) transmission with an aggregate rate of 256 Gb/s, the system robustness improves from a minimum of 31.7% for a single link to 100% after dual-link diversity combining. The results demonstrate that the proposed technique can significantly enhance the capacity and reliability of FSO communications and is promising for practical applications.

title: Fidelity-Age-Aware Scheduling in Quantum Repeater Networks
authors: Ozgur Ercetin, Zafer Gedik
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Quantum repeater networks distribute entanglement over long distances but must balance fidelity, delay, and resource contention. Prior work optimized throughput and end-to-end fidelity, yet little attention has been paid to the freshness of entanglement-the time since a usable Bell pair was last delivered. We introduce the Fidelity-Age (FA) metric, which measures this interval for states whose fidelity exceeds a threshold <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$F_{\min}$</tex>. A renewal formulation links slot-level success probability to long-run average <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{F A}$</tex>, enabling a stochastic control problem that minimizes FA under budget and memory limits. Two lightweight schedulers, FA-THR and FA-INDEX, approximate Lyapunov-drift-optimal control. Simulations on slotted repeater grids show that FA-aware scheduling preserves throughput while reducing extreme-age events by up to two orders of magnitude. FidelityAge thus provides a tractable, physically grounded metric for reliable and timely entanglement delivery in quantum networks.

title: An Ensemble Deep Learning Approach for Enhanced Lung Cancer Detection from CT Images
authors: Md Nazmul Hossain, Mozahidul Haque Shoyeb, Md. Sifat Bin Asad, Rafid Kabir, Muntasir Hasan Porag, T. M. Shahriar Sazzad
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Lung cancer remains a leading cause of cancer mortality worldwide, with early detection being critical to improving patient outcomes. This study proposes a heterogeneous ensemble framework combining deep learning-based feature extraction with traditional machine learning classifiers to enhance lung cancer detection from computed tomography (CT) images. The framework includes comprehensive preprocessing steps such as local histogram equalization, morphological filtering, and U-Net-based segmentation to accurately identify regions of interest. Deep features extracted via a pre-trained VGG16 model are refined through Random Forest and XGBoost-based feature selection, reducing redundancy and computational complexity. Subsequently, selected features are classified using Support Vector Machine, Random Forest, and XGBoost classifiers, with majority voting employed to enhance robustness. Evaluated on a public CT dataset, the model achieved 97.5% accuracy, 97.93% precision, 99.48% recall, and a 98.69% F1 score. These results demonstrate the efficacy of integrating deep learning and traditional classifiers for reliable lung cancer detection, providing a promising foundation for clinical diagnostic support systems.

title: Enhancement of Imprint Reliability in $\text{Hf}_{0.5} \text{Zr}_{0.5} \mathrm{O}_{2}$ FeRAMs: The Role of Symmetric $\text{AlO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ Interlayers and Wake-Up Strategy
authors: Wen Sun, Bowen Shen, Yuyan Wang, Mingcheng Shi, Yi Ding, Benjamin Yang, Eunhye Baek, Bin Gao, Jianshi Tang, He Qian, Huaqiang Wu
abstract: This study systematically investigates the regulation of the imprint effect in <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{Hf}_{0.5} \text{Zr}_{0.5} \mathrm{O}_{2}$</tex> (HZO) ferroelectric capacitors for multi-level memory applications, emphasizing a bilayer <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{AlO}_{\mathrm{x}}$</tex> interlayer structure integrated with a bottom-electrode wake-up strategy. Electrical characterization indicates that this approach facilitates symmetric oxygen vacancy aggregation at both the top and bottom interfaces, effectively reducing voltage shift to as low as 0.11 V while achieving a high remanent polarization (2Pr) of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$34.1 \mu \mathrm{C} / \text{cm}^{2}$</tex> in the imprinted state. Microstructural and elemental analyses conducted via Electron Energy Loss Spectroscopy (EELS), and atomic-scale integrated Differential Phase Contrast (iDPC) characterizations, demonstrate that the adopted structure not only stabilizes the ferroelectric orthorhombic phase in the central HZO region but also induces the formation of a large-period domain configuration. This reduces domain-wall density and mitigates domain-wall pinning. The insights gained from this study enabled the development of a mechanistic model correlating oxygen vacancy distribution with domain configuration, elucidating the synergistic achievement of high 2Pr and minimal voltage shift. This work offers a robust structural and processing solution for suppressing the imprint effect in ferroelectric memory devices and enhances the microscopic understanding of the interrelationship between oxygen vacancies and domain dynamics.

title: Analysis on SER failure mechanism and improvement by implementing RHBD in DRAM
authors: Jungho Jin, Chulseung Lim, Iloh Jang, Seyoung Kim, Kiseok Lee, Daesik Yim, Kiheung Kim, Yoongyu Song, Yuchul Hwang, Kichul Chun, Jonghoon Kim
abstract: Single event upset (SEU) caused by cosmic rays continuously occur throughout the device's lifetime and poses serious threats to the quality and reliability of dynamic random access memory (DRAM). The dominant mechanism of single event functional interrupt (SEFI) in DRAM was identified and verified through both simulation and neutron-irradiation experiment. SEFI is a misaddressed phenomenon caused by latch flip of redundant circuit in DRAM. In order to mitigate the vulnerability of the dual interlocked storage cell (DICE) latch, the charge sharing isolation (CSI) design was applied to the latest <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$12-\text{nm}$</tex> process LPDDR5x (LP5x) product. After applying the CSI design, soft error rate (SER) improved by 61.8 % at the unit latch level and 75.6% at the product level compared with the previous generation. A SEFI-induced malfunction in DRAM does not cause cumulative damages, it can be recovered by initializing or rebooting the device. The newly introduced radiation hardened by design (RHBD) technique will be adopted in DRAM and will contribute to the improvement of DRAM reliability.

title: Communication-Sensing Joint Anti-Jamming in DMA-Assisted Integrated Sensing and Communication System
authors: Shiheng Jiao, Zhou Zhong, Xiaoming Xu, Shuaifang Xiao, Keming Ma
abstract: This letter proposes a DMA-assisted joint anti-jamming scheme for ISAC systems. By densely arranging DMA elements, we enhance spatial processing gain and compensate for mutual coupling via equivalent channels. We formulate a mixed-integer problem to maximize sensing SINR under communication constraints, solved by an SDP-GA-AO algorithm. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed scheme achieves a 5 dB sensing SINR gain over existing benchmarks, with lower computational cost.

title: Cross-Domain Resource Scheduling and QoS Guarantee in LEO Satellite Networks: A Multi-Level Hypergraph Approach
authors: Yaping Cui, Yan Zhu, Dapeng Wu, Peng He, Ruyan Wang
abstract: Low earth orbit (LEO) satellite networks, as integrated service systems, are typically divided into domains based on application functions, such as observation and communication. However, independent domain resources and the high dynamics of satellites make cross-domain resource interactions difficult to capture, posing challenges to large-scale satellite network resource coordination and degrading the quality of service (QoS) for task flows. To address this, a multi-level hypergraph (MLH) is introduced to represent domain resources across temporal, resource type and spatial dimensions. MLH consolidates similar resource features via hyperedges, reducing redundant connections. The cross-domain resource coordination problem is then modeled as a mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) problem to maximize the sum of the minimum priorities of scheduled tasks. Furthermore, by leveraging MLHs topological nesting, a multi-dimensional resource dual-level scheduling algorithm (MRDSA) is proposed, decomposing the problem into two subproblems solved using the hyperpath scheduling algorithm (HSA) and internal path scheduling algorithm (IPSA). Simulations demonstrate that the proposed method enhances QoS, reduces computational complexity and improves resource utilization ratio in LEO satellite networks.

title: Designing an Emulation Platform for Realistic Experimentation on Quantum Networks
authors: Blanca Lopez, Ivan Vidal, Francisco Valera, Diego R. Lopez
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: The realization of a Quantum Internet is expected to transform communication networks by enabling intrinsically secure information exchange and distributed quantum applications. However, the experimental study of large-scale quantum networks remains limited by the high cost and operational constraints of quantum hardware. To address this challenge, this paper presents the design of a platform conceived to support research and experimentation in the field of quantum networking. The proposed system provides an emulation environment that mirrors the behavior of a real quantum network, enabling researchers to test protocols and mechanisms through a set of quantum operation instructions accessible via an Application Programming Interface (API). Emulated quantum nodes can be deployed across physically distributed locations, using physical or virtual machines or virtualization containers, allowing experiments to reproduce realistic network conditions and inter-node interactions. This approach enables applications to be executed over a persistent, configurable, and interactive environment that integrates both quantum and classical communication layers. To validate the feasibility of the proposed design, a prototype has been built and used to demonstrate its operation through a multi-hop quantum teleportation proof of concept. The results confirm the viability and flexibility of the architecture, laying the foundation for a distributed framework that facilitates the development and evaluation of quantum network architectures, control mechanisms, and experimental use cases.

title: QuMod: Parallel Quantum Job Scheduling on Modular QPUs Using Circuit Cutting
authors: Vinooth Kulkarni, Aaron Orenstein, Xinpeng Li, Shuai Xu, Daniel Blankenberg, Vipin Chaudhary
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: The quantum computing community is increasingly positioning quantum processors as accelerators within classical HPC workflows, analogous to GPUs and TPUs. However, many real-world applications require scaling to hundreds or thousands of physical qubits to realize logical qubits via error correction. To reach these scales, hardware vendors employing diverse technologies-such as trapped ions, photonics, neutral atoms, and superconducting circuits-are moving beyond single, monolithic QPUs toward modular architectures connected via interconnects. For example, IonQ has proposed photonic links for scaling, while IBM has demonstrated a modular QPU architecture by classically linking two 127-qubit devices. Using dynamic circuits, Bell-pairbased teleportation, and circuit cutting, they have shown how to execute a large quantum circuit that cannot fit on a single QPU. As interest in quantum computing grows, cloud providers must ensure fair and efficient resource allocation for multiple users sharing such modular systems. Classical interconnection of QPUs introduces new scheduling challenges, particularly when multiple jobs execute in parallel. In this work, we develop a multi-programmable scheduler for modular quantum systems that jointly considers qubit mapping, parallel circuit execution, measurement synchronization across subcircuits, and teleportation operations between QPUs using dynamic circuits.

title: A Formal Verification Approach for Measures of Effectiveness Based on Satisfiability Modulo Theories
authors: Yongji Yuan, Guoxin Wang, Yihui Gong, Jinzhi Lu, Shouxuan Wu, Mengru Dong
abstract: Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) provides a formal and integrated framework for managing multidisciplinary information throughout the system design lifecycle. Within this framework, Measures of Effectiveness (MOEs) serve as the key quantitative metrics for evaluating whether a system design meets mission requirements. Current approaches for verifying MOE constraints in MBSE typically rely on mathematical relationships established through SysML parametric diagrams, with metrics analysis performed using built-in computational plug-ins or external specialized software. However, these approaches face challenges in unified representation of multitheory hybrid constraints, automated exploration for feasible solutions, and tool-level data consistency. This limits the reliability and completeness of verification results. This paper proposes a formal verification approach for MOE by integrating the multi-architecture modeling language KARMA with Satisfiability Modulo Theories (SMT). The approach provides a unified firstorder logic (FOL) representation of MOE-related metrics. It also automates the evaluation of SysML parametric diagrams through SMT solver, enabling constraint satisfiability checking and reverse derivation of optimal design solutions. A satellite electric power system (EPS) case study demonstrates the feasibility and effectiveness of the proposed approach. The results show that the approach ensures the consistency between system metrics and requirements, and improves automation and completeness of MOE verification within the MBSE framework.

title: Predictive Quality Engineering in Distributed Data Platforms Using Machine Learning
authors: Jay Bharat Mehta
abstract: This article examines the role of predictive quality engineering in distributed data platforms by applying machine learning techniques to telemetry data. Modern software systems emit large volumes of logs, metrics, and traces, yet much of this data remains underutilized for quality assurance and failure prevention. This paper presents a practical framework that integrates telemetry ingestion, preprocessing, feature engineering, model selection, and real-time feedback loops to enable early detection of reliability risks. The framework is illustrated through three representative use cases: log anomaly detection, prediction of test flakiness in CI pipelines, and forecasting of performance regressions.In operational deployments used as case studies, the approach reduced major incident frequency by approximately 70%, decreased mean time to resolution by more than 65%, and improved detection of unstable test behavior with F1-scores above 0.85 when compared to rule-based baselines. Rather than proposing a novel algorithm, this work focuses on the systems architecture, engineering practices, and deployment considerations required to operationalize predictive quality engineering in real-world environments. The results suggest that telemetry-driven predictive methods can transform quality assurance from a reactive activity into a proactive engineering discipline.

title: Dual-Branch Cross-Temporal Graph Neural Network for EEG-based Depression Detection
authors: Yiye Wang, Tao Zhao, Hao Yang, Xiaoyan Zhou, Wenming Zheng
abstract: Recent studies have demonstrated the effectiveness of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) in EEG-based depression detection. However, two critical challenges remain unresolved. These challenges limit the ability of GNN-based methods to fully exploit spatio-temporal (ST) features for EEG-based depression detection. Firstly, the depression-relevant cross-temporal interactions that represent the complex relationships between different electrodes across various time steps are not fully considered. Secondly, although depression is closely associated with multiple distributed brain regions and their interactions, the existing methods provide limited exploration of brain-region-level ST features, failing to fully exploit the depression-related neural patterns at the brain-region level. To tackle these limitations, in this paper we propose a Dual-branch Cross-Temporal Graph Neural Network (DCTGNN) to effectively capture cross-temporal (CT) interactions. Specifically, a CT graph structure is introduced to explicitly model relationships between electrodes (or regions) across different time steps. To extract region-level ST features, we design a dedicated region-level branch that integrates electrode features into brain regions. A region-level CT graph is then constructed to capture region-level ST interactions. Extensive experiments validate the effectiveness of DCTGNN, achieving consistent improvements across the three datasets. Furthermore, CT graph-based analysis reveals that the frontal and parietal lobes are highly associated with depression, aligning with previous studies.

title: Privacy and Performance Trade-Offs in Both Estimation and Detection for Large-Scale Systems
authors: Xinlei Li, Tao Liu, Kun Liu, Chenggang Xia, Yong-po Zhang, Yuanqing Xia
publish: IEEE Transactions on Control of Network Systems
abstract: This paper investigates the trade-offs between privacy and performance in both estimation and detection for large-scale systems. Each subsystem estimates its local state using local information and data received from its neighbours. Considering the unreliability of the communication networks, we assume that the privacy data is vulnerable to eavesdropping and bias injection attacks. To maintain privacy, we propose a stochastic quantization-based privacy scheme to preserve the measurement outputs, where the privacy level is measured by differential privacy. However, quantization may lead to degradation in both estimation and detection performance. Therefore, we firstly investigate the trade-off between privacy level and estimation performance and establish an optimization problem to obtain the optimal quantization interval. Then, we analyze the trade-off between privacy level and detection performance and formulate an optimization problem to obtain the optimal quantization interval. Finally, a numerical example is provided to verify the effectiveness of theoretical results.

title: Automatic Red Teaming LLM-based Agents with Model Context Protocol Tools
authors: Ping He, Changjiang Li, Binbin Zhao, Tianyu Du, Shouling Ji
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security
abstract: The remarkable capability of large language models (LLMs) has led to the wide application of LLM-based agents in various domains. To standardize interactions between LLM-based agents and external resources, model context protocol (MCP) tools have become the de facto standard and are now widely integrated into these agents. However, the incorporation of MCP tools introduces the risk of tool poisoning attacks, in which malicious MCP tools can steer the behavior of LLM-based agents toward unintended outcomes. Although previous studies have identified such vulnerabilities, their red teaming approaches have largely remained at the proof-of-concept stage, leaving the automatic red teaming of LLM-based agents under the MCP tool poisoning paradigm an open question. To bridge this gap, we propose AutoMalTool, an automated red teaming framework for LLM-based agents by generating malicious MCP tools. Our extensive evaluation shows that AutoMalTool effectively generates malicious MCP tools capable of manipulating the behavior of mainstream LLM-based agents while evading current detection mechanisms, thereby revealing new security risks in these agents.

title: Quality-Aware Minutiae Matching for Forensic Fingerprints with Explicit No-Score Decisions
authors: Ziyi Wang
abstract: Fingerprint recognition in forensic scenarios remains challenging due to noise, partial impressions, unstable ridge structure, and frequent matching failures under low evidence.We present a reproducible and auditable forensic fingerprint pipeline that integrates robust preprocessing, multi-scale orientation fusion, structure-guided minutiae extraction, and adaptive minutiae matching with geometric consistency and quality-aware score fusion, together with an explicit no-score (abstention) mechanism driven by per-pair quality and geometric-consensus telemetry; an optional lightweight edge embedding branch (LightFeatNet) is provided for fast deployment. Experiments on FVC2002, a controlled robustness benchmark, and NIST SD302 genuine latent fingerprints show that profile-level operating control enables practical accuracy–reliability trade-offs, and that the abstention mechanism is operationally necessary on forensic-quality inputs (67.1% abstention on SD302). We further quantify operational behavior by reporting sensitivity to no-score handling, coverage–accuracy trade-offs, bootstrap confidence intervals for key metrics, and a cross-dataset out-of-distribution check for the learning branch. A side property of our design is that the abstention mechanism is implemented as a modular component that depends only on four per-pair telemetry fields (orientation coherence, extraction failure, inlier count, and the pair score itself); we verify this modularity by applying it on top of NBIS with the same parameters, improving NBIS from EER=0.053 to 0.032 and TPR@FAR=10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−3</sup>=0.832 to 0.925 on the scored subset, which is additional evidence that the abstention policy is a clean component rather than an ad-hoc trick bound to our scorer.

title: Real-time Dual Sensing of cTnI and Myoglobin via Printable Graphitized Electrodes_supp1-3690757.pdf
authors: Sanket Goel
abstract: Acute myocardial infarction (AMI) has emerged as a serious global health concern. According to the World Health Organization (WHO), health authorities report approximately 100 million cases annually worldwide, with about 9 million associated mortalities. As AMI can affect individuals regardless of their age or prior health status, making early and pre-symptomatic detection is crucial for clinical intervention. Targeting AMI biomarkers such as cardiac troponin I (cTnI) and myoglobin is therefore essential for timely diagnosis and treatment. Hence, the development of ultra-sensitive and highly specific sensors is crucial. Herein, a laser-induced graphene (LIG)-based electrochemical biosensor capable of dual detection of two pivotal AMI biomarkers, cTnI and myoglobin, is presented, hence addressing the need for simultaneous biomarker monitoring. Electrochemical analytical techniques such as Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) techniques were employed, achieving limits of detection (LoD) and quantification (LoQ) of 0.035 ng/mL and 0.105 ng/mL for cTnI, and 1.39 ng/mL and 4.25 ng/mL for myoglobin, respectively. The fabricated electrodes exhibited excellent stability and reproducibility, supported by physicochemical characterizations confirming detailed electrode morphology. Furthermore, this dual-detection platform was successfully tested for real-time detection in human blood serum, yielding high recovery values and demonstrating its potential for practical, real-world applications.

title: An Intermediate Electrolytic Capacitor-Less Single-Stage Wireless Power Transfer Converter with Direct Power Conversion and Power Factor Correction
authors: Guangdi Li, Zicheng Wang, Bowen Zhou, Dongsheng Yang, Yan-fei Liu
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Industrial Electronics
abstract: An intermediate electrolytic capacitor-less single-stage wireless power transfer (WPT) converter with direct power conversion and power factor correction (PFC) is analyzed and implemented in this paper. The proposed single-stage WPT converter eliminates the intermediate electrolytic capacitors that are typically required in traditional multi-stage power conversion systems. The proposed direct power conversion topology integrates both PFC stage and WPT stage into a single stage, resulting in a simple, compact and efficient design with a reduced number of components. Furthermore, eliminating the intermediate electrolytic capacitors improves long-term reliability and reduces maintenance requirements. What's more, the single-stage architecture enhances overall efficiency by minimizing the number of components and soft switching operation. Finally, an experimental prototype is built and tested to validate the proposed topology, and the experimental results confirm its feasibility and performance. The system demonstrates efficient power conversion, stable wireless power transfer, and soft switching operation. The advantages of reduced size, the conventional plug-in system, WPT offers significant advantages such as convenience, no physical connection, safe operation, isolation, and so on. These benefits make it highly applicable in applications such as consumer electronics, medical devices, smart homes, and electric vehicles [4-9]. For example, in the consumer sector, wireless charging of cell phones can greatly enhance user experience [10]; in the electric vehicle industry, wireless charging has the potential to revolutionize charging station infrastructure, making it more intelligent and convenient [11]. Multi-stage Transmitter Vin PFC DC/AC Receiver M RL Fig. 1 Two-stage circuit architecture for multi-stage WPT converter [12]. intermediate electrolytic capacitor-less, and soft-switching operation make the proposed converter a promising solution for the WPT applications.

title: Modeling Product-Process-Resource Variability in Early Design Phases of Production Systems
authors: Lasse Beers, Hamied Nabizada, Alain Chahine, Felix Gehlhoff, Alexander Fay
abstract: Designing modern production systems requires models that can represent structural and behavioral complexity while systematically capturing variability across products, processes, and resources. This paper introduces an integrated modeling approach that combines Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) with feature modeling to address this challenge. Dedicated Systems Modeling Language (SysML) profiles formalize the semantics of the Product-Process-Resource (PPR) domains by aligning product descriptions with ISO 10303, process representations with VDI/VDE 3682, and resource structures with the OPC UA Robotics specification. Variability within and across these domains is expressed through feature models enriched with Object Constraint Language (OCL) rules, enabling the explicit specification of dependencies such as material-tool compatibility or process-resource requirements. By linking system models with variability models through variation points, the approach supports automated derivation of valid PPR configurations and ensures consistent propagation of design decisions across all domains. This allows engineers to analyze how product features influence required process steps and resource capabilities, and conversely, how resource limitations constrain feasible product variants. The method is demonstrated using an industrial use case from aircraft structural assembly, where variant-dependent drilling and riveting operations impose strict constraints on tooling and sequencing. The results indicate that the proposed approach supports early-phase decision-making by improving traceability, reducing configuration inconsistencies, and offering a structured basis for exploring design alternatives in complex, variant-rich production environments.

title: $\mathrm{D}_{\text{it}}-\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{t}}$ Correlation in Pbti Stressed Soi Nmosfet Via Low Frequency Noise Measurements
authors: A. Albouy, A. G. Viey, F. Ponthenier, S. Joblot, D. Golanski, J. Lacord
abstract: This paper investigates the correlation between interface trap density <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{D}_{\text {it }}$</tex> and oxide trap density near the interface <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$N_{t}$</tex> by performing Low Frequency Noise (LFN) measurements on pBTI degraded Silicon-On-Insulator (SOI) nMOS. Mesa isolated SOI nMOS transistors with nitrided gate oxide dedicated to 2.5 V applications were characterized under various stress conditions. LFN measurements were conducted on both unstressed and stressed devices. The results reveal the presence of flicker noise across all stress conditions, along with a significant increase in noise levels with prolonged stress times. The <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$N_{t}$</tex> parameter was extracted by fitting the experimental data to the CNF/CMF flicker noise model. The results demonstrate a significant increase in <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$N_{t}$</tex> with stress time. This increase in <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$N_{t}$</tex> is further correlated with the evolution of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$D_{\text {it }}$</tex> with stress time. The study highlights the critical impact of interface quality on LFN levels in devices where degradation is induced exclusively by stress conditions, rather than variations in fabrication processes.

title: Robust Power-Rail Clamp Circuit for ESD and Surge Protection in FinFET Integrated Circuits
authors: Ya-Ting Lin, Ming-Dou Ker, Ta-Yi Huang, Jih-San Li
abstract: A novel FinFET device structure is proposed to achieve a balanced design between Electrostatic Discharge (ESD) robustness and Electrical Overstress (EOS) Surge immunity for FinFET integrated circuits (ICs). FinFET devices become increasingly susceptible to long pulse EOS events due to reduced effective silicon volume, worse thermal dissipation, and lower breakdown voltage. Currently, industry practices primarily depend on circuit design methodologies to mitigate EOS Surge events. However, several critical challenges must be addressed in circuit design, including design complexity, response delay, and power consumption. To overcome these issues, a component-level solution on the FinFET device with merged LDD implantation under the dummy gate was proposed to effectively enhance HBM robustness and Surge immunity of the power-rail clamp circuit. Notably, these improvements were achieved without the necessity for additional mask or process step, simplifying the fabrication process, and reducing both manufacturing costs and complexity for using in FinFET ICs.

title: Telemetry-Driven Time-Dependent Correction Factor Modeling for Shape Parameter Adjustment in Predictive DRAM Reliability
authors: Hyunwoo Cha, JungChul Lee, Eunchul Kwon, Anthony Yoon, Hyun-Ah Park, Karis Kwon, Jonghoon Kim
abstract: As demand for new data center deployments continues to grow, an increasing fraction of DRAM products are evaluated and operated during early deployment stages, including newly launched devices. Under these conditions, reliability assessment must often rely on limited early-life data, where short observation windows fundamentally hinder stable estimation of hazard behavior, particularly the Weibull shape parameter. Bias introduced at this stage propagates directly into long-term reliability projections. In this work, we show that long-term reliability prediction is fundamentally limited not by the choice of forecasting models, but by systematic bias in extrapolating the Weibull shape parameter from short-term observations, which persists across both statistical and machine-learning-based approaches. To address this limitation, we define a timedependent correction factor based on recurring temporal patterns of shape extrapolation bias consistently observed under similar early observation conditions across historical products. By incorporating this correction framework, long-term hazard behavior can be reasonably inferred from early telemetry alone, without reliance on long-horizon observations from the target system. Validation using large-scale field datasets shows that the proposed approach stabilizes shape trajectories, with failure-rate metrics emerging naturally as consistent secondary outcomes. The framework is broadly applicable to early-stage reliability assessment across field deployment, internal qualification, and customer evaluation scenarios.

title: Formalizing the Madelung Rule for Hysteresis Modeling in Piezoelectric Actuators
authors: Rui Li, Ru Xue, Kairui Cao, Zhi Li, Jinmei Xie
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: Piezoelectric actuators (PEAs) are significantly limited in positioning accuracy within the field of precision engineering due to their inherent hysteresis nonlinearity. To address this issue, a previously reported hysteresis description algorithm based on the Madelung rule successfully improved modeling accuracy. However, this algorithm is primarily presented in the form of flowcharts, which entails limitations such as semantic ambiguity and a lack of closed-form mathematical expressions, thereby hindering its deep integration with modern control theory. This paper aims to formalize the Madelung-rulebased description algorithm by deriving and establishing a unified Madelung analytical mathematical formula. Through a symbolic system, a rigorous and unified description of the algorithmic logic is achieved, eliminating the ambiguity inherent in flowchart representations. Furthermore, experimental validation was conducted using complex, non-periodic signals to verify the model's efficacy. Results indicate that the formalized Madelung model accurately predicts asymmetric hysteresis with a Root-Mean-Square Error (RMSE) of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.063 \mu \mathrm{m}$</tex> and a maximum absolute error within <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.176 \mu \mathrm{m}$</tex>. This research overcomes the limitations of the Madelung rule, which previously remained confined to a logical level, and establishes a solid theoretical and empirical foundation for high-precision piezoelectric compensation control.

title: Modeling and Stability Analysis of Generator-Side System for Semi-Direct Drive PMSG Wind Turbines Based on Linear Time Periodic Theory
authors: Shilong Liu, Jianhang Zhu, Wenyi Tian, Jiabing Hu
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: With the increasing size of wind turbines, the impact of periodic aerodynamic loads caused by wind shear and tower shadow effects on the stable operation of the units has become increasingly significant. Traditional analysis methods based on linear time invariant (LTI) models struggle to accurately characterize such periodic time-varying characteristics. Thus, for the generator-side system of a semi-direct drive permanent magnet synchronous generator (PMSG) - based wind turbine, this paper establishes a linear time periodic (LTP) state-space model for its generator-side system, which can effectively characterize the electromechanical coupling dynamics under periodic loads. Then, based on Floquet-Lyapunov theory, the influence of the speed feedback delay time constant on system stability is analyzed. The increase in the delay time constant is proven to induce system instability. Finally, simulation results verify the validity of the proposed analysis method.

title: Digital Product Passport for the Electronics Industry
authors: Timon Hoebert, Andreas Krimbacher, Tassilo Pellegrini, Christin Otto, Thomas J. Lampoltshammer, Klaus-Dieter Rauch, Mario Fasching, Anna Cossa, Munir Merdan
abstract: The increased production and use of electronic products in recent decades have resulted in a significant rise in electronic waste. The Ecodesign for Sustainable Products Regulation (ESPR) introduces the Digital Product Passport (DPP) as a key instrument for enabling data-driven circularity. Although the initial focus is on batteries, the electronics sector will soon be affected. However, practical approaches for integrating DPPs into existing industrial workflows remain largely unexplored. Our research addresses this gap by designing and prototyping a DPP specifically for electronic products that is aligned with ESPR requirements and relevant standards. Existing enterprise data and processes along the electronics value chain have been analysed, and the information, interoperability, and governance requirements for DPP deployment have been identified. Based on these findings, an agile data management infrastructure has been developed to enable smoother data flow within trusted DPP ecosystems. We also present a prototype DPP implemented in the electronics industry and demonstrate it on the use case of a remote controller.

title: Smart Curtain System for Real-Time Adaptive Directional Sunlight Control Using ESP-NOW
authors: Md. Mostafizur Rahman, Md. Tanvirul Islam, Rony Ghosh, Nahian Tasnim
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Smart home automation systems demand energyefficient, independent solutions that minimize internet dependence while enhancing comfort. Existing automated curtain systems face limitations- fixed scheduling, high power consumption and single-point failures. This paper presents a distributed smart curtain control mechanism that autonomously responds to real-time directional sunlight using multiple BH1750 ambient light sensors positioned for directional luminance data. The system uses an ESP8266 microcontroller as a master node with a TCA9548A I2C multiplexer wirelessly communicating with ESP32 receiver nodes via the power-efficient ESP-NOW protocol. Each ESP32 controls motor-driven curtain segments for flexible, fault-tolerant operation. Experimental results show 94.2% accuracy in directional light detection and sub-10ms communication latency. The system maintained steady communication within a 25-meter range. The distributed segmentation of sensing and actuation reduces the risk of full-system shutdown in case of node failure and supports scalability for multi-window installations. This low-cost ($45 per window), replicable design demonstrates efficient accumulation of light IoT protocols, split sensing and autonomous environmental adaptation without requiring constant internet connectivity or cloud infrastructure.

title: Research on the Application of Ultrasonic Phased Array Testing Technology for Nozzle Fillet Welds in Power Station Boilers
authors: Yanchun Mu, Jiansheng Hu
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: This paper analyzes the problems and difficulties associated with conventional ultrasonic testing (UT) technology for inspecting nozzle fillet welds in power station boilers. It outlines the principles and advantages of ultrasonic phased array (PA) testing technology and conducts an analytical study on the configuration of key procedural parameters for PA inspection of these welds. Furthermore, the application of this technology and its inspection effectiveness are illustrated with examples from the examination of nozzle fillet welds in power station boilers.

title: Survey-Based Analysis of Social Media Behavior and Clinical Scales for Mental Health Prediction Using Machine Learning
authors: Md Mahbubur Rahman, Dipraj Howlader, Chinmay Bepery, Md. Atikqur Rahaman
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: The impact of social media on mental health has become a significant global issue. The study uses ethically collected, survey-based behavioral data reflecting individuals' social media usage patterns and their association with mental health outcomes. This research aims to predict mental health outcomes, such as depression, anxiety, and stress, using social media data and machine learning (ML) and deep learning (DL) algorithms. A key challenge in this research is to work with imbalance dataset. Individuals with mental health issues are underrepresented, which makes it hard for predictive models to accurately identify at-risk users. The research applies machine learning algorithms like Logistic Regression, XGBoost, Random Forest, Support Vector Machines, Naive Bayes and so on. It also uses deep learning techniques like Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) to analyze social media usage patterns. The models are evaluated using performance metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score. Also shows correlation between different parameters from user's data. Stratified K-Fold Cross-Validation helps manage the data imbalance and Grid Search Cross-Validation for parameter tunning. The results show that models like Random Forest and Logistic Regression can predict mental health outcomes using behavioral data. The findings emphasize the value of ethically collected, self-reported behavioral data for non-invasive mental health monitoring, especially in resource-limited settings for Bangladeshi people.

title: Mobile Healthcare analysis: An approach to estimate performability and availability requirements
authors: Bruno F F Souza, Gustavo Callou, Jamilson R Dantas
abstract: Mobile cloud computing has garnered significant attention from both researchers and industry due to its potential to optimize mobile computing benefits across diverse sectors, including healthcare, sports and business. However, the intrinsic limitations of mobile computing, such as battery constraints and wireless network reliability, can delay the realization of mobile cloud advantages. The unified cloud-fog-edge computing system forms a tiered infrastructure designed to enhance operational connectivity for users across all network endpoints.In this context mobile health care system can be a proper environment to assess performance and availability because its critical data sharing and processing. This paper presents a hierarchical modeling approach based on Stochastic Petri Nets (SPN) and Reliability Block Diagram (RBD) to evaluate and estimate performability and availability of mobile healthcare cloud architecture systems. Our results demonstrate that service disruptions can be substantially reduced by implementing redundancy in components for mobile devices and cloud infrastructure and strategically analyzing some performance metrics like utilization, response time, throughput, discard probability, uptime and downtime. Our analysis reveals that the strategic use of redundant components has slightly improved the system availability by $0.5 \%$ and reduced downtime by $8.97 \%$. Our metrics show results that help in decision making in how to improve system performance.

title: Reliability of In-Silicon Etched Microfluidic Cooling Systems
authors: Venkata Chivukula, Paul Mescher, Husam Alissa, Dennis Trieu, Sashikanth Majety, Baris Dogruoz, Ali Siddiqui, Yann Meier, Samuel Higginbotham
abstract: Silicon etched microfluidic cooling offers a compelling path to managing future Graphical Processing Units (GPU) and Central Processing Unit (CPU) heat fluxes approaching 1 kW/cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>, but integrating liquid microchannels into the silicon substrate introduces important reliability challenges. This work presents a comprehensive, multi-stage evaluation of thermomechanical, hydraulic, and thermal reliability of in-silicon microchannel structures embedded within the advanced High Performance Computing (HPC) packages. Mechanical testing-including 3-point bend, thermal cycling, and warpage characterization-shows that devices with microchannels maintain or improve flexural strength, while microcracks observed only after 700-1000 h of extreme cycling do not propagate into active regions. Long duration clogging and erosion studies for over six months reveal only a small number of events causing major hydraulic changes, with no measurable silicon erosion. Node level GPU testing confirms stable junction-to-fluid thermal resistance throughout the stress period. These results demonstrate that properly engineered silicon microfluidics can meet the reliability requirements needed for deployment in next generation datacenter scale HPC systems.

title: RoboManipBaselines: A Unified Framework for Imitation Learning in Robotic Manipulation across Real and Simulation Environments
authors: Masaki Murooka, Tomohiro Motoda, Ryoichi Nakajo, Hanbit Oh, Koshi Makihara, Keisuke Shirai, Tetsuya Ogata, Yukiyasu Domae
abstract: We present RoboManipBaselines, an open-source software framework for imitation learning research in robotic manipulation. The framework supports the entire imitation learning pipeline, including data collection, policy training, and rollout, across both simulation and real-world environments. Its design emphasizes integration through a consistentworkflow, generality across diverse environments and robot platforms, extensibility for easily adding new robots, tasks, and policies, and reproducibility through evaluations using publicly available datasets. RoboManipBaselines systematically implements the core components of imitation learning: environment, dataset, and policy. Through a unified interface, the framework supports multiple simulators and real robot environments, as well as multimodal sensors and a wide variety of policy models. We further present benchmark evaluations in both simulation and real-world environments and introduce several research applications, including data augmentation, integration with tactile models, interactive robotic systems, 3D sensing evaluation, and hardware extensions. These results demonstrate that RoboManipBaselines provides a useful foundation for advancing research and experimental validation in robotic manipulation using imitation learning. https://isri-aist.github.io/RoboManipBaselines-ProjectPage.

title: Integrating Risk Management and Monitoring Strategies in Agile Software Development
authors: Firdews A. Alsalman
abstract: Agile software development requires continuous risk monitoring, lightweight governance, and iterative delivery, but organizational impediments (e.g., handoff friction, unclear rules, and weak documentation) often reduce the effectiveness of these practices. This study examines how Agile Risk Management Practices (APRM) relate to Challenges/Barriers (CB) and Project Outcomes (PO), and whether barriers mediate the APRM–outcomes link. We conducted a cross-sectional survey of N = 100 agile practitioners (Scrum Masters, developers, product owners, coaches) using a structured instrument covering proactive risk identification, ceremony-based risk handling, role clarity, built-in monitoring, and risk-sensitive outcomes (schedule/cost variance, escaped defects, risk closure, unplanned work). Common collaboration tools used by respondents (Zoom, Slack, Microsoft Teams) contextualize distributed coordination. Data were analyzed in SPSS with PROCESS mediation. APRM correlated negatively with barriers (r= −.511, p<.001). A multiple regression predicting outcomes from APRM and CB was significant (R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> =.19). In PROCESS (Model 4; 5,000 bootstraps), APRM reduced barriers (a= −.563, p<.001); Challenges and barriers were significantly associated with project outcomes when controlling for APRM (b =.421, p <.001), reflecting the adverse coding of the barrier construct (higher scores indicate greater barriers). The direct APRM→PO effect remained positive (c′ =.305, p=.002). The indirect effect was significant (−.237, 95% BC CI [−.355, −.131]), indicating mediation that appears inconsistent due to the adverse coding of the barrier variable. The findings indicate that stronger Agile risk management practices are associated with fewer implementation barriers and improved project outcomes. However, because the study relies on cross-sectional self-reported data, the results should be interpreted as associative rather than causal.

title: DRGM: Dynamic Rotation Group Masking for Low-Interaction Secure Aggregation in Internet-of-Vehicles Federated Learning
authors: Mingchen Ma, Mohammad S. Obaidat, Jingxiao Ma, Yantao Yu, Shousheng Sun, Xing Chang
abstract: Internet of Vehicles federated learning (IoV-FL) faces a practical privacy challenge: vehicles interact with a roadside unit (RSU) only for short and heterogeneous contact periods, participation changes rapidly, and missing uploads are common. These mobility constraints make many existing secure aggregation approaches either too interactive for brief vehicle-to-everything (V2X) encounters or too expensive in computation and communication. We propose Dynamic Rotation Group Masking (DRGM), a low-interaction secure aggregation scheme tailored to IoV-FL with an honest-but-curious RSU. DRGM forms small vehicle groups and pre-establishes masking material for multiple consecutive rounds, so vehicles can mask uploads across rounds without repeating per-round mask negotiations. When some vehicles drop out, DRGM allows the RSU to complete aggregation while keeping individual updates protected. Experiments on three traffic-sign datasets (LISA, BelgiumTS, and GTSRB) show that DRGM preserves learning utility comparable to FedAvg (reaching 93% on GTSRB after 50 rounds) and reduces aggregation cost across multiple secure aggregation baselines. For example, compared with SecAgg, DRGM lowers RSU-side computation time (1.06 s vs. 78.16 s) and reduces per-vehicle setup traffic (4.73 KB vs. 26.04 KB).

title:
Toward a Carbon-Free Aluminum Production: Analysis of Al
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    O
                    <sub>3</sub>
                    Reduction in Nitrogen–Hydrogen RF Plasma
authors: Igor B. Matveev, Serhiy I. Serbin
abstract: The conventional Hall–Héroult process for primary aluminum production, despite long-term optimization, remains energy-intensive and environmentally detrimental due to its reliance on carbon anodes and fluorinated electrolytes. This article proposes a novel, solvent-free alternative method based on the direct reduction of alumina powder (Al<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{3}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>) in a radio frequency (RF) nitrogen–hydrogen plasma. The core concept eliminates carbon electrodes and fluorine compounds, potentially yielding zero direct CO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> and perfluorocarbon (PFC) emissions. We present a detailed thermodynamic analysis to establish the minimum energy requirement for the hydrogen reduction of Al<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub> at ~<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$2000~^{\circ }$</tex-math> </inline-formula>C, calculated to be approximately 6.5 MWh per ton of aluminum. An energy consumption analysis for pilot and scaled-up systems is performed. Preliminary estimates from an APT-100 RF plasma system (~90 kVA) give a specific energy consumption (SEC) of approximately 12.1-MWh/t Al under pilot-scale conditions. Through plasma efficiency improvements and heat recovery integration, the SEC could be reduced to a projected 6.77 MWh/t, approaching the thermodynamic limit and roughly half the current industrial average. This approaches the theoretical thermodynamic limit and is roughly half the current industrial average. The process concurrently generates a valuable by-product, aluminum nitride (AlN), enhancing its economic viability. The findings indicate that RF plasma reduction presents a viable breakthrough pathway toward net-zero emission metallurgy, aligning with global decarbonization goals.

title: Dynamic Virtual Power Plant for EV Coordination for Enhanced Grid Performance and Customer Satisfaction at Higher Penetration Levels
authors: Adithya Ravikumar, Foad Taghizadeh, Sara Deilami
abstract: The rapid growth of electric vehicle (EV) penetration has raised concerns on short-term high peak charging demand and its impact on the existing low-voltage distribution grids. To address these challenges, various EV scheduling frameworks have been developed to enhance grid performance by mitigating voltage deviations, reducing energy losses and preventing transformer overloading. However, existing EV scheduling models exhibit limitations, particularly at high EV penetration level 60%-100%. This paper proposes a novel dynamic virtual power plant (DVPP) framework for EV scheduling coordination at high penetration levels, that integrates real-time EV customer satisfaction model and power loss reduction model. To enable flexible operations, a DVPP utilization strategy is proposed to prioritize and allocate power among DVPP clusters. Case studies confirm the proposed DVPP framework significantly outperforms the existing models by improving the customer satisfaction levels by up to to 45%, while reducing the power losses and voltage deviations across increasing high EV penetration level from 60% and 100%.

title: A Novel Intensity-Modulated POF-Based Sensor for Low-Cost 3D Displacement Tracking
authors: Şekip Esat Hayber, Yunus Görkem, Mustafa Kurban
abstract: This study presents a low-cost, intensity-modulated, polymer optical fiber (POF)-based, three-dimensional displacement sensor (3D-DS) system. The proposed structure uses a single-fiber reflective configuration for each axis, with transmitted and reflected light traveling through the same path. The complex “double-curved” response characteristic observed in dual-fiber sensors is eliminated. The system can simultaneously track displacement along the x-, y-, and z-axes using a mechanically modular transmitter module (TM) and reflector module (RM) architecture. Experimental results demonstrated a broad 0–12 mm monitoring range with an average sensitivity of 0.055 mV/μm, high compliance with the inverse square law (ISL) in the 4–10 mm range (R² = 0.997; 0.062 mV/μm), a linear operating window in the 6–8 mm range (R² = 0.986; 0.034 mV/μm; 50 μm resolution), and a highly linear optimized window in the 6.5–7.5 mm range (R² = 0.996; 0.033 mV/μm; 10 μm practical resolution). The repeatability (SD) remained below 3% on all axes, confirming the system’s mechanical and optical stability. These results demonstrate that the proposed 3D-DS system is a simple-to-align, multi-axis sensing platform with a favorable cost-to-functionality balance, rather than a direct replacement for premium single-axis metrology sensors. The proposed system is a practical and reliable sensor platform for structural health monitoring, robotic positioning, and biomechanical motion measurement.

title: Spatiotemporal Pathway Control for Targeted Drug Delivery: A unified Waveform Modulation in Molecular Communication
authors: Ming Tan, Yue Sun, Hanyu Xiao, Yifan Chen
abstract: Precise control of drug delivery requires coordination across multiple stages, including release timing, propagation dynamics, and targeting efficiency. To address this, a unified waveform modulation framework inspired by molecular communication (MC), under the broader concept of nanoparticle beamforming, is proposed to enable full-chain control over nanoparticle (NP) behavior from release through propagation to reception. Within this framework, pathway optimization is considered as a key component of channel-level pathway control and is implemented via magnetic-field-assisted navigation. The framework supports therapeutic-window regulation across diverse agents for safe and efficient delivery. Magnetic navigation is embedded spatiotemporal pathway control into channel-level routing to guide NPs through the vascular network. COMSOL Multiphysics simulations are used to model NP motion under magnetic spatiotemporal pathway control conditions. Two representative drugs with contrasting therapeutic windows, Digoxin (narrow window) and Ibuprofen (wide window), are used as case studies to evaluate the adaptability of the framework. Key evaluation metrics include maintaining the localized drug concentration between the minimum effective concentration (MEC) and the minimum toxic concentration (MTC).The COMSOL simulations indicate that magnetic-field-assisted pathway control can improve NP accumulation at the target region, with a 75.3% increase in successful targeting rate compared to the case without magnetic-field control. When integrated into the waveform modulation framework, this pathway optimization helps maintain drug concentrations within the therapeutic window for both case studies. For Ibuprofen, effective levels are sustained over a wide range, while for Digoxin, the system supports tighter regulation to reduce the risk of toxicity. These results suggest the potential of waveform modulation as a unifying control paradigm for drug delivery across the release, propagation, and target stages. Implementing magnetic pathway control at the channel level supports the applicability of the framework under the modeled vascular constraints. The results suggest its potential for generalizable and personalized delivery strategies in diverse therapeutic scenarios.

title: Asymmetric Calibration Method Applied to the Calibration of Four-port Composite Probe
authors: Rui-qi Wang, Xiyou Sun, Xinyu Lu, Lei Wang
abstract: In prior research, the capability of the four-port probe to detect three electromagnetic field components (Ez, Hx, and Hy) has been demonstrated. However, additional connectors and transmission lines introduced in practical measurements inevitably lead to considerable asymmetric factors within the multiport test system. The sensing coupling principle of the probe, based on differential-mode and common-mode operations, demands strict symmetry within the probe and the test system. The presence of such asymmetry thus introduces substantial measurement errors. Therefore, the asymmetric calibration method (ACM) is proposed and applied to the aforementioned four-port probe system to solve this problem. The ACM integrates existing rotating and non-rotating calibration methods. Unlike traditional methods using a single calibration factor, this method employs four calibration factors per field component to reduce errors. Two male-to-female adapters are introduced to add asymmetric elements in the testing process, with a highly symmetric microstrip line serving as the calibrator. Experimental results confirm the validity of the proposed ACM. Furthermore, standing wave measurements demonstrate that the proposed ACM effectively extends the probe bandwidth to 20 GHz.

title: Energy-Aware Federated Learning for IoT Intrusion Detection Using Latent Feature Encoding
authors: Vipin Kumar Kushwaha, Deepak Kumar Verma, Satya Prakash Yadav, Himanshu Gupta
abstract: With the rapid rise in Internet of Things (IoT) ecosystems, the attack surface of today’s cyber-physical infrastructure has expanded dramatically. Therefore, there is now an urgent need for scalable and privacy-preserving solutions for intrusion detection systems. Recently, a new paradigm, Federated Learning (FL), has emerged as a means for providing distributed IoT security. Despite this, real-world deployments face considerable hurdles with the heterogeneous capabilities of devices, non-independent and identically distributed (non-IID) data distributions, and significant communication overheads. To help mitigate these challenges, we present our energy-aware Federated Intrusion Detection Framework, which combines the concepts of latent feature encoding with three FL approaches. A compact encoder converts multi-source IoT traffic into a 64-dimensional latent vector, thus enabling efficient on-device training across a broad range of diverse data types. To further illustrate the effectiveness of latent feature encoding in improving the performance of FL models, we have evaluated our baseline centralized model, as well as three FL models (FedAvg, CFL, and FedProx). All methods performed well under heterogeneous conditions. However, CFL and FedProx both exhibited significantly greater performance than FedAvg, with accuracies of 0.967455 and 0.967914, respectively, compared to the accuracy of FedAvg (0.840155). To evaluate the feasibility of deploying our framework on resource-constrained IoT devices, we have developed an energy-aware simulation that models the battery capacity, compute load, communication cost, and client failure. For the combined dataset, the energy consumed by the full FL pipeline was 1055.3694 units, with a total communication volume of 199,106,400 bytes exchanged during five rounds. Ultimately, this analysis demonstrates the efficacy of latent feature encoding in enhancing the performance of FL models, and provides valuable information regarding the patterns of energy consumption for the deployment of real-world IoT intrusion detection systems.

title: Fractional-Turn Transformer with Flux Auto-Balancing Winding for High Power Density Primary Side Regulation Flyback Converters
authors: Song Ding, Lei Li, Qi Liu, Qinsong Qian, Weifeng Sun
abstract: The fractional-turn transformer (FTT) is an effective solution for reducing winding loss and enhancing power density. Meanwhile, primary-side regulation (PSR) provides an effective approach to improve the integration and power density of flyback converters. However, when conventional FTTs are applied in PSR flyback topologies, magnetic-flux imbalance among the outer legs would occur, which could introduce additional losses and distorted PSR sampling voltage. To solve this issue, this article presents the following contributions. Firstly, a general matrix-based analytical method for multi-leg magnetic structures is developed. Secondly, the flux distribution characteristics of typical FTTs and the resulting PSR failure mechanisms in flyback converters are systematically revealed. Thirdly, a flux auto-balancing (FAB) winding scheme is proposed for FTTs to inherently eliminate flux imbalance in PSR flyback converters. The proposed FAB-FTT scheme not only significantly reduces transformer loss but also ensures high PSR feedback accuracy, thereby enhancing overall efficiency and power density. A PSR flyback prototype with the proposed FAB-FTT was developed. Operating over an input range of 160–420 V and delivering 12 V/600 W, the prototype achieves a power density of 1290 W/in³ and a peak efficiency of 93.8%.

title: FALL: Fine-Grained Alignment and Local Information Aggregation Learning for Drone-Satellite Cross-View Geo-Localization
authors: Haolin Du, Jingfei He, Zihan Nan, Chenghu Mi, Zihao Chen
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Cross-View Geo-Localization (CVGL) remains challenging due to severe appearance variations across viewpoints. Existing methods primarily rely on global representations, which are insufficient to capture fine-grained semantic correspondences under cross-view discrepancies. To address this issue, we propose FALL, a fine-grained alignment and local information aggregation framework. FALL first aligns global and local features to alleviate semantic inconsistency across viewpoints. It then models implicit local relationships to enrich the global representation through a Local Information Aggregation (LIA) module. Finally, a Multi-dimensional Collaboration (MDC) module further enhances feature discriminability via cross-dimensional responses. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method, achieving robust and consistent performance across diverse cross-view scenarios.

title: Beamspace Equalization for mmWave Massive MIMO: Algorithms and VLSI Implementations
authors: Seyed Hadi Mirfarshbafan, Christoph Studer
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems I: Regular Papers
abstract: Massive multiuser multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) and millimeter-wave (mmWave) communication are key physical layer technologies in future wireless systems. Their deployment, however, is expected to incur excessive baseband processing hardware cost and power consumption. Beamspace processing leverages the channel sparsity at mmWave frequencies to reduce baseband processing complexity. In this paper, we review existing beamspace data detection algorithms and propose new algorithms as well as corresponding VLSI architectures that reduce data detection power consumption. We present VLSI implementation results for the proposed architectures in a 22 nm FDSOI process. Our results demonstrate that a fully-parallelized implementation of the proposed complex sparsity-adaptive equalizer (CSPADE) achieves up to 54% power savings compared to antenna-domain equalization. Furthermore, our fully-parallelized designs achieve the highest reported throughput among existing massive MIMO data detectors, while achieving better energy and area efficiency. We also present a sequential multiply-accumulate (MAC)-based architecture for CSPADE, which enables even higher power savings, i.e., up to 66%, compared to a MAC-based antenna-domain equalizer.

title: MASCA: Multi-Scale Adaptive Spectral Component Analysis for Hyperspectral Image Classification
authors: Alaa El Ichi, Olga Assainova, Nadine Abdallah Saab, Nesma Settouti, Marwa El Bouz, Mohammed El Amine Bechar
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: In this paper, we propose Multi-Scale Adaptive Spectral Component Analysis (MASCA), a frequency-domain preprocessing method that adaptively identifies discriminative Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) components through multi-scale peak analysis. MASCA assigns a relevance score to each DCT component by detecting magnitude transitions across multiple scales while integrating spatial coherence and spectral stability constraints, with hyperparameters tuned via Bayesian optimization. Experimental results on four benchmark hyperspectral datasets (Indian Pines, Pavia University, Botswana, and Salinas) demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title:
High-Coupling Thickness-Shear Mode Solidly Mounted Resonator Using Y-cut LiNbO
                    <sub>3</sub>
                    Thin Film via Transfer Bonding
authors: Ronghui Wang, Min Zeng, Wenjuan Liu, Zhiwei Wen, Yao Cai, Shishang Guo, Guoqiang Wu, Chengliang Sun
abstract: To meet the growing demand for wideband and high-power-durable radio frequency (RF) filters in 5G/6G systems, this work presents the design, simulation, and experimental demonstration of a thickness-shear solidly mounted resonator (TS-SMR) based on Y-cut LiNbO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub> thin films, achieving high resonant frequency and enhanced effective electromechanical coupling coefficient (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$k_{\mathit {eff}}^{2}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>). The device employs specialized spu-rious suppression structures: trapezoidal electrode apodization and piston-mode designs suppress <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$y$</tex-math> </inline-formula>-propagating spurious modes, while the trenches suppress <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$x$</tex-math> </inline-formula>-propagating spurious modes. The TS-SMR device is fabricated through a 6-mask process, in which a 5-layered 125 nm/165 nm SiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>/W Bragg reflector is deposited on a lithium-niobate-on-insulator (LNOI) wafer. The 350 nm Y-cut LiNbO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub> thin film is transferred to a silicon handle wafer through a wafer bonding process. Measurements of the TS-SMR prototype demonstrate resonance at 4.316 GHz with a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$k_{\mathit {eff}}^{2}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> of 22.18% and a power handling capability of 33 dBm with suppressed spurious responses. The prototype offers a potential solution for next-generation wideband RF filters based on LNOI platform.[2025-0146]
