title: EHSNet: An Efficient Hybrid Segmentation Network for Skin Lesion Segmentation
authors: Humayra Atia Joarder, Sk Md Raihan Shifat, Md. Anik Anjam Joarder
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Skin lesion segmentation is an important step of skin cancer early diagnosis, and conduces significant effects on the patient's survival. In this paper we present EHSNet, an Efficient Hybrid Segmentation Network for skin-lesion segmentation. Our framework exploits the effectiveness of the pre-trained EfficientNet-B4 encoder and incorporates the Hybrid Attention Mechanisms and Multi-Scale Global Context Aggregation. The hybrid attention mechanism facilitates the model to pay more attention on informative features, and the multi-sclae fusion module guarantees the integrity of context aggregation across distinct spatial resolutions, which is especially important for lesions with diverse sizes, irregular borders and complex backgrounds. We provide extensive experiments on ISIC 2017 and ISIC 2018 datasets that contain images with various skin lesion and demonstrate better performance in Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC) and Mean Intersection over Union (mIOU). The model obtained a Dice Similarity Coefficient of 87.32 and Mean Intersection over Union of 80.28 on ISIC 2017 data and it achieved a Dice Similarity Coefficient of 86.08 and Mean Intersection over Union of 78.24 on ISIC 2018 data. These findings indicate that EHSNet is a reliable, efficient, and extensible method for skin lesion segmentation and can be potentially applicable in clinical practice.

title: Physics Aware Mutual Information Feature Optimization for Effective Load Forecasting
authors: Tanvir Imroz, Fakir Sharif Hossain
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Load forecasting is critical for reliable and economic operation of modern power systems. While deep learning and hybrid approaches have achieved notable accuracy, they often lack explainability and physical interpretability. This paper proposes a Physics Aware Mutual Information Feature Optimization (PA-MIFO) framework for short term load forecasting. The method selects input features by maximizing their mutual information with the target load while minimizing redundancy and enforcing physics based consistency constraints. Using a publicly available dataset from power generation company of Bangladesh, PA-MIFO improves mean absolute percentage error by 14.2% compared to the typical LSTM trained on all features. The proposed method utilizes the hourly dataset from 2022 to 2024. The approach is model-agnostic, enhances interpretability and reduces data collection requirements for utilities. Results suggest that PA-MIFO provides a mathematically grounded and physically consistent approach to feature optimization in power system forecasting.

title: FASTVIS: A Lightweight and Hybrid-Compatible Method for Occlusion Detection in 3D Meshes Using Spherical Geometry
authors: Amin Khakpour Komarsofla, Meaghan Charest-Finn, Scott Nokleby, Joshua K. Pickard
abstract: This paper presents FASTVIS (Fast Angular Spherical Triangle VISibility), a fast and lightweight method for determining triangle-level visibility in 3D meshes from a single viewpoint. Unlike conventional ray tracing techniques that rely on spatial acceleration structures and intersection testing, FASTVIS projects triangle centroids and vertices into spherical coordinates relative to the viewpoint and determines visibility through angular overlap and centroid inclusion tests. This eliminates the need for per-triangle ray casting and enables rapid visibility classification using only geometric reasoning.The effectiveness of FASTVIS has been evaluated through a series of experiments comparing it to a triangle-based ray tracing implementation using the Embree kernel. The evaluation was conducted across a wide range of mesh sizes and ten different camera-to-mesh distances. The results demonstrate that FASTVIS consistently outperforms ray tracing in scenarios where the viewpoint is located near the surface of the mesh, where ray-tracing overhead and self-intersection artifacts become significant. In contrast, ray tracing retains an advantage at greater distances, where its acceleration structures operate more efficiently.These complementary strengths demonstrates advantages of a hybrid visibility pipeline, in which FASTVIS is employed for near-viewpoint scenarios and ray tracing is used when the viewpoint is relatively distant. Such a pipeline offers improved overall performance and adaptability in applications such as robotic inspection, mesh preprocessing, and view-dependent optimization. FASTVIS is portable, simple to implement, and provides a compelling alternative to ray-based visibility analysis in mesh-centric workflows.

title: Tunneling Based Atom Interferometry Using Bose-Einstein Condensate in a Double-Well Potential
authors: S Benel, Sandra Babu, Simon A Haine, Manju Perumbil
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: This work presents a numerical study of a tunnelingbased interferometer implemented using a Bose-Einstein condensate (BEC) confined in a static double-well potential, where the condensate dynamics are simulated using the one-dimensional Gross-Pitaevskii equation to capture the essential mean-field behavior. By carefully configuring the central barrier parameters, the double well is engineered to act as a coherent matterwave beam splitter. A relative phase is introduced between the two arms of the interferometer, enabling the extraction of phase information through interference. We analyze the resulting interference contrast to determine the best barrier configurations that allow the system to function effectively as an interferometer. It was found that the interferometric contrast remains robust under realistic fluctuations in the barrier parameters, confirming experimental feasibility. Using a fixed barrier for both the splitting and recombination steps, we avoid the need to dynamically ramp the barrier, as in other approaches. This allows us to realize a simple and experimentally accessible interferometry scheme in the double-well potential.

title: YOLOv11-IAF: An Improved YOLOv11 Method for Power Insulator Recognition and Detection
authors: Zeyu Zhang
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: The safe operation of transmission lines relies heavily on the stability of insulators. To address the challenges of small scale, weak contrast, and complex backgrounds of insulators in drone-captured images, we propose YOLOv11-IAF, an improved detection model based on YOLOv11. We employ Adaptive Training Sample Selection (ATSS) for dynamic positive and negative sample allocation. On the classification side, we utilize BCE with Logits combined with Focal Loss to strengthen the learning of hard examples and suppress easy examples in longtailed distributions. On the regression side, we jointly optimize with Distribution Focal Loss (DFL) and Complete IoU (CIoU). DFL models bounding box regression as a discrete distribution, providing finer-grained localization resolution and more stable gradients, which is particularly beneficial for refining small objects and weak-contrast boundaries. CIoU introduces center distance and aspect ratio components to the standard IoU, accelerating convergence and reducing the interference of degraded boxes. Synergized with a newly added P2 detection branch and CARAFE upsampling, the training process maintains stable updates in weak-texture regions. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method significantly improves small object recall and overall mAP@0.5/0.5:0.95, while simultaneously decreasing the false detection rate.

title: Physics-Informed Neural Network-Assisted Compact Modeling for Process Variation in Non-Volatile Capacitive Crossbar Arrays
authors: Gyujun Jeong, Jungyoun Kwak, Madhav Vadlamani, Junmo Lee, Shimeng Yu
abstract: Ferroelectric non-volatile capacitor (nvCAP) device is a new candidate for charge-domain computing. The impact of process variation (PV) is yet to be quantified. This work presents a physics-informed neural network (PINN)-assisted compact model that accurately captures effects under PV parameters for scaled nvCAP devices, demonstrating superior physical accuracy when trained on sparse training data compared to a purely data-driven neural network model. Array-level simulations reveal that PV-induced variations cause the effective number of bits (ENOB) variation to 20%, making tight process control crucial for reliable operation. This work highlights the advantages of PINN-assisted compact modeling in assessing PV-induced reliability predictions for emerging devices.

title: MacRandomizer: Automated IEEE-Compliant MAC Address Randomization for IoT Privacy Protection
authors: Minhaj Uddin Hassan, Nazia Sultana Plabon, Ashok Pal Emon, Mohammod Hamed Hasan, Joy Dey, Indra Jit Barua
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Modern wireless devices face persistent privacy threats through their hardware identifiers, with studies showing over 78% of public Wi-Fi networks actively track user movements. Existing MAC randomization solutions remain largely inaccessible for resource-constrained IoT devices, creating a critical security gap in our increasingly connected world. This research developed MacRandomizer, the first Arduino library implementing full IEEE-compliant MAC address randomization for microcontrollers. The novel approach combines bitmask operations with lightweight randomization algorithms to generate valid locallyadministered addresses while maintaining under 2KB memory usage. The system integrates seamlessly with standard Wi-Fi connection protocols through careful timing of address assignment. Experimental results demonstrated 100% standards compliance across 10,000 generated addresses, with zero connection failures in real-world testing. The solution added only 0.85ms (± 0.12 ms) to connection times while reducing identifier-based trackability by 97% compared to static MAC addresses. Testing confirmed compatibility across ESP32, ESP8266, and NodeMCU platforms. This work establishes a new benchmark for IoT privacy, making enterprise-grade anonymity accessible to hobbyist and commercial developers alike. The library's successful deployment in smart home and industrial applications proves its practical value. As wireless tracking grows more sophisticated, this solution provides essential protection for the next generation of connected devices.

title: Quality & Reliability Characterization of EMIB-T (TSV) Advanced Packaging Technology
authors: E. Armagan, A. Johnson, T. Chang, B. Lakshminarayanan, R. Bose, T. Yang, S. Kumar, R. Eluri, K. Jen
abstract: This paper presents package quality and reliability characterization of Intel's <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1^{\text{st}}$</tex> generation EMIB-T (TSV) technology as the next evolution of embedded multi-die interconnect bridge (EMIB). EMIB-T advanced packaging technology enables a direct power delivery path through EMIB bridges via through silicon via (TSV), from the package bottom to ASIC and HBM. As a result, EMIB-T technology significantly improves package power delivery efficiency and enables ultra-high-speed die-to-die (D2D) communications. This paper discusses reliability performance observed during initial development of EMIB-T technology. Thermo-mechanical reliability data collected with pathfinding test vehicles and observed fail mechanisms and improvements will be shared which have been used to build a foundation for robust overall package quality and reliability performance.

title: IoT-based Fall Detection System for the Elderly Using Machine Learning Algorithms
authors: Md Sabbir Hossain, Abdullah Al Niloy, Md. Akhtaruzzaman, Md Wasiul Haque Al Shaan, Md. Abdullah Al Noman, Md. Mahmudul Hasan
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Those who are elderly face a significant danger to their health when they fall. On the other hand, if the situation is not brought to light promptly, it can result in the loss of life or disability of senior people, which in turn lowers their quality of life. In this research, we propose a solution to this criteria by presenting a Fall Detection System that is based on the Internet of Things (FallDS-IoT). These systems are designed to detect falls that occur among senior individuals through the use of a wearable system. To obtain precise results while detecting falls, we make use of accelerometers and gyroscopes with our sensors. We divide the actions that elderly people engage in daily into 4 categories: walking, sitting, sleeping, and falling. Specifically, the K-Nearest Neighbours algorithm and the decision-tree algorithm are the 2 recognizable ML algorithms that we employ to handle the work described above. Accuracy rates of 97.85 percent and 89.65 percent, respectively, were gained by the dataset. In light of this, we were able to conclude that K-NN provides a higher level of accuracy when detecting falls, and that this method applies to categorization. Every time a fall happens, a Python script will send a preset message to a registered phone number informing about the incident. This message will be delivered whenever a fall occurs.

title: Enhancing the Sustainment Warfighter Function in Constructive Training
authors: Madison Oliver, Abigail Crocker
abstract: Though the U.S. Army has over two decades of experience in Live, Virtual, and Constructive (LVC) exercises, the fidelity of constructive and virtual sustainment operations has often lagged behind that of maneuver operations. Training for high-level staffs, in particular, often employs constructive components due to the logistical challenges and high costs associated with live training for large units. In recent years, the Army has begun implementing Operational Data Teams (ODTs) to fully utilize available data, improve real-time decision making, and enhance mission readiness. Combining an increased focus on live data and automated analytics with increasingly accessible data platforms and tools, corps staffs are empowered to use data at scale within their warfighting functions. The systems architecture presented details a method for more fully engaging sustainment members of a corps staff in the constructive portion of an exercise, enabled by unit organic ODTs. The architecture incorporates three distinct Integration Definition (IDEF) modeling languages to capture the functions, data, and processes used to address critical capability gaps. Furthermore, it provides a road map for other organizations seeking higherfidelity training and data analysis for sustainment members of a high-level staff, where live exercises involving all of the unit’s members and equipment are prohibitively expensive.

title: Cross-Condition Bearing Fault Diagnosis Based on TCN-CBAM-DANN
authors: Jianwei Wu, Changming Zhang, Quanli Jiang
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: Maintaining the operational integrity of rotating machinery hinges on the health of rolling bearings, yet shifting working conditions often compromise standard diagnostic models. To address this, we developed a TCN-CBAM-DANN framework for cross-condition analysis. This approach integrates Temporal Convolutional Networks to capture signal dependencies, refined by a CBAM mechanism to prioritize critical fault signatures. By employing a Domain-Adversarial Neural Network, the model synchronizes feature distributions across domains, ensuring robust performance under variable loads. Empirical tests on CWRU datasets demonstrate superior accuracy over existing benchmarks, confirming its efficacy in complex industrial scenarios.

title: Soft Constrained Reinforcement Learning for USV Dynamics System Identification
authors: Haipeng Li, He Shen, Yixin Yang
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: Dynamics system identification for unmanned surface vehicles is challenging due to the complex fluid-solid interactions and the requirement for labor-intensive tests. This paper presents a soft constrained reinforcement learning method to obtain stable and reliable dynamic parameters for unmanned surface vehicles using limited real-world trials. First, a simplified three degree of freedom planar model with nine unknown physical parameters is established. Second, a robust cost function with high-residual-ratio truncation is developed to mitigate the influence of outliers by discarding a small fraction of time steps associated with the largest residuals. To avoid losing physical meaning, soft constraints are applied to the unknowns through a sigmoid mapping. The means and variances of the unknown parameters are updated using a symmetric sampling policygradient scheme, which improves the consistency of the identified results. Finally, the proposed method is validated using real-world operation data. The identified parameter values exhibit improved consistency across operating conditions. Comparison against conventional least squares and Markov chain Monte Carlo methods demonstrates that the proposed approach effectively avoids physically implausible parameter estimates and excessive estimation dispersion, thereby enhancing the robustness of USV dynamic-parameter identification.

title: Machdishari: IoT- Enabled Aquaculture Using Federated Machine Learning and XAI
authors: Shishir Bhuiya, Md. Mahibullah Howlader
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: The integration of Internet of Things (IoT) and Machine Learning (ML) presents a transformative approach to enhancing traditional fish farming. Conventional fish farming often faces challenges due to high labor costs, unmonitored changes in water quality, outbreaks of fish diseases, and recommendations for optimal fish species. In this paper, we present the MachDishari system, which is an IoT-based ML recommendation system designed to automate water quality monitoring and suggest appropriate fish species based on various water quality parameters—including pH, temperature, TDS, water level, DO, and turbidity. We proposed work for real-time data on water parameters with real-time feedback. Federated Learning (FL) is incorporated into our design to ensure data privacy and to train machine learning models collaboratively without centralizing sensitive data. This approach preserves data privacy and enables farms in remote areas to collaboratively improve model performance without risking data leakage. XAI methods were applied in the present study to enhance the interpretability and transparency of fish recommendations. Among tested models XGBoost, which achieved the best performance, with 99.8% accuracy, was deployed in a web interface and app, enabling farmers to receive real-time water quality assessments and fish species recommendations, and is expected to enhance fish yield in modern aquaculture while preserving data privacy.

title: Radiation-Induced Instability Mechanism and Hardening Method of Nanoscale Devices
authors: Chang Goo Kang, Sujin Kim, Yongsu Lee, Jeong Min Park, Seungkwon Hwang, Yonghun Kim, Hae-Won Lee, Byoung Hun Lee
abstract: In this study, the effects of interface engineering on two types of nanoscale devices were investigated under gamma-ray and high-energy proton irradiation. The effects of gamma-ray irradiation on the electrical variations of molybdenum disulfide (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{MoS}_{2}$</tex>) field-effect transistors (FETs) with a buried local back-gate structure were investigated, revealing that device instability is mainly caused by interface carrier trap sites. Additionally, the radiation hardness of high-energy protons in ZnO thin-film transistors (TFTs) with a 3.5 nm thickness was investigated. <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{An} \text{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$</tex> passivation layer was introduced to significantly improve the radiation hardness of ZnO TFTs against high-energy protons of up to 100 MeV. These results suggest that irradiationinduced variability in nanoscale devices is mainly caused by interface states rather than the device itself and can be significantly minimized through interface engineering.

title: Applying Model-Based Risk Management to a Representative Unmanned Maritime System
authors: David C. Gross, Carmen E. Araujo, Daniel N. Horn, Naqash Ali, Matthew Bosworth, Christina Kalantzi
abstract: Risk management is a core element of systems engineering practice, yet it remains weakly integrated into ModelBased Systems Engineering (MBSE) workflows. In particular, the Systems Modeling Language (SysML) provides no explicit constructs for representing, resulting in risk assessments that are often maintained outside the system model and disconnected from architectural decision-making.This paper applies a previously developed model-based risk management method to a representative unmanned maritime system (UxS) to evaluate its portability and effectiveness in a domain dominated by operational uncertainty, autonomy, and mission-level trade-offs rather than technology readiness alone. A bounded UxS case is defined using a block-level system context and a limited set of representative risks spanning autonomy, power, sonar, integration, cybersecurity, schedule, and environmental considerations. Risks are formulated as structured IF-THEN statements, linked to system elements, scored using likelihood-consequence assessments, and traced to explicit mitigation actions within a SysML-based system model.Results demonstrate that the method transfers effectively across contrasting naval system contexts. Cross-case comparison with a prior electric ship application highlights that while the mechanics of the method remain invariant, risk interpretation and acceptability are strongly context-dependent. The findings suggest that explicitly integrating risk into system models improves transparency, traceability, and stakeholder communication for unmanned maritime decision-making.

title: Terman Method to Study Threshold Voltage Hysteresis of Si/SiGe Heterostructures at Cryogenic Temperatures
authors: B. Veryser, F. Stampfl, J.M. Gonzalez-Medina, C. Godfrin, B. Raes, D. Wan, K. De Greve, R. Loo, Y. Shimura, L. Panarella, M. Waltl, A. Grill, M. Houssa, S.E. Tyaginov
abstract: Semiconductor device reliability at cryogenic temperatures has emerged as an important research area driven by quantum computing applications. Si/SiGe heterostructures have established themselves as a leading platform for quantum devices, yet charge trapping at interfaces remains a key reliability concern. This work presents a phenomenological approach to characterize interface trapping in Si/SiGe heterostacks using fixed charge approximations. We demonstrate that threshold voltage hysteresis observed in capacitance-voltage measurements from 4.2 K to 80 K can be accurately modeled by assigning fixed charges at each channel interface. By combining this methodology with the Terman method, we extract the trap energy distribution, revealing a peak located approximately 0.1 eV below the conduction band with a maximum density of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$5 \times 10^{12} \text{cm}^{-2} \text{eV}^{-1}$</tex>. The extracted distributions show strong agreement with interface trap density of states reported in literature, supporting the hypothesis that fast-responding interface traps are the primary mechanism driving the observed hysteresis.

title: QKAN-LSTM: Quantum-Inspired Kolmogorov–Arnold Long Short-Term Memory
authors: Yu-Chao Hsu, Jiun-Cheng Jiang, Chun-Hua Lin, Kuo-Chung Peng, Nan-Yow Chen, Samuel Yen-Chi Chen, En-Jui Kuo, Hsi-Sheng Goan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Long short-term memory (LSTM) models are a particular type of recurrent neural networks (RNNs) that are central to sequential modeling tasks in domains such as urban telecommunication forecasting, where temporal correlations and nonlinear dependencies dominate. However, conventional LSTMs suffer from high parameter redundancy and limited nonlinear expressivity. In this work, we propose the Quantum-inspired Kolmogorov–Arnold Long Short-Term Memory (QKAN-LSTM), which integrates Data Re-Uploading Activation (DARUAN) modules into the gating structure of LSTMs. Each DARUAN acts as a quantum variational activation function (QVAF), enhancing frequency adaptability and enabling an exponentially enriched spectral representation without multi-qubit entanglement. The resulting architecture preserves quantum-level expressivity while remaining fully executable on classical hardware. Empirical evaluations on three datasets, Damped Simple Harmonic Motion, Bessel Function, and Urban Telecommunication, demonstrate that QKAN-LSTM achieves superior predictive accuracy and generalization with a 79% reduction in trainable parameters compared to classical LSTMs. We extend the framework to the Jiang–Huang–Chen–Goan Network (JHCG Net), which generalizes KAN to encoder–decoder structures, and then further use QKAN to realize the latent KAN, thereby creating a Hybrid QKAN (HQKAN) for hierarchical representation learning. The proposed HQKAN-LSTM thus provides a scalable and interpretable pathway toward quantum-inspired sequential modeling in real-world data environments.

title: Real Quantum Computers Applied to Audio
authors: Alex Alani, Eduardo Reck Miranda
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Applications of quantum computing have expanded beyond cryptography, optimization, or chemistry into fields such as audio signal processing. Processing a 0.5 second 440 Hz sine wave on ibm_fez produces nearly zero correlation at 100k shots, while simulation achieves near-perfect reconstruction of the audio signal, revealing significant NISQ hardware limitations for quantum audio applications on real quantum computers. The methodology involves comparing real hardware and simulation results through quantitative analysis of spectrograms, correlation values, and spectral centroids. This is early documentation of larger experiments of digital audio data being encoded and decoded on ibm_fez.

title: A Post-Disaster UAV-Based Communication Scheme: Victim Status Monitoring via Ambient Backscattering
authors: Ali Gaber Mohamed, Mohamed El-Rakaiby, Mohammed Karmoose, Mohamed A. Aboulhassan
abstract: Rapid deployment of resilient emergency networks is critical in disaster-stricken regions where traditional infrastructure is compromised. To address this challenge, this paper proposes an autonomous, self-organizing communication framework utilizing Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) as intelligent relays for victim status monitoring. Rather than treating all nodes strictly as power-constrained endpoints, our framework relies on distinguishing victim status-specifically prioritizing critical versus normal conditions-to drive autonomous resource allocation in an ambient backscattering environment. The core contribution is a joint optimization of UAV-to-victim assignment and time-slot scheduling. We introduce a priority-aware Time Division Multiple Access (TDMA) scheduler that autonomously balances the trade-off between outage probability and the Age of Information (AoI), ensuring that critical updates receive precedence. To enable robust autonomy, we develop and compare a gradient-based optimization approach with a Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) framework utilizing Deep Q-Networks (DQN). By modeling the system as a Markov Decision Process (MDP), the DRL agents learn optimal deployment policies and adapt to dynamic network topologies and heterogeneous traffic urgency without requiring continuous human intervention. Extensive simulations validate that the proposed autonomous architecture significantly outperforms baseline methods. The scheme effectively maintains connectivity, reduces collision rates, and guarantees the timely delivery of critical status information in high-density post-disaster scenarios, demonstrating the vital role of intelligent autonomous systems in complex humanitarian missions.

title: Toward a Model-Based Resilience Engineering Framework for Maritime Power Systems
authors: Christina Kalantzi, David C. Gross
abstract: Maritime power systems are undergoing a significant transformation driven by the electrification of loads, the increasing demands of high-energy mission systems, and deeper cyber-physical integration. These trends have substantially increased system complexity, which in turn raises the likelihood of faults and failures while complicating degraded-mode operation and graceful failure behavior. As a result, engineers face growing challenges in designing, verifying, and sustaining resilient system behavior under faulted and degraded conditions. It is well established that traditional document-based engineering approaches are insufficient to address these challenges. While model-based systems engineering (MBSE) and its evolution into digital engineering offer promising capabilities, the existing literature provides limited guidance on how these approaches can be systematically applied to engineering resilience in maritime power systems. This paper proposes a resilience-focused MBSE framework, implemented within a digital engineering context, for maritime power systems that supports early reasoning about loss events, recovery strategies, and associated metrics and evidence. The framework establishes explicit, traceable relationships among resilience-related needs, system architecture, recovery behaviors, and verification artifacts, while digital engineering practices enable continuous integration of information across design, simulation, test, and operational domains. By leveraging authoritative models and digital connectivity, the framework supports early identification of vulnerabilities, structured evaluation of recovery strategies, and lifecycle-consistent resilience assessment. The approach is intended to augment existing MBSE practices by making resilience-related assumptions, trade-offs, and evidence explicit, thereby supporting more informed decision-making in the design and sustainment of maritime power systems operating under faulted and degraded conditions.

title:
Improved Electrical Stress Reliability and Breakdown in MoS
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    FETs by Se-Alloying
authors: Aadil Bashir Dar, S Tehmeena Andrabi, Megha Yadav, Asif A Shah, Anand K Rai, R Archana B M, Rupali Verma, Utpreksh Patbhaje, Mayank Shrivastava
abstract: In this work, for the first time, we investigate the electrical stress reliability and breakdown behavior of Sealloyed <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{MoS}_{2}\left(\text{MoS}_{2(1-\mathrm{x})} \text{Se}_{2 \mathrm{x}}\right)$</tex> field-effect transistors (FETs), benchmarked against pristine MoS<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> devices. Devices were subjected to gate and drain bias stressing up to 1000 s to evaluate the evolution of key device parameters, including threshold voltage <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(V_{\text{th }})$</tex> and field-effect mobility <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\mu)$</tex>. <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$V_{\text{th}}$</tex> shifts are seen to be more pronounced in pristine MoS<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> compared to the alloyed devices. Furthermore, breakdown measurements performed under set current compliance revealed a higher breakdown voltage in the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{MoS}_{2(1-\mathrm{x})} \text{Se}_{2 \mathrm{x}}$</tex> devices compared to MoS<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> for identical channel geometries, confirming the superior robustness of the alloyed material. These results provide a systematic insight into the stress-induced reliability dynamics and breakdown mechanisms in alloyed <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{MoS}_{2(1-\mathrm{x})} \text{Se}_{2 \mathrm{x}}$</tex> transistors, establishing alloy engineering as an effective pathway to enhance the electrothermal stability of 2D semiconductor devices.

title: Construction and Practice of Evaluation Model for Curriculum Objective Achievement of Electrical and Electronic Technology Under Digital Transformation
authors: Lijing Yan
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: Driven by the dual impetus of digital transformation and engineering education accreditation, the traditional evaluation system for the course Electrical and Electronic Technology is confronted with prominent issues, including rigid evaluation dimensions, fragmented processes, insufficient data support, and obstacles in quantifying the achievement of course objectives. Centering on the core goal of course objective attainment, this study, guided by the Outcome-Based Education philosophy and framed by the Context, Input, Process, Product model, constructs a three-dimensional digital evaluation model consisting of the Objective Layer, Method Layer, and Implementation Layer. Technically supported by the Chaoxing Learning Platform, its AI Workbench and the Multisim simulation platform, the proposed model integrates formative, summative, and value-added evaluation methodologies to achieve full-cycle teaching data acquisition, dynamic diagnosis, and closed-loop optimization. Two rounds of empirical practice indicate that the experimental class outperforms the control class in key metrics such as course objective attainment rate, final examination scores, pass rate, and digital application proficiency. This model facilitates the transformation of evaluation from an “experience-driven” paradigm to a “data-driven” one, providing standardized quantitative support for engineering education program accreditation and offering a replicable implementation pathway and reference for the digital evaluation reform of similar engineering courses.

title: Robust and Reliable, Radiation-Hardened Digital Logic based Dynamic Voltage Comparator
authors: Kartikay Mani Tripathi, Nilotpal Sarma, Narendra Singh, Ashutosh Yadav, Madhav Pathak, Venkatraman Ramakrishnan, Sanjeev Manhas, Anand Bulusu
abstract: This work presents a robust and reliable digital dynamic voltage comparator suitable for mixed-signal designs. The design employs an input stage of logic gates with series/parallel stacked transistors driving a back-to-back inverting logic at the output, ensuring a wide input common-mode range and strong resilience against PVT and temporal variations. The circuit was simulated for delay and energy delay product (EDP) in commercial 65nm bulk and 22nm FDSOI CMOS processes to analyze process, mismatch, and aging effects. The averaged input offset standard deviation (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sigma$</tex>) remains low at 0.4 mV and 0.48 mV for 65 nm and 22 nm CMOS processes over various process corners (SS, TT, FF, SF, FS). Aging simulations in 22 nm show only 7% delay degradation after 10 years. Single Event Upset (SEU) robustness, validated using SPICE-level and 3-D Sentaurus TCAD simulations on a 180 nm measurement calibrated NMOS device. We extended this analysis and demonstrated that increasing the output stage NMOS width <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(5 \times)$</tex> increases the critical charge <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\left(\mathrm{Q}_{\text{crit }}\right)$</tex> by 100% and reduces delay by 70%, confirming the effectiveness of the sizing strategy for radiation tolerance and performance.

title: Research on Comprehensive Technical Benefit Evaluation of Power Transmission and Transformation Projects based on IFS-EW-TOPSIS
authors: Shuo Han, Jiayin Li
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: In the context of the “dual carbon” goal and the construction of new power systems, the focus of power transmission and transformation projects has shifted to the pursuit of advanced technology, system coordination and comprehensive efficiency. Firstly, based on the technical efficiency of the whole life cycle of the project, a multi-level comprehensive benefit evaluation index system of power transmission and transformation engineering technology is constructed from four dimensions: technical performance, system benefit, maturity and risk, and comprehensive benefit. Secondly, the intuitive fuzzy set theory is introduced to characterize the fuzzy uncertainty of expert evaluation information, and the intuitive fuzzy entropy weight method is used to calculate the objective weight of the index. On this basis, the introduction of game theory ideas to integrate subjective and objective weights, and the construction of a deviation minimization model to obtain the optimal comprehensive weight, effectively improve the scientificity of weighting. Finally, through the analysis of engineering examples and the comparison of methods, the effectiveness and robustness of the index system and evaluation model in processing fuzzy information and quantifying the comprehensive benefits of technology are verified, which provides a reliable basis for the technical route selection and scientific investment decision of power transmission and transformation engineering.

title: Hybrid Framework with Feature Selection and Explainable AI for IoT Intrusion Detection
authors: Arshadul Islam Sourav, Md. Saiful Islam, Umma Sabikun Nahar, Mahmudul Islam Nayon, Md. Tofael Ahmed
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Securing Internet of Things (IoT) environments has become increasingly critical as the scale and diversity of connected devices continue to grow, exposing networks to sophisticated threats such as DDoS, scanning, and exploitation attacks. Existing intrusion detection systems (IDS) often fail to cope with the high dimensionality of IoT traffic and the imbalance across attack categories. To address these challenges, we propose a hybrid framework that integrates Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) for feature selection, a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for representation learning, and XGBoost for robust classification. PSO reduces redundancy by selecting the 20 most informative features from the RT-IoT2022 dataset, while CNN generates dense embeddings that are classified by XGBoost. To improve transparency, SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) is employed to reveal the contribution of individual features at both global and local levels. By combining feature optimization, hybrid learning, and interpretability, the framework provides a scalable and practical solution for IoT security. Notably, it achieves an accuracy of 98.46%, surpassing conventional machine learning and standalone deep learning baselines.

title: Design and Implementation of an Ethernet Twisted Pair Cable Tester Based on FPGA and TDR
authors: Xiangyu Li, Xinghai Wang, Haibo Zhang, Wen Wang
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: Based on the objective of developing a portable multi-parameter tester for Ethernet twisted pair cables, this study adopts FPGA as the core hardware platform. The underlying logic is constructed using Verilog, integrating Time Domain Reflectometry (TDR) and matrix testing methods. The system supports both dual-end and single-end detection modes, enabling comprehensive measurements of cable connection relationships, cable type, cable length, short-circuit locations, DC resistance, and AC attenuation, with results displayed on an LCD screen. Experimental tests demonstrate that the system achieves millisecond-level measurement times for all functions, with all measurement errors falling within the design specifications. The system exhibits high integration, comprehensive functionality, stability, and reliability, fully meeting the design requirements.

title: Analyzing Road Network Inefficiency via Simulation: Towards a MARL Evaluation Testbed
authors: Shima Rahmani, Mélanie Bouroche, Brian Caulfield, Ivana Budinská
abstract: The Price of Anarchy (PoA) quantifies the efficiency loss that emerges in traffic networks when drivers make route choices selfishly rather than cooperatively. While prior work has shown that control interventions such as speed limit design or signal optimization can reduce this inefficiency, the mechanisms behind inefficiency and the conditions under which cooperation becomes worthwhile-remain less understood. This paper presents a systematic simulation-based analysis of how network inefficiency emerges from the interaction of infrastructure design, traffic signal control, and driver behavioural heterogeneity in a simplified road network with two competing parallel routes. From a complex systems and system-of-systems perspective, the network is modelled as an ensemble of interacting subsystems-drivers, control policies, and infrastructure-whose decentralized decisions give rise to non-linear and emergent congestion patterns. Results show that inefficiency is not solely driven by route choice, but is critically shaped by policy-infrastructure conflicts, signal timing, reaction time, and speed variability. Building on this analysis, we propose a scalable evaluation testbed for the Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) community. By identifying scenarios where significant quantifiable inefficiency ($\mathrm{PoA}\gt$ 1.25) emerges with as low as 150 agents (vehicles), our testbed addresses a critical gap for researchers who require meaningful experimental scenarios (e.g., for cooperative routing, intelligent signal control, and speed harmonization) and allows them to focus on algorithmic improvements and coordination techniques rather than the computational overhead of environment design or large-scale city simulations. Furthermore, the framework supports Curriculum Training; its high tunability, specifically regarding traffic signal ‘hostility’ and driver erraticness, allows researchers to incrementally increase the ‘efficiency gap’ that agents must close. This provides a clear, quantifiable benchmark for evaluating an algorithm’s scalability, convergence properties, and performance in bridging the gap between selfish behaviour and global optimum.

title: Model-Based Systems Engineering Framework for Trustworthy Agent-Native Financial Gateways
authors: Han Li, Benjamin Stirgwolt
abstract: AI agents are starting to initiate and settle payments without a human in the loop. Current financial gateways were designed for human-driven flows, so institutions struggle to provide model-based, auditable evidence that agent transactions are safe, compliant, and performant. This paper presents a Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) framework for an agent-native financial gateway that creates a reusable digital thread from requirements to SysML v2 models, STPA-derived safety constraints, executable policies, and runtime proof. We model the gateway control structure (agents, spend API, risk engine, ledger adapters, policy store) and encode trust requirements for safety, security, privacy, explainability, and reliability. A prototype SysML-STPA Safety Toolkit maintains reusable SysML v2 libraries, derives unsafe control actions and constraints, replays traces with scenario injection, and outputs an evidence bundle linking each decision to constraint IDs. Model status: the SysML v2 repository has been instantiated for a sandbox financial application using current gateway interfaces and regional policy rules, enabling end-to-end replay and constraint enforcement on synthetic traces. Planned evaluation compares detection against expert review, measures compliance-score uplift when privacy and explainability requirements are active, and quantifies median latency overhead when constraints are enforced at $1,000 \mathrm{TPS}$.

title: Detection and Classification of Poultry Diseases Using Deep Convolutional Neural Networks: A Ten-Class Image-Based Approach
authors: Sadikul Hasan Mridha Atul, A.O. M. Shamsuddoha, S M Monirul Islam, Mou Rani Biswas, Humayun Kabir Forhad Hai, S M Nawshed
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: The poultry industry plays a vital role in global food security but remains highly vulnerable to infectious diseases that cause severe economic and welfare losses. Conventional diagnostic practices are slow, require expert interpretation, and are unsuitable for large-scale or real-time use. This paper presents a deep learning framework for automated poultry disease detection and classification using image data. A balanced dataset of ten categories including nine major diseases and one healthy class was developed to train and evaluate three convolutional neural network (CNN) architectures: ResNet50, VGG16, and MobileNet. Unlike prior studies that focus on limited classes or single-model evaluation, this work provides a comprehensive benchmark across multiple architectures. Experimental results show that ResNet50 achieves the highest performance with 95.1 % accuracy (precision 0.96, recall 0.94, F1-score 0.95). VGG16 reaches 88.2 % accuracy (precision 0.86, recall 0.89, F1-score 0.87), while MobileNet records 74.4 % accuracy (precision 0.73, recall 0.71, F1-score 0.72), offering computational efficiency at the expense of diagnostic accuracy. These findings highlight the trade-off between deep residual networks and lightweight models, establishing a robust baseline for ten-class poultry disease recognition. The proposed approach advances intelligent livestock health monitoring and provides a scalable foundation for practical deployment in both commercial farms and resource-constrained environments.

title: Evolving Approaches to Requirements Management in Defence Programs: Balancing As-Built Fidelity and Forward-Looking Intent in the Era of Digital Engineering
authors: Amanda Weissman
abstract: Effective requirements management is critical for ensuring system traceability, scope management, verification, and alignment with strategic objectives in complex defence programs. This paper analyzes three primary requirements management strategies-as-built, forward-looking enterprise, and hybrid approaches-highlighting their advantages, limitations, and realworld implications observed in defence programs. As-built requirements enable agile adaptation but can obscure long-term objectives, whereas forward-looking enterprise requirements preserve strategic intent yet introduce verification challenges. Hybrid approaches, including synchronized-artifact and singleartifact strategies, balance operational accuracy with enterprise continuity, leveraging digital engineering to support lifecycle traceability. Practitioner observations and lessons learned emphasize that selecting a requirements management approach early and intentionally is essential for minimizing organizational conflict, maintaining digital thread integrity, and ensuring both short-term responsiveness and long-term system alignment.

title: Experimental- and Simulation-Based Analysis of SRAM Data Retention Voltage Degradation Under Consideration of Time-Dependent-Variability
authors: Christian Bogner, Christian Schlünder, Peter Huber, Gunther Lehmann, Hans Reisinger, Tibor Grasser
abstract: Simulation based assessment of the lifetime failure probability of SRAM macros at near nominal conditions has proven particularly challenging. This is due to the fact, that SRAM cell failures at nominal operating conditions occur typically at a parts per billion (ppb) level. As a consequence, a very large number of SRAM cells need to be simulated in order to estimate the Single Bit Failure (SBF) probability with sufficient accuracy. In this work we will present our approach to simulate SRAM macro yield during data retention. The investigation of data retention is critical since many modern SRAM macros support a low power mode, where the SRAM supply voltage is lowered during phases of inactivity, which reduces leakage currents and, thus, saves energy. Thereby, the lowest voltage where the SRAM macro can hold the data content without read/write access is the retention voltage, denoted as <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$V_{\text {ret }}$</tex>, is the critical figure of merit. In the following, the simulation approach to extract the SRAM macro yield as a function of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$V_{\text {ret }}$</tex> will be presented. Based on this methodology, also the impact of BTI degradation together with the impact of time dependentvariability (TDV) will be analyzed.

title: Resilient Cybersecurity and DDoS Attack Classification for AMI Smart Meter Networks in Smart Grid Environments
authors: Rayappa David Amar Raj, Advaith Krishna, Bhuvan Rajasekar, Archana Pallakonda, Rama Muni Reddy Yanamala, Edris Pouresmaeil, Jamshid Aghaei
publish: IEEE Transactions on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics: Systems
abstract: This article introduces a multilayer cyber–physical security system to protect advanced metering infrastructure (AMI) networks from growing cyber threats, integrating detection, control, communication security, and edge deployment. Key contributions of this article comprise anomaly detection using long short-term memory (LSTM) with multilayer perceptron (MLP) for classification of normal and attack data, achieving an average accuracy of 98%, and multiclass classification using attentive interpretable tabular learning (TabNet) with MLP, achieving an average of 95% accuracy across multiple datasets. Adversarial machine learning (evasion attacks) were tested with logistic regression and CatBoost, with logistic regression maintaining 98.49% accuracy under adversarial conditions. The models were deployed on PYNQ-Z2 and Coral Dev TPU boards, achieving 660 and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$15~\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>s per instance, respectively. A hybrid encryption mechanism leveraging advanced encryption standard-Galois counter mode (AES-GCM) encryption, module-lattice key encapsulation mechanism (ML-KEM), honey fallback, and hash-based message authentication code (HMAC) is proposed, with ciphertext-plaintext correlation of 0.14 and Shannon entropy of 6.87, ensuring quantum-resistant, secure communication. In addition, dynamic rate limiting and IP blacklisting offer real-time distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attack mitigation, and zero-knowledge proofs (ZKPs) reassure privacy and verifiability in anomaly detection. The framework displays exceptional performance, scalability, and robustness, making it a practical solution for securing AMI networks against a wide range of cyber threats.

title: Edge-Cloud Collaborative Calibration of Industrial Digital Twin Models
authors: Jinfeng Wu, Defu Yang, Shaowen Lu, Yajun Zhang
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Industrial Electronics
abstract: This paper proposes an edge-cloud collaborative model calibration framework for industrial digital twins that decomposes the traditional Kennedy-O'Hagan (KOH) Bayesian calibration into cloud-side surrogate training and edge-side bias compensation, enabling online calibration under resource constraints. Our key innovation lies in three aspects: (1) a formal task decomposition of the KOH framework that enables asynchronous cloud-edge calibration while preserving theoretical consistency; (2) a positive information gain active learning (PIG-AL) algorithm that selectively filters noise-contaminated samples for robust edge-side model training; and (3) a bidirectional synchronization protocol that maintains calibration accuracy across different update frequencies. Results from simulations and an industrial fused magnesia furnace case study demonstrate the effectiveness and practical feasibility of the proposed approach.

title: A Simple Proof of Nehari's Theorem Based on Duality
authors: Cristian R. Rojas
abstract: In this technical note we provide a simple proof of Nehari's theorem on the optimal approximation by <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathcal {H}_\infty$</tex-math></inline-formula> functions, based on convex duality.

title: Targeted augmentation domain-mixed network for single-source domain generalization fault diagnosis
authors: He Ren, Jun Wang, Zhongkui Zhu, Yi Zhang, Song Zhang
abstract: Machinery typically operates under constantly changing working conditions in real-time production. Directly applying a diagnostic model, which has been trained solely on monitoring data from a single working condition, to a new unseen working condition poses a notably challenging task called single-source domain generalization (SSDG) fault diagnosis. Current research mainly aims to augment source-domain training samples but struggles to maintain health state information invariance for diverse augmented samples. Therefore, a targeted augmentation domain-mixed network (TADNet) is proposed in this paper for SSDG fault diagnosis of rotating machinery. The TADNet constructs a targeted augmentation chain, which generates samples in an augmented domain with different distributions from the source-domain samples through a chained generation structure, and ensures the semantic consistency of state features. Additionally, a domain random mixing strategy is established to synthesize a new mixed domain with diverse distributions, through probabilistically and randomly mixing the feature statistics of the samples from the source and augmented domains, for further augmentation of samples. The TADNet effectively balances the distribution diversity and semantic consistency of augmented samples in the course of model training. The superior diagnosis generalization ability of the proposed method to unseen working conditions is demonstrated on two rotating machinery datasets.

title: Explainable Multihop Social Link Prediction Based on Temporal Logical Rules in Dynamic Social Networks
authors: Wei Jia, Ruizhe Ma, Li Yan, Weinan Niu, Zongmin Ma
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
abstract: The social link prediction poses a fundamental challenge in social network analysis, aiming to forecast missing interactions among users. Given the dynamic evolution mechanism of social networks, prevailing efforts have introduced embedding-based approaches to address temporal link prediction in dynamic social networks. However, these approaches often struggle to handle explainability, multirelations, and multihop relation prediction simultaneously. To overcome these limitations, we present an innovative multihop temporal social link prediction model based on temporal logic embedding (TLE), which leverages temporal knowledge graphs and logic rules. First, inspired by the temporal knowledge graph, we construct temporal social knowledge graphs (TSKGs) to model dynamic social networks. Then, we incorporate the orthogonal transformation matrix into the graph neural networks (GNNs), thereby facilitating the learning of time-aware relation representations. Furthermore, we define temporal social random walks from the TSKG to generate temporal social rules. Subsequently, TLE employs time-aware relation embedding to calculate the confidence associated with each rule. Finally, TLE combines the confidence score and time difference to obtain the final link prediction, providing explainability for multihop link predictions. The experiments carried out on four datasets indicate the superiority of TLE in the social link prediction within dynamic social networks.

title: Three-Dimensional PLL Model Considering Frequency-Impedance Coupling for Accurate Whole Process Dynamic Capture and Transient Stability Analysis
authors: Mingyao Ren, Ziqian Zhang, Chuang Liu, Zhe Zhang, Chao Liu, Robert Schuerhuber, Ikromjon Usmonovich Rakhmonov
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: Transient synchronization stability of grid-connected converter is critically affected by phase-locked loop (PLL) dynamics. However, frequency-impedance coupling induced by PLL frequency deviation is often neglected in conventional quasi-steady-state (QSS) PLL model, which may lead to an inaccurate post-fault stability assessment. To address this issue, this paper proposes a three-dimensional frequency–impedance coupling (FIC) PLL model that explicitly incorporates frequency drift during faults, enabling whole-process transient stability analysis from fault occurrence to recovery. Based on that, the existence conditions of stable equilibrium points and the evolution of the domain of attraction (DOA) is investigated comprehensively with respect to grid strengths, PLL parameters and fault durations. Furthermore, a DOA-oriented parameter design framework is developed to enhance transient stability. Finally, experimental results on a down-scaled GCC platform validate that the FIC-PLL model provides more accurate stability assessments than conventional QSS models, and the optimized PLL parameters effectively improve transient stability under weak grid conditions.
