title: Key Learnings from Deploying Scale-Up Fabric on AI Rack Solutions
authors: Anna Mary Mathew, Sharjil Khan, Krishna Kakarla
abstract: The rapid growth of AI workloads has driven the adoption of rack-scale architectures in modern data centers, where GPUs are interconnected through high-speed Scale-Out and Scale-Up Fabric networks to form unified computational domains. These scalable architectures deliver exceptional bandwidth and low latency, enabling large-scale model training and inference. However, they introduce significant operational challenges and reliability concerns across thermal, mechanical, power, and firmware domains. The effect of these failures often manifests as intermittent link outages, elevated bit error rates and/or degraded GPU performance, which can severely impact the throughput of batched AI workloads and compromise service-level objectives. Despite the availability of industry standard and vendor specific telemetry frameworks that provide GPU and other interconnect health metrics, these debug capabilities do not always fully consider all sources of mechanical, thermal, power, and firmware failures. Debug data is typically fragmented across multiple rack and node-level components complicating root-cause analysis. Aggregating debug data from multiple sources often slows root cause analysis and can significantly prolong debug and deployment timelines. This paper documents key learnings from rack-scale GPU deployments, offering a comprehensive view of the complexity of managing interdependent systems at both rack and node levels by examining failure modes, telemetry limitations, and the influence mechanical factors.

title: Adaptive PID Algorithm for Dynamic Control Systems with BLDC Motor Validation
authors: Md. Maruful Islam, Md. Mobin Khan, Md. Galib Hasan
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: For high precision applications of Brushless DC (BLDC) motor for instance in unmanned aerial vehicle, a precise speed control mechanism is required for smooth operation. Conventional PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative) controller struggles to tune its gains in critical variable load condition because it needs a manual controlling set up. This paper presents an adaptive PID controller algorithm that tunes its gains on its own by calculating its error within a very short time that makes it suitable for these types of applications. The proposed model is simulated in MATLAB/Simulink to analyze its performance where adaptive PID demostrates up to 51.69% lower settling time in higher speed, 31.57% lower overshoot than a conventional PID.

title: Aging Characteristics of Oil-Paper Insulation Under High-Frequency AC Electric Field
authors: Tao Zhu, Xin Tong, Qing He, Yunfei Wu, Qijia Xie, Guiyuan Li, Bo Qi, Tianhe Yang
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: The valve-side insulation of converter transformers is subjected to complex voltage stresses containing high-frequency harmonic components under actual operating conditions. However, research on the aging characteristics of oil-paper insulation under high-frequency components remains insufficient. To address this, a high-frequency electro-thermal aging platform was established in this study to conduct accelerated aging tests on oil-paper insulation under 100 kHz AC voltage. The aging conditions were set at an electric field strength of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$5 \text{kV} / \text{mm}$</tex> and a temperature of 120° C, with aging durations ranging from 0 to 48 hours. Frequency Domain Spectroscopy (FDS) and Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) were employed to systematically investigate the evolution of the macroscopic dielectric properties and microscopic molecular structure of the insulating paper. The results indicate that with increasing aging time, the relative permittivity and dielectric loss factor of the insulating paper increase significantly across the entire frequency range. After 48 hours of aging, the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\tan \delta$</tex> value at the low-frequency end increased from 0.01 to nearly 0.1, indicating significantly enhanced interfacial polarization and conductivity loss. FTIR analysis revealed a marked decrease in the intensity of the characteristic peak corresponding to the β-glycosidic bond (1001.4 <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{cm}^{-1}$</tex>), confirming the scission of cellulose molecular chains and a reduction in the degree of polymerization. This study demonstrates that high-frequency voltage components significantly accelerate the aging process of oil-paper insulation. The degradation mechanism involves the synergistic effect of cellulose oxidative depolymerization and the accumulation of polar by-products. The findings provide a theoretical basis for insulation condition assessment and operational maintenance of converter transformers.

title: Adaptive Reflection Loops for Large Language Models Reducing Hallucinations and Enhancing Robust Reasoning
authors: Mohammed Shakawat Hossen, Tuli Jahan, Adrishikhar Barua, Mohammed Zeaul Kader Opel
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated exceptional performance in natural language understanding and reasoning tasks. However, they often exhibit reasoning errors, overconfidence, and hallucinations, especially in multi-step problems where logical consistency is critical. This paper presents Adaptive Reflection Loops (ARL), a meta-cognitive framework that enhances the robustness and reliability of reasoning in LLMs through iterative self-critique, dynamic revision, and adaptive control. The proposed approach integrates three modules: an Initial Reasoning Generator that produces structured reasoning traces, a Reflection Module that evaluates intermediate logic and confidence, and an Adaptive Controller that determines the required number of reflection loops based on uncertainty levels. To ensure consistent evaluation, standardized preprocessing was applied to reasoning-focused datasets, including GSM8K, BIGBench Logical Deduction, and HotpotQA. Experimental results show that ARL achieves an average accuracy improvement of 8-12% and reduces hallucination rates by approximately 30% compared to conventional chain-of-thought and self-consistency techniques, while maintaining moderate computational overhead. These findings demonstrate that adaptive self-reflection mechanisms can significantly enhance the reliability of reasoning in LLMs, offering a practical path toward developing more trustworthy, self-regulating, and interpretable AI systems suitable for critical real-world applications.

title: DD-YOLO: Real-Time Pill Defect Detection Based on an Improved YOLO Framework with Feature Complementary Mapping
authors: Yiyue Hu
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: Pharmaceutical quality control relies heavily on the accurate detection of pill defects during production to ensure patient safety and regulatory compliance. Traditional manual inspection is prone to human error and fatigue, while conventional machine vision systems often struggle with complex surface variations and real-time processing requirements. This paper proposes DD-YOLO, a novel object detection framework based on YOLOv11, specifically designed for automated pill defect inspection in high-speed environments. The framework incorporates a Feature Complementary Mapping Module (FCM) to enhance spatial-semantic feature fusion and improve the detection of subtle anomalies such as micro-cracks and contamination. A dedicated pill defect dataset was constructed for this study, comprising 960 high-resolution images categorized into contamination, crack, and good classes to reflect real-world production scenarios. Extensive experiments were conducted to evaluate the performance of DD-YOLO against state-of-the-art detection models under identical conditions. The results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves mAP50 of 94.2%, outperforming the baseline YOLOv11 by 2.1% while maintaining computational efficiency. Furthermore, the system sustains a processing speed of 85 FPS, satisfying the stringent latency requirements of industrial production lines. A real-time visualization interface was also developed to facilitate operator monitoring and data logging. These findings confirm that DD-YOLO offers a robust and efficient solution for pharmaceutical manufacturing quality assurance, effectively balancing high detection accuracy with the low latency necessary for seamless integration into existing assembly processes.

title: Regression Prediction of Communication Signal Bit Error Rate under Anti-interference Based on Machine Learning Algorithms
authors: Shuang Wu, Zhenzhen Li
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: During the operation of modern communication systems, the complexity of the electromagnetic environment is constantly increasing, and the existence of various artificial and natural interferences seriously affects the stability of signal transmission, posing a great challenge to the precise prediction of communication signal signal-to-noise ratio and bit error rate. Machine learning algorithms, with their core advantages of being able to extract potential correlations from massive measured data and not relying on precise mathematical modeling, provide a new solution path for this problem. However, existing machine learning algorithms still have shortcomings such as insufficient feature extraction and slow model convergence speed in communication signal prediction applications. To address these issues, this paper proposes the DE-Transformer-BiLSTM regression algorithm. Firstly, it processes the data through correlation analysis and violin plot analysis, and then conducts comparative experiments by combining multiple machine learning algorithms. The experimental results show that the prediction performance of this algorithm is significantly superior to other traditional machine learning algorithms. Its mean square error, root mean square error, and mean absolute error are the lowest among all models, the average absolute percentage error remains at a low level, the coefficient of determination is as high as 0.988, and the fitting degree and prediction accuracy are both optimal. This research provides effective technical support for improving the signal transmission stability and prediction accuracy of modern communication systems.

title: Analysis and Design of a Dual-Type Outputs Wireless Power Transfer System
authors: Jiabin Wang, Dukju Ahn, Chao Liu, Hongjing Ouyang, Yunhui Shang, Xiangpeng Cheng, Yiming Zhang
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: In the application of wireless power transfer (WPT) systems, different types of load devices typically require distinct power supply inputs. This study introduces a WPT system that features a single-input dual-output (SIDO) configuration. The system adopts a single transmitting coil and receiving coil setup, thereby preventing additional cross-coupling interference between the coils, which eliminates the necessity for complex decoupling strategies and streamlines the design process. Furthermore, the receiver only contains a compensation inductor and a compensation capacitor, which ensures both the lightweight and cost-effectiveness. The feasibility of the system is confirmed through experiments, demonstrating its constant current (CC) and constant voltage (CV) output performance under zerovoltage switching (ZVS) operation, with the system achieving a peak efficiency of 88.1%.

title: IEL-YOLO: A Low-Light Robust Wood Defect Detection Method Based on YOLOv11
authors: Junjie Zheng
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: This paper proposes an improved wood surface defect detection method that integrates image enhancement with YOLO for smart manufacturing. To address low illumination, complex wood grain interference, and low defect contrast in production images, an Image Enhancement Layer is introduced at the network input. The module builds an HVI color space to decouple brightness and color information, uses polar mapping to overcome HSV hue discontinuity, and applies a learnable intensity compression function to suppress dark noise and improve contrast. A dual-branch design further separates illumination and texture modeling, producing clearer and more stable enhanced images for defect detection. Experiments on both a public dataset and a selfcollected industrial dataset, together with comparative and ablation studies, verify that the proposed method improves the detection of typical defects while preserving real-time performance, showing strong potential for industrial deployment.

title: Modeling the Impact of Network Link Dropout on Robust Federated Learning in Distributed Compute Environments
authors: Neena Imam, Tomasz Bednarz
abstract: Federated Learning (FL) is increasingly deployed in large-scale distributed compute environments spanning cloud, edge, and heterogeneous networked systems. In such systems, a central server aggregates model updates from distributed clients, making aggregation behavior a critical determinant of learning stability under failures. While prior research has focused on resilience against adversarial attacks, less attention has been paid to the interaction between robust aggregation methods and systemlevel communication failures, such as stochastic link dropout and partial client participation. In real-world distributed systems, link instability and intermittent connectivity are common and can significantly degrade learning stability and convergence. In this work, we present a modeling and simulation study that characterizes the impact of network link dropout on FL dynamics and evaluates the resilience properties of robust aggregation mechanisms under such conditions. Building on our prior work on parameter-free robust aggregation (rKrum), we simulate distributed FL systems subject to varying link dropout rates, client availability patterns, and heterogeneous participation. Our results show that robust aggregation methods originally designed to tolerate adversarial attacks also provide fault tolerance against communication-induced failures. Through systematic simulation across multiple dropout regimes, we demonstrate that our rKrum-based aggregation improves convergence stability and robustness compared to standard aggregation methods such as Federated Averaging (FedAvg). These findings highlight the dual role of robust aggregation as both a security mechanism and a system-level resilience primitive. The study provides design insights for FL systems operating in unreliable distributed environments and highlights the importance of incorporating communication dynamics for evaluating learning at scale.

title: Novel Test Framework for Hyperscale Qualification of AI Platforms at Microsoft Azure
authors: Anna Mary Mathew, Michael Chhor, Rigdha Acharya
abstract: As artificial intelligence platforms continue to scale in response to the growing demands of hyperscale cloud environments, the complexity and volume of hardware required to support these systems have increased dramatically. Traditional hardware validation methods—often manual, time-consuming, and limited in scope—are no longer sufficient to detect lowoccurrence, high-impact issues that can compromise production deployments. These elusive faults, which may only surface under specific workloads or environmental conditions, pose a significant risk to system reliability and operational efficiency.This paper introduces a novel, fully automated hyperscale test framework developed by Microsoft Azure to address the unique challenges of AI hardware qualification at scale. Designed to operate across hundreds of systems simultaneously, the framework orchestrates end-to-end workflows including system provisioning, firmware and software updates, test execution, telemetry capture, and result analysis. It leverages cloud-based storage for scalable data retention and integrates with PowerBI dashboards to provide real-time visualization and actionable insights to engineering teams.One of the framework’s key strengths lies in its flexibility. It supports customizable test plans and dynamic configuration options, enabling teams to target specific failure modes or performance bottlenecks. The telemetry pipeline captures granular data across CPU, GPU, memory, and interconnect subsystems, facilitating deep analysis and rapid root cause identification. Compatibility with both Windows and Linux environments ensures broad applicability across diverse hardware platforms and deployment scenarios.By automating the qualification process and embedding intelligence into every stage of the workflow, the framework empowers engineers to detect marginal hardware issues early, optimize system performance, and significantly reduce the risk of costly failures in production datacenters. Its deployment has led to measurable improvements in debug velocity, qualification throughput, and overall system reliability. Moreover, the framework’s modular design allows it to evolve alongside emerging AI workloads and hardware architectures, making it a future-proof solution for hyperscale validation.This work sets a new benchmark for AI hardware qualification, demonstrating how cloud-native automation and telemetry-driven analysis can transform traditional validation paradigms. It offers a compelling blueprint for other hyperscale providers seeking to enhance reliability, reduce operational risk, and accelerate innovation in the AI hardware space.

title: Digital Twin for Underwater Vehicles: MATLAB-Based Real-Time UROV Motion Simulation Frameworks for Predictive Monitoring
authors: Tashdid Ahmed Shitab, Ratul Debnath, Md. Emam Hossain Emon, Shah Md Newaz Sharif Ahad, Md Tamim Hassan, Al Sharear Hossain Rohan, Osman Md Amin
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Underwater Remotely Operated Vehicles (UROVs) are progressively used in inspection, monitoring and exploration activities but their operations are often constrained by environmental disturbances, nonlinear behavior and insufficient real-time feedback. This paper presents a digital twin framework for the TRITON UROV, developed using MATLAB-Simulink, to enable real-time motion simulation and predictive monitoring. The framework combines a 6 degrees-of-freedom (6-DOF) hydrodynamic model, thruster configuration mapping, and a multi-loop PID control system. A predictive monitoring layer overlays the baseline control loop to forecast system states, detect anomalies, and estimate power consumption. The performance of the proposed approach is proven by simulation case studies such as the lawnmower and helix paths, the plane-dive-and-turn operations, the disturbance rejection, and the thruster malfunction situations. Findings show that the digital twin not only enhances trajectory tracking accuracy but also provides actionable predictive insights for fault-tolerant and energy-aware operations. The research provides a demonstration on how digital twins can be incorporated into autonomous underwater vehicle systems.

title: Routing Entanglement in Complex Quantum Networks Using GHZ States
authors: Xin-An Chen, Caitao Zhan, Joaquin Chung, Jeffrey Larson
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Distributing entanglement to distant parties in a network is a central task in quantum information processing and quantum networking. However, the sensitivity of entangled states to loss necessitates the use of entanglement routing strategies. In this work, we adapt a previously proposed entanglement routing protocol based on Greenberger-Horne-Zeilinger (GHZ) measurements to explicitly consider the varying measurement success probabilities. We analyze the performance of our protocol in three network models: square grid networks, Waxman networks, and scale-free networks. We also analyze the performance on the network topology of SURFnet, a real-world network in the Netherlands. We find that in many network topologies, particularly ones with small average node degrees, this protocol enables a higher end-to-end entanglement distribution rate than that of the conventional Bell state protocol.

title: A Hybrid Energy Storage Optimization Configuration Algorithm Based on Improved Bat Algorithm
authors: Linhai Wang, Chao Wang, Xiaofeng Bao
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: Hybrid energy storage plays an important role in intelligent grid and renewable energy generation realization. To meet the dual requirements of stability and economy of microgrids, this paper proposes a hybrid energy storage optimization configuration algorithm based on the improved bat algorithm. A penalty cost of power fluctuation is introduced to improving the objective function of capacity configuration. Then, a nonlinear loudness dynamic balance is introduced in the pulse rate regulation mechanism to balance the global search and local search of the bat algorithm. And the Levy flight is utilized to enhance the global search ability and the ability to escape from local optimal solutions of the bat algorithm. Experiment results show that the convergence speed of the improved bat algorithm is 47.7 % higher than those of the comparison algorithms, providing a stronger optimization performance.

title: A Clean 2D Floquet Logical Qubit From a Purely Imaginary Phase Drive
authors: Cinque McFarlane-Blake
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: I present a disorder-free, two-dimensional Floquet stabilization mechanism that maintains a clean periodic orbit for more than <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 0 0 0}$</tex> cycles on a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$30 \times 30$</tex> square lattice. The drive consists of a global transverse field and a purely imaginary nearest-neighbor interaction with a slow spiral phase profile of maximum amplitude <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pi / 512$</tex>. Mean-field product-state simulations (size 1800, fourth-order Runge-Kutta, 200 steps per period) show that this imaginary phase twist passively suppresses Floquet heating for thousands of periods without disorder, feedback, or long-range couplings. A hardware test on a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3 \times 3$</tex> patch of an IBM superconducting device (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 0}$</tex> periods, approximately 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">6</sup> physical gates) produced no detectable subharmonic response, consistent with the expected sensitivity of the mechanism to global coherence and the destructive effect of individual addressing. To verify that the stabilized orbit encodes an effective logical manifold, I applied a global logical- <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$X$</tex> operation across three qualitatively distinct initial states (polarized, Néel, and random). In all cases the sign of the logical magnetization <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$Z_{L}$</tex> inverts while the magnitude remains small (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sim 10^{-3}$</tex>), indicating that the operation acts as a symmetry of the Floquet orbit rather than a state-preparation artifact. The resulting drive maps directly to a single global radio-frequency field plus a static magneticfield gradient on current two-dimensional trapped-ion platforms, suggesting a route toward robust low-frequency logical qubits and potential applications in isotope- or chirality-selective sensing.

title: Tangram: Encryption-friendly SNARK framework under Pedersen committed engines
authors: Gweonho Jeong, Myeongkyun Moon, Geonho Yoon, Hyunok Oh, Jihye Kim
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: SNARKs are frequently used to prove encryption, yet the circuit size often becomes large due to the intricate operations inherent in encryption. It entails considerable computational overhead for a prover and can also lead to an increase in the size of the public parameters (e.g., evaluation key). We propose an encryption-friendly SNARK framework, Tangram, which allows anyone to construct a system by using their desired encryption and proof system. Our approach revises existing encryption schemes to produce Pedersen-like ciphertext, including identity-based, hierarchical identity-based, and attribute-based encryption. Afterward, to prove the knowledge of the encryption, we utilize a modular manner of commit-and-prove SNARKs, which uses commitment as a ‘bridge’. With our framework, one can prove encryption significantly faster than proving the whole encryption within the circuit. We implement various Tangram gadgets and evaluate their performance. Our results show 12x - 3500x times better performance than encryption-in-the-circuit.

title: Unbiased Minimum-Variance Filter for Flexible Landing with Mixed State Constraints
authors: Zelong Chen, Pingyuan Cui, Dantong Ge, Shengying Zhu, Shisheng Cui
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Flexible landing on small celestial bodies involves mixed state constraints arising from bounded structural deformation and unit-quaternion attitude representation. Incorporating these constraints into unbiased minimum-variance (UMV) filtering is challenging because the corresponding gain-design problem is high-dimensional and non-convex. This paper develops a UMV-constrained filter for flexible landing with mixed state constraints. It is shown that, by introducing an increment vector and a tailored objective function, the original gain-design problem can be reformulated, without loss of optimality, as a bilevel optimization problem. The bilevel problem comprises an inner problem with a closed-form solution and an outer problem that optimizes over the low-dimensional increment vector, thereby enabling dimensionality reduction in the optimization variables. By analyzing the structure of its solution space, a spatial decomposition strategy is developed to split the outer problem into three subproblems. Two subproblems admit closed-form solutions derived via the Lagrange multipliers method, whereas the remaining one is solved numerically. In the considered flexible-landing scenario, the proposed bilevel reformulation and spatial decomposition strategy enable efficient computation of the UMV-constrained estimate, with an average runtime of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$1.23\times 10^{-2}$</tex-math></inline-formula>s. Simulation results further demonstrate that the proposed filter explicitly satisfies the mixed constraints and provides more accurate position and attitude estimates than the unconstrained filter and the estimated projection method.

title: Power Smoothing Strategy for PMSG Based on Adaptive Hybrid Weighted Filter Rotor Kinetic Energy Control and Linear Active Disturbance Rejection Pitch Control
authors: Shuaiqi Xu, Ying Zhu, Xinhang Zhou, Zhinong Wei
abstract: To address the impact of wind power fluctuations on grid stability under complex turbulent conditions, this paper proposes an adaptive hybrid weighted filter (HWF) algorithm based on power command differential variance, coupled with a linear active disturbance rejection variable pitch control strategy for wind power smoothing. First, an adaptive hybrid weighted filtering algorithm, founded on the variance of the power command differential, is developed. This algorithm dynamically fuses the power smoothing outputs of extended Kalman filter (EKF) and recursive mean filter (RMF) to yield a filtered power smoothing reference. The reference rotational speed is subsequently computed from the rotor kinetic energy. Secondly, a pitch controller incorporating linear active disturbance rejection is developed. The self-tuning bandwidth adjustment mechanism, grounded in Lyapunov stability theory, is introduced to enhance system robustness. Finally, simulation and experimental results demonstrate that the proposed strategy effectively suppresses power fluctuations under complex turbulent conditions while reducing fatigue loads on wind turbine components.

title: Correcting Wave-Induced Reflectance Anomalies in Pan-Sharpened Very High-Resolution Satellite Data
authors: Eike M. Sch¨utt, Daniyal Kazempour, Peer Kr¨oger, Natascha Oppelt
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: The increasing spatial resolution of satellite sensors– now reaching sub-meter scale– enables advanced applications in aquatic remote sensing, including highly detailed bathymetry or habitat mapping. However, this fine resolution also increases pixel-level susceptibility to wave glint and whitecap contami nation. These wave-induced reflectance anomalies (WIA) can severely degrade image quality and reduce the accuracy of derived products. Although strategic acquisition planning can help to minimize WIA, users often have limited control over satellite tasking. As a result, WIA contamination is difficult to prevent in practice. In this study, we present a novel WIA correction method for pan-sharpened Pl´ eiades-1 imagery that detects and reconstructs affected pixel values. WIA features are identified with an ensemble of similarity-weighted SVMs, which exploit spatial curvature and near-infrared reflectance. Detected anomalies are then corrected with a spatially adaptive gap filling technique. Our method operates on top-of-atmosphere reflectances, allowing users flexibility in further data preprocessing steps like atmospheric correction. Our approach demonstrates very high accuracy in WIA detection and shows comparable variance reductions in optically deep water compared to the popular Hedley glint correction and the Water Anomaly Filter. In optically shallow environments, our method outperforms these benchmarks by effectively removing WIA while preserving benthic spatial detail. Requiring minimal parameter tuning, the approach is well-suited for integration into automated preprocessing pipelines. It provides a practical tool for improving data quality and enhancing the reliability of downstream applications. Our approach is also easily adaptable to other very high-resolution sensors with similar spatial resolutions.

title: Winding and Magnet Temperature Decoupled Tracking for PMSM Eliminating Saturation using Small Position Signal Injection and Kalman Filter
authors: Xingyu Chen, Fan Wu, Beichen Ding, Chunyan Lai, Guodong Feng
abstract: This paper proposes a Kalman filter-based stator winding (SW) and permanent magnet (PM) temperature tracking approach for PM synchronous motors (PMSMs), where temperature tracking of SW and PM is decoupled and free from magnetic saturation and thus achieves improved performance. In the proposed approach, a small position signal is added to extract two decoupled temperature-sensitive measurements, a linear state-space model is constructed from the thermal model, and the Kalman filter is employed to accurately track the SW and PM temperatures by using the state-space model and measurements. The small position signal will not affect machine operation, while the temperature tracking can effectively avoid the cross influence between SW and PM. Moreover, there is no need for machine inductances and thus the temperature tracking is not affected by saturation as well as inverter distortion. In the proposed approach, the temperature tracking model includes secant and cosine operations of the current angle, which can effectively improve the signal-to-noise ratio especially under low-load conditions. The effectiveness of the proposed approach is validated on a laboratory test machine with extensive experiments under various load and speed conditions.

title: Physics-informed network for wavefront sensorless correction in satellite laser communications
authors: Zhidong Wen, Yu Hou, Feng Li, Ran Wang, Minxia Ding, Shunshuo Cai, Kunpeng Zhang, Xurui Peng, Song Yue, Zichen Zhang
abstract: Satellite laser communication, a cornerstone of next-generation global connectivity, is significantly challenged by wavefront distortions of received beams at spaceborne terminals. These distortions come from both intersatellite thermal deformation and atmospheric turbulence. While deep learning-based sensorless correction offers a promising alternative to wavefront sensor-based adaptive optics, existing data-driven approaches often lack integration with physical principles, particularly limiting their performance under strong turbulence. To address this, we propose the Laser Communication Phase Retrieval Unet (LCPR-Unet), a physics-informed neural network for sensorless wavefront sensing and correction. Its key innovation lies in a novel physical loss function that incorporates a varied-weight Strehl loss, which embeds optical performance metrics and their sensitivity to different aberrations directly into the training objective. We evaluate LCPR-Unet through static and dynamic correction experiments. In dynamic turbulence correction, the system achieves power gains of 3.4 dB, 8.59 dB, and 10.79 dB for D/r₀ = 5, 10, and 20, respectively, while reducing average centroid drift to 0.2, 4.52, and 7.6 pixels. These results demonstrate the robustness and effectiveness of our physics-informed approach across varying distortion regimes—from thermal effects to strong turbulence. Our work provides a viable pathway toward enhancing tracking accuracy and communication quality in future satellite laser terminals without relying on wavefront sensors.

title: A di/dt -Based Short-Circuit Protection Circuit With Decoupled Hard-Switching-Fault and Fault-Under-Load Protection Thresholds for 10 kV SiC MOSFETs
authors: Ruirui Chen, Yang Xu, Fred Wang, Hua Bai, Leon M. Tolbert
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: 10 kV silicon carbide (SiC) MOSFETs have been receiving increasing research attention in medium voltage (MV) applications. Compared with silicon (Si) IGBT counterparts, 10 kV SiC MOSFETs exhibit shorter short-circuit withstand time (SCWT), which requires fast and reliable short-circuit protection (SCP) circuits. The di/dt-based SCP circuit is a promising solution because of its fast response and simple implementation without additional current sensors. However, a major limitation of the di/dt-based method is its inability to detect the dc component of the current, resulting in prolonged detection time during a fault-under-load (FUL) event. This paper proposes a novel di/dt-based SCP circuit with decoupled SCP thresholds for hard-switching-fault (HSF) and FUL of 10 kV SiC MOSFETs. The proposed circuit differentiates HSF and FUL states by monitoring the gate voltage and automatically adjusts the SCP threshold voltage for FUL, thereby accelerating short-circuit (SC) detection for FUL events. In addition, a simple constant-current soft-turn-off (STO) circuit is proposed to minimize the SC turn-off overvoltage. Experimental results with a 10 kV SiC MOSFET module verified the effectiveness of the proposed circuits.

title: Mitigating Sybil Attacks in Underwater Acoustic Sensor Networks via Multi-Attribute Trust Cognition
authors: Shuaifeng Hao, Feng Tong, Haoci Zheng, Junhui Yao, Yifan Qiu, Weihua Jiang
abstract: Underwater acoustic sensor networks (UASNs) have been drawing extensive attention from the diverse marine fields. However, the inherently high bit error rates coupled with the wireless nature as well as the sound propagation characteristics of the underwater acoustic medium makes UASNs vulnerable to various malicious attacks, yet, limited consideration has been given to this security challenge. This paper presents the design of a novel Sybil attacks mitigation protocol for UASNs by formulating a comprehensive trust recognition mechanism via passive and active cognition at physical, MAC and routing layer. Specifically, the Technique for Order Preference by Similarity to Ideal Solution (TOPSIS) is adopted to optimize the multi-attribute trade-off between security and link reliability, the Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) is employed to balance the weights of multi-attributes under adverse UWA environment. While simulation results indicated an approximate 16% Packet Delivery Ratio (PDR) enhancement compared to mainstream protocols, field test conducted in shallow sea environments similarly demonstrate that the proposed scheme can achieve better network performance when suffering Sybil attacks.

title: A Global Cyber Threat Resilient Cloud Collaboration Framework for Geographically Distributed Data Centers
authors: Smruti Rekha Swain, Anshu Parashar, Deepika Saxena, Ashutosh Kumar Singh, Chung-Nan Lee
abstract: The rapid growth of geographically distributed cloud data centers has intensified the demand for secure, privacy-preserving, and resource-efficient collaboration among mutually untrusted cloud environments. This paper presents BlockFed, a blockchain-empowered federated learning framework designed to enable cyber-threat-resilient collaboration across geographically distributed data centers. In BlockFed, each data center independently trains local models using private workload data and shares only the computed gradients rather than raw data, with a centralized aggregation server through a secure blockchain layer. A Proof-of-Work consensus mechanism is employed to validate gradient transactions and maintain an immutable, tamper-resistant ledger, ensuring trust and integrity among participating entities. The centralized server aggregates blockchain-verified updates to construct a global model, which is iteratively redistributed to support collaborative learning. This integrated learning process enhances task-level resource demand prediction while simultaneously improving system security and operational efficiency across all participating data centers. Extensive simulations on the Google Cluster Dataset show that BlockFed outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, including ISTM, ETP-WE, First-Fit, Best-Fit, and Random-Fit, in resource utilization, power consumption, and active server reduction. BlockFed achieves up to 7-81% higher resource utilization, 41-58% lower power consumption, and 31-65% fewer active servers, while attaining a cyber-threat estimation accuracy of 93.19%.

title: Ethical Risk Assessment for AI-Driven Fraud Detection in Healthcare Claims
authors: Gayathri Surianarayanan, Srikanth Singireddy, Abinaya Mettupatti Sivagnanam
abstract: Healthcare providers have begun to use artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) models to help detect and prevent fraud, waste and abuse in their claims processing systems. Healthcare claims processing systems process large amounts of high dimensional data including claim dollar amount, diagnosis codes, procedure patterns, and time series of data from provider behavior. The use of AI for detecting healthcare fraud has been shown to improve both the efficiency of detecting fraud and increase the rate of detection; however, this technology may introduce some unaddressed ethics risks including: discrimination or bias in algorithms, lack of transparency in decision making, automated enforcement actions without human oversight, misuse of probabilistic outputs for fraud detection, and disclosure of confidential patient health information.Regulatory agencies will consider ethical failure to be an unethical claim denial based upon no justifiable reason, discriminatory review of certain types of providers, loss of public trust in providers and patients, and potential violation of regulatory provisions such as HIPAA and CMS Program Integrity Guidelines. Traditional model validations typically evaluate predictive performance of ML/AI models and do not provide a method to systematically assess, quantify, or mitigate ethics risks before deployment.The authors propose an Ethical Risk Assessment Framework (ERAF), which is designed to integrate ethics evaluation into the model validation lifecycle of AI/ML driven healthcare fraud detection systems. The ERAF uses a structured approach to assess four areas of ethics risk: data governance, fairness, explainability, and operational governance, as well as robustness of the system. By using simulated healthcare claims data and test cases to simulate different scenarios, the authors demonstrate that the ERAF can reduce the measurable level of ethics risk associated with AI/ML driven healthcare fraud detection systems and maintain predictive performance. The authors believe that their proposed approach will enable healthcare organizations to deploy AI/ML systems responsibly in high stakes fraud detection environments with the capability to audit their deployments to meet governance needs. The framework was demonstrated using a synthetic dataset created with the goal of mimicking the most common features of healthcare claims data, in order to provide an initial feasibility assessment, as opposed to a full-scale production deployment study.

title: Safe Reinforcement Learning for Autonomous Electric Vehicles: A Comparative Analysis of RL
authors: Md Asraful Islam, Abdullah Al Mamun, Md Kamal Hossen, Md Mithun Ali
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Autonomous vehicles (AVs) represent a key frontier in transportation innovation, offering advancements in safety, efficiency, and accessibility. Reinforcement Learning (RL), a specialized branch of machine learning, supports adaptive decision-making and autonomous control by enabling agents to learn optimal behaviors through interaction with dynamic environments. This study integrates safe reinforcement learning (Safe RL) techniques into diverse algorithms and simulated driving scenarios to enhance both performance and compliance with safety requirements. Specifically, we evaluate Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), Deep Q-Networks (DQN), and Constrained Policy Optimization (CPO) within safety-constrained environments. Using the CARLA simulator, we model complex tasks such as urban driving, highway navigation, and emergency response. Benchmarking results are assessed through key metrics, including collision rate, traffic rule compliance, and computational efficiency. Among the algorithms, the CPO method demonstrates superior performance, achieving the lowest collision rate and the highest level of safety compliance. These results demonstrate the ability of Safe RL to significantly improve the reliability and robustness of autonomous vehicles in dynamic and unpredictable real-world conditions.

title: Gate/Channel Interface Co-Design for Highly Reliable 3D FE NAND
authors: Runhao Han, Jia Yang, Tao Hu, Mingkai Bai, Yajing Ding, Xianzhou Shao, Wenliang Liu, Junshuai Chai, Hao Xu, Xiaolei Wang, Wenwu Wang, Tianchun Ye
abstract: We propose a gate and channel interlayers (G.IL/C.IL) co-design scheme to improve the reliability of metal-insulator-ferroelectric-insulator-semiconductor (MIFIS) FeFET. By implementing a thick high- <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{\kappa}$</tex> AHA G.IL and a nitridated C.IL, we effectively suppress charge detrapping for retention and reduce the electric field in the G.IL (EG.IL) for endurance. We achieve an MIFIS with 3-bit operations under +14/-13V operating voltage (6 V memory window, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$5 \times 10^{5}$</tex> endurance cycles, and only 2.7% retention loss after 10 years). The devices also exhibit strong immunity to read disturb. Reliability tests under elevated temperatures also demonstrate excellent endurance, data retention, and read-disturb immunity. Furthermore, this scheme is verified on the FE vertical gate-all-around (VGAA) NAND cell <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\mathbf{L}_{\text{gate }}=\mathbf{6 0 n m}$</tex>) and also improves its reliability. This work presents a practical gate stack design for high reliability 3D FE NAND.

title: A Quantum Variational Approach to Prototypical Recurrent Unit
authors: Mahyar Sadeghi Garjan, Tommaso Cesari, Michel Barbeau
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: We introduce a lightweight Quantum Prototypical Recurrent Unit (QPRU) that requires significantly fewer parameters than both classical recurrent architectures, such as Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) and Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU), and quantum variants, including Quantum LSTM (QLSTM) and Quantum GRU (QGRU). Despite its compact design, the QPRU achieves competitive forecasting performance, matching state-of-the-art baselines while offering important structural and practical advantages, including enhanced scalability and a reduced number of trainable parameters.

title: Audio-Driven Landmark Regression (ADLR): A Multi-Task Transformer Framework for Explainable Lip-Sync Deepfake Detection
authors: Kalyan Kanti Halder, Joy Debnath, Md Abdul Masud
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Detecting lip-sync deepfake videos in which real speech is combined with fake movements of the person's lips - has become more difficult as these forgeries has become more sophisticated. We present a relatively unique approach which makes modifications to use Landmark Regression, a method typically used for giving as a detection signal about the talking faces. Our framework, known as Audio-Driven Landmark Regression (ADLR) solves the problem of detecting deepfakes by using multi-task learning that is an explicit modeling of audiovisual synchrony. The method works by matching cropped pieces of the mouths into equivalent one second pieces of audio. We extract the spatiotemporal features by using TimeSFormer for video and a HuBERT based encoder for audio and then route these through two parallel heads; one that predicts lip-corner landmarks in the audio-only mode and another that establishes video clips as authentic or synthetic. Training these components make robust detector to achieve impressive performance <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$- 9 9. 9 0 \%$</tex> ROC-AUC and 99.83% accuracy on FakeAVCeleb/VoxCeleb2 and 99.50% ROC-AUC, 99.53% accuracy on DeepFakeTIMIT/VidTIMIT. More importantly, the method describes landscapes using easy-to-understand landmarks, which makes the findings both highly reliable and easy to present.

title: Design and Performance Study of a Novel Three-Directional Tuned Particle Damper
authors: Yu Zhou, Wangqiang Xiao, Zhiqin Cai
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: Conventional Tuned Mass Dampers (TMD) exhibit limitations in controlling multi-directional coupled vibrations, while Particle Dampers (PD) lack frequency selectivity. To address the three-dimensional vibration control requirements of multi-degree-of-freedom structures, this paper proposes a composite three-directional tuned particle damper device and its design methodology. Leveraging the geometric symmetry of a regular tetrahedron, the device achieves identical stiffness characteristics in the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$X, Y$</tex>, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$Z$</tex> directions, requiring only a single tuning to effectively control vibrations from any direction, thereby overcoming the installation direction limitations of traditional TMDs. Based on the principle of virtual work, a dynamic model of the controlled structure with the attached damper is established, a closed-form solution for the system stiffness matrix is derived, and a spring stiffness design formula based on the target frequency is proposed, achieving optimal three-directional tuning design. Multi-body dynamics simulation results demonstrate that the damper possesses identical natural frequencies in the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Y}$</tex>, and Z directions, validating the consistency of its three-directional dynamic characteristics. Experimental results show that after damper installation, the vibration responses of the cantilever beam in three directions are significantly suppressed, with vibration reduction efficiencies reaching <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$76.0 \%, 77.2 \%$</tex>, and 74.5 %, respectively. Furthermore, when the damper installation surface is changed from the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{Z}$</tex>-direction to the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{Y}$</tex>-direction, its control effect on Z-direction vibration remains essentially consistent with that of Z-direction installation, with a difference in vibration reduction efficiency of only <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2. 2 \%}$</tex>.

title: Study on In-Orbit Single Event Effect Detection System for Nanoscale SRAM
authors: Yinghui Liu, He Wang, Lei Luo, Nana Huai, Yan Zhao, Jingze Zhang
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: Nanoscale SRAM exhibits variations in Single Event Effect (SEE) sensitivity and novel SEE phenomena. This paper focuses on nanoscale SRAM, investigating in-orbit SEE detection methods, developing an in-orbit SEE detection system, and completing in-orbit SEE detection for nanoscale SRAM under real space radiation environments. This work provides in-orbit data support for studying SEE sensitivity variations and novel SEE mechanisms in nanoscale SRAM, as well as for correcting groundbased SEE simulation tests and in-orbit prediction methods for nanoscale SRAM.

title: A GRU-TSSA-iTransformer-Based Framework for Multi-Step Wind Power Load Forecasting
authors: Fengzhe Wu
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: This work presents a novel hybrid architecture-GRU-TSSA-iTransformer-for multi-step wind power load forecasting. We first construct multivariate time-series samples via a sliding window and temporal feature encoding. Each sample is embedded and sequen-tially processed through a GRU layer for short-term dependency extraction, a time-series self-attention decay (TSSA) mechanism to suppress noise and enhance global correlations, and an improved Transformer encoder to fuse deep representations. A final linear mapping yields the next three-step predictions. On a public 15-minute interval dataset, our approach achieves an average <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">${R}$</tex>-squared of 0.9675 and MAPE of 29.92%, outperforming the baseline Transformer, and demonstrating competitive absolute error relative to simple historical repetition. Compared with classical regressors-linear regression, random forest and XGBoost - the proposed model offers a favorable balance between variance explanation and robustness in multistep forecasting.

title: Hybrid Quantum Temporal Convolutional Networks
authors: Junghoon Justin Park, Maria Pak, Sebin Lee, Samuel Yen-Chi Chen, Shinjae Yoo, Huan-Hsin Tseng, Jiook Cha
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Quantum machine learning models for sequential data face scalability challenges with complex multivariate signals. We introduce the Hybrid Quantum Temporal Convolutional Network (HQTCN), which combines classical temporal windowing with a quantum convolutional neural network core. By applying a shared quantum circuit across temporal windows, HQTCN captures long-range dependencies while achieving significant parameter reduction. Evaluated on synthetic NARMA sequences and high-dimensional EEG time-series, HQTCN performs competitively with classical baselines on univariate data and outperforms all baselines on multivariate tasks. The model demonstrates particular strength under data-limited conditions, maintaining high performance with substantially fewer parameters than conventional approaches. These results establish HQTCN as a parameter-efficient approach for multivariate time-series analysis.

title: DQA-DETR: Dynamic Query Aggregation for Oriented Object Detection in Remote Sensing Images
authors: Yongchen Yao, Songwei Pei, Yuanzhou Huang, Qian Li, Shangguang Wang
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Oriented object detection in remote sensing images faces challenges, such as arbitrary object orientations and uneven distribution of object quantities. Although detection transformer (DETR)-based methods have recently achieved promising progress in remote sensing object detection, they still face challenges when using a fixed query budget in scenes where object quantities vary significantly due to large-scale imaging and complex spatial distributions. A fixed query budget can be poorly matched to such variability, producing redundant queries in sparse scenes and many high-quality negatives under one-to-one matching, which may hinder optimization efficiency and degrade performance. To address this issue, we propose a transformer-based dynamic query aggregation detection framework (DQA-DETR) to alleviate query redundancy in remote sensing scenarios. DQA-DETR incorporates three key modules: aggregation center predictor, aggregation center selector, and query aggregator, which are responsible for predicting the required number of representative queries, selecting high-quality aggregation centers, and aggregating semantically related queries via a multihead attention mechanism, respectively. This strategy adaptively adjusts the number of queries involved in matching according to the object quantity in each image, effectively reducing redundancy and enhancing the model’s adaptability in both sparse and dense scenes. Extensive experiments on the DOTA v1.0 and v1.5 datasets demonstrate that DQA-DETR improves detection performance, while maintaining the end-to-end advantages of DETR, exhibiting strong competitiveness among state-of-the-art methods.

title: Investigation into Temperature Dependent 2T-nC FeRAM Readout Operation
authors: Siyuan Li, Jiahui Duan, Shan Deng, Sizhe Ma, Kai Ni, Rudra Biswas, Vijaykrishnan Narayanan, Rajiv Joshi
abstract: In this work, we study the temperature dependence of the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2 T}$</tex>-nC ferroelectric random-access memory (FeRAM) cell. We experimentally demonstrate the successful fabrication of a 2T-64C cell that shows robust memory operation with clearly distinguishable '0' and '1' states. Characterization at various temperatures reveals uniform readout currents across all 64 ferroelectric capacitors. Increase of the current in state '0' originates from the increased read transistor current. Furthermore, we provide a comprehensive evaluation of the pattern dependence of the read operation at different temperatures. We show that simple delay between the sensing of two capacitors suffer from the pattern dependence while a discharge of the floating node between the two reads can eliminate the dependence at a cost of read endurance.

title: Analysis and Measurement of Nonlinear Characteristics of EGR Valves in Hybrid Vehicle Engines
authors: Chunsheng Mao, Pandeng Meng, Zijun Wang, Chao Yuan
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: EGR technology is vital for balancing performance and emissions in hybrid engines by optimizing gas recirculation and suppressing knock. This paper investigates an electronically controlled EGR valve driven by a Limited Angle Torque Motor or LATM. By analyzing model uncertainties—including torquecurrent and torque-angle nonlinearities, hysteresis, and mechanical errors—the study defines the actuator's complex mechanism. An ARM-based master-slave test system was developed to measure real-time output characteristics across various working angles. The results provide a robust electromagnetic torque model and experimental validation, offering critical support for high-precision controller design.

title: Data-Model Hybrid-Driven Method for Topology Identification and Impedance Estimation in Low-Voltage Distribution Networks
authors: Yiqi Qiu, Jun Su, Yaoyi Wu
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: To address the difficulty of accurate topology acquisition and the long-term reliance on archival assumptions for line parameters in low-voltage distribution areas, this paper proposes a data-model hybrid-driven approach for topology identification and impedance estimation based on node power correlation and an impedance network model. Using feeder-box voltage, current, and power data, a topology connection matrix is generated by least-squares polynomial fitting and verified with electrical constraints to improve accuracy. On this basis, voltage drops and complex currents for each branch and phase are calculated to estimate complex impedances, which are then decomposed into resistance, reactance, and impedance magnitude. In addition, robustness to measurement noise and missing data is evaluated. A real case in Fujian Province shows that the proposed method can accurately recover the primary wiring topology and effectively reflect branch impedance levels and phase-to-phase differences, providing reliable data for structure verification and key branch analysis in the low-voltage distribution area.

title: Deep Reinforcement Learning for Dynamic Task Scheduling and Optimization in Multi-Robot Collaborative Sorting Systems
authors: Hongwen Liu
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: Multi-robot collaborative sorting in intelligent logistics and smart manufacturing faces challenges in dynamic task scheduling due to real-time arrivals, varied robot capabilities, and task priorities. This paper introduces a deep reinforcement learning framework for optimizing multi-robot task scheduling in such settings. We model the problem as a Markov Decision Process (MDP) considering task priorities, robot differences, and spatial-temporal constraints. An actor-critic attention network (AC-Attn-DRL) produces near-optimal task sequences online, using priority embeddings and dynamic state inputs. Our method allows robots to adapt scheduling in real time, optimizing makespan, priority satisfaction, and robot utilization. Simulations in warehouse sorting with different task loads and team sizes show our approach outperforms traditional heuristics (Hungarian and genetic algorithms) and baseline DRL, cutting task time by 15.3%, improving priority satisfaction by 22.7%, and boosting computational efficiency. The framework also generalizes well to new tasks and robot setups

title: A Unified Platform for Staff Assignment Using ECARGO and Group Role Assignment
authors: Evan Wells, Haibin Zhu
abstract: Optimal assignment problems often involve a wide range of constraints and contextual considerations, making it nearly impossible for a single algorithm to account for every possible scenario. To address this complexity, we present a unified platform for staff assignment that integrates multiple constraints and decision factors within a single adaptable system. The platform is built upon the E-CARGO model and the Role-Based Collaboration (RBC) framework, both of which leverage an object-oriented approach to solving complex, real-world coordination and optimization problems. It incorporates algorithms such as Group Role Assignment (GRA), Group Multi-Role Assignment (GMRA), and selected variants of Group Role Assignment with Multiple Objectives (GRA++). Usability testing demonstrates that the platform effectively supports diverse operational scenarios, including role conflicts, overlapping responsibilities, and multi-role assignments. The system enhances decision-making and improves assignment quality by adapting to user-defined needs, making it well-suited for practical applications in staff and task allocation.

title: A Chaos-Enhanced Dung Beetle Optimizer for 3D Global Path Planning of a Biomimetic Amphibious Robot
authors: Chenqing Sun, Xiaoqiang Dai, Jinxing Liu
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: Due to the strong environmental adaptability and flexibility of bionic amphibious robots, such robots have become a frontier field in robotics research. However, amphibious robots face enormous challenges when crossing different physical media, including complex dynamic changes, kinematic discontinuity and environmental uncertainty. This needs appropriate path planning algorithms. Therefore, a Chaos-enhanced Dung Beetle Optimizer (C-DBO) has been proposed for the 3D path planning problem of the robot inspired by the black ghost knifefish. This method incorporates the Circle chaotic mapping strategy to enhance the global search capability, and a composite cost function reflecting medium-specific resistance is constructed. Simulation results show that compared with the standard DBO, PSO and GWO, C-DBO significantly improves convergence stability, reduces energy consumption and enhances path smoothness.

title: FinFet LDMOS Prompt Shift Effect on Wi-Fi RF Power Amplifier Performance
authors: Arul Balasubramaniyan, Abdellatif Bellaouar
abstract: This paper discusses “Prompt Shift” effect observed in LDMOS devices when stressed at high voltages in FinFet technologies for Wi-Fi/IOT RF Power Amplifiers (PA). The measured performance change in RF PA due to this prompt shift is demonstrated. A design technique to mitigate the LDMOS prompt shift in RF PA and a novel, necessary on-chip stress circuit to achieve model to hardware correlation for Wi-Fi/IOT RF PA is illustrated.

title: Toward an NLP Toolkit for Extracting Requirements from Standards
authors: Naqash Ali, Ryan Carmichael, David C. Gross, Matthew Bosworth, Carmen E. Araujo, Karl Schoder
abstract: Engineering standards are critical to interoperability, safety, and compliance in complex systems, yet their document-centric and unstructured formats hinder adoption in digital engineering (DE) workflows. Building on prior work presented at the IEEE Electric Ship Technologies Symposium (ESTS) 2025 in Case Study in Leveraging Existing Standards: Knowledge Elicitation through External Tools and Integration with the System Model, this paper advances an automated approach for extracting requirements from standards and integrating them into system models.The method extracts text from Portable Document Format (PDF) files, cleans and reconstructs paragraphs to preserve multipage continuity, and parses requirement hierarchy using numbering and structural conventions for military standards dictated by the Department of Defense. A plugin imports the tagged hierarchical output directly into the modeling environment and instantiates Systems Modeling Language (SysML) requirements with their containment hierarchy and identifiers intact, enabling requirement-to-architecture traceability within the system model. Results of testing on a military standard document demonstrate reduced manual curation effort, improved model coverage, and preserved compliance semantics. This work contributes a reusable standards-to-model workflow and produces structured outputs that support future generalization using natural language processing (NLP) and machine learning (ML), aligning with ongoing National Science Foundation (NSF) efforts in knowledge automation and cross-domain interoperability for DE workflows.

title: Effect of Intelligent Cybersecurity Systems on Data Protection and Breach Reduction in Bangladeshi IT Firms
authors: Kh. Mustafizur Rahman, Md Mehedi Hasan Emon, Mohammad Shariful Islam, Mowdud Ahmed, Saleh Ahmed Jalal Siam, Hasibul Hasan Rifat
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: The purpose of this study was to examine how PST, PV, RE, SE, and PRC impacted on DPBR in Bangladeshi IT companies. This paper used a quantitative design, which involved sampling 178 IT professionals with the help of a structured questionnaire and processed the answers with the PLS-SEM. PST, PV, SE, and PRC were found to have significant influence on DPBR but no significant influence was noted with RE. The model accounted 84 percent variance in DPBR, which is a high predictability of the chosen constructs. In practice, the study offers advice to IT managers and policymakers to focus on cybersecurity investment, staff self-efficacy, and perceived response cost-management in order to improve the results in data protection. At the social level, it helps to make digital practices safer within organizations and encourages the implementation of intelligent cybersecurity systems by the wider range of emerging economies. The research is novel that the application of PMT to the Bangladesh case of IT, providing empirical evidence of drivers of ICS adoption. Such limitations are convenience sampling and crosssectional design.

title: Lifetime Model for $p$-Contact Induced Failures in Monolithic GaAs-on-Si Nano-Ridge Lasers Considering Junction Heating
authors: Ping-Yi Hsieh, David Coenen, Barry O'Sullivan, Artemisia Tsiara, Debi P. Panda, Anjanashree M. R. Sharma, Didit Yudistira, Bernardette Kunert, Joris Van Campenhout, Ingrid De Wolf
abstract: A competing bimodal lifetime distribution, incorporating the Joule heating effect, is adopted to describe the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$p$</tex> contact induced optical power failures occurring in GaAs nanoridge lasers monolithically integrated on 300 mm Si. Accelerated aging tests are performed at various temperatures and current densities to better understand the physics of the two dominant failure mechanisms - impurity diffusion and GaAs elemental interdiffusion. The activation energies and power law exponents for both mechanisms are determined by calibrating the junction temperatures using an experimentally validated electrothermal model. The former mechanism shows a weak current density dependence, suggesting a neutral diffusion source. In addition, the latter mechanism is linked to a recombination-enhanced process. Finally, lifetime projection is achieved by leveraging this model, enabling proposals of reliability-improved designs.

title: Characterization on Plasma Induced Damage in Thick Oxide Device at FinFET Technology
authors: Tae-Young Jeong, Bongyong Jeong, Hyewon Shim, Sungman Rhee, Shinyoung Chung, Jong-Ho Lee
abstract: In this study, we investigated the plasma-induced damage (PID) characteristics of 14 nm FinFET with thick gate oxides, which are utilized in diverse applications such as highvoltage input/output (I/O), capacitors, and more. Test patterns were designed to examine various factors, including gate area, antenna ratio (the ratio of metal or via to gate area), antenna layer stack options, and the presence or absence of diodes. The experimental results of PID measurements for each metal layer and stack option revealed that plasma-induced damage in thick oxide devices tends to accumulate. Furthermore, we discovered that the allowable cumulative antenna ratio (cAR), which ensures safety from PID, decreases as the gate area increases, following a power-law dependence. Additionally, the experimental results for diodes showed significant differences in discharging efficiency depending on the junction type. The design guide derived from this study is expected to mitigate quality and reliability risks caused by PID and enhance circuit design efficiency.

title: Addressing a Device in a Quantum Network: A Quantum Approach Including Routing
authors: Alexander Pirker
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: In this work we propose an addressing scheme for quantum networks which relies on quantum states held by devices. Quantum network devices use their address state together with a request state that encodes the tasks to be executed. Our approach not only removes the necessity to classically communicate addresses, but also the need to communicate the operations a device must apply. It turns out that utilizing entanglement to encode addresses of devices in a quantum network leads to interesting applications such as overlaying different network states. We present a distributed quantum routing protocol using entanglement that coherently selects a route in a network of Bell-states for controlled-teleportation and lastly we prove that addressing using quantum states is equivalent to performing tasks in superposition in a quantum network.

title: Characterization of Well Charging-Based Plasma Induced Damage (PID) in FDSOI
authors: Rameez Raja Shaik, Elodie Ebrard, Manjunatha Prabhu, Tam Lyn Tan
abstract: Plasma-induced damage (PID) is a key reliability issue in advanced semiconductor nodes, primarily caused by antenna charging during plasma processing. Traditional metal-to-gate ratio rules mitigated PID in older technologies but are inadequate for modern layouts. Recent studies on bulk technologies show that well-based aggressors, such as isolated p-wells in deep n-wells, create indirect charging paths that stress gate dielectrics. Building on the limitations of antenna rules, this work extends PID analysis to Fully Depleted Silicon-On-Insulator (FDSOI) technologies, where the buried oxide (BOX) layer introduces additional charge isolation and new failure modes. Experiments on specialized test structures designed for FDSOI, incorporating gate and drain victims with well-based aggressors and stressed at elevated voltages for thin-oxide core devices and thick-oxide I/O devices, reveal a strong dependence on well geometry. Smaller well area when combined with large metal-to-gate ratio result in systematic high PID fail-rate, whereas large well area mitigate damage. These experimental outcomes demonstrate that traditional antenna rules are insufficient for advanced nodes and highlight the need for new design methodologies that incorporate well-charging antenna rules and structurallevel analysis. By specifically accounting for aggressor wells and isolation from BOX, these enhanced frameworks can improve robustness, yield and long-term reliability in scaled CMOS and FDSOI technologies.

title: Accurate Beam Tracking for Robust USV-to-Satellite Transmission Under Wave Fluctuation
authors: Jinsong Yu, Cunqing Hua, Lingya Liu, Pengwenlong Gu, Mingcheng He
abstract: In satellite-assisted maritime communications, wave-induced rotational motions of unmanned surface vessels (USVs) cause severe beam misalignment with satellites, significantly degrading transmission performance. To overcome this challenge, we propose equipping the USV with a smart metasurface-based antenna to enable adaptive beamforming that dynamically compensates for USV rolling in harsh sea conditions. To facilitate effective beam tracking under long feedback delays, we design a transmission framework that ensures accurate channel state information (CSI) acquisition. Within this framework, a BLTNet-based model is developed to predict the instantaneous rolling angle of the USV, which is then used to infer the USV-to-satellite CSI for beamforming optimization. We further formulate a stochastic optimization problem to maximize the ergodic achievable rate of the uplink transmission and design a robust beamformer accordingly. Simulation results demonstrate the high accuracy of the proposed rolling angle prediction model under various settings and sea states, confirming that the corresponding robust beamforming design substantially enhances the USV-to-satellite transmission performance.

title: A Machine Learning-Driven NWDAF Architecture For Intelligent 5G Core Networks
authors: Tsung-Fang Chen, Chien Chen, Jyh-Cheng Chen
publish: Proceedings of the 1st free5GC World Forum 2025
abstract: None

title: EDLB0: A Lightweight Garbage Classification Using a Modern CNN Model with Explainable AI
authors: Md. Kazi Ikram Uddin, Nazmul Hasan Naeem, Omer Faruq
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: According to studies, the total amount of municipal solid waste produced worldwide is expected to increase massively in a few years, creating a serious environmental and social threat. Manual processing and other traditional garbage disposal techniques are laborious, costly, and cause serious health risks to laborers. In this regard, automated image-based garbage classification systems offer an alternative by making recycling solutions scalable and effective. In this study, we propose a deep learning framework that uses EfficientNetB0 as a lightweight but powerful feature extractor, combined with advanced augmentation strategies, including MixUp and label smoothing, to address dataset imbalance and improve generalization. We conduct a rigorous comparative analysis against heavyweight (ResNet50) and lightweight (MobileNetV2) baselines. Our proposed model achieves state-of-the-art accuracy of 99.56%, outperforming ResNet50 <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(98.61 \%)$</tex>. For optimal stability and efficiency, training is performed in two phases: a head-only training stage and a fine-tuning stage with cosine-decayed learning rates and AdamW optimization. To improve clarity and trust, we further integrate Explainable AI (XAI) with Grad-CAM visualizations and quantitative evaluation based on the Insertion and Deletion metrics, which show the selective regions used in the decisionmaking process. The proposed model beats traditional methods in terms of test accuracy and ROC-AUC scores, according to experiments on the Kaggle Garbage Classification dataset. It also provides clear outputs that support its execution in actual recycling facilities.

title: Development and Implementation of Real-Time Data Management Monitoring for Miniature Electric Locomotive
authors: Kittapon Chatwongtong, Teeranart Chatchawanthatri, Pittayut Kleangklom, Tanapat Hanruk, Thanakrith Khanthong, Tosaphol Ratniyomchai
publish: Proceedings of the 2026 15th International Conference on Informatics, Environment, Energy and Applications
abstract: None

title: Enhancing Cold-Start Sequential Recommendation with Large Language Model-derived Semantic Graph and Semantic Distribution Alignment
authors: Wanna Cui, Hak-Keung Lam

title: DiffuST: a Latent Diffusion Model for Spatial Transcriptomics Denoising_supp1-3690748.pdf
authors: Jiajie peng
abstract: Spatial transcriptomics technologies have enabled comprehensive measurements of gene expression profiles while retaining spatial information, with most platforms also providing matched pathology images. However, noise resulting from low RNA capture efficiency and experimental steps needed to keep spatial information may corrupt the biological signals and obstruct analyses. Here, we develop a latent diffusion model DiffuST to denoise spatial transcriptomics. DiffuST employs a graph autoencoder and a pre-trained model to extract different-scale features from spatial information and pathology images. Then, a latent diffusion model is leveraged to map different scales of features to the same space for denoising. The evaluation based on various spatial transcriptomics datasets showed the superiority of DiffuST over existing denoising methods. Furthermore, the results demonstrated that DiffuST can enhance downstream analysis of spatial transcriptomics and yield significant biological insights.
